Original_Search_Title: CPI Indicates Continued Improvement in Economy
Headline: Nigeria: CPI Indicates Continued Improvement in Economy
Body_Text:
Purchasing power of consumers in the last two months ending the last quarter of the year has continued to record an improvement as the composite consumer price index (CPI), the parameter used in measuring change in prices paid for goods and services in both Urban and Rural areas witnessed continued downward trend in October, 2002.
According to Federal Office of Statistics (FOS) report the index recorded 4797.9 in October, 2002 as against 4967.9 noticed in September, 2002, the decline was occassioned by decreases in prices of food items and the fluctuating prices of transport fare, personal household goods and medicaments. The urban and rural indices the office stated fell by 2.8 and 3.6 percent respectively in the month under discourse.
FOS noted that in the 12 months to October, 2002 the average consumer price level went up by 5.4 percent, this was lower than 10.0 percent observed when making a similar comparison in September, 2002 as 7.9 and 4.9 increases were recorded in the urban and rural indices respectively in the same period.
The percentage change in the CPI for the 12 months period ending October, 2002over the average CPI for the previous 12 months was 13.6 percent.
This in effect means that a fixed market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with N100.00 during the 12 months ending October, 2002 sold for N113.6 on the average for the corresponding period ending October, 2002, indicates a decline when compared to 14.8 percent obtained by making a similar comparison in September, 2002.
In the composite food index (CFI), FOS reported a drop by 6.3 percent in October, 2002, this decline in index was attributed to decreases in prices of yam, plantain, beans and sweet potato even though the CFI was higher than the corresponding level a year ago by 0.7 percent.
However, the average annual rate of rise of the index recorded 16.4 percent for the 12 months period ending October, 2002.
In comparison therefore, a market basket of consumer goods and services bought with N100.00 during the 12 months to September 2001 sold for N114.8 in September, 2002.
The CFI in September, 2002 went down by 1.5 percent the drop according to FOS was also as a result of decreases in prices of yam tuber, plantain and sweet potato.
The office reported also that the level of CFI was higher in the corresponding year by 5.7 percent the annual rate of rise witnessed 19.4 percent for the 12 months period ending September, 2002.
The "All item farm produce"which excludes prices of agricultural product went up by 1.6 percent in September, 2002 .
In furtherance to this, FOS reported that in the 12 months to September, 2002 the index increased by 16.2 percent and the average annual rate of increase was 7.6 percent for the12 months to September, 2002.

Original_Search_Title: Ensuring Road Safety
Headline: Nigeria: Ensuring Road Safety
Body_Text:
Kayode Olagunju, Sector Commander of the Lagos State Federal Road
Safety Corps, says road users must imbibe a disciplined road culture
The Lagos State Sector Commander of the Federal Road Safety Corp,
FRSC, Kayode Olagunju, can be said to have a passion for souls. Like
someone who has entered into a vow, he is determined to ensure that
road users in the country stay alive even if it means infringing on
their fundamental human rights, as he maintains that "nobody has the
right to kill himself." Olagunju cuts the picture of a man of great
resolve.
He waxes resolute on issues that are fundamental to the operations of
the road safety body. This will be evident, for instance, when you
listen to him talk about the need for drivers to make use of the seat
belt: "We've also observed that people know the importance of seat
belt when driving but they choose to ignore it ... By the time we
begin to enforce its use next year, the people will know we can bark
and bite." The FRSC will begin to enforce the compulsory use of seat
belt on the road in January 2003. Seat belts properly fastened have
proved to save the lives of many vehicle occupants, especially the
driver, when accidents occurred. And often when a vehicle
somersaults, properly fastened seat belts reduce the risk of
injuries, Olagunju emphasized.
His determination can also be glimpsed from his on-going battle with
touts over new vehicle number plates and driver's licensing scheme.
It is widely believed that it is this duel that accounted for the 13
July 2002 attempted murder on the life of the FRSC boss.
It was interesting watching steely resolve plastered on Olagunju's
face when he, in company of two of his officers, paid a courtesy
visit on the Independent Communications Network Limited, ICNL,
publishers of TheNEWS, TEMPO and PMNews. During the visit, on 28
November 2002, he urged Nigerians to view road safety as a collective
responsibility so as to save more lives from untimely death. He
revealed that current statistics showed that between 1992 to 2002, a
period of 10 years, 170,964 road accidents were recorded on Nigerian
roads with 75,292 deaths and 204,240 injuries. Majority of the
injured persons, Olagunju lamented, are permanently bed-ridden at the
National Orthopedic Hospital, Igbobi, Lagos, and have been conferred
with the sobriquet, 'landlords.' So frightening is the accident rate
in the country that the FRSC boss calculated that 20 lives are lost
in road accidents daily with an average of 55 persons sustaining
injuries. He revealed that in Europe, America and Asia, especially in
Japan, there are just about five vehicle accidents per 10,000
registered vehicles. These contrast sharply with Nigeria's record of
150 deaths per 10,000 registered vehicles.
The Sector Commander blamed the increasing rate of accidents in
Nigeria on the careless attitude of motorists to driving. He noted
that most drivers have misinterpreted 'democracy' for "total freedom
to commit manslaughter on the road" and go scot free. He went on:
"You'll find somebody cruising at 140 kilometre per hour and when you
stop such an individual, he will say you are infringing on his
fundamental human rights." Olagunju wondered if democracy grants to
people the right to commit suicide.
Also, bad roads that dot the nation's landscape and an increase in
vehicles that are not roadworthy have contributed to the frightening
rate of accidents in the country.
The FRSC waits impatiently for the National Assembly to pass into law
the ban on the use of mobile phone while driving. Though the
introduction of mobile phones in the country has facilitated business
development, Olagunju said it would be dangerous to continue to allow
motorists use the device while on the wheel. "It is an offence to
drive with one hand using the other to receive a phone call,
especially if you realize that driving requires 100 per cent
concentration." For those who say there is no law that bans phoning
while driving, Olagunju has this to say: " We can also say there is
no law that says you cannot eat pounded yam while driving. And we all
know too well that you cannot lap a woman when driving. The
fundamental issue is that if you are using one hand to phone, there
are certain actions of yours that are violating traffic regulations."
So, as the Commission awaits the legislation, it would ensure drivers
save their own lives by using both hands when driving.
Already, about 2,500 offenders in Lagos have been arrested and
penalized this year for phoning while driving. Even though fines are
minimal, Olagunju enjoined motorists to avoid being arrested as the
procedure for remitting the fines are 'deliberately made cumbersome
and frustrating.' This, he said, is to discourage road users from
endangering lives. "Some people have often complained about our fines
being low. Yes, it is low because we are not a money-generating
agency. We are created to enforce and restore order on the road. But
beyond the fines, our task is to instill the spirit of compliance and
orderliness on our roads," the FRSC boss maintained.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria's External Reserve Rebounces to $7.74bn
Headline: Nigeria's External Reserve Rebounces to $7.74bn
Body_Text:
Abuja — Nigeria's external reserve has rebounced, rising to a level of $7,741,913,845.13 in October 2002 after several months of downward trend from $7424,026,547.43 in September 2002.
This increase, which is $317,887,297.70 coupled with the inflation rate, which slumped to 5.4 per cent in the same month, the lowest since May 2000, represents a significant improvement.
The Chief Economic Adviser to the President and Vice Chairman, National Planning Commission, Dr. Magnus L. Kpakol made this disclosure yesterday in Abuja at a press briefing on the economy after inaugurating the Inter-Ministerial Committee on Linkage of the 2003-2005 National Rolling Plan with Nigeria's Regional Commitments and Obligations.
Kpakol noted that, "given the success of the Dutch Auction System (DAS) and the stability in the foreign exchange market, we expect our foreign reserve position to continue strong through the coming months."
Kpakol also said food inflation decelerated to 0.7 per cent from 37.1 per cent in October of last year.
Similarly, the chief economic adviser said on a 12-month average basis, inflation fell to 13.6 per cent in the month under review from 14.8 per cent in September 2002. Inflation rate was 2.2 per cent in May 2002.
He explained that, "the 13 per cent inflation rate in September implies that a fixed market of consumer goods and services purchased with N100 during the 12-month period ending October 2001 sold for N113.60 on the average for the corresponding period ending October 2002."
Kpakol attributed the deceleration in inflation to sharp declines in food prices especially yam tubers, plantain, beans and sweet potato, as good rainfall in 2002 has supported a bumper harvest.
He added that government incentives in the area of fertilizer support, access to loans and improving infrastructure are also helping to assure food stability.
Speaking on 2003 budget, he disclosed that the Federal Government did not include privatization proceeds, loot money as part of the revenue to be expected.

Original_Search_Title: So Long, 'Egbon' Fatoyinbo
Headline: Nigeria: So Long, 'Egbon' Fatoyinbo
Body_Text:
Lagos — Bukola Olatunji pays tribute to the creator and co-ordinator of the Communication for Education and Development (COMED) programme and Senior Communication Specialist with the World Bank, Mr. Akintola Fatoyinbo, who passed on suddenly on Sunday, December 1, 2002 at 59
'Friendships are of many kinds
As through our lives we go
Some are short but very sweet
For parting makes them so...'
This may be an unusual piece for this page. But then, it is a short story of an unusual man. Executive Secretary of the Association for the Development of Education in Africa (ADEA), Mr. Mamadou Ndoye described Mr. Akintola Fatoyinbo as "an exceptional man, so generous, so available for everybody."
President of the Nigerian Guild of Editors, Mrs. Remi Oyo said at his graveside last Saturday in Ilesha, that Akin Fatoyinbo 'was a mentor and valuable friend of Nigerian Editors and journalists. He was a wonderful friend."
Indeed he was a great friend to everyone who crossed his path and I must be one of the newest friends he made. I met him for the first time on Sunday, September 1, 2002. Thirteen short Sundays later, and by a cruel coincidence, on December 1, 2002, he was gone. It is hard for me to talk about Fatoyinbo in the past tense, but what can I do now?
He died in Dar-es-Salam, Tanzania where he had gone, as usual, to prepare Tanzanian and other African journalists for the coverage of the African Ministers of Education (MINEDAF) conference, which was held between December 2, and 6.
To say Fatoyinbo's death was a surprise or a shock is saying the least. Mrs. Oyo, who was with him in Dar-es-Salam when he passed on said 'I wish Akin lived forever. Akin would have loved to live forever."
Mr. Wale Ajao of the Vanguard Newspaper who was also at the conference and who saw Fatoyinbo the day before he died told me "even the man himself would have been surprised by his death."
Mr. Akintola Fatoyinbo, who was a staff of the World Bank for over 20 years, designed the Communication for Education and Development (COMED) programme. He was also a prime mover of the ADEA African Education Journalism Award, which I won.
Part of the prize was a two-week study tour of major media organisations which have departments devoted to education in Paris, London and Washington, beginning with seminars in Paris, and ending with visits to the World Bank Headquarters in Washington. The Washington leg was not in the original schedule. My Fatoyinbo had been instrumental to the addition.
Editor of The Comet newspaper, Mr. Gbenga Omotosho and I had arrived Paris, France early morning on Sunday, September 1 2002; to begin the study tour. My story that won the award ('How Far can a Meal Go?) was published in the paper and ADEA had invited the Editors of the publication in which the articles (Two Anglophone and two Francophone) were published to also come on the study tour.
A couple of hours after checking into Hotel Nicolo I went into the small Dinning Room for breakfast and found three people already having theirs. I greeted them and made to sit down when the oldest of them, wearing a beautiful adire shirt, said, "this must be Bukola Olatunji, the award winner. I am Akin Fatoyinbo." I went, "Oh! I am so pleased to meet you sir. I have heard so much about you, but somehow we had never met."
Indeed, I had heard his name mentioned in discussions about training of African journalists, education in Africa and so on. But had neither met him nor seen his photograph.
If I knew that "Egbon (Big Brother)", as Omotosho fondly addressed Fatoyinbo throughout the exciting trip, had exactly three months to live from that day I met him, perhaps I would have prayed fervently, like Jesus Christ did, that the cup pass over him. I might have also asked him to tell me what must have been a very exciting story of his life.
I do not know how many languages he spoke. When we got to the UNESCO headquarters in Paris and Fatoyinbo discovered that the lady who conducted us round the premises was Spanish, he spoke her language to her. Of course he spoke English, French, German and not to say, Yoruba languages, including his 'Ijesha' dialect. How he came about all these languages would have made an interesting read.
When he learnt of his passing, Mr. John Eremu of the New Vision of Uganda, who came second in the ADEA contest wrote, "surely this world is so cruel, but God has his own plans. If it was humanly possible, God should have given Fatoyinbo a few more years. Such a nice man going so quickly when he had just come into our lives."
Ms. Rose Akakpo of Le Point au Quotidien of Benin Republic, who came first in the French Category of the competition did not say a word at the funeral on Saturday. We just hugged and wept, lamenting our common loss.
I did not set out to study him during the tour, but I just got drawn to him form the first seminar on the first day. Mr. Fatoyinbo was a damn good speaker. His voice had this childlike ring that made me want to hear him all the time. But he did not say much per time. He preferred to listen to others speak. He was also down to earth. Though way ahead of us on many counts, he did not show it off. "You speak Spanish too?" I had asked him. "Just a little to get by", he replied with a smile.
Maybe it does not make any difference now, but looking back, I am so glad I told Mr. Fatoyinbo how I felt about him. We parted at the end of the tour on September 15 when I left Washington for Los Angeles. On my third day in LA, I had sent him a long e-mail telling him where I was and what I was doing. I am also glad that I saved a draft of that mail, just as I have kept every copy of his mails to me.
I checked my mailbox this past week to copy the last two paragraphs of that long mail:
"I am sorry if I bore you, but you deserve these details because I would not be here in the first place but for the way God has used you to extend the study visit here."
"I want to add that I thoroughly enjoyed your company. You remind me of my father, a very simple, fair, caring and intelligent man who treats everyone with respect and understanding. I will tell him about you when I go to see him in Kwara State in November by God's grace. Bye for now and God bless, Bukola."
Apart from the qualities listed above, Mr. Fatoyinbo also had my father's build - slim, tall and straight. That is not to talk of that brisk walk of someone rushing to keep an appointment, and the hard work.
At over 70, my father still stands tall and straight. I did not get to see him in November as planned because I postponed it for a family meeting scheduled for December 7 by which time Fatoyinbo was no more. With so much happiness at seeing my siblings and me and planning for a brother's wedding, I could not tell my father about Fatoyinbo anymore. It will still take a while from now to talk about him without forcing back tears.
How many of those kind gestures can I list? As the days of the study tour rolled by, our different weaknesses began to manifest. But Mr. Fatoyinbo was never impatient with any of us. He in fact made excuses for our inadequacies, made everyone of us feel special. He came down to our level and befriended us.
Our last day in Paris featured the Award Night aboard the boat 'Montebello' on the River Seine. At the end of the ceremony, Mr. Fatoyinbo saw Prof. Michael Omolewa, Nigeria's Ambassador and Permanent Delegate to UNESCO to his car and opened the door for him. The humble man that Omolewa is, He refused, saying, "No Egbon. You get in and let me take you to your hotel." Fatoyinbo said he had to make sure everyone left before he did. Omolewa also insisted that he should be the one to open a car door for Fatoyinbo. So the latter had to give up.
We all thought Mr. Fatoyinbo was very English, but he was not as English as we imagined. On our second day in Paris, Mr. Omotoso told us of his visit, with his friend who is lives in the city, to a Nigerian eatery (Miliki Spot) run by a man that preferred to be called "Baba Miliki" and his wife "Iya Miliki". The pounded yam he ate, he said, was excellent. An 'Ijesha' man that he was, Mr. Fatoyinbo said we must check it out later that day. As we left Nicolo, it began to drizzle. I thought that was the end of the outing. But Mr. Fatoyinbo said, "we can't allow this rain to stop us. Come, there is a Monoprix (a departmental store) somewhere down the road." We went in and he bought me a beautiful raincoat (now a memento of great value) and took an umbrella for himself.
The taxi that took us went could not locate the address after going round and round. That cost a fortune. I said we should take the nearest metro back to the hotel, but he said, "never mind, we will find it." We eventually had to call Baba Miliki who came to pick us where we were. It turned out that Baba Miliki and Fatoyinbo were from the same town. Then I heard him speak his Ijesha dialect for the first time. He wanted to know when the couple last visited Nigeria and advised them to do so more often. When the steaming hot pounded yam and fresh fish came, Fatoyinbo ate it with his hands and like President Obasanjo once said, licked his fingers.
Although we were all adults on the trip, Mr. Fatoyinbo and Mrs. Thanh Hoa Desruelles, ADEA's Information and Communication Officer looked after us like their children and were referred to as 'papa' and 'mama'. They cared about our needs, what we ate, where we ate, if we slept well and what have you, without breathing down our backs. Mr. Fatoyinbo even helped us to get the best value for our par diem. he suggested what kind of phone card to buy and where, where to get the best bargain to dry clean our clothes and where to change money. He was available to answer any question. He could read our mood, knew who was uncomfortable and acted swiftly.
On arrival at the Waterloo Station in London, we needed some pounds. He looked at the exchange rate, did a little arithmetic and concluded that we would get more if we changed the money as a group. At the end of the day, Mr. Fatoyinbo over paid some of us. He would rather have it that way than for us to individually change our money and have less.
Listening to others pay their tribute at Fatoyinbo's graveside last Saturday, I say with all humility that my assessment of him was dead accurate.
President of ADEA, Mr. Ahlin-Byll Cataria, who could not hold back the tears as the casket was lowered into the grave said on behalf of the Association, "the story of Akin's life left a strong mark on the circle of people who were close to him: the members of his family and his friends. Akin always displayed noble-heartedness and steadfast caring for his loved ones. Gifted with an outstanding feel for human relationships, he was able to give backing when and where needed and his dealings with one and all were congenial.
"Akin's passing has left an emptiness which cannot be filled,. But our faith, conveyed in our prayers, is that Akin was a good man and that God will recognise one of his own."
The African Education Journalism Award, Cataria said, is now to be known as the Akin Fatoyinbo Award.
The Communications and Partnership staff of the World Bank said, "Akin was great source of inspiration to those he worked with, especially those of us who joined the World bank many years after he did. He very readily shared his experiences and was a mentor to so many of us who work in the communication field in the Bank. He was very much an original."
His friend, Mr. Sunny Aminaghan said "I have known Akin Fatoyinbo for over 25 years. He was a very kind hearted, loving and caring friend and brother." Ndoye had added in a grave voice during my discussion with him, "talking about Akin is very difficult. This is a great loss for me because he was not just a colleague, he was also my friend."
Country Director of the World Bank, Mr. Mark Tomlinson who also attended Saturday's burial ceremonies said, "Akin Fatoyinbo was a wonderful person, a great colleague, a very powerful intellectual and a very talented communicator. We will miss him in the bank; we feel emptiness now that he's left us. It is very sad."
As soon as we arrived in Washington, Mr. Fatoyinbo muttered more than once, "I hope my daughter will be able to come. I have to call my daughter." He did call his daughter, Lola, 21-year-old final year student of Biology at the University of Virginia in the United States, and she came to the Wyndham City Centre where we were lodged. No point stressing how they related. One should not expect any less from a father and his daughter. Mr. Omotosho had to ask if Lola was Fatoyinbo's first child to which he answered "she is the one and only." As Lola confirmed to me last Saturday, it turned out to be the last time of father and daughter being together.
How can I forget the Early Childhood conference in Asmara, Eritrea? How Mr. Fatoyinbo handled the issue of a lady agent who wanted to fleece some of us of our hard earned money. Fatoyinbo was indeed a diplomat.
Then our last meeting at the High level Group meeting on Education for All (EFA) held in Abuja. Mr. Fatoyinbo had written for me three e - mail addresses (another memento) through which I could contact Mrs. Remi Oyo for daily update on MINEDAF, which he did not live to participate in.
Fatoyinbo was simple a to the very end. his burial place was under a big tree on the serene 'Hill Top' ('man pass man', as it was named by the former ownner), where birds make melody all the time, along the Ido Ijesha/Ife Road. Lola told me that her father bought the place from an English man whose name she did not remember, and who was a gold miner.
May his soul rest in perfect peace.

Original_Search_Title: Central Bank Governor ,Sanusi, Scores Economy Low, Puts Deficit At N47.5b
Headline: Nigeria: Central Bank Governor ,Sanusi, Scores Economy Low, Puts Deficit At N47.5b
Body_Text:
Lagos — Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), Chief Joseph Sanusi, yesterday scored the economy low in 2002, saying Gross Domestic Product (GDP) grew at 3.2 % less than the 3.9 % recorded last year and the 4.0 % targeted for the year. He also said the Federal Government recorded a deficit of N47.5 billion in the first 10 months of the year.
However, Chief Economic Adviser to the President, Dr. Magnus Kpakol said Nigeria's external reserve had risen to $8 billion from the $7.4 billion in September, while inflation rate, according to him fell from 14.8 per cent to 13.6 per cent in October. Briefing journalists in Lagos on macro-economic development in the out-going year, Chief Sanusi said the poor growth of the GDP was caused largely by the contraction in the oil sector which overshadowed the improved performance in the non-oil sector.
Provisional data on government operations, the CBN governor said, showed that in the first 10 months, cumulative retained revenue of the Federal Government stood at N665.7 billion including proceeds of the draw-down of external reserves, excess crude account and recovered looted funds, representing a shortfall of 23.6 per cent below the proportionate budget estimate for the period. The shortfall, he observed was traced to lower receipts from oil and non-oil sources. "On the other hand, total expenditure amounted to N713.1 billion, which was 20.5 per cent lower than the budget estimate for the period. The bulk of the expenditure, N553.8 billion or 77.7 per cent, was in recurrent outlays."
Consequently, the Federal Government recorded a deficit of N47.5 billion in the first 10 months of the year. This, accordingly to Sanusi was financed largely by borrowing from the banking system.
In spite of this negative fiscal development, Sanusi maintained that relatively stable macro-economic environment was maintained in fiscal 2002, compared to 2001. "Specifically, the inflation rate decelerated from 18.9 per cent in December 2001 to 13.6 per cent in October, 2002 and is projected to moderate further by year end. The improved inflation performance reflected the impact of light monetary policy and the moderating effect of good agricultural harvests on food prices," he remarked.
He noted that the target of single-digit inflation rate may not be met, due to the expansionary fiscal operations of all tiers of government as well as constraints imposed by structural factors on output growth.
As a result of the expansionary fiscal operations of the three tiers of government and the attendant increase in bank's credit to the sector, growth in monetary aggregates was excessive relative to targets with broad money (M2) rising by 32 per cent to N1.753 trillion, as against the programme target of 15.3 per cent for fiscal 2002, while narrow money (M1) grew by 28.2 per cent to N1.047 trillion as against 12.4 per cent programme target for the year.
lCBN lifts forex ban on 21 banksMeanwhile, the CBN, yesterday lifted partially the suspension from foreign exchange transactions imposed on 21 banks found guilty of forex malpractices. Addressing financial journalists at the end of the 264th Bankers Committee meeting held in Lagos, yesterday, Managing Director Trans International Bank PLC, Mr. Banji Oshankunle in company of Director Research Department, CBN, Dr. O. J. Nnana, said the apex bank decided to lift partially the forex sanctions imposed on the erring banks. Consequently the banks are now allowed to engage only in inter-bank forex transactions for a period of time during which they would be under probation.
During this period, the banks' activities would be subject to scrutiny to assess their compliance with the relevant forex laws. Thereafter, if their performance is satisfactory, they would be admitted into the Dutch Auction System (DAS) bidding sessions for forex on behalf of their customers.
In March, the CBN imposed sanctions on 21 banks found guilty of contravening the Foreign Exchange (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act Number 17 of 1995, which mandates banks to disclose and document all foreign exchange transactions.
Consequently, the banks were suspended from the CBN supervised Inter-bank Foreign Exchange Market (IFEM) for one year.
lForeign reserve has risen, by KpakolHowever, the nation's external reserve has risen to about $8 billion.
Chief Economic Adviser to the President, Dr. Magnus Kpakol disclosed this at the briefing on the latest developments in the economy, in Abuja yesterday. According to him, the figure represents a significant leap from about $7.4 billion in September. The figure had actually been depleted to $7.5 billion at the beginning of the second half of the year. Dr. Kpakol also revealed that inflation rate fell from 14.8 per cent to 13.6 per cent in October.
Reminded that the prices of most household items did not indicate a fall in inflation, the Chief Economic Adviser said that the rise in prices of food items such as rice did not reflect a general trend. He argued that prices of grains and yams had been falling in the last two months, following the commencement of the harvest season.

Original_Search_Title: Kayode Fayemi - Still Fighting
Headline: Nigeria: Kayode Fayemi - Still Fighting
Body_Text:
Lagos —  Dr. Kayode Fayemi says five years of struggle for democracy have
only opened doors for more challenges.
For Dr. Kayode Fayemi, director, Centre for Democracy and Development
(CDD), the struggle to enthrone democracy in Nigeria has just begun.  As a
matter of fact, the battle has shifted from the struggle for transition to
that of consolidation of democracy. "We have moved from the struggle to
remove the military from political authority in Nigeria. But that in itself
has opened up another phase in the struggle."
Kayode Fayemi is not operating in a strange land. For him, activism is
not just pastime but a full-time job. He grew up in a home where his
curiosity was encouraged and developed by parents who noticed early enough a
special trait in their child. At the Christ School, Ado-Ekiti, where his
love for books was honed, he was the secretary of the school's press club
and he represented the school in several schools' debate during the period,
a task which earned him the nickname, Current Affairs.
He blossomed during his undergraduate days at the University of
Lagos. He was editor of the campus newspaper, The Watch. He was also the
editor of Alpha, a newspaper published by the Eni-Njoku Hall.
He would however come into national consciousness with the roles he
played during the heyday of the despotic rule of Sani Abacha, when it became
too dangerous for pro-democratic elements to remain within the borders of
Nigeria. Fayemi joined forces with other members of the famed National
Democratic Coalition (NADECO) to wrest Nigeria from the grip of military
rule. He was one of the pillars behind Radio Kudirat, whose duty it was to
counter the false stories Nigerians were being fed.
But three and half years into democratic rule in Nigeria, the battle,
according to Fayemi, is far from being over. With the level of corruption in
the system, as displayed at all the levels of governance, which has
consigned the country to one of the 20 poorest nations in the world, the
battle is definitely not yet over. This, however, is not enough for
Nigerians to lose hope in their nation.
"With the performance of civil rule in the last three and a half years,
Nigerians, now, more than ever, need to be vigilant. For those of us who
were cut in the earlier struggle, it is important that in spite of all the
imperfections of the last three and half years, we cannot afford to revert
to military rule."
The CDD director believes the basic problem confronting the Nigerian
state is the lack of sustainable structures, which has made it virtually
impossible for any physical gain to be made in any sphere of human endeavour
in Nigeria. Ironically, however, Nigerians have continued to excel in
different fields of endeavours outside the country. This, according to
Dr. Fayemi, is principally because Nigerians have for long placed much
premium on personalities instead of on issues and structures.
Though he argues that both personalities and issues cannot be separated
from issues of state governance, he believes that the right structures will
bring about good leaders.
Another problem which he believes has hindered the progress of Nigeria
as a nation is corruption. Though the problem had been with the country from
the time of independence, it however took a new dimension during the eight
years of Babangida's rule. "Babangida it was who institutionalised
corruption in Nigeria."
Fayemi's Centre for Democracy and Development (CDD) is positioning
itself to meet the needs of Nigeria and indeed the West African sub-region
for democratic solutions and consolidation, which, he says, are fundamental
to political stability and economic growth.
The CDD has in the last five years of its creation been involved in
several projects with many governments in the region. Of particular interest
was its involvement with the Nigerian government in the formation of the
Human Rights Violations and Investigation Commission (HRVIC). The fact that
past leaders could be brought before Nigerians to account for their misdeeds
was enough victory for Fayemi.
In spite of his very tight schedules, which see him travelling round the
world in search of peace for mankind, Fayemi, in his spare time, which of
course is rare, still finds enough time for his foremost hobby of reading
history and biographies. "I read a lot of history and biography, not to copy
those people, but to lean from them." One other thing which the struggle had
not taken away from him is his love for pounded yam and palaver soup.

Original_Search_Title: Akintola Fatoyinbo: It is Well
Headline: Nigeria: Akintola Fatoyinbo: It is Well
Body_Text:
Lagos — A lovely painting of two peacocks hangs at the reception of the Peacock Hotel in Dar-es-Salaam. On the painting these words are inscribed: "Joy is not in things but in us".
Peacock Hotel was the last abode of Akintola Fatoyinbo before he died in a nearby hospital on Sunday, December 1 2002.
These words epitomized the life and times of Akintola Fatoyinbo, who I first met in 1983 when I was a Desk Editor in the News Agency of Nigeria. I was then in charge of the Foreign Desk and it was in this capacity that I became first associated with Akin.
Because he was older than me, culture demanded, and I was willing to so bow, to call him 'Uncle' or 'Bros' or be formal and address him as Mr. He was not formal and insisted that I call him by his first name.
Akin was then on secondment from the World Bank and helping to set up the Africa operations of Inter Press Service News Agency headquartered in Harare, Zimbabwe.
That chance meeting marked the beginning of a privileged friendship that has imparted positively not only on my professional work, in my personal life but on that of my family, colleagues and friends.
To know Akin was to love him. He was warm, considerate, selfless and tireless in the pursuit of his work. A polyglot, he tried hard and failed to make me one. Akin was passionate about his family, his sisters, his work, his country and our continent.
His sterling qualities, especially an unparalleled commitment to the noble profession of journalism shone in his tireless work at building the capacities of journalists in Africa.
In the three years that I have served so far as President of the Nigerian Guild of Editors, members and other journalists have benefited from no less than four capacity building workshops through the indefatigable efforts of this wonderful gentleman and quintessential journalist. He was the backbone and supporter of the Guild and its programmes.
Having served as Senior Editor of Deutsche Welle, the German Radio and written while living in Germany for many Nigerian newspapers, Akin was keen on ensuring that those who practice journalism did so without fear or let but with the goal of service to humanity, to the country and continent.
He often prefaced the beginning of our many conversations with "You know what" and then wonderful ideas would follow. Brilliant ideas that have resulted in the establishment of COMED the Communication for Education and Development Programme as a working group of the Association for the Development and Education in Africa (ADEA), a programme sponsored by the World Bank and other donors.
Akin believed that reward should be given for excellence. He believed the lowly could rise to the top of the world. There was no "can't do" in his lexicon. "That is a possibility", he would say, with such an infectious smile.
Akin had such a way with people. He would easily forgive a slight or any let down. He was willing to provide opportunities for any one. He harboured no ill will and to the chagrin of us lesser mortals, Akin will make excuses for those who had exhibited human foibles and thus fallen short of expectations. His forgiveness was genuine and quick, his advice free and unsolicited.
Although Akin had risen to the world stage, he was so self-effacing. I remember on the eve of his death, I teased him about a front-page photograph in a Nigerian newspaper which caught him in the shadows of President Olusegun Obasanjo meeting his Burkina Faso colleague and the Director-General of UNESCO in Abuja.
"Fame at last", I said and he just smiled and proceeded to tell me about his fresh ideas of capacity building efforts for the Nigerian media and plans for the 2003 African Education Journalism Award.
Akin was already famous, many many years before that photograph. Although he had dined with kings, queens, Presidents Ministers, movers and shakers, he had no qualms about sharing a bowl of pounded yam and vegetable soup with the lowly. Akin was an Ijesha man you see, and proud to be one too.
The love and generousity that Akin so freely shared came to full bloom after his demise. Many agreed that a huge void has been created in the firmament but all admitted that we must celebrate Akin and rededicate ourselves to the ideals he lived and died for
I guess it will be correct to admit that Akin died doing what he loved best. Getting journalists and policy makers to communicate the message of development through education properly.
On the eve of his death, he concluded a training programme for Tanzanian journalists in collaboration with Prof. Alfred Opubor, one of Africa's and Nigeria's leading lights in mass communication and my humble self.
Akin did not get a chance to see if his trainees did well. I can confirm that they did along with their colleagues from Nigeria, Ghana, Uganda and Mauritius, as well as colleagues from PANA.
A minute's silence at the plenary of the African Education Ministers' conference formally announced Akin's death. What followed were tears from men and women, powerful but who together with Akin shared love, love for development, love for humanity.
Akin belonged to the world, he belonged to Africa, he belonged to Nigeria and he belonged to us all who loved him, who held him in awe and admiration and were privileged to benefit from his unparalleled loyalty and genuine friendship. For all you have done for us all, we are all indebted to you, Akin, my friend, big brother and mentor.
Akintola Fatoyinbo, 1943-2002. It is well.
Oyo is president of the Nigerian Guild of Editors.

Original_Search_Title: Ekimogun Day: a Mangled Tradition
Headline: Nigeria: Ekimogun Day: a Mangled Tradition
Body_Text:
Lagos — Ondo Kingdom celebrated its 15th Ekimogun Day last Saturday. Tunde Okoli who witnessed the festival where Juju music maestro, Otunba Sunday Adeniyi Adegeye (KSA) was Chief Launcher, reports that Ekimogun has shelved its traditional festival garbs of yesteryears
Things are fast changing in the globalising world. African culture, tradition, religion, language and ways of life have been the worst for it right from when Western civilisation and religion berthed on the coasts of Africa in the garbs of colonialism and imperialism. Even in contemporary times, these twin agents of Western aggression against traditional Africa have continue their pull-it-down agenda against African culture and religion with the rise of globalisation and Pentecostalism. Today, the relics of what survived the Western onslaught are nothing but mangled remains of what used to be originally African.
Pentecostalism particularly, have done more damage than its begetters - colonialism and imperialism. The latter day Christian concept came with the utopian idea that seem bent on destroying traditional Africa, its fashion, hairstyle, art, music, culture, religion even, ridiculously, food. It regards anything traditional Africa as heathenish, ungodly and vile. In the Pentecostal world, the fiercer a Church minister attack traditional African ways of life and religion, the more his popularity among his congregation. The trend continues unabated.
Contemporary African societies, particcularly Nigeria, have continue to respond by remodifying their social, cultural and religious heritage. Consequently, communities that used to celebrate new yam and other traditional festivals changed the names and contents of the festivals to suit the dictates of these neo-imperial agents. Hence, the plethora of community days as in Ogbomosho Day, Ile Oluji Day, Igbara Oke Day, Oka Day, Ikare Day, Ilawe Day, Idanre Day among others in fad. Even traditional festivals like Igogo in Owo Ondo State, Ojude Oba in Ijebu Ode, Ogun State, Agbaa in Iwaro Oka, Ondo State among others are not fast changing their appellations, their traditional garbs and contents are by now, diluted. Most of these festivals no longer follow the traditional rites for which they were instituted in the first place. One of such festival is the popular Ekimogun festival among the Ondo people of Ondo State. The Ondos distinguished themselves among the Yoruba tribe occupying the South West of Nigeria, for institutionalising the age-long Ekimogun festival in celebration of Ogun, the Yoruba god of iron and custodian of all professionals working with iron instruments.
Ekimogun festival used to draw tourists from far and near. It was then not just a celebration of the life and times of Ogun, it was an expression of the peoples' allegiance to their ancestry. More importantly, the festival marks the end of the harvest season and preparation for another planting season.
Ogun who was said to have possessed dual qualities to create and destroy and a lover of palmwine, roasted yam and dog meat, had instructed his followers on how to celebrate every traditional Ekimogun festival. Before its transformation 15 years ago, hunters, blacksmiths, drivers, farmers, all professionals working with iron instruments worshipped Ogun at every Ekimogun festival with masquerades, roasted yam, palmwine, Ijala and other traditional performances that endeared the festival to tourists in the past.
However, with the rechristening of the festival and the advent 'Ekimogun Day' 15 years ago, the ancient Ekimogun festival has almost completely shelved its traditional essence of Ogun worshipping for which it was instituted. Palmwine are hardly used and the ritual involving the symbolic severance of a dog's head seem eternally rested.
The 15th Ekimogun Day celebrated last Saturday was far from being a tourists' delight. Aside loosing its traditional rites juice, the event could easily pass for a political rally cum fund raising ceremony by the Ondos.
Only two masquerades were admitted into the Ondo Civic Centre venue of the event. Even then, the programme did not accommodate performance slot by the masquerades. Sensing that they have been deliberately left out of the proceedings, the masquerades forced their way into the arena where they thrilled the audience with performances before they were hauled away by the security agents.
The three gubernatorial aspirants to the Ondo State Government House witnessed the event attempted to turn the arena to a campaign rally. Banners, posters and supporters of Chief Olusegun Agagu, former Minister of Power and Steel who is contesting on the platform of the People's Democratic Party (PDP), Dr. Rahman Mimiko, former Commissioner in the cabinet of Governor Adebayo Adefarati of Ondo State, Alliance for Democracy (AD), and Barrister Jimoh Ibrahim, All Nigeria Peoples' Party (ANPP) painted the arena in glowing colours. Of particular spectacle were the supporters of Barrister Jimoh Ibrahim. With talking drums, trumpets and other musical instruments, they followed Ibrahim everywhere he went in the arena creating a carnival-like picture. Even banners and posters of Governor Adebayo Adefarati who shunned the event were placed at strategic points in the arena.
Not done, the politicians also compete for relevance during the launching segment chaired by Ambassador Omotayo Ogunsulire. Each struggle to have the microphone to address the mammoth crowd before announcing their donation.
The Ekimogun Day Launching for this year showed all the trappings of an exciting event right from its planning stage. In the past, the organisers have not been able to convince this year's Chief Launcher, Otunba Sunday Adeniyi Adegeye (KSA), one of Ondo's distinguished sons to play active role in the launching aside his regular all-night performances in the last 10 editions of the festivals. What is more, the launching also had Ayo Olagundoye, MD/CEO of National Bank of Nig. Ltd. and Dr. Temitayo Fawehinmi as co-Chief Launchers respectively.
Welcoming guests to the occasion, the Chairman of Ondo Development Committee, Professor I. O. Oladapo stated that "the Ekimogun Day is an annual festival when all Ondo sons and daughters, at home and abroad, come to celebrate the uniqueness of being an Ondo man" adding that the festival "serves as the rallying point and it is used to harness our energies for the growth and development of the Kingdom."
He explained that Ondo is an ancient civilisation whose indigenes are proud of their heritage. He recalled how Airo (1530-1561), the successor of 1st Pupupu (1516-1530) the first Osemawe of Ondo Kingdom established an administrative, political and military command structure which lasted for centuries until recent changes made by the incumbent Osemawe, Oba (Dr.) Festus Ibidapo Adesanoye Osungbedelola II.
In a comic relief, the event compere asked King Sunny Ade to drop his royal garb to be addressed just as Otunba Sunday Adeniyi Adegeye. According to the compere, it is not possible to have two kings on sit at the occasion. "Since our Kabiyesi Alaiyeluwa, Oba Festus Ibidapo Adedisewo Adesanoye Osungbedelola II is present, Sunny, you will have to remain Otunba Sunday Adegeye," he said.
As expected, King Sunny Ade presence drew the creme of the Nigerian society to the festival. Aside the governorship aspirants mentioned above, the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, President Olusegun Obasanjo and Vice-President Atiku Abubakar were represented by Chief Fashawe at the occasion. Governors Abubakar Audu of Kogi State, Olusegun Osoba of Ogun State, Lam Adesina of Oyo State, Bisi Akande of Osun State, Adeniyi Adebayo of Ekiti State, among others made handsome donations through their representatives at the event.
In all, KSA alone raised about N15 million for Ondo community. Otunba Fashawe captured the influence of the personality of the Chief Launcher when announcing donations on behalf of the President and the Vice-President. He said: "We are all gathered here today particularly because of KSA. President Olusegun Obasanjo said that Sunday is has proved to be a good son of the soil and a worthy ambassador of his country's music. You are the first thing the world cherish in Ondo. The world know the quality in you as a music star. Thank you for your contribution to the promoting of our indigenous music (Juju) throughout the world."
Co-Chief Launcher, Ayo Olagundoye, MD/CEO of National Bank of Nig. Ltd. with the support of friends, colleagues and well-wishers raised over N5 million while Dr. Temitayo Fawehinmi, the Chief Launcher of the festival brochure raked in about N2 million at Ekimogun Day 2002.
King Sunny Ade said it was a rare privilege being asked to be the Chief Launcher of the 15th Ekimogun Day celebrations. "I am extremely happy. In fact, I am a bit nervous, because I have never acted in this capacity before. In previous Ekimogun Days, I have only been one of the spectators during the afternoon segment of the festival only to play at the all-night party. But here I am now, one of the big masquerades performing in the square. The excitement is making me nervous," he told THISDAY.
For him, Ekimogun Day has, over the years, grown to a symbol of identity for Ondo sons and daughters the world over. "Every year, we all eagerly look forward to Ekimogun festival. Wherever you are as Ondo indigene, you will be itching to come home to celebrate and felicitate with your kindred during the festival. It is the unifying point for all our brothers and sisters at home and in the Diaspora. Ekimogun festival is unique in the sense that it always offers one the opportunity to reunite with friends, relations and folks you have lost contact for long," he stressed.
What if he were asked to repeat his role at next year's edition of the festival? KSA chuckled and said: "It is not possible! Do you know how many illustrious sons and daughters of the community that are waiting in the wings to pounce on the opportunity to be Ekimogun Day Chief Launcher? It is not something they can make one person do frequently because there are so many people that are willing to do it that are waiting for their turn to be Chief Launcher. However, if they said I should be the Chief Launcher again next year, why not? I'll oblige. So far as it is for the development of my community," he enthused.
One of the highpoints of Ekimogun Day 2002 was the mini art exhibition/market mounted at the arena by three notable artists of Ondo descent - Olawuyi Oluwasola Israel, Olagunju Lasisi and Bunmi Olaosebikan.
Among works on exhibit were: Olawuyi Oluwasola Israel's Hawkers (pencil), African Mother (oil on paper), At Night (oil on canvass), Arrival I & Arrival II (oil on canvass), Man at Work (terracota), Rwander Child (terracota), Sober Reflection (terracota), Bata Drummer (oil on canvass), Expected Baby (mixed media), and Sea Scape (oil on board). Olagunju Lasisi's Procreation (oil on canvass), Humble Beggar (wood), Drummer (paster on paper), Tribute (oil on paper), Omolope (oil on paper), Drummer (wood), Unity (wood) and, Fruit Seller (metal). Bunmi Olaosebikan's Fulani Traditional Pot (clay), Woman (oil on canvass), and Dancer (wood).
There was also a citation on Chief S. Akinola Makinwa the 2002 Merit Award winner at Ekimogun 2002. The investiture of Chief Makinwa was performed by the Osemawe himself with the assistance of his wife Olori Victoria Anike Adesanoye. Thereafter, selected schools in the town performed the match past the royal box where the Osemawe and his wife Olori Victoria Anike Adesanoye and the launching principals sat and watch in rapt admiration.
After the match past, prizes were awarded to winners in the Ekimogun Day 2002 football, essay, cultural dance, Ise, and draught competitions. This was followed by homage paying by indigenes to the king who waved his horsetail in appreciation to the mammoth crowd that trouped out to perform the act.
Though Ekimogun festival has went through a lot of transformation in recent years, copious traditional rites of the festival remains resilient and are still visible. The Ondos are known to be one of the most traditional peoples of contemporary Nigeria. Chief Segun Agagu averred that "anyone who is culture minded cannot but envy Ondo people for the way they have been able to preserve copious elements of their cultural heritage."
Since the evolution of Ekimogun Day 15 years ago, it has been dedicated to raising funds towards the development of Ondoland. One of such monument that have emerged from the efforts is the near completion of the multi-million naira Ondo Civic Centre long Canon Adeyemi (Ondo/Ore) Road, Ondo. According to Prof. Oladapo, the Yegengha of Ondo, "we have used the occasion of the annual Ekimogun Day to generate funds for the development of our motherland."

Original_Search_Title: Improve Food Storage, Nigerian President Tells African Leaders
Headline: Kenya: Improve Food Storage, Nigerian President Tells African Leaders
Body_Text:
Nairobi — ABUJA, Thursday
Nigeria's President warned fellow African leaders yesterday to get serious about crop development and storage - fighting chronic food shortages that aggravate the continent's conflicts.
Speaking at the opening of a UN Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO) summit in the Nigerian capital of Abuja, President Olusegun Obasanjo urged African governments to improve agriculture and food storage.
Inefficient farming and storage is costing Africa up to half the fruits and vegetables, one-fifth of the rice and grain and one-quarter of the yams and manioc produced on the continent, Obasanjo said.
African nations also should improve co-operation in the production and sell of pesticides, vaccines and veterinary drugs, Obasanjo said.

Original_Search_Title: Nigeria May Be Bankrupt in 2003 - Anya
Headline: Nigeria May Be Bankrupt in 2003 - Anya
Body_Text:
Abuja — The director-general of the Nigeria Economic Summit Group (NES G), Professor Anya O Anya, on Monday examined the economic indices of the country and warned that Nigeria may go bankrupt in 2003.
"Presently, the Nigerian economy is showing critical signs of distress. Inflation as at September 2002 was 14.8 per cent and industrial utilisation was about 41 per cent as at October 2002. Also, the foreign reserve declined from N10 billion as at December 2001 to its present level of about N7 billion.
"Although interest rate has been brought down by fiat at 22.5 per cent (which is still high), it is clearly unsustainable because it has not arisen from strong micro-economic fundamentals," he said.
Anya, who delivered a paper on "Aligning Political Stability To Economic Sta-bility: The Role of the Private Sector" at the on-going Democratic Election Forum seminar in Abuja, stressed that "unemployment is worsening and rising with GDP growth stagnating at 3.3 per cent.
"Besides, the effective exchange rate is about N136 : $1 and the per capita income is less than $300. What is more given the total debt stock the outlook cannot be rosy," he further stated.
According to him, the situation in the country where about 92 per cent of her budget is committed on recurrent expenditure will not augur well for the economy "because when you are eating the seed yam, where will you get the seeds for the next planting season."
On the political front, Anya said "the scenario is neither cheering," noting that "the political actors are oblivious of the nation's economy and I can say that we don't have room for maneuvers.
"The gate of violence has to be reduced because no nation can experience meaningful growth in the absence of peace and tranquillity. We have not enjoyed political stability in 40 years and economic stability for 30 years.
"This country has been on auto pilot for the past five years because no one has been in charge," he stressed.
Calling for retraint from acts that can overheat the system and generate crisis and instability, Anya said these may send wrong signals to the international community.

Original_Search_Title: Audu Ogbeh And the Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Audu Ogbeh And the Economy
Body_Text:
Lagos — I read Olusegun Adeniyi's interesting piece titled "The Vehicle Without a Driver" based on the book, Secrets of the Temple by William Gadner. I cannot but agree with him on our grossly understated inflation figures. Even common sense should tell us that our inflation figures, as dished out by FOS, cannot be correct by whatever indices they have used, be it Oranges or Yams. Its totally absolutely inappropriate for FOS officials to sit down in Lagos and take their sampling. Do you know that sometimes foodstuffs are cheaper in Lagos than the villages? This is because everyone is bringing their products to the urban centres. Thus creating a near surplus situation which affects prices. Anyway enough of the economics.
I honestly empathise with Chief Audu Ogbeh, who in fact by my opinion is the greatest Chairman the PDP is yet to produce. Looking at his educational and political credentials as well as his personality (I had the honour of dealing closely with him during my banking career in the very early nineties). My prayer is that God would grant him the courage to instill the much needed discipline in the party.
As Chairman, he has an opportunity to screen-out candidates who have nothing to offer the "down trodden people, the poor masses, the silent but oppressed majority". He owes Nigerians the duty of ensuring that the party does not put forward the kind of materials they put forward in 1999. I believe the first task towards achieving that would be for the party to review the very exorbitant nomination fees they have prescribed. Otherwise only those who have ill-gotten wealth would qualify just like the elections of 1999. Its only when we put forward credible candidates that we can now begin to have politicians who will address real issues like the economy, education, agriculture, environment, health, industrial growth, etc.
This is the time for Chief Audu Ogbeh to match his words with actions. It's not just Chief Ogbeh's job alone, INEC also owes the masses a duty to ensure that those misfits who either by hook or crook escape their parties' axe do not make it to the elections. I suggest that INEC should not leave the task of screening to the Police and SSS but should rather contract it out to highly reputable security or consultancy agencies. Finally, the masses should embark on dangerous prayers, that those misfits who escape the axe of INEC and end up winning the elections should not make it to swearing-in ceremonies. It's that important if this country is to move forward.
The only time we could have gotten out of this mess (rudderless drift) was if General Tunde Idiagbon (of blessed memory) had been allowed to carry through his social revolution. But No it was too much for certain corrupt elements and his regime was abruptly terminated. And in its place corruption was institutionalised at all facets and levels be it public or private or religious sectors. That is why we are where we are today.
There can be no meaningful reversal of this trend without a revolution of sorts. First the problem has eaten too deep into the social fabric of the society and is beginning to act like a malignant cancer which must be cut-off or else it will consume its victim.
The choice now is everyone's, whether we want to make it peaceful or violent. If we all agree as a people to change our ways and inculcate discipline then we have to, as the bible tells us, die to ourselves right now and begin to learn to do things in a clear and orderly manner like new born. Otherwise we would be making this inevitable change a violent one. But change must come but the way and manner in which it comes could be determined collectively by us.
It is only after this change that we can now begin to build solid and lasting social and economic structures. Anything short of this would be akin to building a house on a sandy or marshy terrain without a proper foundation. Such a house, no matter the embellishments, cannotstand, as it must surely fall.
Solomon Abheke, Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Bringing Sunshine to Ondo State
Headline: Nigeria: Bringing Sunshine to Ondo State
Body_Text:
Lagos — What do the following names Arogbo, Mahintedo, Ugbonla, Mahin, Asewele, Uba and Simerin have in common? They are rural, riverine communities in Ondo State, which, prior to the Adebayo Adafarati administration, had never experienced the phenomenon of electricity in their citizens' lives.
Enter Chief Adebayo Adefarati and behold, those communities situated in the creeks and waters of Ondo State now enjoy continuous flow of light in their island communities.
In fact, the commissioning of the Arogbo - Ijaw electricity project way back 2000 marked the very first time in the history of Ondo State that "light would shine" on and in any Ijaw community and on water, anywhere in the state, perhaps in Nigeria. Some breakthrough!
The visionary leadership of Chief Adefarati has continued to spread democracy dividends amongst the oil - rich peoples of the state by making the development (human and infrastructural) of the petroleum - producing region of the state, the centerpiece of the Rural Transformation Project (RTP) of his government.
And he is progressing "exceedingly at pace" to ensure that the "goose that lays the golden egg" i.e. the riverine people of the state, profit more than most, from their contribution to the portals of the government revenue.
In fact, the current administration has put in place an agency -the Ondo State Oil Producing Areas Development Commission (OSOPADEC) which ensures that 70% of the derivation proceeds accruing to Ondo State is spent within the oil producing communities which would translate into the complete rebirth of those communities by the end of Chief Adefarati's second tenure in 2007.
From the rural resurgence visible in the past three years, the people are united in their call on governor to renew his mandate with the people for another term in 2003 so that the ongoing excellent implementation of the rural agenda would be fully actualized as planned by 2007.
Ondo State farmers have been firmly in the vanguard of feeding the nation (growing cassava, yam, plantain, fruits, etc) and also earning foreign exchange by growing cash crops (cocoa and oil palm).
An integrated road network is a strategic imperative for the millions of farmers in the state who need to transport their crops to the urban cities and towns where they are required for consumption and export.
Since 1999, Governor Adefarati has matched and surpassed the expectation of the farmers and the general citizenry by embarking on the most comprehensive and far - reaching road construction network since the creation of the state in 1976.
From, the 14 key inter city roads ranging from Akure - Alade - Idanre, Ondo - Laje and If ira - Ipesi - Idogun in the Akoko hinterland, Governor Adefarati is opening up the rural areas for the farmers and their goods, to the urban centres and major cities far easy and accessible transportation of produce to sustain and enhance their livelihood.
Apart from this obvious benefit to farmers, this colossal road network has generated invaluable and unquantifiable economic dividends and prosperity for the people, with spin-offs in the areas of
_ Cottage industries have continued to spring up all over the state
_ Enhanced transportation business with new commercial vehicles dotted and strewn over the entire state landscape and, skyline.
_ Unprecedented boom in the advent of new private car owners in the state, as evidenced by the 1000% increase in their revenue generated from the registration of new vehicles since 2000 by the government.
_ New shops and warehouses springing up even in remote areas recently opened up via the new road network which has unleashed a new breed of rural and urban entrepreneurs in the land.
The fall - outs are clearly visible in every part of the state which even Governor Adefarati's staunchest critics would grudgingly accede to.
Suffice to add that all the intra city roads in all the key cities are currently undergoing massive rehabilitation - Akure, Ondo, Owo, Ikare, Okitipupa, to mention a few - Ondo State has never, ever seen anything or experienced any phenomenon close to what Chief Adefarati's government is "planting" on Ondo soil.
When the word "Rangers" is mentioned in the Nigerian parlance, the first connection that readily comes to mind is Enugu and football - Enugu Rangers.
But in Ondo State, the word Rangers has a brand new meaning to the populace - it is the Health Rangers Scheme (ARS), which is the kernel of the free Health Delivery Scheme, designed to take medical care to the doorsteps of the people in every local government in the state.
The scheme is uniquely novel and hinged on simplicity itself;
_ Every week a team of medical personnel including Doctors, Pharmacists, Nurses and paramedical personnel visit a particular local government attending to the health needs of the people, visiting their houses and their community centres.
_ Those requiring secondary attention are referred and taken to the nearest state hospital for free treatment and attention.
_ No area is left out - these are 16 new vehicles and two speed boats exclusively purchased and maintained for this scheme - the first of its kind by any state government in Nigeria.
Governor Adefarati has ensured that nobody in Ondo pays for health care treatment and delivery in the Sunshine State as pressings, drugs and treatments are offered free of charge.
The massive rehabilitation of all hospitals has been virtually completed to bring them in line with the optimal standards set by the World Health Organization. (WHO), which is the benchmark the government has set for itself to ensure that the governor's favourite cliche "Health is Wealth" is being realized and actualized amongst the populace.
Ondo State is perhaps the only state in the federation where all the recommended drugs in the Essentials Drugs List (EDL) of the Federal Government are regularly available round the clock everyday and all year, on recommendation.
Since February 215t 2000, when this government launched the Free Health Care Delivery Scheme, nobody in Ondo State has been turned back from receiving drugs or treatment on account of the drugs not being available.
The "No Drug" syndrome has been expunged from the Ondo State medical vocabulary since the beginning of the new millennium.
As a corollary to this, every local government area, by the end of governor Adefarati's first term would have received the dividend of a general Hospital i.e. for those areas that were without any prior to his becoming governor.
What the citizenry is witnessing in Ondo State is an outpouring of affection and affinity for the colossal achievements of governor Adefarati and his collaborators in literarily transforming sunshine into the lives and hearts of his people.
The bonding and symbiotic relationship that exists between the governor and the people would manifest in a massive approval of the extension of his mandate for a fresh term in 2003 to enable Chief Adefarati etch a lasting legacy of rural upliftment, economic emancipation and a healthy and wealthy citizenry in Ondo State. That is the wish of the people - may the good Lord grant the desires and expectation of the people in the Sunshine State.
Yinka Olomoda writes from Ibadan

Original_Search_Title: Hotel Naigara is Ambitious
Headline: Uganda: Hotel Naigara is Ambitious
Body_Text:
Kampala — One should not mistake Naigara, as in the hotel, with the famous Niagara Falls of North America. From our understanding, this hotel was named in memory of Nayagala, the late mother of its owner.
Less than 10 years ago, nobody would have ventured to set up a hotel complex in the areas of Najanankumbi, Zanna and the like.
In the case of Zanna, the area where Hotel Millennium stands was nothing but swamp. Conventional wisdom would have dictated that such an undertaking was too risky.
Wrong. The area from Kibuye
Round-about all the way to Seguku along Entebbe Road has grown in leaps and bounds, the latest development being the Naigara Hotel. Off the beaten path, it is nestled in a little cul-de-sac, making it into a sort of hideaway; ideal for those who desire seclusion coupled with a feel of country air.
I am full of praise for those who think beyond and plan for the future, as is the case of the owners of this new property.
Though not yet fully complete, it is habitable, with a conference facility that can accommodate about 200 people. It has 16 complete rooms. Last month, Naigara Hotel opened without any fan fare or much publicity. We decided to check out the food. I must say that the al fresco was perfect for a hotel of this size. The owners wisely decided to woo the population who live within the vicinity rather than rely on city-dwellers.
Accordingly, the food has been priced with the locals in mind. Suburbian living is relatively cheaper than life in the inner city. If one is going to attract these people, one must give them a worthwhile incentive. Low price has always been an attraction.
The menu has been chosen as a la carte, but I honestly feel that it is simply too ambitious and unworkable. Whoever is behind this long-winded document would be well advised to go back to the drawing board and come up with something more suited to an out of town joint.
To wit, the lunch or dinner menu, while going for a very inexpensive sh3,000, failed to live up to its expectations. It was wonderful to see that one could get matooke, yams, Irish and sweet potatoes, rice, chapatis, pumpkin, posho, millet et al. These were served with afro-fish stew, beef stew, brown goat stew, chicken stew, luwombo... the list is endless.
In all fairness, the above would rightly never be called a la carte. Here, I blame the chef for getting carried away with what one can only term as his sense of limited understanding of menu engineering. Somebody once remarked that a little knowledge was dangerous. I am afraid that this was all too evident in the form of the menu card that was presented. We would have thought that more than 100 items for a place this size is just sheer overkill.
Matters are not helped by the numerous spelling gaffes one finds on the menu. Sandwitches, persley, becon, omolette, jacked (potatoes), humbergers (sic), just to mention a few. The management has assured us that they are going to correct these aberrations. This is good news and can only help the hotel.
We had some fried fish that was nothing remarkable, served with tepid rice and a sauce of some sort. I have to admit that there is room for vast improvement in the kitchen and I have no doubt that the powers that be shall act accordingly. The owner insisted on picking up the tab, a generous offer that would nevertheless have set us back a mere sh5,000 for the two of us; chump change!
