So, when she came back from her office to charge her car battery at a nearby battery charger's place, and didn't meet her young brother-in-law, she thought that he may have gone to play in the neighbourhood. According to her, "what happened was that on Wednesday, September 4, 2002, when I returned home from the office at about 11.30 in the morning, I observed that the entrance door to our apartment was open and I couldn't find my husband's small cousin that stays with us. In anger I locked up the entire place and returned to the office to continue my work for the rest of the day.
"We thank God that he's been found through radio announcement," Chinweuba says.

Original_Search_Title: The Vehicle Without a Driver (1)
Headline: Nigeria: The Vehicle Without a Driver (1)
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Verdict according to Olusegun Adeniyi
I have always found books on economic issues rather boring yet Secrets of the Temple, the 798-page book on how the Federal Reserve Board runs the American economy and through that, the government, remains the best book I have read in recent times. Perhaps because it is written by a journalist, an award winning one at that, William Gadner, who stripped the issues of all those high fallutin jargons with which economists and bankers deceive the unwary public. It is easy to read while it explains to lay men like me the workings of the American economy, the interplay of political and economic forces, the power struggle between Congress and White House and some of those seemingly innocuous but critical issues which affect the way American voters react on election days.
I have to thank my brother, Segun Ayobolu, for sending me the book which I like to recommend for our policy makers, especially at the federal level. Even when they may find it too voluminous and may not find the time to read it all, one chapter will at least suffice: chapter eleven. It is titled "A Car With Two Drivers" and it is about the over bearing roles the Fed, equivalent of our own Central Bank, plays, side by side the President and the Congress, in determining the direction of the economy and by implications the fate and fortunes of Americans. I have a feeling though that our policy makers may not be interested in reading books because they know that on election days, votes will not be based on issues since they have always had our votes even without telling us anything!
In his book, Gadner was able to show the role banks do and can play in modern economy though under strict supervision. It has, however, also opened my eyes to the fact that many of what we have here are no banks but just bureau de change while I believe Mr. Joseph Sanusi has shown sufficient strength of character as Central Bank Governor given some of the tough but unpopular decisions he has had to take and effect in the last three years under the difficult circumstance in which he finds himself.
While Gadner's book deals with the extraordinary ways in which Paul Volcker ran the Fed as Chairman, it is also a story of how some Presidents failed while others succeeded. Without adopting the political economy approach, the book, in its own unique way, was able to show the symbiosis between politics and economy and how one impacts the other in rather significant ways. It is particularly revealing of Jimmy Carter's presidency and how it ended almost before it began. Carter, we all recall, came to power in 1976 at a time of great moral crisis when the American public was losing faith in their politicians following the Watergate scandal that had consumed President Richard Nixon and it was more his character than his understanding of economic management that gave him (Carter) the job. But by 1979, the economy had gone awry with high inflation rates and several people out of jobs.
Bruno Pasquinelli of Pasquinelli Construction, one of the big time business men at a public hearing in Congress said at the time "...those interest rates have killed me and my business. I am tremendously frightened. I don't sleep at night anymore. I owe money. I don't have anything. When I come to negotiate with a banker, I don't negotiate. It is a card game but I don't have any cards". Another business man said his company had become a "non-profit organisation". It was a bad situation for Americans and it was therefore not surprising that the 1979 campaigns were based mainly on economic issues. So bad was the situation for Carter that even his Party, the Democratic party, nearly dumped him for Senator Edward Moore Kennedy. But while he managed to get the ticket, he, not surprisingly, was roundly defeated by the Republican Ronald Reagan at the election proper.
Reagan's inaugural speech was direct and straight to the point and it is like what people would describe as a "coup speech" here: "we are confronted with an economic affliction of a great proportion...it threatens to shatter the lives of millions of our citizens. Idle industries have cast workers into unemployment, human misery and personal indignity.. in this present crisis, government is not the solution to our problem; government is the problem"
As a way out, Reagan did not preach sermons like his predecessor, Carter, he proposed some far reaching economic reforms that would ginger productivity, create wealth and bring the economy back on line. Unlike what also obtains in some places, he did not pick a come-and-eat cabinet filled with political has-beens. He knew he was on a rescue mission hence he picked some bright young men, seasoned bureaucrats, people who were already gainfully employed and were making sacrifices to leave their jobs to help their country, not jobbers who depend on government to survive. His economic team also had a focus even when some of the men had strong positions on issues. He also consulted widely but the ultimate decisions were his. In doing this, however, he had to carry the Congress and the Senate along. And at the Fed, he had to contend with the Chairman, Volcker, who also had his own ideas of how the economy should run and was at a vantage position to do as he wished. The interplay of these forces, the seriousness of purpose, the management of the intrigues, all combined to help Reagan's administration ultimately turn things around. But that was because he had ideas as to what he wanted to do in office and pursued his mission with zeal and no pretenses.
In contrast, what do we have in Nigeria today? Only the people in authority cannot see that our fate is hanging in the balance. We are in a period of serious economic crisis. Our situation is more than a thousand times far worse than what obtained in America in the late seventies which people were making noise about. Let me give a few examples. The American consumer price index, according to Gadner, was calculated as 100 with 1967 price. By 1970, the price index was at 116. By 1975, it was 161. Four years later, it was 217. The inflationary trend, one would agree, was bad for the American situation but in ours that is really no inflation. From the Federal office of Statistics' figure, the inflationary trend in Nigeria, from 1992 to 2000 reads like this: 44.6 for 1992; 57.2 for 1993; 57 for 1994; 72.8 for 1995; 92.3 for 1996; 8.5 for 1997; 10 for 1998; 6.6 for 1999 and 6.9 for 2000.
But everybody who has been living in Nigeria in the last twenty or even ten years knows that the picture painted above does not tell the real story on ground. Because it is based on a basket of consumables like oranges, yams and not goods denominated in foreign exchange yet we are an import-dependent economy. I don't know how many people can remember the prices of some of the essentials say ten years ago compared with the prices today. We know how much most of the household consumable sold for ten years ago and what they sell for today. We know how much fuel price was ten years ago and what it is today. We know how much local transport fare was ten years ago and what it is today. If we are to use the American index, we may begin to talk of a 100,000 to a million rates in terms of inflation.
Of course it is idle to say we should not compare ourselves with America. The truth is that we should. We also have the propensity, like the Americans, to acquire things that only satisfy the imagination, to consume goods that are unrelated to needs, necessity or even usefulness; things that merely feed the fantasies. Today, you see many Nigerians display their affluence in the midst of grinding poverty as we become a nation where money for some people contains what Gadner calls "illusion of immortal power-the psychic vehicle for defeating time itself by controlling the future...no one can be absolutely certain of living beyond the grave, but their money will.". That is what feeds primitive accumulation of many people in public offices as they not only steal for today but indeed for tomorrow so that their future generations can be secured. But can they really be sure of that in this kind of society?
The problem of Nigeria should, however, not be reduced to corruption on the part of people at the helms of affairs. The real malaise is far deeper. It is an absence of the ideal, the lack of focus, the dearth of ideas as to what to do and just plain planlessness that has reduced governance to adhocism in which case we live for today without a plan for tomorrow.
That is why since the seventies, after the oil boom, there has been a gradual decline in the lives of Nigerians and whether we want to admit it or not, every succeeding administration has been worse than the preceding ones. That is why many now look back and say Abacha's regime, with all its perversions, was better because if they were to look at the human index, they probably fared better then. It of course has little to do with the man in charge today because it has been a gradual descent into this tragedy and that is why I believe if we continue on this road, whoever succeeds Obasanjo is going to be worse than him. And on and on we may slide until there is an intervention one way or the other. We will come to how I arrived at this prognosis as we continue this series. But it is instructive to add that the only person who seems to appreciate the present precarious situation is the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) National Chairman, Mr. Audu Ogbeh.
At the dinner organised for him in Abuja Tuesday night, Ogbeh told his audience which included President Obasanjo, Speaker Ghali Na'Abba and others that rather than address the real issue today which is the economy, politicians are rather pursuing shadows, fanning ethnic sentiments, calling for national conference and all those elite manipulations to gain attention for themselves. Even all the people who want our votes, like the incumbents, are not telling us anything yet the situation is becoming hopeless for millions of Nigerians. We are really in dire strait.
According to Ogbeh "...what bothers me is the triumph of politics over issues. The absolute triumph of politics over economy, over education, agriculture, the environment, health, industrial growth, more foreign exchange earnings. And I keep wondering when shall we find the time to engage in a robust debate on these very serious problems which concern our society ... I worry because some three million young people are coming out of various segments of the educational ladder every year, and finding no job to do...And I keep wondering when shall we find time to engage in robust debate on these very serious problems which concern our society? And everyday, I found that the management of crisis is taking all our time and all our energies. And I wonder what at the end of our tenure we shall say we achieved..."
Even before the PDP chairman said it, Nigerians already know the economy has collapsed and that there is a looming social crisis. Almost all the industries that we used to know have all closed down while churches have taken over the thriving warehouses of old. Banks are no longer lending to manufacturers but to foreign exchange touts which is about the most lucrative business in town. Prices of goods and services are irrational at a time there is no money in circulation and many people are out of jobs. The private sector is dead. Almost all infrastructure have collapsed. Major roads have become impassable such that even the President admitted recently that he is ashamed of them. Public institutions (like health centres and schools) have decayed to intolerable level. The collapse of governance, which did not start today, is evident everywhere. Almost every person who can rent a flat now has to buy his own generator, dig his own borehole, construct heavy burglary proof and gates as security. In short, every Nigerian has become a government unto himself. The waste, the pollution, the sheer cost, how do you begin to quantify all these? Yet, our leaders cannot see this big picture of tragedy in their petty squabbles for control of our resources for their selfish ends.
Ogbeh has said what many people outside the political class have been saying, that Nigeria is on the way to doom, that we are presently on a bus ride to nowhere, that we need to change our present course, that issues about the economy and responsible governance should dominate public discourses. But then how can we do that without the right people? The problem is that we will never get the right people the way we play our politics. Now, it is about five months to the general elections and just a few weeks to the commencement of the PDP primaries. You will expect that our aspirants will come up with how to revive the economy. But no, what you hear is that the presidency must go to the North, it must go to the South-East or it must remain in the South-West. These were the kinds of the considerations that, in the first place, led us to this tragedy which we now have on our hands yet we don't seem to have learnt any lesson.
It is desirable that all ethnic groups in Nigeria have a taste of the Presidency but we must ask the question: how does that affect the down trodden people, the poor masses, the silent but oppressed majority...?

Original_Search_Title: Drugs Destroy Yam Export
Headline: Ghana: Drugs Destroy Yam Export
Body_Text:
Accra — The trade in narcotics and drug trafficking is threatening to destroy Ghana's yam export industry. Drug traffickers are taking undue advantage of the high demand for Ghana's yams abroad, to do their illegal business by concealing narcotics in packaged yams destined for Europe and America at a great cost to the country's export earnings.
These concerns were raised by the Ghana Root Crops and Tubers Exporters Union (GROCTEU), at a press conference in Accra yesterday. The union called for a national policy to bring about sanity in the business of yam export and also to save the image of the country as well as the jobs of the numerous farmers and traders across the country.
The President of the Union, Mr. Kofi A. Asiedu, said Ghana produces in excess of five million tonnes of yam per annum. Out of this figure, he said, the average export to EU alone is about 9,000 tonnes per annum which should under normal circumstances bring Ghana about 7 million dollars every year. "The US market caters for the rest of the export and together, Ghana should earn not less than 20 million dollars every year," he said.
Mr. Asiedu said, the actual export revenue generated from yam exports is only a fraction of this figure. The reason being that, the operations of drug traffickers, who have infiltrated the industry are destroying the trade in yam in terms of pricing on the export market. He said when the drugs concealed in the yams arrive at their destinations in Europe or America, the traffickers take their real exports away and with little else to do with the yams, flood the market with tonnes of cheap yams, "sometimes giving them away almost free of charge and at great loss to the exporters and the country's export revenues."
He said other illegal activities including money laundering, non-payment and non repatriation for exports, undercutting and oversupply among others, are the factors militating against what would have become an important foreign exchange earner for the country. "The resultant effect of all these factors on the economy is that Ghana loses more than half the potential foreign exchange earnings from the yam export trade."
Between May and August 2002, Mr. Asiedu said, the price of a carton of 25kg yam rose to 60 dollars in London but the same quantity could be bought for as low as 9 dollars in the same market. He said this was happening when the cost of yam even in Ghana, was fixed at 12 dollars a carton at wholesale price.
Mr. Asiedu said, the drug traffickers have taken to yam because Ghana's yam currently enjoys the kind of premium that Ghana's cocoa enjoys on the international market. He said some major fast food chains have discovered that it is the "number one" on the market so they have decided to use it as substitute to potatoes.
The union, he said is determined to check the activities of people using export of yams and other crops to do their illegal businesses. The efforts of the union, Mr. Aseidu alleged, are being frustrated by the Ministry of Trade and Industry. He said the union has presented a proposal to the ministry for self-regulation and group marketing, through the creation of a "single corridor system" as a means of checking illegal activities in the industry.
The system, he said, would ensure that all goods meant for export would undergo thorough screening and inspection by highly trained personnel before shipment to Europe and America. He said the union in addition, is proposing that only a few licensed buyers would be selected to receive yams from Ghana on behalf of their clients. He said the union is therefore appealing to the ministry to assist them in the fight, which is more in the interest of the nation than the interest of the individual members of the union.
This story is yet to be confirmed by the Ministry of Trade and Industries and the Narcotics Control Board.

Original_Search_Title: Meet New Deal's Presidential Aspirant, George Klay Kieh, Jr.
Headline: Liberia: Meet New Deal's Presidential Aspirant, George Klay Kieh, Jr.
Body_Text:
Atlanta, Georgia — The place, Trenton, New Jersey, the Date October 26, 2002, the time 10:30 AM, the occasion, an audience with the Northeast executives and members of the New Democratic Alternative for Liberia (New Deal Movement) to formally inform them about his campaign for the presidency for Liberia. The setting was like any American presidential campaign, the handlers (campaign staffers), the cell phones, and the www.Kieh-Tokpa.org logo were visible all over the place.
George Klay Kieh Jr., 46 and his running mate Alaric Tokpa, 44 met with the New Deal Movement Northeastern Region executives and members of the party to formally announce their intention to contest the New Deal Primary in the United States as President and Vice Presidential candidates. There are striking similarities between the Kieh-Tokpa team and another presidential campaign in the United States in 1992. George Klay Kieh, Jr. like Bill Clinton during the 1992 US presidential campaign, is 46 years old, and like Bill Clinton, he is well abreast with Liberian and international policy issues. His running mate Alaric Tokpa, like Al Gore, Clinton's running mate, is 44 years old. Like Al Gore, Alaric Tokpa's trademark is complex policy analysis.
The occasion began with the Liberian continental breakfast-cassava, plantain, yam and fish gravy. The breakfast reminded nearly everyone present at the occasion about the glorious land of liberty, Liberia. However, the breakfast was just a sideshow to what turned out to be an eventful and insightful occasion.
Dressed in his trademark Julius Nyerere like collarless shirt, the presidential candidate sat silently as one of his campaign staffers, a gentleman with a soft southern accent that could pass for a movie star, introduced him. The presidential candidate was greeted to the podium with a standing ovation.
During his remarks, Mr. Kieh spoke about what he calls a "human centered" social and economic development blue print that his campaign is developing for the reconstruction and development of Liberia. He explained that his program for the social and economic development of Liberia is divided three components- the short-range, intermediate and long-range components. Like the professor Kieh is, he briefly dissected the socio-economic in balances in the Liberian society and provided an overview of the prescriptive remedies in his blueprint for Liberia's revitalization. He promised his audience that he would harness the resources of Liberia for the social and economic development of Liberia.
Mr. Kieh and his running mate are no strangers to the fight for social justice in Liberia. The two aspirants like another Liberian presidential aspirant Marcus Dahn, recognized the social and economic inequities that existed in Liberia during the True Whig Party regime. They joined forces with other Liberian political agitators to fight for social and economic justice in Liberia at the expense of their lives. Unlike other Liberian presidential aspirants like Charles Walker Brumskine and others, the problems of governance, accountability, transparency and democracy in Liberia did not start in the 1980s to Mr. Kieh and Tokpa.
Candidate Kieh stated that his education policy would be directed toward the establishment of three major universities in Liberia and the transformation of the William VS Tubman College of Science and Technology into the Institute of Science and Technology. He stated further that he would decentralize the secondary and primary educational system in Liberia to the extent that school boards will appoint the principal of schools and not the government of Liberia.
On transparency and accountability in his government, the candidate intimated that all his political appointees would declare their assets upon taking offices and his Justice Ministry will establish the Bureau of Public Integrity to monitor officials of government for corrupt behaviors.
On why he selected veteran political agitator, Alaric Tokpa has is running mate, Mr. Kieh said that he wants a vice president that will work with him as an equal partner in the social and economic development of Liberia and not a vice president of convenience. He disclosed that historically, Liberian presidents have always selected weak vice presidents, but his administration will be the exception.
Dressed in a black turtle neck T-shirt and a burgundy sport coat, Mr. Tokpa took the stand as the partner Mr. Kieh said he would be, affirmed that their goal for contesting the leadership of Liberia is simple, to bring a complete and balanced socio-economic development to Liberia.
During the questions and answers period, the two men were quizzed about numerous issues of concern including how their lack of experience in governance would impact their administration should they succeed? Mr. Kieh responded that experience in previous failed Liberian government must not be used as a precondition for the presidency and vice presidency. In addition, Mr. Kieh disclosed that his present position as the Dean of International studies at Grand Valley State University (Michigan) puts him in charge of the administration of millions of dollars and complex administrative and budgetary decision making.
When asked about the cost of his "human centered" national reconstruction and development blue print Mr. Kieh stated that when his blue print is completed for submission to the Liberian people, it will have a price tag attached to it.
Notably absent from Mr. Kieh and his running mate's remarks were the usual attacks against Mr. Taylor's failed policies by Liberian politicians and the self anointed opinion leaders. When asked about his failure to attack Mr. Taylor, Mr. Kieh intimated that he is looking beyond Mr. Taylor. His statement could be interpreted that nearly everyone knows the problems of Liberia and Mr. Taylor's failure as a president.
The two men have had their fair share of successes in life. George Kieh, Jr. was educated at the University of Liberia and Northwestern University (Chicago), where he received a Ph.D. in political science in 1986. Mr. Kieh is currently Dean and Professor of International Studies, Grand Valley State University (on leave) and Chair of the Department of political science and professor of political science at Morehouse College in Atlanta. Mr. Kieh previously served as the Director of International Programs at this ‘Harvard' of Black colleges.
Alaric Tokpa was educated at the University of Liberia and the University of Ghana, Legon where he received a graduate degree in political science in 1990. He once served as the Acting Chairman of the Department of Political Science, the University of Liberia. Currently, Alaric Tokpa is a doctoral student at Clark Atlanta University.
Mr. Kieh and his running mate's knowledge of complex Liberian and international issues and their abilities to simplify these issues are very impressive. It remains to be seen if they will have the ability to translate their wealth of knowledge into the development of a backward nation like Liberia. Another obstacle the two men may face in their quest for the Executive Mansion would be if the present occupant will respect the constitution of Liberia (the document he has persistently violated) for a free and fair election to occur in Liberia comes 2003.

Original_Search_Title: Before Kaduna Boils Again
Headline: Nigeria: Before Kaduna Boils Again
Body_Text:
Lagos — I was drawn into deep religious thought in the middle eighties by the satanic waves Kaduna state created around the globe. The irreparable loss of human lives, aftermath regrets, anguish, uncountable horrific testimonies of both Christians and Muslims and their seemingly "never again" resolution almost convinced every one that the strange Kafanchan riot was the first and last of Kaduna State religious drunkenness.
History from then to date has proved us wrong. Kaduna indigenes have earned the inglorious stigma of religious zealots in a negative way by the subsequent and unpredicted manslaughter triggered by religious sentiments.
The demystifying thing about the whole saga is the fact that the main cause of the fighting is grounded in a common denominator that is completely unrelated to the zealots two.
In case you do not know, in Kaduna, you are either a Christian or Muslim. Agreement and disagreement, engagement and disengagement and all social endeavours are contracted along the lines of religious belonging. Interestingly, there is a great convergence of the two faiths in the man Abraham. Angelic ministration, positions of Angel Gabriel (Jibril) and Michael (Mikhail), resurrection, existence of Heaven and hell (Aljanat and Jahanam), the great day of judgment (yaulmulkiyamat) are common beliefs of the two faiths. These are people of one God, one faith, one belief, one hope, one destiny and birth, eating the same food, praying to the same God for the same things: Children, successes, good health, peace, long lives, prosperity, good rain good harvest, protection, guidance, forgiveness, name it! Having the same emotional anchorage- The God of abraham.
Both sides receive answers to their prayers in equal measure. For I see rich Muslims in every Subr, Zuhur, Asar, Magrib and Ishar thanking the God of Abraham for answered prayers just as the rich Christian on every day worship. The poor Muslims and their Christian cousins look up to the same God of Abraham for assistance.
He gives showers of rain from Makarfi to Kwoi. We plant and harvest yams the same times in Zaria and Kafanchan. The God of Abraham has never for once demonstrated preference for one faith over the other in his merciful dealings with us.
Given all these factors, it is rational to seek for what boils the hearts of my people, turning them into objects of avoidable self-destruction. Absolutely, this kind of fighting is not of the God of Abraham by the God of Abraham and for the God of Abraham.
Why are my people being destroyed by the same factors of common understanding? Are we just born fighters? Are we happy with the negative stigmatization and shameful polarization fighting in the name of religion has brought to us?
Are we playing Arab and Hebrew of whose patriarch Abraham we are only beneficiaries? We have to accept that we are just converted Christians and Muslims from African paganism. We must also know that the names we give ourselves - Adams, Noah, Japheth, Aaron, Moses, Solomon, David, Peter and Paul cannot and will not make us Hebrews. Likewise names after Mohammed, Abdulmalik, Yusuf, Haroun, Musa, Sulaiman, Dawoud, Maryam can never ever make us Arabs. No matter the number of churches and mosques you destroy, you cannot change the world of Christianity and Islam.
For the past ten years how many officers have been admitted in the Nigerian Defense Academy from Kaduna? How many graduates do you make in Ahmadu Bello and Federal Polytechnic per session? How many Kaduna indigenes are in Kaduna Refinery and Petrochemicals, Defence Industry Corporation, to mention a few. Of what use is Ahmadu Bello University to you? What is the advantage of having NDA, Nigeria Military School in your soil?
Is relevance not worth fighting for? What about equity? Ownership? If all of these do not draw our attention the issue of saving lives by avoiding war at all cost is both individual and collective responsibility. It is indeed the challenge that faces everyone in this global village to save human lives anywhere anytime. Whatever action therefore needed to be taken with the ultimate result of saving one live should not be trivialized nay politicized.
The people of Kaduna have sharply divided themselves into two. Every time we loss human lives in tens of thousands just to say to whom ever cares to hear that we would not live as one Kaduna. We are different. Common religious principles/fundamental believes that are bolts and nuts of oneness melt when tested in Kaduna social survival.
Religious fighting has held the state economy to ransom. The vital economic factors of development, human capital, natural resources, developed and developing infrastructural facilities are the major recipient of the war arsenals.
The state think-tank is skewed away from strategic development issues like, creation of wealth, welfare enhancement, capacity expansion to thoughts on war defence and aggression.
Several governments that shared our deep concerns towards making Kaduna a peaceful state have made countless efforts. However, the desired result and the causation remain elusive. The recent efforts from the brilliant Makarfi (Sai ka kara yi) are quite enough to quell any bitterness among the natives and usher in a tolerable coexistence.
Constant defeat of peace initiatives portends that forces behind this orgy are not ordinary. The situation is also made hopeless by the stubborn stand of the indigenes on living at poles. They seem to be comfortable with separate sovereignty.
It is my firm and honest believe therefore that one visible and readily available solution to this problem is the separation of the present Kaduna along these ethnic differences. The people desire it. They need it. It will work.
This is the biggest, final, most humane, rational, God accepting fight that I invite everyone to.
Kanya Williams, Kafanchan, Kaduna State

Original_Search_Title: Edo Communities Accuse Police of Plundering Farms
Headline: Nigeria: Edo Communities Accuse Police of Plundering Farms
Body_Text:
Lagos — Demand withdrawal of policemen for being used by the Area Commander and a DPO to execute the agenda of the state Attorney General
Displaced Inhabitants of Edo Community of Ago-Ogbodo/Otuo, have raised alarm, accusing the police of preventing them from returning to their homes and farms since the communal clash between them and the neighboring community of Okpe/Ijaja last August 31.
The people of Ago-Ogbodo/Otuo in Owan East Local Government Area of Edo State and their neighbouring community of Okpe/Ijaja in Akoko Edo Local Government Area of the State had in renewed hostilities over the ownership of the Ago-Ogbodo land and environs engaged in a bloody clash last August 31, leaving two people dead (an Ao-Ogbodo/Otuo indigene and an Idogu, Ondo State indigene), four others missing till date, farms and houses reduced to ashes and over 3,000 inhabitants of the 4,000 Ago-Ogbodo/Otuos displaced.
Since the incident about two months ago, the displaced Ago-Ogbodo/Otuos, who fled to the other quarters of Otuo, have been unable to return to their homes and farms, no thanks to the State Police Command, the Area Commander, Auchi and the Divisional Police Officer (DPO), Igarra, in particular, who they are accusing of allowing themselves to be influenced by the Edo State Attorney-Generaland Commissioner for Justice, Mr.S.I.O. Ekhabafe, an indigene of Uneme, Akoko Edo, and the traditional ruler of Okpe( Olokpe of Okpe) to take side with the Okpe/Ijajas.
The Police, who are said to have been particularly angered by the burning down of the police post in Ago-Ogbodo/Otuo during the crisis, were said to have deployed mobile policemen to the area, beating and chasing the inhabitants, including women and children into the bush.
In the deserted community last weekend, the policemen, who have now completely seized it, were at all corners, wielding guns, daggers and horse whips. They looked rough and wild. Some of them laid on passages of displaced inhabitants while some others were seen cooking meals to eat.
Heaps of raw food as yams, plantain, cocoyam and cassava were at all corners.
One displaced inhabitant, who said he went through the bush path to his farm to attempt to collect food, felt very bad.
"Since the policemen chased us out after the clash, it has not been possible to go to the farm to take food. The policemen would not allow us to go to our farms, we are starving, where we are forced to relocate with our children and wives under trees and shields in the open.
"I managed to get to the farm today, going through the bush because the police have been very cruel to us. They beat up severely anyone they see in the farm or in the area . They have injured so many of us, at times, threatening to shoot any of us found in the area.
"Rather than maintain peace here, they are fueling the crisis, they are chasing us all over, not allowing us to go into our farms while the other party, the Okpe/Ijajas, are living peacefully in their community, carrying on with their daily activities unmolested.
"I am coming from the farm now, I stole in there to take food but as you can see, I am not going home with anything.
"There is nothing in the farm, my yams have been uprooted, the same with my cassava and cocoyam. Even cocoa are plucked, those that are yet to ripe have been cut and pieced all over the place.
"The policemen are combining with the Okpe/Ijajas to be plundering our farms, they have harvested our crops, that is how mindless the mobile policemen are," the aging man claimed, his voice choked with bitterness.
Yet, another, who simply identified himself as Marcus, said the mobile policemen had not only plundered their farms, they had completely looted their homes, slaughter some of their domestic animals to eat and take away the others.
"The policemen are behaving like an army of occupation, they are looting and plundering the place. Can this be the instruction given to them?
"Our people are hungry, we have been chased out now for over two months, we are displaced, living under trees and schools in other Otuo quarters.We cannot go to our farms,we are not even allowed to return to our homes,we are stucked and no one seems to be bothered, our children are not going to school, I don't know what government and the police want to make of us. They must realise that we have to survive, the policemen are not allowing us to go to our farms, they are not allowing us to return to our houses. Those of us arrested by them, trying to go to the farm are given the beaten of their lives and taken to Igarra police station to detain.
"They collect thousands of naira from our kinsmen to secure their freedom, the people are really brutal," Marcus explained.
The situation in the camps of the displaced Ago-Ogbodo/Otuos had reportedly degenerated so badly that the Otuo community decided early last week that they were moved back to resettle in their homes.
But as the vehicles entered the embattled Ago-Ogbodo, the mobile policmen stationed there were said to have attacked and shelled the buses with tear gas, leaving the children, women and many of the men dazed.
While a number of them ran for their dear lives, many others, including women and children were said to have been arrested,beaten,tortured and taken in the vehicles to the Igarra Police Station,where they were detained.
The community was said to have paid N3,000 each to secure the freedom of the about 200 detained men, women and children. The DPO of the station had allegedly insisted that unless the sum was paid in respect of each of them, he would not only hold them for as long as he wished, he would charge them to court for disturbing the peace of the area, arson and murder. He was said to have openly boasted that he had the support of his superior officers in the state.
Nothing could better prove that to be true as the lukewarm attitude allegedly displayed by the Area Commander, Auchi, whose control the entire area falls, to the protests of the Otuos against the actions of the policemen in Ago-Ogbodo and the DPO, Igarra Police Station in particular.
While neither the DPO nor the Area Commander could be reached at Igarra or Auchi for their reactions and position on the matter, one of the policemen on ground at Ago-Ogbodo said every evidence before them point to the fact that the Ago-Ogbodo/Otuos were the aggressors in the crisis.
Nothing, he said, was more annoying to the police than the fact that the Police Post in the area was set ablaze during the crisis.
Asked why they were singling out the Otuos for the sort of treatment they were allegedly visiting on them, realising that the clash involved the two parties and it could not be safely said who set the police post ablaze, the police sergeant said before they moved in their officers had made them realised that the Otuos were the aggressors and that they would have been responsible for the burning of the station.
On the charges of looting and plundering of the homes and farms of the Otuos, he said: "It is not true. Of course, we are here to maintain peace and order. Very unfortunately, we are not being fed or paid any allowance by the authorities.
"We have been on ground for nearly two months, our brief is that we ensure peace and order here, we are not being fed, we are on our own.
"While it is not true that we are plundering their farms and looting their properties, I won't deny the fact that occasionally we go to the farms to get yams, plantains and fruits to eat.
"To ensure peace here, we have to survive, and if government is not responsible for our feeding, those, who caused our being here, have to be responsible directly or indirectly", the policeman was blunt.

Original_Search_Title: CPI Records a Decline of 0.8 Per Cent
Headline: Nigeria: CPI Records a Decline of 0.8 Per Cent
Body_Text:
The latest report of the Composite Consumer Price Index (CCPI), an instrument used in determining prices paid by consumers living in both urban and rural areas released by federal office of statistics (FOS) dropped to 4985.6 from 5028.2 in August and July, 2002 respectively, this shows a decline by 0.8 per cent. The decrease according to the office was as a result of low prices in stables, such as vegetables and fruits. However, the prices of drinks, clothing, footwear and cost of vehicle maintenance rose in many centres which indicates a fall in prices of items in urban and rural areas by 3.6 and 0.3 per cent accordingly. There was an advancement in the average consumer price level in the 12 months to August 2002 by 12.3 per cent. This was lower than 15.6 observed in July by making similar comparison. In this regard, the rural and urban indices indicated increases by 10.4 and 12.7. The percentage in change of the average CCPI for the 12 months ending August, 2002 over the average of the CPI for the previous 12 months was 15.6.
This invariably means that a fixed market basket of consumer goods and services bought with N100.00 during the 12 months ending August, 2001sold for N115.6 on the average for the corresponding period ending August, 2002.
In effect this indicated a decline when compared to 16.2 per cent obtained in July by making similar comparison. Accordingly, the composite food index dropped by 2. 6 per cent in August, 2002.The decline in the index was largely due to the decreases in prices of yam tubers, maize grain, vegetables and fruits.
The level of the composite food index was higher than the corresponding level a year ago by 10.5 per cent correspondingly, the average annual rate of rise of the index was 22.2 per cent for the 12 months period ending August, 2002.
The farm index produce which excludes prices of agriculture went up by 3.0 per cent in August, 2002. In the months under appraisal the index rose by 13.2 per cent and the average rate of rise of the index was 5.7 per cent for the 12 months period ending August, 2002.
In comparison therefore, the urban and rural indices went up by 2.8 and 5.6 per cent in July. This result invariably means that a fixed market basket of consumer goods and services purchased with N100.00 during the previous 12 months period ending July, 2001 sold for N116. 2 on the average for the corresponding period ending July, 2002.
Meanwhile, this also indicates a decline when compared to 16.4 per cent obtained in June, 2002. In this regard, the average consumer price level in July, 2002 was higher than the corresponding level a year ago by 15.6, this was also higher than 12.2 per cent recorded by making similar comparison in June,2002.
From the foregoing, the urban and rural indices therefore showed an increase of 16.4 and 15.4 per cent accordingly over the same period.
In furtherance to FOS report the composite food index (CFI) took an upward trend by 6.3 per cent in July, 2002 as the increase was caused by rise in prices of rice, plantain, yam tuber, and edible oil.
As a result of this, the CFI was higher in the corresponding year by 15.1 per cent. Aside this the annual rate of rise of the index also was 24.3 per cent for the 12 months period ending July,2002.In the 12 months of July, 2002 the index rose by 13.4 per cent indicating a total average annual rate of rise of the index was put at 4.7 per cent for the 12 months period ending July, 2002 compared to 13.2 per cent annual rate of rise of the index also put at 5.7 per cent for the 12 months ending August, 2002.

Original_Search_Title: Don Cautions Against Eating 'Fast Foods'
Headline: Nigeria: Don Cautions Against Eating 'Fast Foods'
Body_Text:
Ilorin — Refrain from food that has colourants, seasonings and flavourants which are especially common with "fast foods", has been recommended as panacea to longevity. The Dean of the Students Affairs of the University of Ilorin, Prof. Musbau Aderoumi Akanji, has said.
In a 53-page inaugural lecture at the University of Ilorin, Akanji said that the presence of additives makes digestion difficult and diminishes human metabolism. "We should refrain from food additive, that do not add our value to our food other than fancy. Examples are colourant, seasoning flavourants, dyes etc. it is very difficult for the cells to metablise and get rid of them. They stay for long periods in the cellular system and cause a lot of havoc. They are the matagens and carcinogens that eventually destroy the cells and kill you by little."
In the lecture titled; "Eat and Die by Little", the professor of Bio-chemistry stated that fasting or and eating once in every 12 hours is the best way to extend long life. Quoting Holy Prophet Mohammed who advised that "do not eat while you are not hungry," and "when you are hungry and you want to eat, divide your stomach into three portions; fill one with food fill one with water; and have third empty so that you can refect deeply."
While recommending the 1-0-1 eating formula, he asserted that taking fast food is the shortest way to "eat and die by little." He added that its avoidance is the beginning of metabolic wisdom". We should eat foods that are as barren of endogenous materials as possible. Like eba, pounded yam, rice, beans, meat etc.

Original_Search_Title: Kwame Pianim is Right - The Statistical war
Headline: Ghana: Kwame Pianim is Right - The Statistical war
Body_Text:
Accra — I read the reported altercations between Kwame Pianim and Dr. Joe Abbey with shock and dismay ("Dr Joe Abbey & Kwame Pianim clash over statistics" General News of Friday, 04 October 2002, I was particularly disappointed with Dr. Abbey's attitude. From the newspaper account, he is behaving as if the inefficiencies of the Statistical Services and consequently the inaccuracies of the nation's statistics is something that started the day before he joined CEPA. I expected Dr. Abbey to be more intellectually honest about this.
As a member of the National Development Planning Commission from 1983 to 1996, I expected Dr. Abbey to have known and acknowledged the fact that part of the reason why our development plans (including the Economic Recovery Programme and the Structural Adjustment Programmes of the P/NDC era) never worked as planned is because planners based them on inaccurate facts and figures conjured by fertile brains and spewed out by the statistical services for political expediency, in many cases.
Even as a layman in both economics and accounting, I acknowledged this fact more than ten years ago, when my job with an NGO required that I determined annual and multi-year budgets, make long term financial projections which involved taking inflation rates into account. Every single time I had to do this job, I marked up the figures for inflation rates that Dr. Kwesi Botchwey or his successor gave out in their annual budgets by 10 percentage points and used the new figure in my projections.
For instance, if the Government said inflation rate was 40%, I made it 50%. And believe me, I was consistently 98% correct in my projections. Every time the Government gave its end of year reports as part of budget presentations for the new fiscal year, the actual inflation rate they reported for the year under review was always closer to mine than to their projections.
The simple truth is that since the Acheampong days, our approach to data collection and usage has never been efficient. In many cases, professionalism was sacrificed, as government officials pandered to political expediencies. Let me give you two clear examples of why this happened.
Case 1: In July 1995, the NGO community in Northern Ghana started warning of an imminent famine in the entire north in the coming 1996 "hungry season". The NGOs demanded meetings with the Regional Ministers to discuss the problem. At the meeting in Northern Region, I witnessed an awful scene, which cemented my conviction that there is something wrong with the way we collect and report figures in this country.
While the personnel from the NGOs, the University for Development Studies and the Ministry of Health were busy setting up projectors to present their findings from thorough assessments on why they thought there was a problem, next to me was the Regional Director of Agriculture, busily consulting with his staffer about estimated production levels of various crops in the Northern Region. This is how the conversation went on:
Director: So, Saboba-Chereponi, how many hectares of maize do you think they cultivated?
Staff: Mmmmh, about 200. Or 250. We can make it 300.
Director: Ok. Let's make it 250 times (then he punched in a predetermined production rate per hectare that certainly had no bearing with the reality). How about Yendi, Bimbilla and the rest?
Staff: They will be about the same.
Director: Ok. Now yams. What is the acreage? Etc
I could not stand it any longer. I took a walk out, to wait for the Regional Minister who was already one hour late to the scheduled meeting (another statistical problem). When Gilbert Iddi, the Deputy Regional Minister finally arrived amidst apologies, everyone in the room except the Regional Director of Agriculture agreed there was a problem coming up. The NADMO representatives in the room said that "from our sources on the ground, there is a problem". Even the Deputy Regional Minister, an Agriculturist by training indicated that from his personal observations as he travels round, there seems to be a problem coming due to the drought we were witnessing. But the Regional Director would not budge in his convictions.
A month or two later, Rawlings, the President at the time came to Tamale expressly to meet with the NGOs and other bodies to assess the situation. I refused to attend that meeting because of my experiences with the last one. From the accounts I got from colleagues who attended, President Rawlings in his outrage at the apparent lies of the Ministry of Agriculture staff, flew at one of the MOH representatives, mistaking him for the Regional Director for Agriculture, shouting - "And you people have been feeding us with lies all along".
Case 2: I was discussing the problem of inaccurate statistical reporting with a colleague who worked for the Ghana Highways Authority. He volunteered his experiences with the road sector. He was a member of a team that had been commissioned to assess a newly resurfaced road, prior to official commissioning by President Rawlings. I believe this was the Tema, Akosombo-Ho road. According to him, when the team presented their findings, the Director for the Division responsible screamed: "You people want me to lose my job? Please go and do something about these figures before we can present them." So, they took back the report, doctored the figures and made it look nice. Readers will recall the media war that went on between President Rawlings and the Engineers of the Ghana Highways Authority, when Rawlings remarked about apparent shoddy work on the same road when he went to commission it. The rest is history.
My case: The problem with wrong statistics not new, as Dr. Joe Abbey seems to present. On the contrary, an endemic national problem had its roots in several areas. I will stick with two examples again:
a) A cultural problem?: It would seem that collecting data and using them to make decision is not part of our modern educated culture. I make a distinction between us, the so-called educated generation and our grandparents. Unlike us, they kept track of everything without the aid of pencils and paper. Part of the problem with us, the educated ones is that our educational system has thought us to memorize available facts and figures, not to make observations, discern patterns and make decisions or judgements based on those patterns. Take a simple example - how many of us can tell off hand approximately how long it takes us to walk to our most favourite spot next to our houses or drive to work? Why are we all habitually late to pre-arranged functions when we all wear watches? Watches are ornaments, not instruments of data collection.
Against this background of generalized lack of appreciation of data, the collection and use of data for decision-making purposes is a forced phenomenon. In other words, unless someone else is insisting that we collect good information and use it, we would rather not do it. That's what Dr. Abbey admits to when he invokes the displeasure of the IMF and the World Bank as the basis for his arguments about the inefficiencies of the Statistical Service. We need to do a good job because the IMF and the World Bank say so, not because it is vital for our effective planning.
But the corollary to this attitude is that we do not have any qualms about giving out any information irrespective of the quality as long as it gets us off the hook.
I recall that the P/NDC government defended itself against wrongful figures presented to the same world bodies with the statement that it had merely used "creative accounting" practices. I recall Ekow Spio Garbrah making this case on national television. This practice of creative accounting, took place between when Dr. Joe Abbey was a member of the National Development Planning Commission, and was definitely proposing development plans for this country based on these creatively conjured figures.
On assumption of office, the Kufuor administration had to clean up the records of its predecessor. In deed, their commitment to do this was commended by the World Bank and the IMF. So for Dr. Abbey to say that the "International Monetary Fund (IMF) has of late been complaining about the quality of Ghana's statistics issued by the Statistical Service" (my emphasis) is to say the least, a distortion of the truth. The problem is not a recent one.
b) An incurable problem? I say definitely no. The problem with unprofessional data collection and reporting in official circles is, in my view, another outcome of the indiscipline that our society has suffered since the 1970s. I have reason for my stating this. I was in primary school in the mid 60s and early 70s. At the time, in the head teachers' office in every school, a blackboard had the name of every teacher, his or her highest qualification, the year of completion, the classes they were teaching and the number of children in the class. Every morning and every evening, the teachers dutifully marked the registers and we all had to answer "Present Sir" individually, or "Absent Sir" in a chorus when a person called out was not there. Never mind that the teacher was a lady. In many cases, especially for the lower classes, that did not make a difference.
By 1990, most schools in the three Northern Regions had no registers. I recall the bitter exchanges my organisation had with the Director of Basic Schools at GES headquarters in Accra. We wanted to buy or pay for the printing of registers to enable us supply them to primary schools in the three northern regions because we needed them to collect data for determining the impact of our interventions in promoting enrolment and attendance in primary schools. Her curt response was - "No. You have no business in the supply of registers".

Original_Search_Title: To Me, East, West, Home Was Worst
Headline: Kenya: To Me, East, West, Home Was Worst
Body_Text:
Kampala — East or west home is best. No, not always. I proved this age-old adage down right wrong recently when I decided to travel home after being nestled in Uganda for two solid years.
The night before my journey, I felt my mind wander as if to take a secret ride to my homeland and then slowly return. I was longing for home.
Desiring once again to step in Kisumu -- a land flowing with fish and posho; a land where Luo language reigns so supreme that you may earn a criminal sentence for speaking in another tongue. Little did I know how much of a stranger I had become to my own country and in my own homeland.
It is exactly 12:00 noon, when I disembark from a Busia -bound taxi at the border.
I clear up with the immigration and trudge the dusty road across "no man's land" to the Kenyan side. My heart immediately beats with excitement and a feeling of relief dawns on me as soon as I hear a young man talking to me in my language.
"Jatelo idhi Kisumu?" ("Big man are you travelling to Kisumu?") he asks. Before I respond, five other taxi touts have surrounded me. They all want to drag me to their parked matatus (taxis). I have neither time nor a right to make an independent choice. The five men begin to serve themselves my luggage, each taking a different direction like they were my brothers welcoming me home. Having been pruned down by Buganda culture of humility for over two years, I cannot dare shout. Touched by my innocence and ignorance of the Kenyan way of life, an elderly man pleads on my behalf and my luggage is returned and parked on one taxi.
My patience wanes as we wait in a stuffed taxi, which is filled to the brim. I am dumb struck by the discourtesy with which the Kenyan conductors handle passengers. It is long since I was last pushed, packed and squeezed with my fellow passengers like commercial goods.
"Umelipa pesa ngapi Mzee?" ("How much money have you paid?") The conductor asked me in the same tone that a man would use to know why his houseboy has left the toilet unflashed. I responded softly but shocked.
Kenyan taxis have their own unique culture usually exhibited in the language used by conductors when collecting of money from passengers and the rough unfriendly style in which passengers are handled.
Unlike their Ugandan counterparts, the fares are collected before the vehicle takes off. This is a secret strategy, which helps them to "disarm" the passengers and restrain them from transferring to another taxi.
At 2:00pm, our taxi finally takes off. We begin wondering if we shall reach our destination safely because the mechanical condition of the vehicle leaves every heart doubting.
The stuffed taxi literally "cries" and "complains" of its ribs and creaking joints whenever we approach road bumps. My ribs have started complaining too. The speed is high.
The conductor hangs loosely at the entrance and invites prospective passengers. "Kisumu ! Kisumu, gari ni wazi" ("Kisumu! Kisumu! the vehicle is still empty,") he announces as he slaps the vehicle hard as if punishing a pickpocket.
My arrival in Kisumu is a relief, which I celebrate by taking a deep breath. Yes, it is a relief not only from the suffocating stench emanating from the conductor's armpit, but also from the pinching metal protruding from my seat.
The first three days spelt disaster for me. My family made every effort to make me feel entertained and comfortable. Oops! All these landed on a solid wall. Their meals fell far much below what I would consider as an average meal going by Buganda standards.
Plain posho and fish stared at me straight from the dining table. As I pulled my chair nearer, my mind took a flight like a butterfly. Memories of matooke, cassava, yams, delicious dishes prepared in luwombo, coupled with somebody kneeling to serve them haunted me and almost suppressed my appetite.
For the first time I realised I was a total stranger to the Kenyan menu.
My two weeks stay in Kisumu were days of starvation. Starving with food around me. Of course my family members are bound to curse Uganda for what it has made out of their son.
All the blame (or is it praise?) should be directed to my sweet-heart Margaret Nagawa (the Col. Kayanja of my heart) who has carried out an operation and transformed me from fish & posho menu to luwombo and matooke. Aah! Thank God I am back to Uganda.

Original_Search_Title: Our Food Situation is Still [Difficult]
Headline: Ghana: Our Food Situation is Still [Difficult]
Body_Text:
Accra — At his second press conference in Accra, President John Agyekum Kufuor told newsmen that as a result of good rains this year and the efforts of farmers throughout the country, there is a food glut in the country. Prices of food items have therefore come down. At the 22nd World Food Day flag-raising ceremony in Accra, Minister of Food and Agriculture, Courage Quarshigah assured the nation of his ministry's commitment towards a further boost in food production from the current four per cent annually to six percent.
One of the minister's strategies is to develop access to technology for sustainable natural resource management like the efficient use of water resources for agricultural production.
Read his lips: "We can find enough water to feed the world. But we must pool our resources and strengthen our commitment to improve water management for agricultural production."
Public Agenda does not believe we have reached the stage where the country could feed all its citizens without breaking the backs of most families. We might have done well for ourselves this year. But there are many families out there who simply cannot feed themselves. And it is not entirely their fault.
With all the food glut the President talks about, the cost of an average size tuber of yam is ¢4,000 in Accra, more than half the average daily wage. A ball of kenkey sells at ¢1,000 and the smallest finger of fish is ¢2,000. In the rural areas, prices are even more prohibitive. Many people are finding cheaper means of filling the stomach without the requisite nutritional value.
Compared to our earning capabilities, Ghana, certainly, is one of the most expensive places to buy food in the West African Sub-Region.
A nation that cannot feed its citizens would have a problem with development and human advancement. That is why the Weekend Agenda calls for more effort to reduce the cost of food in this country. We believe the food situation would improve tremendously if concerted efforts were put into eliminating the many middlemen in the food business. In the markets across the country, there are the so-called 'market queens' who simply collect commissions on things sold in the markets. They add nothing to the actual process of bringing food and goods to the markets, but they put themselves in the middle of the poor women who struggle to bring the food items and the buyer.
The state of our roads is also not helping the food cause. Many food items end up on the rubbish tips instead of the homes of consumers as a result of the deplorable roads that lead to the hinterland. If and when the road situation improves, there is every evidence that most of the food going waste in the hinterlands will be carted to the needy in the many towns and villages.
Talk of transportation and some of our drivers charge exorbitant fares for carting food items. Exhortations to our drivers to be reasonable with the fare system appear to have fallen on deaf ears and someone ought to get tough with them. In the long run, farmers would have to be encouraged with subsidies to feed the nation and for export. The so-called advanced nations give their farmers all sorts of incentives to produce. We cannot abandon our farmers to their fate and expect them to be motivated to feed the nation.

Original_Search_Title: Prioritize Farming - Ghanaian Ambassador
Headline: Liberia: Prioritize Farming - Ghanaian Ambassador
Body_Text:
Monrovia — The Ambassador of Ghana near Monrovia, Kwame Amoah Awuah, is urging
the food and agricultural authorities in Liberia to attach importance
to the farming in Liberia.
Ambassador Awuah said the setting up of incentives in the form of
awarding annual prizes to best farmers will encourage competition
among farmers and would increase food production in the country.
The Ghanaian Ambassador spoke to The NEWS in Buchanan following his
participation in the 22nd Anniversary of the World Food Day at the
Buchanan Trade Fair Center last Friday.
Describing the celebration of the World Food Day as "very important,"
he explained that Ghanaian authorities have set aside every first
Monday of December as National Farmers Day.
Ambassador Awuah noted that the Ghanian Government awards the best
farmer of the year a farm equipment, either a tractor, a truck or a
twin-cabin pickup.
"This has increased the level of agriculture in Ghana," he said.
At present, Ghanaians are exporting basic food commodities such as
cassava, plantain, cocoa and yam amongst others, even though that
country was dependent and insufficient in food production in the
early and mid eighties.
Ambassador Awuah said the annual award program is credited for the
rise in the level of production in Ghana.
On the presence and contribution of the Ghanian community, the
Ambassador praised the Ghanaian community for its efforts towards
providing the needs of Liberians.
He said Ghanaians in Liberia have been here fishing a hundred year
ago since they migrated.
The ambassador promised to collaborate with the Food & Agriculture
Organization (FAO) to erect fish dryers in the Fanti communities so
as to enhance their preservation of fish for the consuming market
through out the year.
Commenting on the theme of this year's World Food Day, he said "Water
is the source of life, without water, we all would perish, we can't
have food and the body will be bad off," he said.
He commended the FAO for its projects, the fish drier which he said
would ensure the availability of fish on the local market and the
lowland farming which when cultivated would ensure environmental
protection.
The FAO Resident Representative, Castro Camarada, during the tour of
the Fish Processor and Dryer, assured some Bassa fishermen in
Kingsville that the PAO was aimed at ensuring a rise in the income of
low level income earners in the agricultural sector.
He said the dryer, though little, was a manifestation of this agenda,
saying "sometimes little things make big impact.

Original_Search_Title: Tinubu Plans Ethnic Justice Groups
Headline: Nigeria: Tinubu Plans Ethnic Justice Groups
Body_Text:
Lagos — Lagos State Governor, Bola Ahmed Tinubu said that his administration had concluded plans to inaugurate Justice Group in the major ethnic groups in the state.
Tinubu made the disclosure at the weekend through the Secretary to the State Government, Alhaji Aro Lambo, at the 2002 annual Igbo New Yam Festival organised by the Eze Igbo of Lagos State, Eze H. N. Ohazurike, at the State Grammar School, Ifako-Ijaiye, Local Government in the state.
The essence of inaugurating the justice group, Tinubu said, was to explore opportunities of interactions with the ethnic groups to ensure peace, unity and progress, adding that the meeting with the Igbo "today" had been the much needed opportunity he had long nurtured.
He described Ohazurike's address, which centred on peace, justice and equity as timely.
He informed Ndigbo that the suspended breakfast meeting with the governor at the Lagos House, Marina Lagos would soon resume.
He promised that there would be more progress next year in the country and urged all Nigerians to denounce violence at all levels.
The festival witnessed the conferment chieftaincy titles on 12 Igbo ladies and six men in the presence of Chief M.C.K. Ajuluchukwu and Dr Ozuo Okoye, the Traditional Prime Minister of Igboland who represented Ikemba Nnewi, Chief Odimegwu Ojukwu.

Original_Search_Title: Edumadze Bemoans Falling Standard of Education in CR
Headline: Ghana: Edumadze Bemoans Falling Standard of Education in CR
Body_Text:
THE CENTRAL Regional Minister, Mr. Isaac Edumadze, has admitted that the falling standard of education in his region is of great concern and is "an indictment on the region," reputed to have some of the best endowed Senior Secondary Schools (SSS) in the country.
He deplored the fact that in spite of the endowment, the region's sons and daughters cannot access them through no fault of theirs. "In the past these schools virtually provided the manpower requirements of the country both at home and abroad.
Hon. Edumadze said this when he had his turn in the "meet-the-press" series, organised by the Ministry of Information and Presidential Affairs in Accra, yesterday.
Some of them are distinguished corporate managers in industry, banking, academia and even international diplomats of no mean repute (Kofi Annan). Mr. Isaac Edumadze said the region had come to grips with "this disaster" and is determined not to bask in the glory of its distant past.
He said to reverse this trend, the Regional Co-ordinating Council (RCC), as a first stop, directed all district assemblies to identify the present problems confronting the development of education in the districts.
The reports submitted were collated and discussed at a forum involving all stakeholders in the provision of education in the region.
He said the strategy to address the problem has been formulated and is being implemented by the various district assemblies, adding the RCC has put in place a task force to periodically monitor the implementation of the strategy in the district on monthly basis to enable the RCC keep track of the objective.
He explained that the security system in the region has an enviable record of being among the relatively peaceful areas in the country, adding that though everything is not perfect they are just managing their differences through dialogue and conflict resolutions mechanisms.
Hon Edumadze noted that the two disturbing problems in the region are chieftaincy and land disputes but said efforts are being made to assist the Regional House of Chiefs solve the numerous chieftaincy problems in the region.
On agriculture the minister said farming and fishing have remained the oldest and dominant occupations of the people with about 60.4% engaged in farming and 9.6% in fishing Major crops grown in the area include cocoa, oil palm, citrus, coconut, pineapple, sugarcane and coconut with the major crops as maize, cassava, yam, plantain, and coco yam.
He disclosed that the Ministry of Works and Housing is undertaking the dredging of the Benya Lagoon and the canoe anchorage next year under the poverty reduction programme.
In a bid to alleviate the poverty of the fishmongers in the storage and marketing of fish, the Ministry of Women and Children's Affairs last year gave out loans to the tune of ¢400 million from its Human Development Fund to women's groups at Apam, Moree, Elmina and Cape Coast.
Through the same fund, women farmers have also been given ¢500 million. On regional trade and investment the minister said, in pursuance of poverty reduction, job creation with the aim of making the region a prime destination for investments the RCC organised a regional trade and investment fair from August 30 - September 6, this year, under the theme, 'Harnessing the trade and investment potentials of the Central Region.
On the Mankessim chieftaicy dispute which Chronicle wanted more information on his stand, he said as Regional Minister he had no authority to dabble in chieftaincy disputes. His only role is to ensure peace and stability in the region.
He said Chronicle sometimes sends its scouts to his village to ferret information about him, after he had spoken to them, it appears a different picture is portrayed thereafter.
Recently, the Chronicle on Saturday published a story about his alleged interest in the Mankessim chieftaincy dispute and the subsequent transfer of the registrar of the Mankessim traditional council from the area.
He denied his personal interest in the dispute and said the president of the Central Regional House of Chiefs, Nana Amanfo Poku, maintains that as far as the office of the Central Regional House of Chiefs is concerned Nana Amanfo Edu, the Omanhene, who is legally gazetted, has inflamed the protracted chieftaincy dispute in the Mankessim state is still the chief of Mankessim, until his case pending before the court is resolved.
But Chronicle can reveal that the other faction led by Nana Opoku Akwaa II has continued to produce documentary evidence to th effect that there had been rulings against Nana Amanfo Edu by a Cape Coast High Court.
The president of the Central Regional House of Chiefs, Nana Amanfo Poku who was before the Regional Security Council and said he knew nothing about the removal of Richard Hagan's name from the Register of Chiefs.
One account had it that Nana Amanfo Poku was the person who instructed his registrar to write a covering letter dated January 17, 2002 to the National House of Chiefs for Richard Hagan's name to be deleted from the National Register of Chiefs.
Another account the Chronicle sighted has also revealed that the president of the Central Regional House of Chiefs who last Friday declared that Nana Amanfo Edu (Richard Hagan) is the recognised chief of Mankessim and that there is a case pending before the court on him, sat as chairman on the committee which was said to have nullified Nana Amanfo Edu's gazette in 1995.
The Mankessim chieftaincy dispute started when Richard Hagan (Nana Amanfo Edu II) was purportedly nominated and allegedly installed as chief, contrary to laid down customary practices.
He was reported to have been confined on a Friday afternoon and outdoored the following day against the normal practice of confining a candidate seven days before installation.
Meanwhile, Mr. Edumadze yesterday admitted giving instructions to the Deputy Director of the Central Regional Coordinating Council, Mr. Ben Asomani of the Regional Council's inability to recognize Nana Opoku Akwaa II as the acting president of the Mankessim Traditional Council in a letter dated September 27, 2002.
A follow up to that question by Chronicle's Linda Akrasi, as to whether the Regional Co-ordinating Council had powers to determine who should act as president of the Mankessim Traditional Council, was overruled by the Information Minister, Jake Obetsebi Lamptey, on grounds that Edumadze had stated clearly that the Regional House of Chief's president said his records still recognized Richard Hagan as paramount chief in the disputed area.

Original_Search_Title: Volta Lake Transport Under Threat
Headline: Ghana: Volta Lake Transport Under Threat
Body_Text:
Afram Plains — THE VOLTA Lake Transport Company (VLTC) may soon withdraw its pontoon services on the Afram lake between Adawso in the Kwahu South district and Ekye Amanfrom in the Afram Plains district of the Eastern Region.
This is due to the fact that the company has been running at a loss since the district assembly enacted a law to ban charcoal burning and formed a task force including some army personnel to enforce the by-law on it.
A source at the VTLC headoffice at Akosombo told Chronicle that since the company is a limited liability company which operates for profit, it could be very difficult for it to run its services at a loss as is happening at Adawso-Ekye Amanfrom.
The source said, there are so many routes which need pontoon services and if the trend continues the company will have no choice but to withdraw its pontoon, "Nana Kwasi Gyimah," at the Adawso-Ekye Amanfrom to a profitable route.
This means travellers on that portion of the lake would resort to the unsafe means of using canoes and small boats which had already claimed countless lives in crossing the lake.
When the station officer of the company at the Adawso station, Mr. Samuel Kwaku Bonney was contacted, he confirmed the ban on drivers using their trucks for carting charcoal from the Afram Plains has affected the company's revenue greatly since it forms more than 40 per cent of its revenue.
He said he has informed the company's headoffice about the situation and will soon meet and take a decision on it.
A resident farmer and a transport owner, Mr. Kwaku Osei, told this reporter that most truck drivers had withdrawn their vehicles on the Afram Plains route due to the constant harassment by the assembly's task force.
He said the task force sometimes forces the drivers to offload yam and other foodstuffs they had taken over three days to load, to look for charcoal, which the task force claimed were hidden in the trucks.
Osei stated that if the situation does not change, foodstuffs and other farm produce from the areas may pile up and go to waste since the farmers would not get vehicles to cart them to the market centres.
At the moment, the harvesting of yam is in progress but the drivers are refusing to go to the area.
Chronicle's visit to the affected communities such as Maame Krobo, Kwasi Kune, Kwasi Fante, Kwae Kese, Asanyansu Dagarti junction, German Akuraa, Nyame Bekyere, Dome and several other areas saw thousands of tonnes of bags of charcoal piled up due to the ban.
The charcoal burners and traders who said they were in a difficult situation appealed to the authorities to give a human face to the whole exercise and allow them to cart the charcoal they had already burnt to the market centers.
They claimed that constant rains in the area did not allow them to cart the charcoal from the bush, so they could not beat the deadline set them by the Afram Plains district assembly, which was the ending of September, this year.
When the Afram Plains district administration was contacted, the district co-ordinating director, Mr. Thomas Ba-Innimayeh, stated that the assembly took the decision in banning charcoal burning in the district when the Abetifihene and the Adontenghene of Kwahu traditional area, Nana Asiedu Agyemang III, who has a vast land in the plains complained of the activities of charcoal burning and its devastating effect on the land and the environment.
He told Chronicle that due to the problems the burning was creating, the Eastern Regional Minister, Dr. Francis Osafo Mensah, and his Ashanti Region counterpart, Mr. Sampson Kwaku Boafo, who controls the Sekyere East district in the Ashanti Region has also ordered the discontinuation of charcoal burning at the end of November, this year to enable the two districts which share a common boundary to agree on the same date.

Original_Search_Title: A Trip to Ouagadougou And Back By Road
Headline: Nigeria: A Trip to Ouagadougou And Back By Road
Body_Text:
On Thursday, September 26, 2002 like every other Thursday, Daily Trust held its Editorial Board meeting, and brainstormed on numerous issues among which was the crisis in Cote d'Ivoire. By that time the Ivorien conflict was just one week old.
The editorial advisory board, comprising many eminent personalities, recommended among others points, that the newspaper should, besides writing an editorial to caution on the dangers of allowing the crisis to escalate further, send two reporters to Cote d'Ivoire and its northern neighbour, Burkina Faso, to carry out thorough investigations on the crisis.
Specifically, the reporters were to investigate into the genesis, dimensions and implications of the crisis, for not just the two countries, but the West African sub-region. These recommendations were graciously approved by the Editor-in-Chief of the newspapers who is also the Chairman of the board.
As a member of the editorial advisory board, it crossed my mind while at the meeting, that I may be one of the two reporters to be called upon to carry out this assignment. This sudden thought that came to my mind was neither borne out of any over zealousness, nor a belief in my ability to deploy any extraneous means to get assigned. Rather, it was simply one of those natural instincts.
And at about 6 p.m. while I was preparing to close from the office the same day, the Editor Daily Trust summoned the Features Editor and myself to his office to tell the two of us that we have been chosen to go for the assignment. The Editor also briefed us on what specifically the company expects us to do. While my colleague was sent to Cote d'Ivoire, I was asked to go to Burkina Faso.
Burkina Faso, as I later came to know is the "land of Honest people." Originally called 'Mossi Kingdom' after the age-old West African empire founded by the Mossi people, the country changed names six times in less than a century. It was named 'Colonie de Haute Senegal-Niger' when it and several other territories, including Cote d'Ivoire came under French control in 1904.
About 15 years later, it became 'Colonie de Haute Volta' (Upper Volta) after it was split from other territories.
Further changes took place when in 1932 the country was carved up and integrated into the administrative territories of neighbouring colonies.
