Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo's Self-Inflicted Injury
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo's Self-Inflicted Injury
Body_Text:
Lagos — A BBC correspondent was in 1978 taken aback when a former Nigerian Head of State told him that he would like Aminu Kano to win the 1979 presidential election because of his honesty. The correspondent asked: "Is honesty the sole qualification for leadership in your country?"
Even if a presidential candidate was unintelligent or naive but honest, the former military ruler would have still regarded him as a fit and proper person for the leadership of Africa's largest black nation in a very complex world which was getting more and more sophisticated.
I have in the last few weeks been reflecting on what our elite regard to be the functions of leadership in every society and the appropriate qualifications prescribed for those who want to assume leadership. It is dispiriting to hear otherwise knowledgeable people voice their disappointment with President Olusegun Obasanjo's uninspiring score-card when they expected so much from him given his prison experience and his credentials as a former military ruler, and as a born-again Christian. No-one ever speaks as a requirement for good leadership the critical element which Obafemi Awolowo called mental magnitude, that is, an intelligence far above average, defined by wide reading, good exposure, imaginative thought and a measure of philosophical speculation. Yet, it is a trite fact to acknowledge that it is not possible for any society to make appreciable progress if its leadership is not possessed of mental rigour. Think of John Kennedy, Bill Clinton, Winston Churchill, Lee Kuan Yew, Yoweri Museveni, Mahathir Mohammed, etc, and the monumental changes they unleashed on their societies at critical moments in history.
In his book, This House Has Fallen: Midnight In Nigeria, Karl Mier, the perceptive American journalist, quotes Bamanga Tukur as comparing Olusegan Obasanjo to a truck driver and Alex Ekwueme to a Rolls Royce driver. Still, Tukur spent considerable resources in 1998 and 1999 on Obasanjo's emergence as the presidential candidate of the Peoples Democratic Party over and above Ekwueme. Tukur's reason for giving solid support to Obasanjo, against Ekwueme, according to this book, derived from Tukur's reasoning that Nigeria was in need of a truck driver, rather than his Rolls Royce counterpart as its president. Bamanga Tukur was not alone.
And this is the tragedy of the Nigerian elite. What manner of elite would proudly tell foreign journalists that the leader of his country in the 21st century should be someone comparable to a truck driver, even when a Rolls Royce driver is available and willing to do the job? What do people like Tukur think of leadership and the qualities of someone who should lead Nigeria out of its present development morass?
The late Ken Saro-Wiwa used to be appalled at the tendency of Africans and blacks to abandon critical thinking in favour of cheap, illogical and sometimes superstitious ways out of every problem. In his novel-in-progress, Prisoners of Jebs, Ken argued vigorously that the failure to think creatively and deeply had made blacks be at the bottom of the global heap. Ken was right.
Obasanjo was not mentally prepared for the office of the President when elements like Ibrahim Babangida, acting in concert with top officials of the departing Abdulsalami Abubakar government, resolved to foist him on the Nigerian people. He made no promises, and never offered any insights into his understanding of the Nigerian condition. I was one of the organisers of a scheduled live television debate between him and Olu Falae, joint presidential candidate of the All People's Party and the Alliance for Democracy. Up to less than an hour to the event, we were assured that Obasanjo would participate in the debate at NICON Hilton Hotel in Abuja where he was also lodging. But Obasanjo didn't show up, after all. Falae gave a good account of himself on the live programme compered by James Audu and John Momoh.
After his election as President, Obasanjo was asked the same questions on an NTA live programme as Falae was asked, word for word. His performance was sheer embarrassment. For instance, when asked about what he would do to ensure that Nigeria joined the Information Technology revolution, he went into an anecdotal joke about pounded yam, egusi soup, bush meat, a piece of rock, warts and all.
The tragedy of Obasanjo's ill-preparedness has since been exacerbated by his choice of ministers and the portfolios assigned to them. How can one be enthused at the choice of Sule Lamido as Foreign Minister, Adamu Ciroma as Finance Minister, Sunday Afolabi as Internal Affairs Minister, Pauline Tallen as Minister of State for Science and Technology and Tony Anenih as Minister of Works in today's very complex world?
Obasanjo's preference to work with Evan Enwerem and Anyim Pius Anyim as Senate President was not borne out of a genuine concern for the nation but by a consuming desire to run an imperial presidency , unfettered by a system of checks and balances which a vibrant legislature would cause to be put in place. Though the President does not have a wonderful opinion about the abilities of Anyim and Enwerem, he insisted fiercely on their becoming Senate President because, in his language "there cannot be two captains in one ship". That's why he spared no resources, including the use of about 100 riot policemen at an odd hour, to intimidate and unseat Chuba Okadigbo as Senate President.
Obasanjo's hijack of the national leadership of the ruling Peoples Democratic Party and turning the party lights of the Peoples Democratic Movement (PDM) faction of the PDP was to cause severe problems for the nation. The PDM is a perfect example of a group which knows how to grab power but without purpose. The likes of Works Minister Anenih are not people imbued with any development vision or any kind of redemptive consciousness. All they know is, to borrow the words of Umaru Shinkafi, to put the nation on a perpetual tempo of politicking and electioneering campaign. Hence, the obsession with "re- election", "2003" and "no vacancy" right from the first year of this administration.
Obasanjo last April 26 officially launched his campaign re-election in general polls scheduled to take place in March, 2003, that is, a whole eleven months away. Let us compare this development with what happens elsewhere. President Jacques Chirac of France announced only last February his interest in recontesting in an election coming up two months later, but stated that he would not begin to campaign until April, the very month the first round of election was taking place. Chancellor Schroeder of Germany announced only last June his interest in the election taking place three months later but began campaign on August 6 would take him to 40 cities. In other words, he's campaigning for one month. In the Philippines, candidates campaign for only six weeks.
Taking a cue from the Obasanjo re-election hysteria generated by the PDM, state governors have adopted all manner of tactics to return to power by all means possible, including doing such grotesque and bizarre this, as their adoption in the second year of their four-year-tenure by their parties as gubernational candidates. Yet, there are clear party guidelines about how such candidates can emerge during special conventions.
No-one should be surprised at the deplorable state of federal roads or the parlous condition of our economy. After all, Anenih and Ciroma, arrow heads of the Obasanjo campaign organisation, are now more or less part-time Works and Finance Ministers respectively. Does Obasanjo need be told that when President George Bush, Sr., chose Secretary of State Jim Baker to co-ordinate his re-election campaign, Baker quit the government?
It is curious that despite PDP's overwhelming majority in the National Assembly, Obasanjo has been in a gridlock with the law-makers. This is because of lack of party cohesion, party discipline and party solidarity, a situation Obasanjo created more than any person. Didn't he very early in the life of the administration get APP and AD senators as well as renegade PDP senators to ensure the triumph of Enwerem, over Okadigbo, the official candidate of his own party, a party he himself leads by virtue of his position? And to think that Enwerem was an APP member a few months earlier, but also one of the so-called two million-man marchers for Sani Abacha's perpetual presidency!
As though to add insult to injury, Obasanjo, Abubakar, Anyim and Anenih decided that Okwesilieze Nwodo should make way for Vincent Ogbulafor as the PDP National Secretary. None of the stakeholders in the South-east zone of the PDP was consulted, as Ogbulafor, an APP member until two weeks earlier, was imposed on the party. By his new position, Ogbulafor became the custodian of whatever values the PDP has, a position Pope John Paul 11 spinted determination of the faith". No step was considered indecent in the to stop Nwodo and others from contesting at the November 2001 convention.
Obasanjo and his henchmen did succeed in having mostly pipsqueaks in the PDP leadership. But most of them are not popular with the party membership, nor are they competent. As the PDP leadership tries to rein in its National Assembly members who have engaged the president in a fight to a finish Audu Ogbeh of all the members of the national working committee has been inspiring a measure of confidence. But a tree cannot make a forest.
Obasanjo has paralysed the PDP. The same way he has paralysed governance. His is a self-inflicted tragedy. Too bad.
Adinaba is head of the West African Office Discovery International Communicators.

Original_Search_Title: Options for the Rainbow Alliance
Headline: Kenya: Options for the Rainbow Alliance
Body_Text:
Nairobi — With Musalia Mudavadi having decamped from the Rainbow Alliance, one thing is certain even in the uncertain and constantly shifting waters of Moi Succession politics: the other key members of the alliance will have to re-evaluate their political positions and options.
Most political analysts expect Tourism and Information Minister Stephen Kalonzo Musyoka to follow Mudavadi's example and ditch the alliance any time soon.
In fact, conventional wisdom had it that Musyoka would be first of the Rainbow quartet to buckle when push came to shove. But as it happened, Musyoka has surprisingly so far managed to hold his own to the extent that he even parried off direct pressure from President Moi to ditch the alliance about a fortnight ago.
But just how long Musyoka will last now that Mudavadi has caved in to pressure remains the big question. Nevertheless, to many the question of Musyoka's going back to the main fold is not one of "if" but rather "when."
Whereas for Mudavadi, the Transport and Communications Minister, it may not have been very difficult eating the humble pie and going back to Mama na baba singing mea culpa, mea culpa, for other key, nay, principle players in the Rainbow Alliance-former Vice-President George Saitoti and Kanu Secretary General Raila Odinga- things may not be that simple.
As things stand, for Saitoti and Raila, chances are that they have gone beyond the point of no return. And as such, for them backtracking now from the anti-Project Uhuru path that they have chartered in the last couple of weeks may not be a very viable political option.
In fact, talking to sources close to the two politicians it emerges quite clearly that, at least for now, the two have adopted as their political maxim "forward ever, backwards never."
But on the other hand, the chief proponent of Project Uhuru and the man holding the yam and the knife and is still wielding the big stick, President Moi, has taken as his political mantra the words "its all or nothing" as far as Project Uhuru goes.
As such, with both Saitoti and Raila being experienced and streetwise politicians, it is obviously not lost to them that continuing to pursue their anti-Uhuru political agenda from within the precincts of Kanu will be the political equivalent of hitting their heads against a brick wall and hoping to make a pathway. Remember Mathew Adams Karauri's words?
But for some reason, it seems like although the only viable option open to both Raila and Saitoti is to cut loose and ditch Kanu, the two appear determined to tough it out until Kasarani II.
According to sources close to the Rainbow Alliance duo, the former V-P and the Kanu S-G are at the moment "not even considering ditching Kanu as a political option."
Their argument for sticking with the ruling party until Kasarani II is born out of the perception that by doing so they may be able to instil "an element of fair play" in the presidential nominations.
But this is an argument that is not necessarily based on sound political judgement. Given the amount of political carnage that the Kanu chairman has brought to bear on anyone who stands on the way of Project Uhuru, it would be politically naive for any politician to go to Kasarani II with the expectation of pushing an act outside the pre-determined script.
Political prudence dictates that any serious politician in Kanu who does not subscribe to Project Uhuru should either re-think his position and come to the realisation that "national unity is paramount to individual interests" as many are wont to claim when push comes to shove, or simply take a walk and fight from outside.
Mudavadi has taken the former route and Musyoka may soon follow suit. As for Saitoti and Raila, it may as well be the time to call it a day in Kanu.
From the firing of his vice-president last Friday, to the deals that got Njenga Karume ditching Mwai Kibaki last Sunday and onto the behind the scenes manoeuvres that persuaded Mudavadi to decamp from the Rainbow Alliance yesterday, it is becoming increasingly clear that to President Moi, no price is too high, no road is too long and no political manoeuvres will be too complicated when it comes to getting Uhuru in the State House.

Original_Search_Title: The Pros And Cons of Harnessing Biotechnology to Speed Africa's Sustainable Development
Headline: Ghana: The Pros And Cons of Harnessing Biotechnology to Speed Africa's Sustainable Development
Body_Text:
The World Summit on Sustainable Development (WSSD), which began on 26 September in Johannesburg, South Africa, aims to accelerate the implementation of the environmental agenda, established in the Rio Summit a decade ago, and the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs) for human development adopted at the Millennium Summit.
Sustainable development is the merger of human well-being and natural resource stewardship. For Africa, our stakes are highest in the upcoming Summit because our sustainability issues are more acute than in other regions. At current trends, only ten of our countries will meet the poverty reduction, education and health MDGs.  Chronic water shortages are being faced in 14 countries. About 5 million hectares of forests are lost annually in Africa, mostly due to the expansion of crop area. To make matters worse, our ability to grow our own food is being rapidly eroded. Africa is the only region where food production per person has declined over the last 40 years. On a continent where agriculture is the predominant industry and the most important source of income for the poor, this is nothing short of disastrous. About 70% of Africa's population live in rural areas and depend, directly or indirectly, on agriculture. Should the current trend continue, it is estimated that by 2010, more than 35% of the population of sub-Saharan Africa will be undernourished, the highest rates among all world regions.
At a time when over 10 millions people in Southern Africa face drought and hunger, we have no choice but to put our heads together and come up with innovative ways of preserving our soil and feeding our people.
In Africa, the debate on genetically modified (GM) crops has reached new levels of controversy. In the developed countries in particular, there have long been consumer concerns, environmental concerns, ethical, religious, and other societal concerns. All these concerns are to a greater or lesser extent valid, and they apply equally to Africa. Nevertheless, we cannot deny the potential benefits of specific technologies.
We at the Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) believe that modern agricultural biotechnologies, or "green" biotechnology, can help increase food security in Africa, by offering crops with higher yields, stronger resistance to pests and diseases, and greater nutritional value. The use of agricultural biotechnologies, such as disease-free bananas, higher yields sweet potato or vitamin A enriched rice, can effectively complement conventional plant breeding, and provide new tools to achieve food security.
We also believe that technology applied to health, or "red" biotechnology, can make a major impact on African development. Breakthroughs in medical biotechnologies are opening up new prospects for preventing, diagnosing, managing, treating and even curing diseases. The solutions provided by red biotechnology range from affordable HIV/AIDS treatment to mosquitoes genetically engineered not to carry malaria.
In a newly released report entitled Harnessing Technology for Development, we argue that Africa has an important stake in the debate, dictated by the urgent need to feed its growing population.  However, the new technologies are no panacea or silver bullet. African faces a number of daunting challenges that need to be addressed before these technologies can be applied to the benefit of the continent.  We believe that biotechnology should not be seen as a technological "quick fix" for Africa's hunger and poverty problems. Rather, it should be viewed as one part of a comprehensive, sustainable poverty reduction strategy. African countries must exploit a range of options to ensure that future biotechnology initiatives reach their full potential for alleviating poverty, combating disease and securing food security.
The issue at hand for all of us is how the use of these technologies can be implemented and monitored in such a way as to maximize the opportunities and minimize the safety risks. Since poor people and poor countries lack resources, infrastructure, and the business environment to attract new technologies and related investments, a technology-infused development strategy needs to be developed within Africa's limitations. The strategy should include lessons learned and best practice examples from across countries in the continent, about strategic partnerships that are successful, products and processes proven to work, and ways to encourage new ideas and initiatives.
Specifically, and among other options, African countries should seek to:
* Promote African-focused biotechnology research in which emphasis is laid on: the diseases and their strains prevalent in Africa, particularly HIV/AIDS, TB and Malaria; and "orphan crops", particularly cassava, millet sorghum, sweet potato and yams but also other cereals such as maize, rice and wheat;
* Develop African-owned biotechnology policies whereby all the relevant stakeholders, including civil society, private sector farmer organizations, are involved in the formulation of national plans;
* Establish national regulatory institutions for risk assessment and management since most African countries have inadequate human resource capacity to perform these functions;
* Increase investment in modern biotechnology research. The current levels in most African countries are very low (hardly 2% of the total agricultural research funds); and
* Promote public/private sector partnership in modern biotechnology research;
The experience of African countries that have deployed genetically modified (GM) crops shows that success depends on the extent to which countries have pursued these options. Countries cited as success stories include South Africa (maize and cotton), Kenya (sweet potato), and Egypt (maize, faba beans and cotton).
If the options we spell out are taken into account, there is a good chance that biotechnologies can be harnessed to accelerate agricultural productivity and epidemiological transition. Yet the risks are clear to see, and these must be managed so that they never outweigh the benefits.
But the greatest risk of all is for us to do nothing, letting the biotechnology revolution pass Africa by.
* K.Y. Amoako is Executive Secretary of the Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia. E-mail: ecainfo@uneca.org, Website: www.uneca.org

Original_Search_Title: Wem 2002: Lest We Forget
Headline: Nigeria: Wem 2002: Lest We Forget
Body_Text:
Lagos — The World Education Market (WEM) 2002, which took place in Lisbon, Portugal, has come and gone. But with the recent announcement by the organisers, Reed Midem Organisation, Paris, that companies are already booking their stands for next year's edition and knowing that the event will still hold in Portugal, Bukola Olatunji, writes that the country should be prepared to do better than it did this year.
Twice in one day, Director of the World Education Market (WEM), Ms. Elaine Legault told "a moving story of a Nigerian journalist, Rotimi (Oyekanmi) who is not here."
First at the opening press conference of WEM 2002 held at the Lisboa (Portuguese word for Lisbon) Conference Centre, Lisbon, Portugal on Tuesday, May 22 and in her remark at the 'Spotlight on Nigeria' later that day.
Mr. Oyekanmi, who had gone to cover WEM 2001held in Canada, had walked up to her and observed that only three people represented Nigeria. These were Registrar of the Joint Admissions and Matriculation Board JAMB), Prof. Bello Salim, Deputy Director, Research and Planning at the National Broadcasting Commission, Lagos, Dr. Ronke Ogunmakin, as well as former Minister of Education and Deputy Vice Chancellor, Academics of the University of Benin (UNIBEN), Prof. Abraham Imogie.
The fourth Nigerian was Prof. Felicia Oyekanmi (no relation of the journalist), formerly of the University of Lagos (UNILAG), now Senior Programme Officer, Council for the Development of Social Science Research in Africa (CODESRIA), Senegal.
But she was there to discuss how advancing the policy and practice of capacity building in international co-operation has enhanced the achievement of educational goals in emerging economies (a panel session on capacity building), with Senegal as a case study.
This attendance was however, an improvement on the maiden edition (WEM 2000) also held at the Vancouver Convention and Exhibition Centre, British Columbia, Canada. Only Dr. Ogunmakin, who is now a member of the WEM advisory board, was there.
In an interview with THISDAY, Dr. Ogunmakin, an instructional technologist, said she was in California in 1998, doing some networking for her sister who was seeking political office in the United States, when WEM was being conceived. She heard about it through some investors, had discussions with them, and later got invited to the maiden edition.
Upon her return, she organised a workshop in June 2000, for stakeholders within the university. Minister of Information, Prof. Jerry Gana and Prof. Imogie attended the workshop held in Lagos.
Participants were able to look at distance learning, using the mass media to propagate it in Nigerian universities. One of resolutions made was that there should be a network station, which the minister announced, and the Nigerian Television Authority (NTA) was chosen to carry educational materials.
So Oyekanmi told Ms. Legault last year how impressed he was by the quality of the market and promised to go back home and do all he could to see that Nigeria had a bigger representation at this year's edition.
The journalist kept his word. Legault met with the Minister of Education, Prof. Babalola Borishade, along with Oyekanmi and this writer, during last year's 31st General Session of the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organisation (UNESCO) in Paris.
There the foundation for Nigeria's participation at this year's WEM was laid. The rest, as they say, is history. The Federal Ministry of Education approached the Education Tax Fund and the latter's huge financial support ensured that Nigeria had a good showing.
But when WEM 2002 opened its doors on May 21, Rotimi Oyekanmi, and many others who had worked hard to be there, were conspicuously missing. Many people, including Oyekanmi were denied visa by the Portuguese government. Only the Embassy can tell the number. They cut across different parastatals in the Federal and State Ministries of Education.
Executive Secretary of the National Universities Commission (NUC), Prof. Peter Okebukola, who represented the head of the Nigerian delegation and Minister of State for Education, Alhaji Bello Usman, said in an interview that the Abubakar Tafawa Balewa University (ATBU), Bauchi was supposed to be at the show.
According to him, "the institution is developing some very interesting solar-power-driven devices including solar powered computer technology equipment. We wanted to show them (the West) where we are so that we can seek partners from more developed countries to help improve on this prototype." But that did not happen.
To get to that point, the ministry had set up a technical committee chaired by Okebukola, to organise what he called "a mini WEM" at the NUC premises in Abuja, weeks before the event. There every potential participant displayed and demonstrated what they planned to take to Lisbon. For five hours, the Minister of Education, Prof. Babalola Borishade went round the stands. Some were approved, some asked to improve on what they had, and some others knocked off. Those asked to improve on what they had also had to show what improvement they had made.
After all these, some were still denied entry visa. Visa denial is not a big deal and it happens all the time. But there has to be a reason for it. In this case, none was given at all. Many of the passports, like Oyekanmi's were not virgins. They had several other visas in them.
After delegates began to arrive and the organisers got wind of what was going on, WEM officials entered into constant dialogue with the Mayor of Lisbon, the Nigerian Embassy in Portugal and and the Portuguese Embassy in Nigeria. All explanations and pleas fell on deaf ears. One parastatal, Agency for Mass Education had no representative at all.
In the first place, it was as if the Portuguese embassy in Nigeria here did not know about WEM or if it did, never knew how much interest Nigeria had in it. It was completely overwhelmed by the number of applicants. About 80 participants, including officials of State Ministries of Education, and the private sector (schools and computer companies) were able to attend.
But how much better did some of those who got the (15 -day) visa fare? Perhaps one should quickly mention the case of the proverbial outsiders that wept louder than the bereaved - immigration officials at the Schipol Airport, Amsterdam. The only reason we (some officials of the Pulse Marketing Communications or Nigerian Education Exchange, official organisers of the event; Mr. Laolu Harolds and this writer) found ourselves at that airport was because we travelled via KLM (Royal Dutch Airlines). All we wanted was to board the next available plane to Lisbon, which was due in about four hours. But the immigration officials would not let us go, because we did not have proof of our hotel bookings (whereas 120 rooms had been booked and paid for at the prestigious Hotel Estoril Sol) for all participants. Our explanation that we were part of that group fell on deaf ears for a while. Meanwhile, THISDAY and The Guardian had a second booking at Hotel Real Parque, courtesy of WEM.
Eventually this explanation was taken; we then needed to prove that we could feed ourselves for one week! Would they not leave the immigration officials in Lisbon to worry about this? "No way, all Shengen states have agreed to do this" we were told. If ours was like the Paris - bound flight that took off less than an hour after our arrival, and a family friend who was on the same flight with us from Nigeria took it, we would have missed it.
It took one reasonable immigration official to remind his colleagues that quite a number of people from all over the world were heading in the same direction, so they should allow us to go.
Even the former goalkeeper of Super Eagles, Peter Rufai, who had a foreign passport and was on the same flight with us, was not spared. He was surveyed all over by the official and then his passport very slowly scrutinised with a hand lens or magnifying glass. Thank goodness, we eventually got to Lisbon.
In his opening remarks, Chief Executive of Reed Midem Organisation, Mr. Xavier Roy, said that WEM remains faithful to its primary objective: To provide an unparalleled opportunity for business, for networking, learning and building partnerships across sectors and on a global stage.
WEM - where content, technology and expertise converge to shape the future of learning.
Ms. Legault also said in an interview with THISDAY that WEM came about out of the need that was expressed to Reed Midem "for an international platform that would bring the best solutions, practices and resources from around the world to one location so that those who are looking for these resources and solutions could be adapted to their own circumstances".
According to her, "this happens because we are facing a tremendous rise in demand. We have identified with two key associations and organisations like UNESCO, the need to mobilise our forces to accomplish the goals of education for all and WEM is one tool that allows us to get closer to bridging the gap in terms of access to learning opportunities." The host country has to understand that. Nigeria, like other participants, wants to get the full benefit of its investment.
The first and second WEM held in Canada, Legault said, because her organisation wanted the event to be very international from the beginning "and Canada has many links around the world to the commonwealth and to the Francophony. It is one of the countries that have a great deal of experience in education whether through broadcasting or distance learning. It is the heart of the North American continent, which is the most advanced in terms of their educational systems. It has proximity to Asia, and Vancouver is an attractive city that has the kind of infrastructure that we needed for this kind of event so we succeeded in that goal in that we had over 60 countries present both in 2000 and 2001. So when WEM became international, it became easier to move it around and that is what brought us to Lisbon."
It is also interesting to note that WEM had hosting requests from Australia, Asia, Africa and all over Europe. WEM went from 62 countries in Vancouver last year to 71 this year. Legault said, "obviously, our being in Portugal would enable many countries that were not able to attend, to come." And that explains why the market is going there again next year.
In choosing a location, Reed Midem, she said, considers "what our participants want, where they think is convenient and works for them. When I say participants, I mean those who have come as exhibitors and those who have only come to walk in and see. Then we look at very concrete things-the conference facilities, technology infrastructure, international travel access, conviviality of the location, hotels, it is a total experience." The host country has to think very deeply about this.
A journey that should have been real fun left a sour taste in the mouth. That one is reporting this instead of a travelogue is a clear indication of what it was. Although there was hardly any time to explore one of the oldest, yet beautiful cities in Europe. There was not even enough time to cover all the conference programmes. One had to make the sometimes-painful choice of where to be per time.
The opening cocktail that held at the ancient St. George V's Castle for example gave a breathtaking view of the city. Nice people (I believe), beautiful weather, very much like Nigeria's (and both countries are on the same time zone), a long stretch of beach, relatively cheap things to buy, but with little time.
One should not forget to mention that being one of the poorest countries in Europe has not stopped Portugal from maintaining clean streets and an efficient metro system, though wages are largely poor.
How can one forget the only Nigerian eatery in Lisbon, and perhaps, Portugal - Wazobia Kitchen (Restaurant and cafe bar) on Avenida Almirante Reis? The owner, an Igbo man simply called Jones, once played football in Nigeria. He and his wife (and their cute six - month old baby boy, Junior) had lots of pounded-yam, ogbono or egusi soup, as well as goat meat pepper soup, among others to serve several members of the Nigerian delegation that flooded the restaurant every day of the conference. The couple also ran a store where one could get smoked fish, stockfish, cray fish, raw egusi, ogbono. etc. With only about 200 legally resident Nigerians in the entire Portugal, it is easy to understand why Jones' Wazobia is one of a kind. Not cashing in on the monopoly, Jones tried his best to make his customers from home feel at home.
Of course there was the bad tempered driver of one of the two buses hired to ferry delegates through out the fair. The old man could not understand why delegates could not keep to time scheduled for boarding or getting off the bus.
Needless to say, the non-issuance of entry visa to some well deserving people generated a lot of bad blood.
But was the market worth all the trouble? Okebukola said Nigeria was "able to show to the world that development in education in our country is still impressive despite the problems and obstacles along the path to development. We are happy that the world has been able to appreciate what is going on in Nigeria"
The country was also able to forge partnerships with other countries, notably Canada, France, and Germany for developing the educational sector, especially in the area of e learning.
E-learning, he said, is at the heart of educational development in the world. "It is a relatively cheap way of delivering education primary, secondary, and higher."
But how far can e learning go in Nigeria with erratic power supply, inadequate telephone service and so on? The professor of science education said "obstacles to e learning in Nigeria are plenty, but I think we should not look at the obstacles as a way of not making us to begin. I think we should step gallantly on the path of e learning and ensure that within the next five years, we have structures on ground that can enable us beat our chest in another World Education Market about the progress that we have made as a result of our participation in the 2002 edition." That indeed is the crux of the matter.
According to him, "there are always ways round problems. Talk about NEPA, there are solar-powered computers and computer technology material that we can use to deliver e learning. So I would not see the problems of electricity to be one that we cannot surmount. My suggestion here is that we should look for solutions, and these are available by several providers, that can allow us to use solar powered panels to drive the computer technology for delivering e learning."
Okebukola added that "the idea again, is not for you to go national for a start, but to run a pilot project and build up. We have been able to see from so many countries that are represented at WEM that they started with just a handful of schools at the primary levels, secondary and higher. The message for me is that " do not wait until everything is in place" -that NEPA is running 100 per cent, connectivity all over the country, that computers are there before you begin to move."
The Executive Secretary of National Universities Commission (NUC) said the commission is going to organise a national seminar on e learning in higher education. "We want to brainstorm and see. We must begin somewhere and begin quick because every moment we are marking time, the world is moving several paces forward. So no matter how small the level of our movement, we want to move. We want to see how at the Colleges of Education, Polytech-nics and Universities levels, we can introduce some element of e learning albeit on a pilot scale, maybe in the large cities where electricity is not a problem. The University of Lagos is very hot in championing that, so may be we when we meet nationally, we will be to see how we can proceed."
For the next WEM, he said, Nigeria is going to improve on "where we are in terms of technology in delivery. Only the universities were able to put up some bit of technology having to do with our digital library project." Rating Nigeria's overall participation however, Okebukola said "the minister of Education of Zimbabwe and the representative of Cote d' Ivoire said Nigeria has done Africa proud. That in fact, the Nigerian stand is all Africa."

Original_Search_Title: Eating Wrong Foods for Wrong Reasons
Headline: Nigeria: Eating Wrong Foods for Wrong Reasons
Body_Text:
Lagos — Most Nigerians eat foods they don't need and experts blame this on ignorance on the right food to eat to stay healthy
His small status beclouds his age. His intellect is not better. At 10 years, Lukman looks and behaves like a five-year-old. Except for the wrinkles on his face and rough skin, one would not have believed that he is truly a 10-year-old boy.
Although Lukman is not retarded, he seems to have some difficulties in learning. His problem is malnutrition or lack of adequate nutrients in his food intakes.
Despite his situation, Lukman can be counted among the lucky ones. The United Nations Children's Fund, UNICEF, report indicates that 187 out of every 1,000 Nigerian children die of malnutrition before age five. Hence, UNICEF's campaign for exclusive breast-feeding in the first six months of their lifetime.
Poor nutrition is not an exclusive problem of children. In fact, it is universal. One third of the world's population is not getting adequate micronutrients from their diets, which in turn is responsible for poor health, low intelligence, reduced working capacity and high mortality rate.
A study carried out and released by vitamin information centre in   South Africa  , indicates that most people depend on the traditional staple starchy foods for sustenance. They only produce energy and are lacking in all the essential micronutrients required by the body. The foods include cereal meals such as wheat, maize and rice, or root and tuber plants like cassava, yam and potato. Though some of the foods are sometimes combined with herbs or vegetables and fruits, still people don't get enough nutrients from them.
As the study indicates, the only option left for the  Third World  nations is to fortify the traditional diets in order to increase their nutritional contents and thus reduce cases of malnutrition. It is also believed that by so doing, there would be no risk of excess energy, through appropriate use of micronutrient-rich oils and fats.
Many governments in African countries have embarked on food fortification. Florence Wambubugu, first African biotechnologist, described the project as the "fastest and easiest way to lift  Africa  out of malnutrition and poverty."   Nigeria   is not left out. It formally launched its own food fortification project in December 2000, so as to control vitamin "A" deficiency. As such, government has been working with all donor agencies on how to improve nutritional value of staple foods like maize, yam, cassava and potatoes as well as improve their yields.
At the moment, the federal government is working in collaboration with the International Institute of Tropical Agriculture and United States Agency for International Development, to fortify Nigerian staple foods with vitamin A. Busie Maziya-Dixon, head of nutrition department of IITA, initiated the project. The joint effort is expected to produce for the country a policy on food and nutrition; food and nutritional education as well as create an avenue for food security. According to an international report, the plan was that by this year, the fortification of oil, sugar and flour with vitamin "A" would also have been fully implemented in the country.
Emeka Omaliko, chief executive of the National Biotechnology Agency, said plans were on the way to establish well-equipped and specialised biotechnology laboratories at some institutions in all the six geo-political zones of the country.
One of the government's areas of concerns is that majority of Nigerians do not have access to eat balanced diets, especially animal proteins. Hence, their immunity to sicknesses is very low. In fact, a study report indicates that only people with a good income or those who hunt for meat in the bush, or who go fishing or those who keep livestock could be found to consume significant amounts of animal protein required in daily diets.
Fatty acids are needed for the proper functioning of the body systems, but excessive protenous intakes from plant and animal  could also be dangerous. Experts say they can lead to obesity and increase the risk of coronary heart disease and cancer. High consumption of starchy traditional staple foods, especially those made with white sugar and flour, as well as drinking of sugary soft drinks are said to be in this category.
Dieticians have thus, warned that the arrival of fast food joints, which is becoming a new fad in the country, could also be injurious to health. Processed foods, they say, are not filling until too many calories are consumed, and to make matters worse, they have negligible nutritional value. That's not all. They are known to absorb quickly into the bloodstream, causing the body to release a gush of insulin that would accelerate the conversion of calories into fat. That is very dangerous, indeed.
"The risk of colon cancer is higher for people who have a deficiency in vitamin D intake or who maintain a sustained high fat diet. Therefore, maintaining the recommended vitamin D intake and lowering the amount of fat in the diet would be one way to help prevent colon cancer," David Magelsdoff, head of research team at Howard Hughes Medical Institute at the University of Texas, Dallas, United States, said in a study. According to experts, the fats that can be easily stored in the body are contained in vitamins A, D, E and K.
By contrast, fruits, vegetables and grains meals are densely filled with life-supporting micronutrients such as vitamins B and C as well as iron compounds for blood purification. Vitamin B complex contains antidotes against rashes and ringworm.
The vitamins are absorbed gradually, and do not cause upsurge of insulin in the body. According to experts, plant foods have high content of water and fibre, but low energy and micronutrient densities that are similarly necessary. To get the required nutrients from plant meals, people would need to eat more than is normally possible.
Dairy products like egg, milk, cheese and yoghurt are equally important in food intakes as they are responsible for strong bones and teeth.
Many Nigerians, however, depend on multi-vitamin diet to supplement daily requirement of micronutrients along with minerals and water. But they are divided over their effectiveness. For instance, Adebayo Williams, a doctor at General Hospital, Ikeja, recommended vitamins supplements because Nigerian staple foods are basically carbohydrates and people just eat to fill their stomachs rather than for balanced diets. "We need it. That is why in the   United States   they eat more of vitamins. But most of our foods here are carbohydrates," Williams argued.
But Abraham Udoudom, a medical practitioner at the   University  of  Calabar Teaching Hospital  , Calabar, differs. He told Newswatch that supplementing vitamins could help to replace some of the missing micronutrients, but they would not fully give the required nutrients in the body.
To get full complements of vitamins in foods, the federal government has put in place some measures aimed at ameliorating the situation. By January last year, it became mandatory to enrich wheat flour with vitamins B1, B2, and that salt either produced in Nigeria or imported must be iodized. But fortification of food items such as margarine with A, D, B1, and B2; malt drinks with C, B1, B2 and B6; cocoa-based beverages enriched with vitamins and minerals; custard powder (corn-starch) fortified with vitamins and minerals; bouillon cubes fortified with vitamins and minerals and milk enriched with vitamins and minerals, were still voluntary.
Udoudom said that apart from the fact that many Nigerians are not feeding well, majority of them lack knowledge about the right kind of foods to eat. "For Nigerians to eat well they need to be very rich and have knowledge of nutritional value of what they eat," Udoudom said.
Even if they knew what to eat to get all the necessary micronutrients, he believes they might not be able to afford the kind of foods that could give them enough nutrients because of poverty. He prescribed poverty alleviation programme, nutritional education, proper agricultural methods and favourable agricultural policy as some of the ways out of the problems.
Additional reports by Eno Reuben, Annette Edo and Rosemary Udoh.

Original_Search_Title: Entrepreneur Pledges Development, Relevance for Imo
Headline: Nigeria: Entrepreneur Pledges Development, Relevance for Imo
Body_Text:
Lagos — An entrepreneur aspiring for the governorship seat in Imo State, Mr. Ndubueze Emenike Nwana has promised the people of the state rapid economic development, political relevance and social resurgence for the masses to readily feel the impact of the government's efforts to improve their lot.
Addressing a press conference in Lagos on Tuesday, Nwana said " It is my desire to restore Imo to the pre-eminent position in Igbo politics by making it the hub of sound political acts, visionary economic and humane social programmes to guarantee not only the survival of the people but also prosperity in terms of living."
He said that, " our people in the East are traders. It is our determination to put in place an enabling environment to help them realise their passion. Intra state roads will receive utmost attention and we will relate meaningfully with whatever at the centre to ensure that federal responsibilities to Imo State roads are diligently discharged."
Nwana stressed that, " the three senatorial zones in the state will benefit from an even handed industrialisation policy, which will promote forward integration in core areas such as pharmaceuticals, electronics, toiletries, food and beverages and in every industrial zone with the provision of portable water, access roads, special micro credit and good supply of energy to propel Imo into an industrial giant."
"My government will endeavour to provide food through machanised farming. We will encourage foreign investors as well as encourage Nigerians to invest in the agricultural sector. Core areas of investment include palm produce, pineapple, cashew nut, citrus, cassava, yam and vegetables." He noted.
He said, " Provision of basic social services is very important to meaningful living on the part of the people. We will reinvigorate education through the expansion and rehabilitation of existing educational facilities to ensure quality education that places equal emphasis on moral and academic excellence. There is very important need to establish more adult education centres to redress adult illiteracy."
" We will place emphasis on primary and preventive health care delivery system. Our clinics, maternity homes, and other health institutions will receive adequate support.

Original_Search_Title: Emerging Technologies Can Work in Africa Only If the Climate is Right
Headline: Gambia: Emerging Technologies Can Work in Africa Only If the Climate is Right
Body_Text:
Banjul — If effectively harnessed, new and emerging technologies can help
catalyze Africa's transition to sustainable development by lowering the
incidence of disease, reducing food insecurity and decreasing vulnerability
to environmental damage by allowing more flexible crop management
systems.
However, the expected benefits of both medical (red) and agricultural
(green) biotechnology can only be realized if a number of key challenges are
addressed, including the extent to which the technologies are relevant to
Africa are pro-poor and mitigate biosafety and related risks. Biotechnology
should be viewed as one part of a comprehensive sustainable poverty
reduction strategy, and not as a technological quick fix for Africa's hunger
and poverty problems.
These are key messages of a new policy research report entitled
"Harnessing Technologies for Sustainable Development", to be released by the
Economic Commission for Africa (ECA) to coincide with the World Summit on
Sustainable Development (WSSD), which is currently going on in Johannesburg,
South Africa.
The report identifies red and green biotechnologies as important
ingredients often overlooked as a basis for sustainable development.
The recent advances in biotechnologies offer crops that have greater
yield, resist pests and disease, and offer other positive nutritional,
health, and environmental attribute. The report notes that Africa, which
depends heavily on agriculture - contributing 30 percent of gross domestic
product and 70 percent of employment - stands to benefit from any technology
that can increase the production of food, enhance its nutritional quality,
and minimize the exploitation of forests and marginal lands.
Illustrating the range and nature of current risks and opportunities
inherent in the biotechnologies, the report focuses on how to ensure that
poor farmers in Africa stand to gain. Similarly, the breakthroughs in
medical biotechnologies are revolutionizing the prevention, diagnosis,
management, treatment, and cure of disease: diagnostic tests are being
simplified; more accurate medicines are being produced through
pharmacogenics; gene therapy brings the possibility of directly correcting
genetic disorders before they appear; and vaccines are being produced that
tackle a wider range of disease such as HIV/AIDS and with greater
efficiency. The report notes that these new possibilities are especially
needed in Africa at a time that the toll of the HIV/AIDS tuberculosis and
malaria epidemic are reversing the health gains achieved over the past four
decades.
African countries must exploit a range of options to ensure that future
biotechnology initiatives reach their full potential for alleviating poverty
combating disease and securing food security.  Specifically and among other
options African countries should seek to:
- Promote African-focused biotechnology research in which emphasis is laid
on the disease and their strains prevalent particularly HIV/AIDS, TB and
malaria and "orphan crops" particularly cassava, millet, sorghum, sweet
potato and yams but also other cereals such as maize, rice and wheat;
develop African-owned biotechnology policies whereby all the relevant
stakeholders, including civil society, private sector and farmers'
organizations are involved in the formulation of national plans. They will
also have to establish national regulatory institutions for risk assessment
and management since most African countries have inadequate human resource
capacity to perform these functions;
- Increase investment in modern biotechnology research. The current
levels in most African countries are very low (hardly 2 percent of the total
agricultural research funds); and
- Promote public/private sector partnership in modern biotechnology
research;
- Beyond individual countries, the report states that achieving
sustainable development will require the production of regional and global
public goods services or resources whose benefits are shared among countries
in a region or more broadly. These regional and global public goods include
the knowledge, regimes, standards and rules required to address cross-border
problem such as infectious disease control and use of GM crops; the
institution that monitor and enforce the rules and regimes; and the benefits
that arise and are shared indiscriminately among countries.
To ensure the provision of these goods in sufficient quantity,
international collective action will be critical, because no individual
country has an incentive to pay for such things as the prevention of
contagious diseases, the preservation of biodiversity, or research to
develop new crops, vaccines, or drugs to treat tropical diseases. The
provision of these goods stresses the report, will require new and
innovative financing at the regional level.  Since development assistance
remains anchored in country-based projects and programmes, greater
flexibility will be needed to finance regional programmes for providing
regional public goods.  Around $16 billion is allocated annually to
international resource transfer for global public goods in health,
environment, and knowledge creation, states the Report. Roughly $11 billion
of this goes to support national infrastructure for public goods provision -
such as basic health care systems and environmental management - leaving
only a small share for regional and global public goods. Thus much more
needs to be done at the regional level.
Additionally, the Report features innovative new indicators that track
the progress of African countries towards sustainable development.
These indicators combine 27 key economic, environmental, and
institutional variables to track the performance of 38 African countries
during the 1975-2000 period, summarizing reasonably the evolution of the
current state of sustainable development. They also help identifying key
factors that determine success and failures in achieving sustainable
development, and priority areas for policy intervention. The indicators
reveal sobering challenges.
While some countries have made good progress, many have slipped down the
rankings. In fact, only three countries - Mauritius, South Africa, and
Botswana - accounting for about 5.6 percent of the continent's population,
recorded relatively high overall sustainability throughout the period. Even
more telling is the fact that among the 38 countries studied, the proportion
of the populations living in those with low sustainability rose from
one-third in 1975-84 to half in 1995-2000.

Original_Search_Title: Emerging Technologies Can Work for Africa Only if the Climate is Right
Headline: Emerging Technologies Can Work for Africa Only if the Climate is Right

Content from a Premium Partner

the Report focuses on how to ensure that poor farmers in Africa stand to
gain.
Similarly, the breakthroughs in medical biotechnologies are
revolutionizing the prevention, diagnosis, management, treatment, and cure
of diseases: diagnostic tests are being simplified; more accurate medicines
are being produced through pharmacogenics; gene therapy brings the
possibility of directly correcting genetic disorders before they appear; and
vaccines are being produced that tackle a wider range of diseases, such as
HIV/AIDS, and with greater efficiency.  The Report notes that these new
possibilities are especially needed in Africa at a time that the toll of the
HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria epidemics are reversing the health gains
achieved over the past four decades.
The Report articulates the potential benefits of specific technologies
and argues that the biggest risk for Africa would be to do nothing and let
the biotechnology revolution bypass the continent. In doing so, it stresses
that the new technologies are no panacea or silver bullet, and highlights a
number of key challenges faced by Africa, among them:
- The current focus of biotechnology research on crops grown and disease
strains that are prevalent in developed, rather than developing countries; -
The fact that in health, only 10% of research and development spending is
directed to the health problems of 90% of the world's people; - The reality
that most African countries are not well equipped to address the potential
risks of these technologies to human and animal health, and the environment;
Recognizing that poor people and poor countries lack resources,
infrastructure, and the business environment to attract new technologies and
related investments, the Report outlines key components for a
technology-infused development strategy within these limitations. It
includes lessons learned and best practice examples from across countries in
the continent, about strategic partnerships that are successful, products
and processes proven to work, and ways to encourage new ideas and
initiatives.
Biotechnology, the Report emphasizes, is only one of a suite of
technologies to be embedded in established breeding and selection
programmes. It argues that reaping the full benefit of the technological
revolution will require critical planning and strategic investments -- by
regional and international organizations and governments, private sectors,
and civil society.
African countries must exploit a range of options to ensure that future
biotechnology initiatives reach their full potential for alleviating
poverty, combating disease and securing food security. Specifically, and
among other options, African countries should seek to:
- Promote African-focused biotechnology research in which emphasis is
laid on: the diseases and their strains prevalent in Africa, particularly
HIV/AIDS, TB and Malaria; and "orphan crops", particularly cassava, millet
sorghum, sweet potato and yams but also other cereals such as maize, rice
and wheat; - Develop African-owned biotechnology policies whereby all the
relevant stakeholders, including civil society, private sector farmer
organizations, are involved in the formulation of national plans; -
Establish national regulatory institutions for risk assessment and
management since most African countries have inadequate human resource
capacity to perform these functions; - Increase investment in modern
biotechnology research. The current levels in most African countries are
very low (hardly 2% of the total agricultural research funds); and - Promote
public/private sector partnership in modern biotechnology research;
The experience of African countries that have deployed genetically
modified (GM) crops shows that success depends on the extent to which
countries have pursued these options. Countries cited as success stories
include South Africa (maize and cotton), Kenya (sweet potato), and Egypt
(maize, faba beans and cotton).
Beyond individual countries, the Report states that achieving
sustainable development will require the production of regional and global
public goods, services or resources whose benefits are shared among
countries in a region or more broadly. These regional and global public
goods include the knowledge, regimes, standards and rules required to
address cross-border problems such as infectious disease control and use of
GM crops; the institutions that monitor and enforce the rules and regimes;
and the benefits that arise and are shared indiscriminately among
countries.
To ensure the provision of these goods in sufficient quantity,
international collective action will be critical, because no individual
country has an incentive to pay for such things as the prevention of
contagious diseases, the preservation of biodiversity, or research to
develop new crops, vaccines, or drugs to treat tropical diseases.
The provision of these goods, stresses the Report, will require new and
innovative financing at the regional level. Since development assistance
remains anchored in country-based projects and programmes, greater
flexibility will be needed to finance regional programmes for providing
regional public goods.
Around $16 billion is allocated annually to international resource
transfers for global public goods in health, environment, and knowledge
creation, states the Report. Roughly $11 billion of this goes to support
national infrastructure for public goods provision -- such as basic health
care systems and environmental management -- leaving only a small share for
regional and global public goods.  Thus much more needs to be done at the
regional level.
Additionally, the Report features innovative new indicators that track
the progress of African countries towards sustainable development. These
indicators combine 27 key economic, environmental, and institutional
variables to track the performance of 38 African countries during the
1975-2000 period, summarizing reasonably the evolution of the current state
of sustainable development. They also help identifying key factors that
determine success and failures in achieving sustainable development, and
priority areas for policy intervention.
The indicators reveal sobering challenges. While some countries have
made good progress, many have slipped down the rankings. In fact, only three
countries -- Mauritius, South Africa, and Botswana -- accounting for about
6.5% of the continent's population, recorded relatively high overall
sustainability throughout the period. Even more telling is the fact that
among the 38 countries studied, the proportion of the populations living in
those with low sustainability rose from one-third in 1975-84 to half in
1995-2000.

Original_Search_Title: Confession of a Suckling Mother
Headline: Sierra Leone: Confession of a Suckling Mother
Body_Text:
The war had intensified in the Southern part of the country. The
rebels had overrun the mainland villages of Bumpe, the heartland of
the Sherbros. There were rumours that the marauders were planning to
infiltrate the riverrain villages, making the whole Sherbro land
unsafe.
One evening another rumour spread across Yengesa, a mainland
settlement, that the rebels had attacked two nearby villages in quick
succession and were advancing towards Yengesa.
Sixty-year-old Brima Rogers was a prosperous farmer in Yengesa. He
had a large family with three wives. The eldest, Sonya, was in her
early forties. The third and youngest, Bettie, was entering her
twenties and was suckling a nine month old baby.
That evening, Brima Rogers assembled his entire family and told them
about the imminent attack on Yengesa.
"We are moving out of this place straight away before it is too
late," he told his bewildered family.
"Where are we going this night," asked the eldest wife, worried about
the fate of the family. She knew that the rebels were always
targeting wealthy families.
"We are going to Mokebe, across the river," Brima Rogers said to his
wives, adding, "The chief there is my friend, we were in the army
together," he added.
"But what about the rice we are about to harvest," his eldest son,
Foday enquired. "Are we " "Forget about that farm," the father cut
in, in a more desperate mood. "Our lives are more precious than
that," he said and ordered every one to pack his/her belongings
immediately.
It was close to midnight. Brima Rogers and his family had barely left
Yengesa that night when they heard the first bomb expulsion, but they
were miles away at the time.
They had loaded all their belongings in a large canoe, which Brima
Rogers had built to run his business in the riverrain villages.
Two of the sons sat on each end of the canoe, paddling with all the
strength they possessed to escape the rebels.
It was dark and the two men had to battle with the water as they
tried to avoid the rough parts. Luckily for them the river was serene
and calm that night. The father held a touch light, which he
intermittently flashed across the river to show them the way.
The cock had just crowed when the fleeing family landed at Mokebe
village, a small fishing settlement on the Bumpe River.
The villagers, a friendly and hospitable community, warmly received
Brima Rogers and his family. They immediately led them to the village
chief, Biabom The Chief was overtly sympathetic towards the
strangers. He warmly embraced his old friend. That morning he
allocated a hut to them as temporary shelter.
"You and your family will occupy this one until we find you a
suitable place," Biabom said to the strangers, his eyes flashing
across the faces of women before him like the hungry cat prying at a
mouse. He swallowed a lump of saliva involuntarily, ravenously
licking his lower lips. He thought of his five wives but he knew in
his heart of heart that none was as pretty as the woman with the
suckling child.
Brima Rogers was full of thanks for the chief, and pleaded that he
and his family be allowed to stay in Mokebe until such a time when
they shall return to Yengesa. Biabom heartily welcomed the request
and further assured the displaced family of his protection.
"Whatever you need don't hesitate to ask," he said.
Time flew fast. It was now six months since Brima Rogers and his
family came to settle in Mokebe. They were gradually adjusting to the
village environment and the culture of their hosts. Brima Rogers had
asked chief Biabom for a piece of land to cultivate, since he had
lost the Yengesa farm to the invaders.
The chief had cut a portion of his own bush and gladly loaned it to
his old friend.
"The chief has given me a piece of land," he broke the good news to
his wives one evening. "We are going to start with cassava farming,"
he told them.
In a space of time, Rogers and his family (most of his children were
grown-ups) had cleared the bush and planted cassava stalks.
Every morning the whole family will go to the farm, leaving behind
the suckling Bettie and the little kids.
One day, chief Biabom was strolling purposefully along Brima Rogers'
hut when his eyes caught Bettie tending to her child.
"I see, you don't go to the farm," he started, making a conscious
effort to engage the woman in dialogue.
"I have to take care of the little ones," said Bettie, barely turning
to the chief with a wane smile.
"One of these days I will send one of my boys to you." "To do what?"
Bettie asked, a little surprise at the chief's advances.
"You will know later," Biabom said. He wanted to divulge what was
hidden in his heart for the pretty girl. But hesitating, he
disappeared into the grove, to another section of the village, making
sure nobody had spotted him.
For the rest of that day, Bettie remained confused. Why should the
chief send someone to her, she wondered.
Should she inform her husband about it? She asked herself. After a
second thought, she discountenanced the idea and decided to wait and
see what the chief has for her.
One morning, chief Biabom summoned one of his nephews to his private
room. The young man was his most trusted ally. He had always confided
in him his secrets, and the latter had always kept those secrets very
secret.
"Tua, I have called you on a very personal matter," the chief
whispered, making sure no one was eavesdropping.
"This is between you and I. I don't want to hear it among my wives,
please," he warned.
Tua, in his mid-twenties, stood speechless. He wondered what the
chief was going to tell him. He was amazed by the chief's humility.
He recalled a recent mission in the neighbouring village where he had
gone to lure a young girl on the chief's behalf. He had succeeded to
get the girl for him. He wondered what other mission his uncle had
for him.
"You know Brima Rogers, that displaced man?" the chief began in a low
voice. "You know his young wife, the suckling one?" Tua nodded. "I am
just from their place," Tua added.
Biabom turned round and brought out something wrapped in a piece of
cloth. It was a piece of yam.
"Take it to her. Tell her I sent it for her. Let her cook it for the
kids. You know these people are displaced. I have to assist them from
time to time," the chief explained to the young man.
Tua, as usual assured the chief, took the yam and left to run his
errand.
To be continued next edition.
In our next edition find out if Tua accomplished his mission. What
did he tell Bettie? What was her reaction?

Original_Search_Title: Eating Well Will Keep the Doctor Out of Way
Headline: Kenya: Eating Well Will Keep the Doctor Out of Way
Body_Text:
Nutrition plays a very vital role in the health of all human beings. And as was recently emphasised by the Hon Minister for Health, Prof Sam Ongeri, nutrition is an even greater investment for individuals who are infected with the Aids-causing virus, or those who are already have Aids.
Diet therapy has been known to reverse the immune dysfunction related to malnutrition in people with Aids. It is also known that proper diet helps fight opportunistic infections.
So, naturally, nutrition comes out as a powerful tool for successful management of HIV/Aids. Unfortunately, not enough emphasis is put on this.
If we were keener or better learners we should have discovered the benefits of good and healthy eating habits a little earlier. In the United States, for example, the first food outreach programme for people with Aids was initiated in 1988, only a few years after the discovery of Aids in that country.
This eating well affair may well be one of the key reasons why the mortality rate of HIV-infected persons in the United States has not risen significantly. Likewise, fewer and fewer Aids-related deaths are reported each year.
Now, back home. The call to "healthy eating for vitality and longer life" should also have come at the emergence of the Aids scourge in this country, or even a decade later... but it is better late than never, shall we say? Or are we only keen on aping America's trash?
Most people's natural body vitality - especially for urbanites - has gone down tremendously. This is mainly because of their poor dietary habits. They seem hell-bent on assimilating the life-threatening diets of the West, to the detriment of their own well-being and health.
In a sense, we have all more or less become consumers of the hyped-up, attractively packaged unhealthy foods found in our chain stores.
If you looked around you, starting with your own household, you'll find out that most kitchens are packed with quick-to-make "convenience" foods which are considered classy.
Very little thought is put on the fact that these foods could be a time-bomb to our health, only waiting to explode. If this trend continues then we can as well bid farewell to the good old days of relishing fresh home-made steaming-hot foods.
Are we putting our health and lives at stake because we are ignorant?
Those with whom we went to ABC classes must still remember the "Eat a wholesome and balanced diet" jargon in home science and traces of health education. And even in this dot.com era, things have not changed yet as similar topics are still taught in the 8-4-4 system.
In any case, we have also lived to sample the longevity of some of our foreparents, who in their 80s are still going strong. These wonderful octogenarians have known no other snack except fruit or roots.
To them, candy is as foreign as yam is to the "margarine" child.
Back to my train of thought. The aims of nutritional therapy in HIV/Aids patients include: preventing nutrient deficiency and food poisoning; surmounting obstacles to nutrient intake and absorption; and minimising loss of body mass.
Even though dietary supplements are so much a part of culture today, I would be hesitant in popping a vitamin-pill or a mineral capsule in the mouth to replace the intake of fresh fruit or vegetable. unless your physician highly recommends. In which case, the pros and cons must first outweigh the cons.
Before I wind up, I want to leave you with some suggested golden rules for healthy eating habits.
Eat wholesome: Refined foods have been raped of all their goodness.
Eat Natural: Processed foods have chemical additives which can be harmful to your health.
In any case, indigenous in-season foods have never been known to kill. These will do you no harm except a lot of good.
Drink, drink and drink yet some more: Plenty of water and fresh fruit, or vegetable juices.
Remember the rule of five: Carbohydrates, proteins, vitamins, minerals elements and water could rightly be called the energy-boosters, body-builders, protective and anti-biotic infection fighters, and cleansing combo.
Lest I forget.
A daily intake of garlic also keeps the doctor away. This pungent "camphor of the poor" is nature's antiseptic and antibiotic and has wonderful properties that help ward off opportunistic infections.
Some recap now?
Grandma and grandpa eat natural, indigenous and unrefined foods. They are much healthier and happier. Young Mensa eats off the mall's coveted imported food shelf, and she is doctor's monthly visitor.
But said and done, I still strongly believe that companionship and encouragement are the best antidotes to HIV/Aids sufferers. We can surely put back life into their days by loving and accepting them as they are.
This is the greatest story that will help us save lives and hopefully, change the world.

Original_Search_Title: JAK and the Palm Tree: Business of Government
Headline: Ghana: JAK and the Palm Tree: Business of Government
Body_Text:
President John Agyekum Kufuor and his team that toured Malaysia could
hardly wait to tell Ghanaians the miracle, which the Malaysians have wrought
with the palm tree they 'borrowed' or 'stole' from us.
While the President was his unemotional self, the others simply waxed
rhapsodic about what they saw and heard. Of course, we in Ghana have always
had a great deal of knowledge as to what uses we could put parts of the palm
tree.
Indeed, we have come to regard the palm tree as a tree none of whose
parts can be thrown away without being put to some good use. The examples
are many. We produce two kinds of oil from the palm nut, namely, red palm
oil and palm kernel oil. Red palm oil is said to contain Vitamin A, and is
thus good for the eyes.
It is used to prepare various kinds of food such as 'oto'(mashed yam or
plantain or cocoyam), kontomire sauce and to fry ripe plantain and
'tatale'. It is also used on boils to treat them. Palm kernel oil also has
both nutritional and curative uses.
It is used to fry food, to apply to dry skin and also used to treat
earache. Palm nut soup is regarded as such a delicacy that there is a saying
in Akan, thus: Kafo didi nso kafo enni abenkwan (Literally, a debtor also
eats but he does not eat palm nut soup). It is expensive to prepare it
properly.
We produce two different kinds of brooms from the palm tree. After the
boiled palm nut has been pounded in a mortar, the 'mefe' (sponge-like
substance) is used to 'smoke' water pots so that the water poured into them
will have a specially pleasant taste or flavour.
After the palm has been cracked to take out the kernel, the hard shell
is used as fuel. Palm wine is also another product of the palm tree. Palm
wine is cheaper than such bottled drinks as beer, whisky, brandy or
schnapps.
Consequently, it is within the pocket of the ordinary person who cares
for the drink. Akpeteshie is also distilled from the palm wine. Some
traditional buildings have branches of the palm tree as the framework before
the mud work is done.
Until the arrival of the modern canopy with steel and cloth, canopies
consisted of wooden frames and palm fronds. One product of palm wine tapping
is the 'akokono,' the grub that is seen as a delicacy by some people. This
is but an incomplete account of the uses to which we have learnt to put the
palm tree.
In contrast to what we do with the palm tree, which is primitive (or
primary) President Kufuor saw what might be des­cribed as secondary, or
possibly tertiary, uses of the palm tree in Malaysia.
While our 'technology' for dealing with the palm tree is demonstrably
primitive, what the team saw was the use of modern science and technology to
get the best out of an endowment of God to human beings. They saw many
products such as various forms of cosmetics, and they saw the conversion of
the humble palm oil into fuel for vehicle engines. They also saw chipboards
made from the palm tree.
Compared to the patchwork that passes for oil palm farms here, the team
saw hectares and hectares of oil palm plantations. It was as if, while we
were still content to ride economic bicycles, the Malaysians had taken off
in economic jets, at least as far as the palm tree industry was
concerned.
What has been the reaction of the President and his govern­ment on being
shown the Malaysian miracle that has become a multi-billion dollar industry?
Quickly, quickly, invite the Malaysians here, give them every assistance
and opportunity to acquire land, establish plantations and start an industry
that should benefit them and us. What, the Malaysians again?
Is it not these same Malaysians who, under shockingly bad agreements
entered into during the NDC regime, messed up our drydock and shipyards as
well as our telephone system?
As I remember, they were accused of controlling everything telephonic
though they had only 30 per cent representation. They were also accused of
failing to deliver on their promise to produce more lines. If they did a
good job with our telephone system, why have we entered into a new agreement
with Telenor? Owusu Agyapong, where are you?
Well, perhaps there is a difference between Malaysians who work in the
oil palm industry and Malaysians who are supposed to be experts in
telecommunications and in the repair of ships. Whatever it is, we are
entitled to expect the Kufuor regime to do a better job with the Malaysians
than the NDC did.
This country has suffered enough since independence from exploitation at
the hands of our so-called leaders. It is not a bad idea for government to
seek to attract foreign investments into the country.
However, past experience should teach us to be careful of so-called
investors who expect to reap where they have not sown, people who are no
more than smart crooks out to exploit our eagerness for economic
development.
Government should do a careful scrutiny of claims by fo­reigners who
come here promising an economic heaven when, in fact, they will end up
putting us in hell. Whatever agreements government enters into with such
people or companies should be mutually beneficial.
The kind of agreements that allowed the Malaysians to take over our
telecommunications system, our drydocks and our film industry should not be
repeated. Apart from strict honesty and integrity on the part of government,
we should also expect government to do more than provide the so-called
enabling environment for economic growth.
I know that official circles spout the line that it is not the business
of government to be in business and that the private sector should be seen
as "the engine of growth." I agree that those Ghanaians who have the
resources and the will to establish industries to provide goods and services
should be encouraged.
They should be helped in the acquisition of land, for example, loans on
favourable terms and, possibly, reasonable tax breaks. But government should
not adopt a totally hands-off policy ­that leaves everything in the hands of
the private sector.
Centuries ago, Western governments and their people acquired the
enormous wealth they enjoy today through colonial and imperial exploitation,
pillage, piracy, burning, killing and maiming of subject peoples. Spain,
Portugal, Belgium, France and Britain were the most guilty in this
respect.
Old-style colonialism is dead but new ways have been found by the West
to continue with the exploitation. The World Bank and the International
Monetary Fund (IMF) are two very useful and devastating tools in the hands
of the West.
The West preaches economic liberalism for the rest of us while it
imposes all kinds of barriers to stop manufactured goods from the rest of us
