fallen policemen's service guns were later found in her apartment.
Onyiye's story is that of a prodigal daughter who never found a
reason for living until the bubble burst. Her cousin, Blessing,
attested to the fact that Onyiye was like the proverbial obstinate
fly that follows the corpse to the grave: "Everybody in our family
has quarrelled with her to stop the bad life but she did not listen.
We begged her to come home and we always warned her... But she did
not listen. In fact, when her first boyfriend died, we all called her
and advised her to go and learn a job and stop all these rubbish. She
got us into this mess now," a very bitter Blessing said out.
Even when she was forced to learn tailoring, Onyinye's concentration
was elsewhere. She therefore spent little or no time at her shop
while she camped in hotels with Emeka, her robber husband whom she
claimed charmed her so she would not marry anybody else.
When she learnt that the police were cracking down on robbers in
Lagos (in fact, a few of her friends were already in the police net,)
she ran for dear life to her village, Isiala Mbanu, in Imo State,
abandoning all her guns in her sister's house. Men of the Lagos State
Anti Robbery Squad (SARS), however traced her to that rustic village
and there, she was arrested and brought back to Lagos. Her
confessions helped the police in cracking down some members of the
gang but her boyfriend and leader of the gang, Emeka is still at
large with a broken thigh bone. Onyinye told this magazine that she
was nursing the tigh before her arrest.
As painful as her arrest is to her however, Onyiye's headache and
worry now is finding a man to marry her. "No man will marry me
because I have soiled my image".
In this interview with Lara Owoeye - Wise & Richard Elesho, Onyiye
tells the story of her life.
The Story Of My Life
- Onyinye
Q: Who are you?
A: My name is Onyinye Abosi. I am from Isiala Mbanu, Imo State.
I am twenty one years old. I came here because I was arrested about a
bag Emeka gave me to keep. I kept the bag and travelled.
Q: What was in the bag?
A: Guns, but I didn't know so I travelled. I was not at home when
they came. I did not check the bag. I just kept it, because Emeka
told me he wanted to travel. So I just collected it and kept it. Then
when I got to the village in Mbanu, Imo State the police came to
arrest me, My mother then decided to come down to Lagos.
Q: Why did Emeka give you the bag. Are you his girlfriend?
A: The Emeka police arrested is not my boyfriend. He had a shop
beside us, so we were just like brothers. I have known him for about
eight years now, but that was the first time I would keep gun for
him. Allah. Give me something to swear. I am telling you the truth.
Please help me.
Q: If you want us to help you, then tell us the whole truth about
your life and your contact with the robbers?
A: Okay, I want to tell you the truth now. One day as I was walking
on the street, Emeka came to meet me. He said he wanted me to do
something for him. I asked him what. He said I should agree I will do
it before he make the request. I refused. But he pressed on and
before God and man Emeka come give me N5,000. I told him I would not
want his girl friend to accuse me of man snatching, but I collected
the money. I was learning fashion design at Orile. So one day Emeka
brought one bag and asked me to keep it for him. When I came back
from work, I saw the bag but I did not open it. He kept the bag
straight with another one underneath. The bag was there before I
travelled but I did not know what was in it.
Q: What did he ask you to use the N5,000 for?
A: He did not tell me. He just gave me and I collected it. He used to
give me money before like N200 or N500. He was not my boy friend. I
don't know his work, but he used to stay in his shop. I have known
him for a long time. Even before I started living in the Hotel and
even when I was in the primary school at Orile. I also attended
Gaskiya High School at Ijora before I dropped out in JSS III, to live
in the hotel.
Q: What were you doing in the Hotel?
A: I dey hustle
Q: How much were you making?
A: I was making like N2,5000, N3000. But if I do all night, I make
like N5000 per day. I don't drink beer or take drugs. But I am very
stubborn and I drink malt. Allah. I don't tell lies. Please help me.
Q: But you are telling lies now. Tell us everything about the armed
robbers that you know, if you want help
A: I will tell you the truth now. There are two Emekas that I know.
Emeka the shop owner who is now with the police and Emeka chemist
that gave me bag, the other Emeka my boyfriend. I knew him through
the chemist. So one day I saw Emeka chemist, he was treating one boy
called Ichor, he said he had an accident and sustained wounds in the
hand. I did not believe him. Which kind accident way go make person
hand wan fall?
He said he was his friend. Later Emeka my boyfriend started coming to
our house and we started going to the hotel together. We dey do till
day break
Small time all of us started meeting in Mushin. Sometimes they will
carry me with Okada to the Hotel Remembrance or to Mushin.
One day, Ichor, that boy that had wound came to give me a gun to
hold. I come ask am say this thing wey you give me now wetin, I go
take am do? But he said him brother wey be policeman no give am
money, so e come seize him gun. So I told Emeka about it. He said I
should collect and keep it that his brother is truly a policeman.
The next day, Emeka came to collect key from my hand to collect the
gun. That time they have not brought the bag o. So he later has his
own key and he would come and collect the gun at anytime from my
house. I saw one v-boot car with them, but they said it is the boy
that own it. That night, they carried the car away.
When they came back they talked about Oyingbo operation. But for
about four days I did not see my boyfriend.
One day he sent one of them to come and call me that I should come to
Mushin. When I got there, they were sharing money inside the house. I
asked Emeka where they got the money. E say na money wey in boy sell
for market.
So Emeka chemist then put me on the machine, took me to the hotel and
lodged me. Later my own Emeka came to meet me in the hotel and we
played and slept. He gave me N3,000 that day before I went to where I
was learning fashion designing.
Q: Is Emeka the only armed robber - boy friend that you have?
A: No
Q: Tell me about the others
A: The name of my first boyfriend is Ekene. But he is dead now
Q: What killed him?
A: They said they killed and burnt him. They said he went to rob and
they killed him. I will tell you the truth. One day in Mile 2, I was
walking with Ekene when a boy harassed him and called him a pick-
pocket. They fought seriously and I ran away. Ekene came to me after
to say the boy was telling lies.
Q: How many times have you gone for robbery?
A: I never rob before
Q: When you follow armed robbers to Mushin what do you people talk
about?
A: They talk about many things. One day Emeka told me that they have
killed one policeman called Owolabi, but I later saw Owolabi on the
road, they did not kill him. Allah. But they always talk about
policemen. When they come back from operation. I usually don't stay
with them, but they always carry me for lodging
Q: Why did you travel?
A: There is one hotel in my area called Joy Centre. One day people
told me that the police had arrested Ngozi, that they went to rob and
the owner of the hotel gave them off, they told me she was looking
for us up and down. So I went to look for my friends Obi that she
should go since they had arrested Ngozi. I don't know the people that
robbed, but Ngozi is not Ichor's girl friend, She is the girl friend
of stubborn Naughty
Q: Who is he?
A: Na armed robber. It was Chikito that told me how they hold Ngozi,
so I come travel
Q: What of Emeka your boyfriend, what is his father's name and where
is he now?
A: I don't know his father's name and I don't know where he is now.
Before he sustained injury in his leg from accident, he wasn't going
out. There is x-ray. It is not gunshot. He told me it was accident
that broke the bones into two.
Q: Did you not know your boy friend was an armed robber?
A: I did not know
Q; And you were taking care of his leg in the hotel for two weeks?
A: No it was his house, that my self and his younger brother were
taking care of him. But his brother knows he was an armed robber.
Please help me. I want to say something.
One day, Emeka carried me to one Juju man to take oath. Even when one
man wanted to marry me, he did not allow me to leave. The juju man
put mark on my breasts e com say make me and Emeka fuck, and we fuck
finish, e come put hand for my private part comot something come say
if I leave Emeka go marry another man, I go die. The man then gave me
water to drink. That is why since then I no fit leave Emeka.
Q: How can you now convince us you have never robbed before, with all
these things?
A: I never rob, I just dey stubborn, my father is dead. My mother
roasts yam for sale, they asked me to go to school but I refused and
went to live in the hotels. I cannot explain why I behaved like that.
No be say Emeka dey fuck me well I no fit say na in prick make me
follow Emeka. I have lived im hotels before, so it is not because of
his penis at all. I don't know why I followed him. No be say na fine
person, e tall, e dey like man sha. I beg.
Q: Do you go to church?
A: Yes. I am an Anglican, I don do confirm self - my church is in
Amukoko. As it is now, no man will like to marry me and I want to
have a good husband. May God forgive me and give me a bright person.
The juju in my body has failed since I left him and travelled. Other
girls must learn from my case. They must ask their boyfriends the
source of their income, and background. I have made a mistake please
help me beg my sister and family. I want to go and learn my work
well, but I am afraid my past might not allow me have a good husband.
Friends brought me to this. Please help me beg my family. I wan marry
a good man.
She Lives A Bad Life
- Blessing Abosi
What is your name?
A: My name is Blessing
Q: Why are you here?
A; I am here because one early morning policemen came and asked of my
sister with one boy. That time my sister was not around and I told
them. Then they were searching my house. As they were searching, they
saw gun under the old big box in the house. They searched the shop
and found a gun too. So the police arrested me and my little sister.
I was beaten and tortured. They asked me how I got the gun and the
whereabouts of my sister. I told them I didn't know anything about
the gun.
As for my sister, I told them she had gone to the village.
They asked me to take them to the village. When we travelled to the
village, by the Grace of God, we saw my sister, they arrested her.
And on our way to Lagos, they asked her certain questions to which
she answered.
I did not know there was a gun in my room. She kept it there. I am a
market seller and every morning I go to the market to sell my wares
with that my small sister, and around ten we return home. I am not a
thief.
Q: How did you know Onyinye
A: She is my cousin
Q: What does she do?
A: She does anything
Q: Anything like what?
A: Yes, because she no dey do anything. All I know is that she had
lived in a hotel before, everybody in our family was quarrelling with
her to stop the bad life, but she did not listen. We begged her to
come home, but she refused.
We always warned her. Even my younger sister always quarrel with her
to stop, now she has brought us to this mess.
For two weeks before she travelled, she was not staying at home. I
went to the market and when I came back, they told me she had dropped
the key and travelled.
She knew Emeka was an armed robber. All of us knew he is an armed
robber. She is telling lies. Emeka is one boy that all of us have
being telling her to leave because of his rough life. I knew she was
following an armed robber. We have all talked to her but she did not
listen. Infact, when her first boy friend died, we all called her and
advised her to go and learn a job and stop all this rubbish she was
doing. Initially we thought it was because of adolescent problem, but
now we know it is not, because she is old enough to differentiate
good from bad.

Original_Search_Title: The North As the Issue
Headline: Nigeria: The North As the Issue
Body_Text:
"The truth is that the north decides who becomes Nigeria leader whether military or civilian."
The political foundation of this country was laid on a tripod of the geographical expression grouped into north, west and east. Expectedly the political growth of the country also went along the geo-political equation. Although it must be noted that the growth along this line was facilitated by the existence of the three main ethnic groups in the country, the Hausa Fulani in the north, the Yoruba in the west and the Igbo in the East.
It is perhaps not by accident that the early political fathers of the nation emerged from within the three main ethnic groups. The late Owelle of Onitsha Dr. Nnamdi Azikiwe, an Igbo who became the first president of Nigeria at the nation's independence in 1960, the Sardauna of Sokoto, Sir Ahmadu Bello, Premier of the defunct northern region whose nominee, Alhaji Abubakar Tafawa Belewa became the first Prime Minister of the country at independence and of course, the legend of Yoruba politics, Chief Obafemi Awolowo who was the Premier of the west.
Since Nigeria independence 42 years ago, the socio-political economic developments of the three geo-political areas have continued to be influenced by the ideals of these great leaders.
Even the delineation of the country into states which started in late 60s to douse the rising regional and ethnic sentiments, could not wipe out the influence of these men. Not even the prolonged military presence in our polity could ignore the demagoguery of these political personalities.
But of the three geo-political zones, the north overtime demonstrated and still demonstrates better knowledge of political environment called Nigeria. As a result, the struggle for power whether civil or military has always ended in their favour not necessarily because of their size and population which is reasonably larger than the other two but more on their ability to manage and co-ordinate themselves to survive in a plural environment.
Late premier of the northern region Sir Bello with his endowed vision successfully laid the foundation that saw the north emerge the way it is today. Over time the geo-political north continues to establish that they hold both the yam and the knife in our polity. When late Major General Aguiyi Ironsi as the military head of state in the 60s came up with his naive political idea, the geo-political north were angry moreso when it was largely believed in the north that the coup that brought him (Ironsi) was sponsored by the Igbo. It was easy for them to push him away after six months to install their own son Yakubu Gowon who reigned for nine good years.
The next time a non-northerner came close to political power was by accident just as was the case with General Ironsi. This time General Obasanjo as the second in command to late general Murtala Mohammed who died by an aborted February 13, 1976 coup was "allowed" to climb the ladder to be the Head of State, on the condition that he hand over power soonest. He survived by doing just that handing over to a northerner, Alhaji Shehu Shagari in 1979 amidst celebrated protest by his kinsmen.
Since then the north held on to power rotating it among themselves from Shagari to Buhari through Babangida to Abacha and Abubakar before returning it to the same Obasanjo. It was obvious that in 1999 Obasanjo came to power simply because the geo-political north wanted him. His geo-political area - the South-West did not want him and they stressed their dislike through their mass vote for their choice Chief Olu Falae even when they knew that Obasanjo was going to win.
It is an obvious thing that although the East voted massively for Obasanjo in 1999, he did not win because they wanted him. Even if his South-West people had wanted him, he would not have been president without the north. The truth is that the north decides who becomes Nigeria leader whether military or civilian. If the political climate in the country seems hot now, it is because Obasanjo and his friends are rightly or wrongly beginning to think that they can do with the north.
This might seem dangerous in reality because no serious effort was made by him to create a matching South, no effort was made to court the friendship of the East and the West, the other tripods that could have matched the north. If the north seems powerful overtime, blame it on the Southerners selfish attitude and northerners strong belief in the ideals of late Ahmadu Bello of building a geo-political area that believes in unity in diversity.

Original_Search_Title: Have Compassion for the Poor, Says Owusu-Tabiri
Headline: Ghana: Have Compassion for the Poor, Says Owusu-Tabiri
Body_Text:
Sunyani — THE SPIRITUAL leader and founder of the Bethel Prayer Ministry International, Bishop Paul Owusu Tabiri, has asked churches and religious groups in the country to have compassion on the hungry and less privileged in the society to keep the body, the spirit and the soul together.
He said the church must complement the efforts of government to bring quality life to the people.
Bishop Owusu Tabiri was speaking at the first anniversary celebration of the Bethel Vocational Training Institute as part of the Church's Easter convention in Sunyani,Brong Ahafo Region.
The institute was established by the Bethel Prayer Ministry International to facilitate, encourage and harness the development of the creative talents of the less fortunate youth in society.
The training is entirely free for all the students.
The students are given uniforms, sewing machines and accessories.
Graduates from the institute are also given sewing machines and other equipment, as well as capital, to set them up to meet the challenges of their trade.
Bishop Tabiri said the church, after setting the people free from spiritual bondage should be able to carve a better material life for them as well.
Outlining some of the projects and programmes being pursued by the Bethel Prayer Minister International, Bishop Tabiri disclosed that the church will soon establish a Co-operative Credit Union and a Rural Bank to help the people to set up small-scale businesses.
He said the church has acquired a vast tract of land to establish a university in the Brong Ahafo Region.
Bishop Tabiri said apart from education, the church has ventured into agriculture.
He said the church is cultivating a ten acre maize farm in Sunyani, yam production in Yeji and Daman Nkwanta in the Brong Ahafo Region.
The bishop said the yield would be used to feed those who the churches prayer grounds free of charge.
He added that the church has also embarked upon commercial poultry and livestock farms.
He presented 50 pairs of Wellington boots, donated by the Canada branch of the church, to those who work at the church's farm at Yeji, Sawla and Buipe.
The church also made 200 sewing machines available for the students who are due to complete their training.
Four bails of second hand clothes from Israel were also presented to the church to be distributed to the needy.
The Exective Director of Caring Kids International, an NGO, Nana Akosua Frempoma, who is also the Dwanetoahemaa of Dormaa Traditional Area, donated four computers to the institute and urged the trainees to make good use of the opportunity the church has offered them.

Original_Search_Title: Saga of Yendi Massacre
Headline: Ghana: Saga of Yendi Massacre
Body_Text:
THE MINISTRY OF Information has announced the arrest of two persons in connection with the March 2 killings at Yendi, assuring that nobody will be shielded in the search for the perpetrators of the criminal acts.
Ferdinand O. Ayim, Special Assistant to the Minister for Information who made the disclosure at a press conference and in an interview yesterday, however, declined to name the suspects being held for now.
"Until the situation is clear and proper charges have been made, I don't think we are going to disclose their identity", Ayim said.
Asked when they were arrested, he said Wednesday but regarding their nationality and the political persuasion they belong to, he said. "I don't even know who they are".
He had received the information from Tamale where a police investigative team led by the officer in-charge of CID, Chief Supt. Appiatu is launching its pursuit of the perpetrators, he explained.
Ayim, for what he termed security reasons, also declined to tell whether the suspects were being held at Tamale for investigations or that they would be sent to Accra to be tried.
In view of the relatively short time taken the police to arrest some suspects, the Special Assistant pleaded with Ghanaians to give the government a 'modicum' of integrity and trust to thoroughly investigate the Yendi crisis.
Asked whether the government would interdict the National Security Co-ordinator, Ministers, District Chief Executives and party officials if implicated, he answered in the affirmative.
"The President has lost no opportunity in telling his people of the need to conduct themselves very well and that he could sacrifice anybody for acts of malfeasance and crimes instead of allowing the entire government to be dragged into the mud," he noted.
Ayim, however, disagreed with the suggestion that officials suspected to be involved in the gruesome act if allowed to remain in office, could cover up their tracks.
He insisted they cannot use their positions to influence anything", because the offences involved - arson, murder and violence are being treated as criminal cases and the police are in charge.
According to the special assistant, another advantage here is that the case can be handled expeditiously and it would not take the several months or years that a commission of enquiry or judicial committee is likely to take to investigate it.
Again he cautioned that whatever action is taken against anybody for their alleged involvement should be grounded in fact and in law.
In that regard, he considered it wrong to force people to resign based on speculations, conjectures, and innuendoes.
He drew attention to the fact that one person mentioned on a radio station recently to have travelled to Burkina Faso, in connection with the Yendi raid has been proved to have stayed in the Greater Accra Region since March 2 without stepping out.
The 'goodnews' that the government official had for Ghanaians is that Yendi had returned to calm and normalcy.
Confronted with media reports that people were leaving the town in droves, he hotly contested the reports, accusing a section of the press of failing to visit Yendi on their own or obtaining information from the police and military.
Rather they rely on information given by the warring factions alone each of whom start the stories to suit their cause.
He recalled that the 'hustle and bustle" at Yendi surprised newsmen taken to the place last Friday and argued that if anything, the situation has improved further.
He pleaded that journalists treat the Yendi issue with circumspection, especially as the district is about to enter the planting season, else fear and anxiety would prevent thousands from planting crops on which they would depend for their livelihoods.
As Ministry of Food and Agriculture statistics confirm, Yendi is the biggest among the 13 districts in the Northern Region in terms of rice production, second in groundnuts, third in cowpea, fifth in maize and sixth in yam production.
In each of these Yendi produces thousands of metric tonnes each year.
He, therefore, stressed the need for the press to cross-check with the military, police and other officials on the ground to be able to present factual stories that would not portray non-existent problems and create panic.

Original_Search_Title: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Headline: Nigeria: Mother of Slain Lady Demands Justice From Police
Body_Text:
Lagos — Questions why the police are holding on to the corpse six years after she was allegedly killed by a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, wants killer cop brought to justice
She may never be able to put behind her for the rest of her life the gruesome death of her 25-year old daughter six years ago from the bullets of a Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi (Force No 110279) at their village of Uokha, Owan East Local Government Area of Edo State.
Frail-looking Madam Olohigbe Jegede, 68, who has remained heavily weighed down by that unexpected sad loss of her fashion designer daughter, Miss Blessing Amore, in September 1996, said as she recalled the incident amidst tears to City Diary last weekend: "Police Sergeant Christopher Nnamdi, without cause, shot and killed my daughter in company of three other policemen detailed to our village, Uokha, to investigate and arrest a lad who allegedly beat another to death while they were fighting.
"We were not living in the house where the incident happened. He shot my daughter, killed her, threw her corpse into their vehicle and drove away without even allowing me to see it. Till date, I have not set my eyes on her corpse. We were told that the corpse was deposited at the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, near Ekpoma, we visited Auchi, Afuze and Benin City several times, demanding for her corpse for proper burial, its six years now since she was killed, the police have refused to release her corpse to us. they are not giving any explanations for it either. But we have continued to ask that the police release the corpse to us so that we can give her a proper burial. The corpse is not seen. The policeman involved has long been transferred away from the Afuze Police Division he was when he committed the offence, so is the Divisional Police Officer (DPO) then, Mr. Kenene Onyire, a Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP)".
"If only I had known that Blessing would not return home," the aging woman, who said she had separated from the late fashion designer's father, continued: "I would not have allowed her to go and fix her hair that day. We had just finished frying garri and she told me she was going to fix her hair. I told her to return quickly as I was going to put yam on fire for pounded yam.
"The next thing I heard were cries, and shouts of they have killed the girl, they have killed the girl. Of course, I never thought that the person in question was my daughter and I have no reason to think other wise, convinced that she was were she was fixing her hair until somebody ran to me to say that the person he saw on the ground looked like her. I threw away what I was doing, ran to the place but the policemen threw the corpse into their vehicle before I got there. I tried to catch a glimpse of the body but they would not allow me. They drove everybody away from the scene and sped off with the corpse and a bleeding young man they shot along with her.
"Then, we began running around their offices from Afuze to the Area Command Headquarters, Auchi, as well as the State Command Headquarters, Benin City. All through, they did not allow me to see her corpse, I am not even sure they carried out any autopsy on it.
"They also did not take any concrete action aganist the policeman who shot her. Each time we asked them what they were doing about the case, they would tell us that they were handling it their own way. The next thing was that they transferred the man and till date, we never heard anything about the matter again neither do we know where the culprit is or what has happened to him".
As the account had it, sergeant Nnamdi, who was attached to the Afuze Divisional Headquarters of the Edo State Police Command then, was detailed along with three other policemen to effect the arrest of a young boy who had beaten another child to death.
The two kids were said to have fought on September 13, 1996 and one of them collapsed in the course of the fight and eventually died on September 23.
The policemen arrived the town at about 4.00pm on that fateful day, moved to the house of the other kid, who allegedly fought with the dead one to effect his arrest but met the house deserted. They then embarked on indiscriminate arrest of people in the area, relying on what they termed magistrate courts rule 2, which empowers the police to arrest as many suspects as possible to a crime committed to make it possible for the real suspect to be arrested. The entire area was said to have been thown into confusion, with people fleeing in all direction to escape being arrested.
Amidst that confusion, the late Blessing Amore was said to be returning from where she went to fix her hair, unaware of what was going on.
Sergent Nnamdi reportedly halted her, pointed his service pistol at her while ordering her to go into their vehicle. The late Miss Amore was said to have quietly obeyed but the unexpected happened. As she was trying to climb into the police van, Nnamdi's gun went off and hit the lady at her side. The bullet was said to have tore through her, uprooted her breast before hitting and wounding a young man, Friday Ohikhena, who was already in the vehicle. Miss Amore fell and died instantly, soaked in her pool of blood to set the community in uproar. The policemen reportedly fired several shots into the air to scare away the gathering crowd, picked up the body, threw it into the van to join the injured young man and sped away with it.
On the advice of the then DPO of the Afuze Police Divisional Headquarters, Mr. Kanene Onyire, the policemen were said to have taken the the corpse and the injured Ohikhena to the Irrua Teaching Hospital, Irrua, for deposit in the mortuary and treatment respectively.

Original_Search_Title: Are Accra And Kumasi Ungovernable?
Headline: Ghana: Are Accra And Kumasi Ungovernable?
Body_Text:
Accra, the capital of Ghana, and Kumasi, the Garden City of Africa, are now on top of the trash heap. Both cities are no more what they used to be. They are engulfed in dust. They are expanding rapidly like brushfires on the sahel, but without any sense. I hate to describe the cities I love this way, but the truth must be told if we want to be able to correct our mistakes.
Because of the attitude and behaviour of the residents, governing the two cities has become one of the toughest jobs. We need to change a few things to bring them back to their past glories.
No doubt Accra and Kumasi are the centres of wealth in the country. It's easier to make it in these places than any other place in the nation. Whereas wealth resulting from political thievery and dubious practices as well as genuine hard work fuel development in Accra, Kumasi's wealth has resulted out of farming and trading, and a little bit of graft, no doubt.
Hard work has always been the hallmark of this traditional city. For decades, Kumasi residents were very proud of their city and always frowned on Accra. However, in the recent past,  residents have moved en mass to Accra to escape the problems.  With them migrated the vices they knew and practised.  Hence, Accra in the last decade has received more than it can handle in terms of lawlessness. That is not to say Kumasi is without lawlessness due to the exodus. More new residents move in from the countryside than migrate from Kumasi to Accra.
Kumasi is the second largest city in the nation. We have to build Accra to international standard, but we cannot gloss over Kumasi and, for that matter, any other city, town, or village in the process.
That is why it is important for us to get the cities back to their past glories. To do that we have to examine why they went from grace to grass. It's simple. We flout laws and by-laws out of the belief that we can do whatever we like. This is our Kumasi; this is our Accra. We can do whatever we like with them rather than make them the best places to live.
Our arrogance is reflected in:
1.  How we build. We build anywhere once we have the funds to do so. Whatever anybody says about the future of the neighbourhood does not matter. So we build across the street, into the street, on swamps, in river valleys, in zoned and unzoned areas. In times of catastrophe, these areas are not accessible to relief and rescue vehicles, but we don't seem to care or listen to anybody. We seem to have the belief that once you build your huge mansion and erect a seven-foot-high concrete wall around it, you are immune from the problems outside. How wrong can we be!  We still have to use the inacessible road to our homes, depend on poor utility hook up, and lousy waste management and disposal.
2.  How we park:  Once you have money to purchase a car and you do, you can park it anywhere. On sidewalks meant for pedestrians and even on grass. Who has the guts to tow a posh car for illegal parking?  All hell will break loose if it happens.
3.  How we drive: And when we drive our posh cars, we can afford to weave in and out of traffic, ignore traffic lights even when they are red, refuse to use our turn signals (trafficators) when making a turn.  Result? Lots of avoidable accidents involving collision with other vehicles and pedestrians. We can drive on road shoulders, honk incessantly at pedestrians and other traffic, stop to say hello to another person and back up traffic behind us, and yell obscenities at other drivers. Also we poke fun at drivers who try to obey traffic laws and make the road safe for us all. And if you dare yield to pedestrians, you are the most uncouth driver in the world.
4.  How we claim retail space: This has, by far, surpassed all other problems. The 'it's the economy' and 'there are no jobs', as well as 'we have to eat' excuses have made selling the norm in the cities and it is very difficult to reason with hawkers and sedentary traders alike.
We could put up with a little hawking so long as the items were razor blades, Robb, handkerchiefs, and small souvenir items that we tend to forget when we go to the stores, but how do we explain it when frozen chicken, live chicken, salted pig feet, beef, plantains, yams, fresh tomatoes, coconuts, watermelons, and conceivably every farm produce and manufactured product are sold on the streets? Every square metre of walking space at the centre of the cities has been claimed by the defiant hawkers to the point of people avoiding the area.
The city centres are so crowded with hawkers and traders it makes shopping difficult, ironically! Filth from food wrappers, discarded clothing, plastic water sachets, coconut shells, rotting vegetables, and faecal matter soaked in urine litter the centre of the cities like they were created as refuse heaps from the start. It wouldn't be long before waduro (mortar) carvers move in too to do their own thing under the prying eyes of the tourists we so badly need because of their supposed fat purses.
There are several countries all over the world that face acute employment problems but will not sacrifice their inner cities the way we have. At the end of the day, it costs more to clean the places of the filth than the cities realize from tolls. That's why Kajetia is left uncleaned and it stinks. And Tudu too! And vehicles cannot move.
5. Our attitude towards the law in general: It is disgusting to watch men and women ease themselves into waterways without shame.  Their reason is that there are no places of convenience. I thought people of these cities could be ingenious enough to find a solution to the problem of places of convenience rather than show 'themselves' to the whole world. In this age of private enterprise, we should not let the government in on this, even if the it has the means to do so.
6. Noise. From churches in particular and car radios and horns.  Sometimes I wonder why people want to live in this chaos.  We'll riot at the least perceived provocation from city officials trying to do their jobs of making the cities decent. Fearing that lives will be lost and the city government would incur the disfavour of the people, the officials always cave in and things remain where they were.  So the rot continues and we blame the AMA and KMA.
Solution:  First there should be a change of attitude among residents. No amount of money pumped into the cities will work if the people themselves are bent on destroying them with their activities. They should be reminded that "wototo wo tekrema we a na wonwee nam biara." (If you roast and eat your tongue, you will not have eaten any desirable meat.)
When we have amended our behaviour towards the cities, we may then put our money where our mouths are. If we claim we love the cities, we can show it by preparing to pay extra taxes to build them. Our cities are dying because working residents don't pay any income tax to them. Every resident should be made to pay at least 1% of gross monthly salary to their city of residence every month. It won't be money they'll miss, but the cities can pivot solutions to their numerous problems on it (income tax).
We'll need all that money to resurface roads and streets, rid them of garbage, create and desilt gutters, build and rebuild schools, provide markets, set up proper safety services, plant trees, install and replace street lights, buy patrol vehicles to help stem crime and criminal activities, provide places of interest to bring in more visitors and tourists to enhance revenue, etc . That's my second suggestion.
Thirdly, the cities should add .5% (half of one percent) to VAT as their development and maintenance share. Cities have to make their own money, and from those who use their services. What we see is that when there is an event in the cities, like 'champion to champion', international and other soccer meetings, swarms of people flock to the cities, and all they leave the cities are heaps of garbage but no money to clean it up.  Every event should be seen as bringing income into the cities where they are held and not just a way of making the city known, or as some writers put it, placing the city on the world map.  What use is a city on any map when all it's got to show is filth and dejection? We need tourists to recognize our country and come to see us.
If we don't turn our misfortunes around and we continue to cultivate the nonchalant attitude towards our own country, cities, towns, villages, homes and selves, the only thing that the tourists will come to see are the refuse heaps that breed the largest rats, mice, houseflies, and mosquitoes in the world.
I know residents will fight new and increased taxes, but if we fail to do these and place all our hopes on central government, we should kiss our hopes of living in decent cities devoid of filth and deprivation goodbye, because no government can build every one of the cities that fall under its administration. No city government or its agency can; Accra and Kumasi being no exception.
We need to change, really.

Original_Search_Title: Give Peace a Chance
Headline: Ghana: Give Peace a Chance
Body_Text:
The full import of what happened in Dagbon last week is now sinking in.
The implications are far-reaching.
For us at The Accra Daily Mail, our immediate concern is for peace to hold so that the people of Dagbon can go back ASAP to doing what they do best: farming.
The issue of food security is something we cannot take for granted. The Dagbon areas of Northern Ghana are well known for yam, rice, maize, millet, guinea corn and livestock farming.
The rains are about to start and therefore the land has to be prepared for planting and sowing.
Without peace that is assured, both sides in the conflict would not feel secure enough to venture into their fields to till the soil.
Should there be any short falls in food production in Dagbon, we are in for real trouble in the coming months.
We are reliably informed that the government has been approaching the crisis with all the seriousness it deserves and may even be seeking mediation from outside. That is very heartening indeed and we must all give the government positive ideas on how to bring this sad episode of our history to an amicable end.
We wish to add our voice to calls for the media to be more circumspect in reporting the crisis. We cannot absolve ourselves because we are part of the media, but we think the media has not been responsible at all in presenting the crisis. Even before the people of Ghana could take in what was happening, certain media houses were taking sides, giving slanted opinions, engaging in media trials and propagating blatant falsehoods.
That things did not get totally out of hands, was perhaps once more due to the Ghanaian's own peace loving disposition than how the Ghanaian media was managing the news coming out of Dagbon. But even the most pacifist  person would sooner or later crack under the barrage of provocative media management.
We agree therefore with the National Savannah Caucus (see front page story) that if the media cannot empathise with the people of Dagbon, then they must "leave Northerners alone to solve this problem as people of one stock."
Our main appeal still is, "Let's give peace a chance".

Original_Search_Title: President Airlifts Warlords to Accra
Headline: Ghana: President Airlifts Warlords to Accra
Body_Text:
... for peace talks
PRESIDENT JOHN Agyekum Kufuor is sending a special aircraft to the Northern Region today to pick leaders of the warring factions at Yendi to meet him in Accra.
Also to be flown back to Accra on the same plane is the Nayiri or King of the Mamprusis who, traditionally, is recognised as a big brother to the Yaa-Naa of Yendi.
Mr. Ferdinand O. Ayim of the Ministry of Information who disclosed this in an interview yesternight explained that the Nayiri will be expected to lend his "weight and authority to the talks to be held between the President and the two factions."
The aim of the talks is to calm tempers and get both sides to the fight to agree to a peaceful resolution as a short term measure, Ayim added.
As a measure to broker a long-lasting solution to the Dagbon chiefgaincy disputes intends constituting a team of three very prominent kings including the Asantehene, the Nayiri and the Yagbonwura who is king of the Gongas to deliberate on the age-long Yendi disputes, the InformationMinistry official further disclosed.
He was happy to note that the situation at Yendi, Tamale and other Dagbon settlements have calmed since the dastard deed was done last Wednesday.
"We are told there is no further breach. And as I talk now the Council of State has a delegation in the area holding talks for peace."
As would be recalled the chashes between the Andani Gate and Abudulai Gate of Yendi hit a crescendo last Wednesday when 25 people including the Yendi chief were massacred aside of billions of cedis worth of property that were destroyed.
The Ministers for the Interior and Northern Region resigned as the government plunged into investigation to expose architects of the assassinations.
The moves to restore peace and nomalcy to Yendi as soon as possible are also considered to be of prime necessity as planting season for the district which is one of the chief yam, rice and maize producers is just around the corner.

Original_Search_Title: Dusk-to-Dawn Curfew in North
Headline: Ghana: Dusk-to-Dawn Curfew in North
Body_Text:
President John Kufuor has declared a state of emergency in the Dagbon area, of the country's Northern Region, following an apparently erroneous official announcement that the traditional chief had been killed in a dispute for power, the Ghana News Agency reported.
However, the counsel for the Adani faction in the Dagbon chieftaincy crisis, Ibrahim Mahama, told the agency that his chief, Ya Naa Yakubu Andani II, was still alive. He said hired assassins had mistakenly beheaded the wrong man.
Police and troops are enforcing a dusk-to-dawn curfew over the entire Dagbon traditional area, and have been ordered to break up the assembly of any groups deemed likely to threaten public order, GNA reported. Furthermore, the Northern Regional Security Committee banned Monday's annual Bugum (Fire) Festival at Yendi.
It is still unclear what actually caused the flare-up, the Accra Mail said in an editorial on Tuesday. However, tension has been mounting between the Andani and Abudu chieftaincy wings over the celebration of the Muslim Eid ul-Adha festival. The Anandi wing, which controls the symbolic instruments of the ruler, was said to be unhappy that the rival Abudu Gate celebrated the festival in the traditional way at the home of its regent, GNA reported. The tension finally erupted when factions of one wing attacked a man from the rival wing and destroyed his bicycle, the agency added. An undisclosed number of people were killed in the five-minute shoot out, GNA reported, quoting the district chief executive of Yendi, Mohammed Habibu Tijani.
Dagbon, the Accra Mail reported, "is one of the most important links" in the Mole-Dagbani Empire, which extends from Northern Ghana all the way to Burkina Faso. "It occupies very large tracts of the Northern Region and holds great potential for food production. Indeed, traditionally, the Dagombas have been associated with rice, maize, millet and yam farming," it added.
Despite this, it said, Dagbon towns and villages "are pathetic and impoverished". It added, "There are no good schools, no good health facilities, no good roads, no pipe borne water."

Original_Search_Title: Kwara Awards N237m Kaiama Road Contract
Headline: Nigeria: Kwara Awards N237m Kaiama Road Contract
Body_Text:
Ilorin — The Kwara State government has approved the award of contract for the construction of a 124-kilomentre road to open up the Kaiama and Baruten Local Government areas and link them properly with other parts of the state. The approval, given by the State Executive Council last week, is in fulfillment of the promise of the State Executive Governor, Alhaji Mohammed Lawal to develop the area which has remained neglected by previous administrations in the state.
A statement signed by the Commissioner for Infor-mation Mr. Wale Oladepo said the road construction which will be in phases, was awarded to Habibu Engineering (Nigeria) Limited at a total sum of N237,090,200. To ensure speedy completion of the road, approval was also given for immediate release of 30 per cent of the contract sum.
The road construction is divided into two sections. The first section which covers 98.5 kilometres will take the road from Kaiama to Adogo while the second section will cover a 25.5 km stretch of difficult terrain from Adogo to Bode-Saadu in the Moro Local Government area of the state where the road will link up with the North-south Highway.
The Bode-Saadu-Adogo section involves the blasting of rocks for allignments and construction of several culverts on streams and rivers.
The scope of work in the first phase involves clearing of bush to allow a total average width of 15 metres and laterite filling as carriage way to sub-grade level.
Baruten Local Government shares Nigeria's border with Benin Republic. The two local government areas are a major food basket of the state with the production of yam, yam flour, gari and rice. Transportation of the huge stockof food stuff has always been through Kishi, a Northern town of Oyo State.
Speaking at the Executive Council meeting, Governor Lawal said it has been a burden in his heart to break the jinx of under-development of the area which all previous governors had shied away from. He said it will be a fulfillment of his electoral promise to the people of the areas and a furtherance of his desire to carry all parts of the state along in his government's programme.
Governor Lawal said he is undaunted by the fact that revenue allocation to the state from the Federation Allocation has dwindled following the crash in oil price in the world market following the September 11 attack on the USA.
Expressing appreciation to the state government on behalf of the people, secretary to the State Government Alhaji Umar Abubakar, and the Commissioner for Women Affairs Mrs. Dorcas Gunu Afeniforo both from the area, thanked the state Governor for taking the laudable step which will further boost the popularity of the government among the Baruten and Kaiama people.

Original_Search_Title: State of Emergency Declared After King Killed in Clashes
Headline: Ghana: Ya Na Assassinated
Body_Text:
Ya-Na Yakubu Andani, king of the Dagbon has been killed in factional fighting in Yendi, the traditional capital of the Dagombas. A number of people are also reported killed in the fighting, which erupted last Monday between the Andani and Abudu chieftaincy gates to Ya-Na Skin.
By mid morning yesterday, rumours were filtering in that the king had died when his palace was attacked by a mob. Sporadic gunshots had been heard during the whole of Tuesday despite the presence of armed soldiers and police in the Yendi Township and in spite of a dusk to dawn curfew. The Northern Regional Security Committee deployed armed soldiers and police to the area and banned the celebration of this year's annual Bugum (Fire) Festival at Yendi that was expected to take place on Monday night when security reports revealed the possibility of disturbances. It is yet to be determined what actually caused the flare up but according to sources, tension had been mounting between the two gates since last January over the celebration of the Muslim Eid ul-Adha festival. The Andani Gate that is currently occupying the Dagbon Skin was said not to have been happy when the rival Abudu Gate celebrated the festival in the traditional way at the house of its regent.
Since then, there had been rumours of an imminent attack and counter-attack by the rival chieftaincy gates, which boiled over into an exchange of gunfire on Monday morning.
At the time of going to press, reports reaching The Accra Daily Mail talked of a call to arms by the different factions in Dagbon - a very volatile situation for the government.
The Ya Na's assassination now further complicates the Yendi skin affair and would polarise Dagbon more. This is the first time in Dagbon history when a Ya Na has died at the hands of his own people.
President Kufuor was said to have received the news of the death with great shock and sadness and spent most of yesterday with his advisors discussing the security implications and political fallout.
The President has put the Northern Region on a state of emergency and the security agencies have extended the dusk to dawn curfew to cover the entire region. The government has also assured the nation that those found to have been responsible for the tragedy, would face the full rigours of the law.
Meanwhile, Nana Amoatia Ofori Panin, the Okyenhene was the first traditional ruler to express his disgust at the tragic events of Yendi. In a statement issued from his Secretariat last night, he called on "all Ghanaians to treat this incident as the national tragedy that it is."
The statement also asked the government to intensify the deployment of security personnel to maintain law and order.
The Okyenhene's statement concludes by calling on all his colleague traditional rulers to join him in condemning the heinous crime.
Below is The Accra Daily mail Editorial of 26 March 2002 on the same subject, which probably was being printed when the assassination was taking place.
WILL DAGBON SIT UP?
Reports of disturbances in the Dagbon traditional capital, Yendi, on Monday, were very disturbing indeed.
Dagbon is one of the most important links in the Mole-Dagbani Empire, which extends from Northern Ghana all the way to Burkina Faso.
It occupies very large tracts of the Northern Region and holds great potential for food production. Indeed, traditionally, the Dagombas have been associated with rice, maize, millet and yam farming.
They are also reputed for trading, travelling all over the country to trade in the food crops they produce.
Since Independence, Dagbon has produced some very well educated individuals for Ghana's labour market, and indeed, even the Diaspora. It is no wonder that the Vice President of the republic in these first years of the 21st century is a product of Dagbon. But one thing has eluded Dagbon, even with so much potential, and that is development.
Towns and villages are pathetic and impoverished. There are no good schools, no good health facilities, no good roads, no pipe borne water, etc. In fact Dagbon is technically a distressed area! But do the people of Dagbon, educated and uneducated alike, think about these problems? The answer seems to be a resounding NO! They are more interested in the partisanship of Abuduyilli or Andanyilli; that is, the two royal gates of ascent to the Dagbon skin! Unlike kings in the Akan areas of Ghana who are enstooled, the Dagomba kings are enskinned.
For decades now, there have been disputes over the Yendi skin between the two royal gates. It was thought that after the internecine fights of the seventies and with the stable incumbency of Na Yakubu Andani, Dagombas had allowed by-gones to be by-gones, since most of them had come to the realisation that without peace and unity, their traditional area could not develop. But now, all of a sudden, there's been this flare-up. It is as stupid as it is crazy. The Dagomba intelligentsia, some of whom are behind this madness, must learn from what the Ashantis, Akyems and some other forward-looking traditional areas are doing to bring progress to their people through their kings. Fighting is not the answer. The way to show loyalty is through development, so if they must be partisan, let it be seen in who has brought what development to Dagbon. For starters, who is willing to give the Ya Na's Palace a face-lift?
We believe there is the need for an enquiry into this recent flare up and those responsible for it must be exposed and sanctioned according to the laws of the land.

Original_Search_Title: Will DagbonSit Up?
Headline: Ghana: Will DagbonSit Up?
Body_Text:

Dagbon is one of the most important links in the Mole-Dagbani Empire, which extends from Northern Ghana all the way to Burkina Faso.

It is no wonder that the Vice President of the republic in these first years of the 21st century is a product of Dagbon. But one thing has eluded Dagbon, even with so much potential, and that is development.
Towns and villages are pathetic and impoverished. There are no good schools, no good health facilities, no good roads, no pipe borne water, etc. In fact Dagbon is technically a distressed area! But do the people of Dagbon, educated and uneducated alike, think about these problems? The answer seems to be a resounding NO! They are more interested in the partisanship of Abuduyilli or Andanyilli; that is, the two royal gates of ascent to the Dagbon skin! Unlike kings in the Akan areas of Ghana who are enstooled, the Dagomba kings are enskinned.
For decades now, there have been disputes over the Yendi skin between the two royal gates. It was thought that after the internecine fights of the seventies and with the stable incumbency of Na Yakubu Andani, Dagombas had allowed by-gones to be by-gones, since most of them had come to the realisation that without peace and unity, their traditional area could not develop. But now, all of a sudden, there's been this flare-up. It is as stupid as it is crazy. The Dagomba intelligentsia, some of whom are behind this madness, must learn from what the Ashantis, Akyems and some other forward-looking traditional areas are doing to bring progress to their people through their kings. Fighting is not the answer. The way to show loyalty is through development, so if they must be partisan, let it be seen in who has brought what development to Dagbon. For starters, who is willing to give the Ya Na's Palace a face-lift?
We believe there is the need for an enquiry into this recent flare up and those responsible for it must be exposed and sanctioned according to the laws of the land.

Original_Search_Title: One More Job for Balogun
Headline: Nigeria: One More Job for Balogun
Body_Text:
Lagos — As Mr. Tafa Balogun, the newly appointed Inspector-General of Police settles down to ensure the provision of security of lives and property in Nigeria, he has an urgent assignment in Osun State, from where he hails.
Fortunately the job at hand does not need any tedious preparation that logistics could hinder. It is not an assignment to chase armed robbers so there is no need to complain that the enemy has more sophisticated weapons. The assignment only needs to be carried out with a human face as a way to re-invigorate the public confidence in the Nigerian Police.
The nature of the assignment to be carried out in Osun State this time around would require only services and not force and such service will bring more glory to the police than a thousand armed policemen using force could accomplish. The assignment has to do with an incident of May 27, 2000 at Ilesa, in osun State. Not many Nigerians who were aware of the incident would remember now but the victim of the ugly police brutality is still going about in pain both mentally and physically.
It is the case of Mr. Joshua Olumakinde Siyanbola, a welder with Abuletagba Social Equipment Rental Services, who was shot by a now dismissed policeman named Oluwatoyin. The story, according to the victim, who now walks on an artificial leg goes thus. Siyanbola was returning from work at about 1pm on a Saturday. The poor welder in a pair of jeans trousers and a white long sleeve shirt was carrying a polythene bag containing yam porridge which he bought for his family at home. Since he did not have enough money, he had to trek. Perhaps the spirit of the Children's Day Celebration made him to think of taking something extra home for his children. So when reached the Okesa area of the town he stopped. The place does not only host a branch of Afribank, it also accommodates a lot of petty traders. The women sell roasted yam, corn, banana as well as sweets, kolanuts and some other related items. Siyanbola decided to buy N5 worth of biscuit out of the N15 he had on him. Just as he took the decision to make his children happy, a figure in an "Agbada" attire landed in front of him and identified himself as a policeman. Siyanbola, who was initially agitated by the sudden appearance of this figure, was relieved when he discovered that he was a person put in charge of security. Even if his sudden "visitor" was an armed robber or thief, Siyanbola thought he would not have lost anything spectacular except his porridge and perhaps, the remaining N10 and sachet of biscuit. Besides, no armed robber really chase cheap things like those he was carrying so he listened to the policeman.
The cop promptly ordered him to disclose what was in the bag. When emptied only the yam porridge and a piece of cloth could be found. It was re-packed and the policeman asked how much Joshua had to which he replied N10. Though disappointed by that meagre amount, the cop still felt "half a loaf is better than none" and demanded for the N10 but the man would not let it go. According to him, he tried to convince the policeman that N10 was too small for a very important personality like him and that he would bring a "reasonable" amount the following week. To convince the cop that he meant business, he said many people in the area knew him and as such he would come and see him as promised.
However, Oluwatoyin would not take any of such "pranks" as he thought Joshua was playing. he threatened to shoot if he failed to release the N10. Siyanbola had to think over it. He had only N10 and if he should release the money, it would mean that aside the porridge, there would be nothing again to eat for the day. However, the wish to stay alive prevailed and according to him, as he was trying to take the money from his pocket, his uninvited visitor pulled the trigger and pumped bullet into his left leg. The assailant took to his heels and the traders who were mostly women, nearby also ran for their lives. The victim who said he was shot around 1pm was not taken to the hospital until about 3pm. The cop had gone back to the police station to report that he shot a thief. By the time his colleagues got to the scene, they discovered that the Joshua was not a thief but a popular man also known as "Pappy" in the area. Afribank donated N10,000 the following Monday and the police at Ijamo station bought some drugs.
The leg was eventually amputated and the erring cop dismissed. After walking on crutches for more than a year, the Rotary Club of Ilesa bought an artificial limb for Siyanbola at a cost he put at N150,000 and he said he spent about N50,000 myself from donations I received. It became a serious media issue not only at home but also at the international community.
Prior to his retirement, Mr. Musiliu Smith, Balogun's predecessor, according to Siyanbola, sent emissaries to visit him with promises to help "but up till now, no money had come from the police and I have to feed myself".
He further told THISDAY that: "I appreciate all that people have been doing for me to appeal to the police. The lawmakers went to Abuja because of this but there was no result. Before Chief Bola Ige died, the Alliance for Democracy (AD) Senate Leader, Chief Mojisoluwa Akinfenwa, took my case to him but to no avail".
Siyanbola lamented, further that "I have been living on peoples goodwill and donation" and added that he had to withdraw his two kids from school.
He also claimed to have approached the state governor, Chief Bisi Akande directly during a meet-the-people programme at Ifewara "but the governor told me that he was not in charge of the police and that it was the Federal Government. He equally said he would refer my case to the social welfare department but up till now I have not seen any help".
"My hope is now on God and the police to compensate me for being a victim of gunshot without any offence. I cannot blame the governor. It is true that the police belongs to the Federal Government but I will appreciate help from anywhere it comes" , he said.
Siyanbola concluded that he was advised to hire a lawyer. "But I have no money to do that, I only want to appeal to police authorities to bail me out of my financial mess" adding "I learnt the Sergeant that shot me has been dismissed but how will that benefit me and put food on the table. How will my children go back to school with his dismissal?"

Original_Search_Title: Bill Clinton Was Here
Headline: Nigeria: Bill Clinton Was Here
Body_Text:
Ushafa, the small community near the federal capital,   Abuja  , visited by former   US  president, becomes a tourist centre
Ushafa, one of the Gbagyi-speaking villages around the federal capital territory,   Abuja  , is fast becoming a mecca of some sort. The once relatively unknown village has been added to the country's tourism map since Bill Clinton, former president of the   United States  , accompanied by his daughter, Chelsea, visited the community late in 2000. Known for its fine pottery works, Ushafa has continued to attract both local and international tourists. Agbani Darego, the current Miss World, visited Ushafa too.
Situated at the foot of one of the many surrounding hills that characterise the Bwari local government area and barely 45 kilometres from Abuja, Ushafa cuts the picture of a town struggling to hold onto its cherished past in the face of modernism. With the glitter and charm of   Abuja  so close by, it is difficult for Ushafa not to bow to the pressures of change.
Traditional mud houses in simple designs are now roofed with galvanised roofing sheets in place of time-tested, temperature-proof thatch roofs. Attached to each mud house is a barn or two as storage houses for farm products. There are no delineated streets but foot paths. The few relatively larger mud or block buildings are a primary school, a health centre (established through community effort), churches, mosques and the Ushafa pottery centre.
Surrounded by other Gbagyi communities like Pewii, Jigou, Mabarra, Koguo, Guto, Apuye, Kushagou, Guita, Yaupe, Sinape and Adiadia, Ushafa enjoys the special privilege of electricity, pipe-borne water made possible because of its proximity to the lower Usman Dam, and its new found popularity.
Ushafa presents a panorama of local architecture, artistry, and folklore in a semi-arid ambience. According to Ndabiya Shiambaguei, the Sarkin Bada of Ushafa, the town was founded by a hunter "long time ago." What led to the hunter's relocation is still a mystery but oral history has it that he arrived at a spot in what is now Ushafa with his wife. He found his new environment habitable enough to start a new life. There and then, the hunter consulted his wife and both agreed to call their new settlement Ushafa - meaning: "Whatever that has been agreed upon becomes a bond." Till date, this has been the guiding spirit of the community. Shiambaguei said that no decision is ever implemented in the village without a consensus.
So far, it has worked for the community and it has placed Ushafa ahead of neighbouring Gbagyi-speaking communities. There is almost a near absence of land or royal disputes in the community. The pervading peace in Ushafa could be said to have brought prosperity, religious pluralism and harmony among the people.
There are eight royal families. The royal stool is, at the moment, occupied by Mohammed Baba Ushafa, who has been on the throne for 31 years. Baba Ushafa is of the Gbagyisa royal house.
Economically, the community's strength lies in farming and local craft. Traditionally, the men engage in farming guineacorn, millet and yams. Dyeing, e.g. Aje, a piece of cloth dyed in deep blue- the type given to   Clinton  when he visited the community ‑ is also part of Ushafa industry, in addition to mat-making. However, with the semi-arid nature of the community, farming is increasingly becoming difficult. Agricultural yields are relatively low and the federal government is not assisting the farmers with fertilisers.
"Before now, government used to supply us with fertilisers but since it discontinued that practice, our problems have become manifold," Shiambaguei said. Accordingly, teenagers and able-bodied young men who would have taken to farming are now compelled to look elsewhere for sustenance. Some of them take to tailoring, furniture-making, hair-dressing, commercial bus driving or operate commercial motor bikes
The young girls, on their part resort to making dresses, running beauty salons, trading or being forced into marriages for fear of being lured into "unholy activities." The more matured and married women engage in pottery. It takes nearly three to four weeks to make a water pot.
Ushafa was once a predominantly pagan community. Shiambaguei could not recall at what point or time the foreign influences changed the social fabric of the village. But he was quite sure that the Gbagyis who migrated from southern   Zaria  to resettle in the area must have been influenced greatly by Islam. But the Christian community has grown exponentially. Ekuono, an indigene, was said to be the founder of the first Catholic church in the village. Till date, the mud-built St. Mathew's Catholic Church still stands erect and competes with the growing number of pentecostal churches in the community.
The growing number of adherents to the many religious groups and the admixture of non-natives like the Yoruba and Igbo who are known for their commercial activities are helping to change the community. Shiambaguei affirmed that some of the popular traditional festivals are now considered as fetish, especially by the Christians. The situation in the community is such that the only festival being observed today is the Jibajin which comes up on January 1, every year. It celebrates the coming of a new year with cultural dances, masquerades and traditional sports.
"Christianity has changed the community so much that even pito, a local brew made from fermented guineacorn, is drunk only by a negligible number of pagans," Shiambaguei said. Yet for all the external influences, nothing could take away the age-old delicacy of pounded yam and Tuwo from their menu.
Ushafa has immense tourist potentials but for now, beyond the pottery centre which is the main attraction, its potentials are largely unexploited. Even the  royal palace is just too colourless  to attract a second glance. Tied to a tree next to the palace was the royal horse that seemed to wonder why visitors kept trooping in and out of his master's court.
According to Shiambaguei, beyond the fanfare and paparazzi that greeted   Clinton  's visit two years ago, government seems to have lost interest in the community. Even the appeal to the federal government to provide a car befitting the royal court has so far fallen on deaf ears, Shiambaguei alleged.
But visitors from far and near would continue to ply the dusty road to see Ushafa pottery. And whatever the developmental changes in the community, it would not change the Gbagyi woman from carrying her load on her back.
Newswatch Volume 35 No 12, March 25, 2002

Original_Search_Title: Re: Communal Clashes And Official Connivance
Headline: Nigeria: Re: Communal Clashes And Official Connivance
Body_Text:
Since the outbreak of hostilities between the Jukun/Fulani and the Tiv, there have been series of diverse comments, views and opinions on the origin, cause and dimensions of the crises. Various groups and individuals from across the country have tried in one way or the other to explain or interpret the conflict the way they saw it. Expectedly, many of these people were carried away by their own sentiments, bordering on personal and sectarian interests, narrow mindedness and prejudice. The government of Taraba State neither opposed, nor condemned any individual or group for their views on the crises, because it believes that everyone had the right to express himself from whatever angle he was looking at the issues at stake. The government refused to be provoked into responding either directly or indirectly to any issue concerning the conflict because it did not believe that was the best way of serving the interest of the teeming victims of the unfortunate incident.
Since the crises started, the Taraba State governor, Reverend Jolly Nyame made it clear that the government is not interested in playing politics with the plight of its citizens who were caught up in the crossfire of this unfortunate human tragedy.
Considering the huge resources already committed by the government and the governor's personal interest in the immediate resolution of the conflict, which has been widely reported in both print and electronic media, no right thinking person can justifiably accuse the government of complicity in the crises. The government had never at any point shrunk from its responsibility of protecting the lives of its citizens anywhere within the state.
Happily enough, the relentless efforts of the government to restore peace and tranquillity to the troubled areas have started yielding positive and impressive results as the refugees have started returning to their homes.
Unfortunately, this development does not seem to go down well with a few people as they are still bent on disrupting these efforts through deliberate incitement and outright falsehood to create confusion. These are the same people who throughout the duration of the crises, had presented themselves as true friends of the Tiv, cajoling and inciting them to action, while in actual fact they did not wish them well. A case in point is the article written by one Mr. Idang Alibi, titled "Communal clashes and official connivance" and carried in the Daily Trust of February 28, 2002.
The article is clearly the most irresponsible, callous and mischievous opinion ever carried on the Tiv/Jukun misunderstanding. The article was not only full of false, misleading and malicious accusations, but also pointedly blamed the Taraba State government of 'conniving' with Jukuns and Fulanis to visit mayhem on the Tiv. Mr. Alibi who claims to be a former editor of the Cross River State owned Sunday Chronicle, displayed a great sense of ignorance, when he sought to create the impression that only Tiv towns and villages were destroyed. To reach this conclusion, he claimed to have "passed through" Jalingo to Wukari on road. There is nothing in the article to suggest that he was in Jalingo or tried to get the views of any senior official of the Taraba State government on an issue in which he came out boldly to accuse the government. It is completely unspeakable and professionally myopic for a former editor of a seemingly national paper to write a one-sided feature on an issue in which he knew next to nothing apart from looking through the window of the vehicle in which he travelled. If he feels that a single journey was enough for him to understand the whole situation, then it is indeed very unfortunate. The truth is that Mr. Alibi has not been following responsible media reports on the crises and the far-reaching efforts made by the state government to bring the situation under control. Ordinarily, one should not have reacted to uncorroborated information, but one cannot imagine the number of right-thinking and responsible members of the society who may be misled by the ill conceived and misrepresented article.
To remove every veil of doubt in the minds of people like Alibi it is important to note the various measures taken by the Taraba State government to curtail the crises and assist victims of the communal war.
By now many Nigerian may be aware of the bravery displayed by Governor Jolly Nyame when he rushed to Wukari to rescue about 20 policemen who strayed into Taraba from Benue State and were apprehended by Jukun youth. Displaying a high sense of diplomacy and patriotism the governor explained to them the implication of manhandling or killing any agent of the federal government. When the youth mounted roadblocks along the Wukari road, Governor Nyame immediately requested the deployment of soldiers in order to clear the roads and allow free flow of vehicles along all major roads of Taraba State. It was in course of handling this very important national assignment along the border road to Kwatan Sule, that 19 soldiers were abducted and killed by Tiv militia. It is important to note that the situation within Taraba territory was completely under control until the gruesome murder of the soldiers which created a backlash resulting in the destruction of Dan Anacha and other villages along the Wukari road. It is indeed very sad that, Mr Alibi is not aware that a retaliatory strike by Tiv youth have resulted in the complete destruction of two major Jukun towns, Kente and Akwana and several villages along the border with Benue State. Today these towns and villages are not only in full ruins but also their inhabitants are refugees in Wukari town. It may interest everyone to note that despite great losses suffered by Jukuns in the crises, the Taraba State government had expressed greater sympathy to the Tiv, since most of them appeared to be heading back to Benue State. In order to assist the displaced Tivs, the government established two refugee camps in Mutum Biyu and Bali. The camps were provided with food, water, beddings and security to make them comfortable. The camps are still operational.
Also, from the outbreak of the hostilities to date, Governor Jolly Nyame has held more than 10 joint and separate meetings with Jukun and Tiv senior civil servants. Similar meetings were held with traditional rulers, chairmen of local governments and opinion leaders in the affected areas. During the last meeting, the governor directed the senior civil servants to go back to their respective areas and gave them one week to convince their people to allow the Tiv people to return. These are the measures that have now facilitated the return of the Tiv to Ibbi Local government area. During a recent visit to a refugee camp in Benue State, Governor Jolly Nyame not only gave them N500,000 for their transport, but also announced a four-week timetable for the full return of the Tiv to Taraba State. Considering the Governor's personal commitment to the restoration of peace to Taraba State, there is a compelling reason to declare that the Tiv are lucky to have Reverend Nyame as the governor of Taraba State. If it were not Governor Nyame at the helm of affairs, the return of the Tiv to Taraba would be unthinkable and impossible due to the level of mistrust displayed by the warring parties. The governor, who is from the northern part of the state has no immediate stake in the crises apart from the fact that as the man divinely bestowed with the responsibility of leading the teeming people of Taraba State, it is his concern that everyone should live in peace with one another, so as to provide a conducive atmosphere for the government to attend to their basic needs.
Based on the foregoing, Governor Nyame and the government of Taraba State have no reason whatsoever to 'connive' with anyone to kill or destroy property belonging to Tiv people. On two occasions the governor drove to Dan Anacha and personally apprehended truckloads of yam belonging to Tiv people. He handed the trucks to the police and directed them to prevent any unauthorised person from harvesting or selling farm produce belonging to displaced persons. If the police failed to live up to expectation, it is left to the public to decide who had not done his job. Certainly, the governor is more interested than anyone to see the end of the hostilities, because the crises has completely drained the resources of the government and slowed down its progress. The government is not interested in diversionary tactics by people who are pushing for personal benefits from the crises. Mr. Alibi and his fellow travellers should look elsewhere for a way to please their godfathers. The government is looking for peacemakers and not faultfinders or hired mourners who have no sympathy with the issue at hand rather than the size of their pockets.

Original_Search_Title: Guber Aspirant Counsels Ekiti On Tax Waivers
Headline: Nigeria: Guber Aspirant Counsels Ekiti On Tax Waivers
Body_Text:
Ado Ekiti — A gubernatorial aspirant of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) in Ekiti State, Mr. Ayo Fayose, has called on the state government to waive stringent taxes that could discourage investors from the state.
Speaking at the commissioning of an ultra modern Eatery Centre called De-Prince & Princess in Ado-Ekiti over the weekend, Mr. Fayose expressed displeasure over the low level of commercial activities in the state.
He commended the initiator of the new business outfit, Chief Christopher Uzomah, who is a native of Enugu State, while urging the administration in Ekiti to consider appointing an Ibo man as commissioner for Commerce to boost commercial activities in the state.
In his welcome address at the occasion, the initiator of the outfit, Chief Uzomah, who is the chairman and chief executive of Uzotraco Nigeria Limited based in Akure, Ondo State appealed to the people of Ekiti to see the new eatery centre as part of the efforts to develop the state.
Also the Managing Director of the new outfit, Mr. John Orji described the eatery as an idea conceived out of the desire to bring such quality services to Ekiti State, as the outfit's products are prepared under the most hygienic conditions.
