The incessant cases of inter-tribal wars being reported at the Taraba/Benue border, that led to the killing of 22 soldiers recently and the Mambila /Fulani war in the Mambila plateau in Taraba State, and many others here in the North that have been recurring from time to time can be traced historically.
While the various governments are trampling on the Hausa/Fulani year after year from 1904 to date, the Christian missionaries are and have all this long been concentrating on the so-called emancipation of the tribes/cum minorities or the Habe districts of the various emirates of the North. First by Christianising them, educating them and economically empowering them with white -cola jobs, in government, in the church and non-governmental organisations. As a result, polluting the minds of the beneficiaries with the notion that the Hausa/Fulani are the people that have denied them all this goodies all along and are not happy that they now could enjoy it.
The current ethnocentric cleansings going on around the nation in which the OPC aims at getting rid of all other Nigerians in Yoruba land, whereas the Bakassi Boys in the east do not want to see any non Ibo selling even tea in the streets of Onitsha and the Egbesu boys destroying the property of genuine investors, the Northern part of the country had no option but to act, and when doing so, contrary to all the evil machinations of the others, the North instead, formed the Arewa Consultative Forum not another terrorist group like all the other regions.
The forum facilitates dialogue within which we relate and cool surging tempers and boiling cauldrons in the region of the North, as led by a gentle man in the person of Alhaji M. D. Yusufu. With the above set-up in motion, some disgruntled ingrates amongst us are still fuelling fires of hate, malaise and ethnic love- lost, causing disruptions to harmony and peace as depicted in Tiv land, Adara and Mambila in Benue, Nasarawa and Taraba states of recent.
While in Benue, Taraba and Nasarawa borders we blame the crises on natural phenomenon, in the sense that, historically, the Tiv being one of the last tribes to arrive on Benue river basin from Congo could not have enough time to establish itself in the basin, and had to continue pushing further into hinterlands of Taraba, Nasarawa and Niger for more land for their yams, beniseed and cassava, this is being resisted by the earlier occupants of same lands in all directions. First Taraba then Nasarawa and soon Niger (as can be seen of Tiv settlements between Gawu-Babangida and Lapai on the road to Lagos from Abuja).
In Mambila plateau in Taraba state, the onus of the matter is the mistake of the Hausa/Fulani in the attempt to settle on the plains of the plateau controlling so much wealth and being in the minority in terms of population, this imbalance created so much envy among the surrounding tribes that they will never allow the Fulani to live in peace until they annihilate the last of them and their vast wealth (hence the killing of all prosperous businessmen any time there is a riot up on the plateau.)
I am convinced that as long as there are communal and religious crises in the federation in this political dispensation, right from the OPC onslaught on Hausa traders in Ketu and on and on except the carnage in Ile Ife and Ogoniland all the others are targeted at the Hausa/Fulani. Either in Zango, Kaduna, Ilorin, Tafawa Balewa, and Kaltungo or in Mambila the victim is always the Hausa/Fulani, irrespective of the colouring the press and government might give the incident and situation.
I here submit that we (the Hausa/Fulani) are neither in power nor near it in any state that we are not in total control and majority, the president today is southern Yoruba man and his vice a tribe from Adamawa state, the Senate President an Ibo man, with our brother Ghali in the seat of Speaker of the House of Representative, even that was not usurped by our majority but was democratically allocated to us by the ruling party.
The individual wealth and various bounties of God in the hands of the Hausa and Fulani man in this country was not by statutory allocation but the result of hundreds of years of trading right from the historic trans-Saharan trade to modern IT marketing. Please we call on Nigerians irrespective of tribe to endeavour to engage in economic activities and stop perpetrating troubles that can only draw us back the more.

Original_Search_Title: Exploring Karmo Market
Headline: Nigeria: Exploring Karmo Market
Body_Text:
Tuesdays are usually very busy for traders in Karmo, a suburb in the Federal Capital Territory as people rush there to buy goods at prices said to be cheaper than other days.
Tanimu who sells fairly used clothes thinks that the market day being slated for Tuesday was as a matter of convenience for the indigenous Karmo which used to be a small settlement.
A random sampling among the traders reveals that most of the traders in the Tuesday market come in from other areas in Abuja; however, traders of assorted bush meat are from Kano and also Azare in Bauchi State.
A visit to the market holds a lot of interest as traders of various goods, ranging from food stuffs, clothing and other accessories are seen with their wares on display from about 500 metres (on both sides of the road) before the main market, under the scorching sun.
Prominent among the goods that are found in this market are yams, beans, salad ingredients and fairly used clothes popularly known as "bend-down boutique or Gwanjo. You could be home, away from home here, as ingredients for soup from different localities are available such as "Okoho" sticks for Idoma(s), Okazi, Uziza, Ora for Igbo(s) and even periwinkle, oysters, snails etc.
Abuja Trust discovered that prices of goods are not so different from other markets, however its unique feature lies in the fact that, despite the inconveniences of buying and selling under the sun, their still exists a high level of understanding and organisation between the sellers and buyers as well as among the traders themselves.

Original_Search_Title: Commodity Exchange: A Failed Economic Policy
Headline: Nigeria: Commodity Exchange: A Failed Economic Policy
Body_Text:
Sometime ago it was announced through the media that Abuja Stock Exchange was closed and that a commodity exchange was to take its place. Few weeks later it was widely publicised that the Federal Ministry of Agriculture was establishing three companies with equity capital of N500,000 million to form the new commodity exchange, the equity is said to be subscribed by the Federal Government, the 36 states government and the over 700 local governments in the country. The activities of the new Commodity Exchange include the main activities of the 36 States and over 700 Local Government agricultural departments. What disturbed me about the publications were the rosy expectations that they raised in many local governments.
I agree that our rural farmers need proper attention but what is the use of creating the three big wasteful companies under the cover of farmers. I am aware that Nigerian farmers have their needs but only the farmers can tell the government what they need and not people sitting in Abuja. I thought the Federal Government should take lessons from past similar companies including the Urban Development Bank etc. In fact, commodity exchanges are commercial and not agricultural activities. One wonders what brought the Federal Ministry of Agriculture into this business where it has no expertise.
Commodity exchanges or commodity markets are places where people assemble to buy and sell or the assembly itself or the business done in commodities. It is in the third sense that we generally speak of the commodity market, e.g. cotton market, or the rice market, or the yam market, or the cocoa market. Though naturally they also have a physical location most markets are located at points through which important quantities of the commodity pass on their way from producer to consumer. Most foreign countries have commodity exchanges or markets. In the United Kingdom the bigger markets are available in London and Liverpool, but the world's biggest commodity exchange is the Chicago Commodity Exchange. The exchange makes possible a continuous and sensitive adjustment of prices to changing demand and supply.
Commodities in Europe and America are dealt in on spot contracts (that is for immediate delivery) but the majority are dealt in for future delivery .The terms of these contracts vary to suit the requirements of buyers and sellers. Contracts and methods of doing business are standardised by committees of trade associations (not co-operative societies). Disputes between members are generally settled by arbitration and recourse to the law courts is seldom necessary. Generally speaking the exchanges are open to members only, and membership is subject to the approval of elected boards or committees. In many cases membership entails the purchase of shares and the payment of substantial entrance fees and annual subscriptions. To be a member of a commodity exchange or trade association is considered a privilege and to maintain their status, care is taken by the executive committees to ensure the integrity and financial standing of members. Therefore the federal government should take note that no government in Europe or America takes part in formation and promotion of any commodity exchange.
I hope the federal government advisers are not confusing the meaning and the purpose of commodity boards with commodity exchanges. While commodity boards were established mainly to export commodities on behalf of the people, the commodity exchange is a market where buyers and sellers meet. In Nigeria commodity exchanges have existed for a very long time, and were formed not by associations as now practised in Europe but in a small way by sole traders and merchants as Europe started hundreds of years ago. In big towns throughout Nigeria one can find commodity merchants in main town markets for all commodities one wants to buy or sell. It is because of these commodity exchanges that the famous Kano pyramid came into being. Nigerians expect the federal government with its might to know that commodity merchants in Nigeria know very well where commodity exchanges are at any particular time.
The establishment of a new commodity exchange as a replacement of Abuja Stock Exchange does not appear to make any economic sense since the two are different in purpose and expect different results. To our own experience only military governments establish government bodies just to satisfy one or two people without any planning. Nigerians will not expect a democratically elected government to make the same mistake. When there is any waste of public funds we shall blame Mr President not any adviser. Without a good prospectus (not bankable proposal) I may regard the proposal as a wasteful proposal and shall not advise any state or local government to subscribe to the shares of the three proposed companies. I wonder why the Federal Ministry of Agriculture (agronomist) can effectively promote the establishment of three new commercial companies to form new commodity exchanges and not the Federal Ministry of Commerce- the professional ministry. From what came from the media, the three proposed companies shall be public companies as such invitation to subscribe must be through prospectus, that is according to the law (Statutory and General Information). I am not sure whether the Federal Ministry of Agriculture can read and understand a prospectus let alone prepare one. I am certain if a prospectus is properly prepared (not bankable prospectus) and presented to Mr President, Chief O. A. Obasanjo, Mr President shall agree he was wrongly advised.
So much money has been wasted in the name of agriculture in the past and more is still being wasted while fewer and fewer tools are provided to the farmers. Most farmers will see government assistance on agriculture in the 1960s as better than what obtains today. Agricultural inputs are not available, and where available are not supplied when needed. It is now time for the federal and state governments to stop supporting wasteful agricultural projects and provide the agricultural sector and rural economies with the proper tools they need to face the future. The Federal Government should discourage imports of agricultural grains and grain products; and put more emphasis on quality, food safety, the environment, and welfare of animals and birds in its programmes. Provision of incentives for Nigerian farmers in any way possible should be increased. We all believe in the unique role played by farmers in the economy in terms of the food they produce and for society at large through their role in keeping the rural areas alive. We are all aware of the hardship and challenges through which farmers live, we believe in the need for a safety net.

Original_Search_Title: Government to Earn N208bn From Agriculture This Year - Report
Headline: Nigeria: Government to Earn N208bn From Agriculture This Year - Report
Body_Text:
DESPITE about N4 billion budgetary allocation to the agricultural
sector in this year s budget, the Federal Ministry of Agriculture has
projected that about N280 billion will be earned from agriculture
this year.
According to a recent report by the Federal Ministry of Agriculture,
Nigeria is projected to earn about N208 billion from cash crops this
year.
Most of the money is expected to come from the sales of oil palm
produce, cocoa, timber/plywood, rubber, cashew, gum arabic,
pineapples, cassava and yam.
The report is predicated on additional land being put under
cultivation, while other cash crops not mentioned in the report is
expected to make substantial contribution to the economy.
The ministry stressed that whilst it is in full support of an
improved agricultural export base, the farmers must be encouraged to
think in value-added terms. The farmers were advised to export
semi-processed goods rather than shipping them raw.
For the projection of the Federal Ministry of Agriculture to be
realised, an expert believes that a lot of work will have to be done
at both at federal and state levels.
Dr Frank Ugwuohia, an Agro-economist has suggested that both the
federal and state governments should encourage modern farm practices
through the use of trained extension advisers who will live among the
farmers, supplying them with improved seedlings and leading by
example by showing what can be achieved in selected demonstration
farm.
He called on the federal government to increase henceforth, its
budgetary allocation to the agricultural sector in order to increase
its contribution to the country s Gross Domestic Product (GDP).
According to him, the government should reactivate the various closed
River Basin Authorities. In addition, he suggested all the World Bank
farms dotted across the country that have been abandoned should be
revived.
"Government should find out what went wrong with these farms with a
view to getting them up and running again. The federal government
once created specialised institutions for farmers only. One wonders
what has become of these institutions. If they were inefficient in
the past, they can be made to work now".
He called for an overhaul of the country s agricultural policy to
ensure that several agricultural initiatives formulated in the past
are relevant to the country s economic development.

Original_Search_Title: Edo: Poser Over N50m Road Contract Sum
Headline: Nigeria: Edo: Poser Over N50m Road Contract Sum
Body_Text:
Communities accuse a construction firm of refusal to move to site a year after collecting the mobilisation sum, surprised at government's quietness over the matter
Communities of Owan East and West Local Governments of Edo State are unhappy with the State Government,questioning why a local construction firm has refused uptil now to move to sight nearly a year after it allegedly collected N50million as mobilisation from it for the completion of a major road, linking the two council areas.
The Lucky Igbinedion administration, following intense and consistent pressures on it by the people of the area, particularly a Lagos-based non-partisan, non-profit civil organisation of the people of the two local governments, Uma'gbeva, was said to have re-awarded the contract last year for the Uzebba-Otuo Road to Setraco (Nig) Ltd for over N300million.
The government reportedly released N50million to the firm as mobilisation fee last April 28, expecting it to immediately move to site which it has not done till date.
"Since last year, we have been chasing the Auchi-based firm, begging it to move to site.
"We virtually turned the house of the Chairman of the company, Alhaji Inu Umoru, to our house last year. As we were going there, so were our kinsmen at home led by the Oba of Ikhin, one of the communities the road passes through, appealing to him to make the firm to move to site and he kept assuring us that they would commence and complete work on the remaining portion of the road. Our people were regularly going to him with gifts and food stuffs even though the company has collected the N50million mobilisation fee from the state government for the road, begging him to make the company to commence work on the road.
"Surprisingly, the last time our people went to Alhaji Inu Umoru towards the end of last year, he chased them away, warning them not to come to him again on the road project," J.F. Igbalajobi, Chairman of Uma'gbeva, who spoke to City Diary in company of the Secretary, Barrister I.O. Jimoh, Engr M.B. Ojebuouboh and Tunde Thompson, on the position of the group on the matter after their meeting at the association's secretariat, 6, Salawu Street, Arowojobe, Oshodi,last weekend, said.
A road linking several communities of the two local governments and such other states as Ondo, Oyo, Kogi and the Federal capital Territory, (FTC), Abuja, for over a century, Uma'gbeva said the people of the area have been agitating for the construction of the road for the past three decades not only because it would serve as an inter-state road between Edo and the States mentioned above, but for the fact that it would link such communities as Erue; Obola, and Okpuje among others in Iuleha, Owan West Local Government Area and Uroe, Ikhin, Arokho, Ohanmi, and Otuo in Owan East Local Government.
"Agitation for the construction of this road has been on for over three decades. In the 1974/75 budget of the then Midwest, the then Military Governor,Brigadier-General Samuel Osaigbovo Ogbemudia,announced the establishment of a task force on some state roads, including the Uzebba-Otuo Road but since then our hope of seeing the road constructed have remained painfully unrealised.
"The road, like we have always told them, links our communities, which have several agricultural commodities and untapped natural resources apart from being a potentially strategic route capable of boosting economic and social development locally and nationally. We have cocoa, cassava, plenty of high quality garri, plantain, timber, rice, yams, palm oil, date palms and all sorts of fruits, which we are unable to easily transport out because of the poor state of the road, this is beside the high concentration of people on the stretch of the road from Uzebba-Otuo.
"During the rains, the place is completely inaccessible, burial ceremonies are delayed for upward of three months because the place is inaccessible during the period," Igbalajobi said of the situation of the road for ages, emphasising that if it was wholly constructed, it would shorten the distance to Abuja as it connects Auchi, and Okene in Kogi.
He recounted past attempts by government to get the road constructed but noted that they had always ended up in the project being abandoned or not executed at all.
Recalling the number of times such attempts have been made without success, he said Inu Umoru and his Setraco company first got involved in the project in the second republic. He said the Ambrose Alli administration had awarded the contract along with others,giving the Afuze-Auchi, Sabogidda-Ora- Uzebba Roads to Niger Cat,while on the advice of some high ranking officials of the government,the Uzebba-Otuo Road was awarded to Setraco on the argument that it was to encourage local contractors .
Little or nothing, he recalled, was done on the road by the company, except that it graded it, provided drainage on it before abandoning it in 1989.
Following persistent pressures on the state government, Uma'gbeva said the government in 1995/96 made it the first project it recommended to the Petroleum Special Trust Fund (PTF). The project, which was costed at N140million,they noted,was not executed by the PTF. The government subsequently re-awarded the contract to Messrs Setraco (Nig) Ltd in 1997/98 for N128million after over a decade of inaction,they claimed.
"Again, work stopped abruptly on the road in November.,1998 at the 12th kilometre of the road, less than a kilometre to Uroe and Arokho, a little after Erue. Even the portion tarred has already gone bad again", Igbalajobi pointed out.
After prolonged efforts at making the current government of Lucky Igbinedion to understand while the remaining portion of the road needed to be completed, the government was said to have re-awarded the contract to Setraco last year and released N50million to it as mobilisation but the company has uptil now not commenced work on the road.
As if that is not enough,hopes that the company woud ever move to site to commence work on the road were dashed recently when the company allegedly denied being paid any mobilisation by the state government in respect of the road,claiming that it was give N90million of the N100 million the government owed it for past jobs done for it to leave a balance of N10million.
That position was, however, disputed by state officials, who said the agreement between the government and the company was that it took N50million as mobilisation for the Uzebba-Otuo Road and N40million as part payment of the N100million it owed it.
Also supporting that position,members of the Uma'gbeva said the company's position was an afterthought.The company's Chairman,Inu Umoru,they said,had a number of times last year confirmed to them that the company had been paid the N50million mobilisation sum by the state government,assuring that they would move to site to begin work on the road.Citing specifically one such occasion when he publicly confirmed that to the people of the area,Uma'gbeva said Inu Umoru had told a gathering of Owan people at the coronation of the Oba of Ikhin last year that the company had received that much from the state government as mobilisation for the completion of the road and wondered why the Chief was now eating his words.
Generally across the state today, the road is perceived as being employed to siphon public funds with successive governments continuously dragging feet on it.
For instance, apart from the wide variations in the contract sum as it is awarded from time to time, there are strong indications, according to independent opinions, that the remaining portion of the road put at 13 kilometres by government and awarded again as such last year to Setraco, was far less than that.
Insiders at the State Ministry of Works and Housing insisted that the portion of the road was just slightly above eight kilometres.
Its patients, having been heavily tasked,Uma'gbeva said it has no reason to enter into any argument with the company as it has no business with it.It called on the state government to immediately bring the company to its senses and make it to go to work on the road in the interest of peace,warning that unless that road was completed before the 2003 elections,the people of the area would not participate in them.

Original_Search_Title: Warring Factions Hail Reconciliation Law
Headline: Ghana: Warring Factions Hail Reconciliation Law
Body_Text:
The Gonjas, Kokonmbas and the Nanumbas in the East Gonja District have hailed the National Reconciliation Law describing it as an opportunity for them to reconcile with one another.
The three ethnic groups have been living in fear of one another following the Kokonkomba-Nanumba ethnic conflict in 1993 and 1994, which spilled over to Salaga and its surrounding areas.
Mr Abu-bakr Saddique Boniface, Deputy Minister of Trade and Industry and an Independent Member of Parliament for Salaga, told the Ghana News Agency in Accra after interacting with his constituents that the people see the law as unique since it would enable them to air their grievances for redress.
Mr Boniface said the people said they were willing to appear before the yet to be formed National Reconciliation Commission to state human rights abuses they suffered.
During the conflict many people died and property including livestock, houses and farms were destroyed and those hurt have been nursing grudges against the perpetrators.
He said the people expressed their satisfaction with the fact that the Commission would encourage people to come out and confess and ask for forgiveness and for those wronged to be compensated in a way by the state.
Mr Boniface said it is necessary for those who have some reservations about the law to understand that the legislation could not receive a hundred per cent support but that it should also be understood that the logistics involved in the work of the Commission is colossal.
"It is, therefore, necessary that the cut off time frame should be limited and those, who also felt wronged in the constitutional regimes could use the "window" the reconciliation law made room for to seek redress."
Mr Boniface said it would be imperative for the members of the Commission to have the decency and the courtesy to entertain those aggrieved ones who would come through the "window".
The Deputy Minister appealed to all to come to terms with the position of Parliament on the reconciliation law.
Mr Alhassan Yahaya, a spokesman of the Minister's campaign team, commended the government for the ongoing rehabilitation work on the roads in the Salaga Township.
He said it takes more than five hours to travel from Mankago to Tamale, a distance of 154 kilometres instead of two hours adding that the area produces large quantities of yams and livestock.
Mr Yahaya said "the two senior secondary schools in the area are under resourced, the district police station is in a rented premises and the last time the area saw a tarred road was about 30 years ago".

Original_Search_Title: Ige - the Mystery of a Great Murder
Headline: Nigeria: Ige - the Mystery of a Great Murder
Body_Text:
The manner of his death simply upped his profile and image all the more, so much that the murdered Bola Ige was considered greater and better loved than a living Bola Ige. But a combination of factors have made Ige to leave planet earth with a re-echoing bang, Eddy Odivwri writes on the murder saga within the context of the confession of the prime suspect Mr. Elubode Adebayo, alias Frayo and grand rites accorded the egregious statesman
What perhaps has bothered most Nigerians on the Dele Ige assassination is not exactly that the man died, or was killed; but the billion dollar question: Who killed Bola Ige?
Given a pedigree of inconclusive or unannounced police investigations, many had concluded that the fog sorrounding those behind the murder, rather than clearing, will be thickening by the day; in such a matter will simply fizzle out.The police had been on edge. The motionless movements of the force in the last two weeks only confirmed the fears of the public. But still, arrest were made, investigations, they said, were still going on. But they declared one Adebayo, alias "Frayo" wanted that was after they (police) had paraded somebody who was said to have also surrendered himself to the police to have been responsible for killing Ige.
After about a week-long of quizzing, the police reversed the hope of the public when they described the man (of whom they had said "he is of sound mind) as now being "unstable and inconsistent. That shut that clue(?) For 21 days the police had been looking for the Frayo, until last Sunday, the news sneaked out that the suspect surrendered himself to a Lagos lawyer, Mr. Festus Keyamo, who in turn, handed him over to the police at a press conference last Monday.
The pronouncement of Frayo are instructive, chilling and definitive in the bestial content of humanity of the degenerate stuck.
Besides the assault on the sensitivity of the civilized stock of humanity, the suspect was to appear in a flowing religious white cassock, armed with a copy of the Bible (KJV) and had dangling from his neck, a big cross, remniscent of the murder of another great holy leader: Jesus Christ. For a man dripping with the blood of the slain minute to clutch a book and insignia that forbid evil, was, to say the least, a gutesque irony.
But that again, defines the man called Bola Ige. A man who lived for the people and git killed by the people - a democratic murder.
As said elsewhere, all assassinations carried out against great men of history, derive from what they stand for or believe in. In Ige's case, it was an issue of greatness. And this was roundly celebrated in more ways than one.
Reading some portion of his Kaduna Boy, it was clear that Chief James Idowu Ajibola Ige (SAN), like most great men of history was born humble , perhaps in lowliness.
But by sheer dint of hardwork, intelligence and some stroke of metaphysical support, Chief Ige attained greatness. A status that was significantly enlarged at death.
Ige's trudge on the planet earth, for 71 years, left several indelible marks both on the pages of history and on the banner of humanity. Not one given to banality, Ige made strong statements with his contact with politics, law practice, classics, humanity and governance.
Given the mega status both in life and death, the rites preceding his final departure from this sinful world have been rich of and bountiful colours.
As written by Yemi Ogunbiyi, a friend of the late Ige, the temptation is indeed strong to draw a parallel between the public reaction and the burial rites of the late Chief Obafemi Awolowo and that of Ige. Both men, though one, the master and the other the subordinate, lived and died great.
But as also noted by other commentators, Awolowo did not have the honuur of a president at his funeral.
But all Nigeria's past rulers (except Babangida) plus the present leaders were in Ibadan to fly Ige's by fery.
The effusive outpour of emotion, postmortem solidarity, love, eulogy and adulation of Chief Bola Ige are faithful testimonies of how great Nigerians and indeed Africans valued the late justice minister and Attorney General of the Federation. Indeed, credited with sundry political feats, one of such recent ones being the crafting of the constitution of all the three existing political parties (PDP, APP, and AD) only goes to confirm the fertility of his brain, thoughts and inclinations.
According to governor Lam Adesina of oyo state, even the name Alliance for Democracy was given by "Uncle Bola right in his bedroom".
The degree and depth of love Ige enjoyed even at death draws from the fact that the entire nation came weeping and desirous of another encounter with the acclaimed Cicero of our time and clime.
Even his political opponents rushed to the Ibadan residence of the junior sage(Awo being the senior sage) as if to say, "oh dear, we disagreed, politically, but we agreed thou art great".
Indeed, he was great, for a period of 19 days that his body remained in hard and cold stretchers in the morgue, Ige's home literally became a national temple, where every soul in the land came to worship, as Ige's spirit presided like a priest.
The avalanche of sympathisers- stretching from the extremes of ideological schools - only butress the love the nation had for the slain minister.
As said in an earlier writing, it is most probably that Ige may not have enjoyed this degree of sympathy and love if he died a natural death. But the fact that he was brazenly murdered, supposedly because of what he believed in, said or did, while in office, there is an understandable volume of affection.
And the signs of greatness are a legion: Whoever heard of President Obasanjo crying in public? Not even the tortuous deals of the late General Sani Abacha against Obasanjo drew the tears of Mr. President.
But at Ige's residence, he shed "presidential" tears. It was a ditto experience for Alhaji Atiku Abubakar, the Vice President, whose eyes also released some hot tear drops. It was neither politics nor sheer playing to the gallery. And from the array of serving and former ministers, past and incumbent governors, senators, other lawmakers, the state legislators, members of the Nigerian Bar Association, the Justice Ministry, the Judiciary, Bank chiefs, chambers of commerce, students and youth leaders, the academia, Diplomatic Corp, religious leaders even including the AMORC fronternity, market women etc, it was clear that a fiercely loud nationalist has left the national stage.
But one other point drawable from the reaction of Nigerians is the uncommon wedge which Ige's death forced on the political class. The national Chairmen of the three political parties came together for Bola Ige. The three not only came, but they agreed to fashion out a code of conduct that will guide future politicking in Nigeria. Indeed, like Chief Audu Ogbeh, the PDP national chairman explained, the three political parties are in contest not conflict. Therefore if Ige's death will bring peace, order and decorum into the rancorous rank of the political class, then the death is not in vain.
Yet another mark of rare greatness is seen in the attention that the Federal Government gave the rites. Besides the personal visits of the President, Vice President and the entire echelon of the executive arm of government, the federal government organised two special sessions: an interdenominational/commendation farewell service and a special federal executive session, all in honour of the late Ige.
But more than that, the federal government on the eve of the final funeral announced the provision of two aircraft to fly willing government officials and interested members of the public from Abuja to Ibadan. It was a rare honour.
The President was to further cap all of that with his presence at the funeral ceremony wherein in his funeral orations he spoke glowingly of the late Ige, employing strictly saintly and superlative words in dressing the late politician's portrait. It was one last way or stressing that Ige was "our brother and my friend". But it was not only the Federal Government that behoved, so humbly, to the personality of Ige. The various state governments were keenly involved. And this has nothing to do with political party solidarity. Infact, the non-AD controlled state governments were greatly saddened by the loss also avidly involved in mourning the late Ige. For instance, Edo and Delta States (PDP controlled) nominated two members each into the national burial committee of the late politician. And ditto Kogi and Kwara states (both controlled by APP). What's more, the Ebonyi State government, last Wednesday wrote and sponsored a network commentary on the life, times and nobility of the late Bola Ige it was erred on network service of Radio Nigeria. And for Benue state government, a people that late Ige was said to have fought for (in legal defence) for a long time, came to Ibadan not with silver and gold but with their own natural resources. Governor George Akume was accompanied to the home of the late Ige (in Ibadan) with a truck load of oranges (40 big bags) and yam (a thousand tubers).
No doubt, the gift of such factory-needed quantity of oranges was remarkable. Sympathisers, journalists and the police men will all bear witness.
Ige was a man for whom the bells tolled. Those in doubt, should ask Lagosians, who on Thursday, January 10, went to the National Stadium to bid the great politician farewell. The newspaper head lines, the following day were succinctly depictive: "Lagos stands still for Ige, "Ige shuts Down Lagos" etc.
The day after, it was the turn of Ibadan, Oshogbo, Ikirun, Ilesha and finally Esa-oke. In all the places, people - the weak and the strong-trooped out in large numbers, some on roof tops, others atop pedestrian bridges or even hills, or simply on the road side, just to do two things, see the last of Ige and then bid him "sun re-o! sleep well.
And for the various media houses, in the country and some from abroad, non went on air or to bed without hearing the latest from Bodija.
Most newspapers - even regional news like Edo-based Observer-stationed their men (reporters and photographers) at the Ige's home permanently.
Blow-by-blow developments were dutifully reported with elan and panache. For the journalists, there was a sense of self-fulfillment in ensuring that no story of a mega personality was missed. It thus engendered perpetual alertness and watchfulness.
The magazines, even those given to lucrid matters celebrated the Bola Ige matrix. And for a season, the Igemania bug, as it were, held the media hostage with in a sticky how.
Some of the newspapers, daily devoted a page or two (of colour production) to photographs of those who come to condole the Iges. And on the day of burial Friday, Tribune Newspapers, with whom Ige run his usually incisor "Uncle Bola Column," "were a black mournful mast head".
Indeed, rather than really mourn, the greatness of Ige, dried up several tears and instead of sorrowing, his funeral was more a celebration of life, as it was aptly described.
As severally described, Ige was a man of many parts, as he drew mournful celebrants from the art world, from the political class, from the legal clan, from Christian and Muslim worlds, from the diplomatic circle from the youths and the aged, from the elite and the lonely, from the rich and the poor, and his admirers and his critics. They all agreed that, by their several comments that a collosus has left the planeth earth. But while the ululation of his greatness will continue to echo after he has gone, the question yet unanswered will be; who killed Bola Ige?. Mr Musiliu Smith, the nation wants your answer.
An answer that will be significantly different from the silence and confusion that followed the murders of Pa Alfred Rewane (for whom the police also regalled some hawkish prisoners as being responsible, but we'hve been a lot wiser) or that of Suliat Adedeji, Kudirat Abiola, Dele Giwa, MKO Abiola etc etc.
Again, let the police disappoint the swelling clan of its skeptics.

Original_Search_Title: Mile 12 Taers Get Relief From Robbers
Headline: Nigeria: Mile 12 Taers Get Relief From Robbers
Body_Text:
Lagos — Traders and buyers at the Mile 12 market in Kosofe Local Government,
who in the past were target of criminals who used to lurk in some
corners of the market waiting for an opportunity to strike, now
conduct their businesses with ease.
In the past 15 months, the market has become less attractive to
criminals.
Reported cases of stealing and robbery which used to be a daily
occurrence in the market has drastically reduced. No small thanks to
the team of a joint military patrol which has been permanently
stationed opposite the market.
Though the primary aim of drafting the soldiers there was to prevent
religious and ethnic violence which at the time was imminent, even
when threat of the clash disappeared, the soldiers are still there
doing the traders and buyers in the market a great service.
Metro Business investigation revealed that before the coming of the
military team, robbers had literally annexed the market, making
shopping for buyers and selling for traders a nightmarish experience,
especially in the early hours of the day and late after the close of
businesse when proceeds of sales were usually snatched from traders.
Market women who spoke to Metro Business said the presence of the
soldiers in the market is a complete blessing to them. According to
them, unlike in the past when they hurriedly close shops for fear of
being attacked by criminals, they now take their time and close
without any fear.
One of the women, Iya Ronke, who deals in canned tomatoes and
vegetable oil, claimed that she has been a victim of attacks from
robbers and that the coming of the soldiers has driven the criminals
far. "The soldiers now act like market monitors. Once they suspect
anybody, they quickly interrogate the person. In fact, they usually
punish people who misbehave in the market and this has helped to
maintain peace," she said.
A police post opposite the market was not enough to deter criminals.
A trader who did not give his name explained that those days when the
soldiers had not come, even when a thief was being pursued, the
policemen in the post would turn their eyes the other way, pretending
that they did not see the thief. "But it is not like that with the
soldiers. A thief dare not run across the market. The soldiers will
go after him even when they are not contacted.
When Metro Business sought to know from some traders what their
response will be like if the team is withdrawn, one of them, a yam
dealer, Bala, said they will organise a protest to get the authority
reverse the decision.
The soldiers, it was gathered, have at various times arrested thieves
in the market and handed them over to the police. Just recently, it
was learnt that about seven armed robbers who had invaded the market
were shot dead by the soldiers.
Mile 12 market has been a target of criminals due to the movement of
huge amount of cash by traders and buyers alike.

Original_Search_Title: Abuja Farmers Lament Poor Harvest
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja Farmers Lament Poor Harvest
Body_Text:
Farmers in Abuja have recorded poor harvest this farming season following the deteriorating fertility of soil, an astute farmer and village head of Piwoyi, an Abuja suburb, chief John Gatta, has said.
The village head expressed his dismay to Abuja Trust at the weekend over the non-availability of fertilizers to boost farm produce, adding that settlers constantly invade most parts of the Federal Capital Territory. This, he said, also contribute to loss of soil fertility.
Speaking on behalf of other farmers in his domain, the chief stated that the sizes of farmlands in the FCT are large enough to produce enough food for FCT and beyond, but soil infertility and scarce fertilizer have made the situation difficult.
"Our land is fast deteriorating in quality. We, the farmers in Abuja earnestly need fertilizer to boost production Can you imagine that a village like Piwoyi, with a population of about 10,000 people, 70 per cent of whom are farmers are provided with only five bags of fertilizers? How do we cope with this?" the chief questioned.
The same thing happens in other farming communities within the FCT. By this distribution, those farmers who are lucky to have a share of the fertilizer could not have more than one mudu while majority are left without any share," the chief reiterated.
He further stated that the farmers were ready to produce food to satisfy the need of Nigerians but they need government's assistance in the supply of fertilizers to boost their productivity.
Said the chief: "There is a pest we call utanta which if a farmer plants yam or guinea corn is capable of destroying them. This is another problem the farmers face in Abuja here."
The chief, who lamented over the small sizes and quantity of yam harvest, appealed to FCT authorities to come to their aid by making fertilizer available to prevent similar situation by next farming season.

Original_Search_Title: Mile 12 Market - Charming Nucleus of Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Mile 12 Market - Charming Nucleus of Lagos
Body_Text:
Lagos — There are famous markets throughout Nigeria, and the popular Mile 12
market in Lagos is one of such.
Mile 12 market is to Lagos what blood is to the human body. It is the
vital essence without which many Lagosians would probably die of
hunger.
This alone Daily Trust gathered, compelled Senator Ahmed Bola Tinubu,
the Executive Governor of Lagos State to quickly yield to pressure by
reversing his order to close down the market for reason of
obstructing traffic flow.
"Many families in this city make their bulk purchases here," declared
a proud Mallam Isa Dangana, a yam dealer in the market. There is no
food item they need that they can't get here. These include yams,
beans, rice, fresh okra and tomatoes, and cocoyams. Mallam Dangana is
not exaggerating. Mile 12 market is a food depot of sort, attracting
traders and farmers from all parts of the country and beyond
patronize it. They converge on the market daily, with lorry loads of
foodstuffs selling them off, and returning to the hinterland for
more.
When Daily Trust visited the market recently, the first thing that
struck this reporter apart from the sprawling expanse of stalls and
lock-up sheds on the outskirts of Lagos, is the noise. It is like a
physical thing: deep, throbbing and threatening. It is only when one
has survived this initial assault that one begins to notice the other
things which taken together, give this big and pulsating market on
the edge of Lagos, a charm of its own.
Here for instance, is a heap of yams looming straight into the
evening sky, immediately bringing to memory the now extinct groundnut
pyramids, of Kano.
Then a little to the left are baskets of ripe red tomatoes,
stretching as far as the eye can see. They look so inviting, so
delicious that one feels like picking up one and putting it in his
mouth.
Mallam Jubril Magaji from Katsina State, chairman of Shukura Yam
Sellers Association of Nigeria at Mile 12 market, told Daily Trust
how the market came into existence. "It was in 1977 during the
military regime of President Olusegun Obasanjo," recalled Magaji. "We
were at Iddo market at the time, and the country was preparing for
Festival of Arts and Culture (FESTAC). The government said we were
holding up traffic, and that we also constitute a health hazard. It
was then that they decided to move us here." It was only a matter of
time before the new market grew into a bustling foodstuff depot. As
though attracted by an invisible magnet, traders from far places like
Sokoto, Maiduguri, Opobo, Otukpo and Onitsha, began to converge on
Mile 12. Its popularity soon spread beyond the country to Cameroun,
Ghana and Niger. They too came, bringing with them the products of
their distant climes.
Within a short while, some of these strangers settled around the
market, renting accommodation, learning the language, and adopting
the culture of their hosts.
Daily life in the market is one complex, and sometimes, confusing
network of changing roles. One moment a man introduces himself as a
rice dealer, and the next, he is at another shed manipulating a
sewing machine. In another instance, he grabs hold of a wheelbarrow,
and is bargaining with a merchant who wants his sack of gari to be
conveyed to the garage.
A man may be a tailor by training, and still buy and sell pepper and
tomatoes on the other side. You see, everybody is basically a
trader," explained one trader.
One secret of Mile 12 market's success and popularity is its
competitive prices. Most foodstuff are cheaper there than those other
places. Mrs.
Bunmi Adeoye, a seamstress and mother of four whom Daily Trust
encountered bargaining for a bag of rice, explained that the Mile 12
market is the best thing that ever happened to the Lagos housewife.
"I don't know how my family could have survived these hard times
without this market. I buy all the foodstuff I need here in bulk and
at a good bargain price." She paused and lifted a big yam our of her
shopping basket: "Could you believe I bought this yam for N40?"
Another advantage which Mile 12 has is its location near a high way
which runs straight into the heart of Lagos.
"Transport is no problem," explained Mrs. Adeoye who lives in
downtown Surulere. "I just take a straight bus from Ojuelegba, and I
am here in a matter of minutes." There are other aspects of the
market, which do not yield themselves to the casual visitor. For
instance, there are many gambling joints in the heart of the market.
This reporter encountered one of them by chance, where he retired to
a "buka" for a meal. There is a dark corner, busy at a game of cards
were four or five young men. Money changed hands regularly as the
cards fluttered to the floor.
"This is not merely a part-time activity the buka proprietor informed
Daily Trust. "These men are professional gamblers and fortunes are
lost and made here." Women of easy virtues are also gradually
registering their presence in the market. Already four or five
brothels have sprung up in the heart of the market, and in the
evening, these are a favourite watering hole for the young and hot
blooded among the traders. A nearby cinema hall, featuring mainly
Indian films, completes the picture.
Still, Mile 12 is essentially a traders market. It is big, noisy and
quite alive.

Original_Search_Title: Modernisation of Agriculture in the North
Headline: Nigeria: Modernisation of Agriculture in the North
Body_Text:
In every state of the North, there are rivers and lakes that have not been utilized for effective farming. At this stage in our development, it is necessary to open up these rivers and lakes and channel their flow for irrigation purposes, in support of effective agriculture, especially during the dry season. Then the veterinarian should be deployed to improve animal breeding and other similar activities, as in developed and progressive countries.
Many wealthy countries at this time have attained their industrial success not from oil wealth alone but from their agricultural success. As a minor example, if we pay attention to the rapid increase in our population and the immigration into Nigeria by the people of our neighbouring countries, our need for improved agriculture and food supply would be better understood and taken care of. Another problem is the fact that many of the able-bodied men who are children of farmers and have been brought up in farming environments have left their villages for the cities and towns in search of their own share of the naira. They have left their old parents on the farms to take care of the farming business. Whatever the old people can produce is what they use for their feeding; what is left over is what they bring to the cities and towns for sale. It is therefore imperative that our leaders mobilise fully to attain modern agricultural process and food sufficiency for our people.
Mechanised farming
This is a very important way to improve agriculture. For example, one caterpillar (tantan) can do the cultivation of about 5,000 farmers. The engine used for seed removal can in one day remove gero, guinea corn, beans, groundnuts, and rice or wheat up to 500 bags.
If many ideas are not taken seriously, it may be possible at any time under the present conditions to see an able-bodied man relying completely on load carriage or other menial jobs just to survive. The money he earns in that way will not be enough for a measure of maize or a tuber of yam. In such condition which may be nationwide, all kinds of difficulties and conflicts may occur leading the country to instability.
The petrol money on which all attention has been put to the point of forgetting the need for proper development of agriculture sooner or later become exhaustible. Even if one tin of petrol can be sold for a hundred dollar, the Nigerian naira may not appreciate. It will depreciate as it is doing now until we go back to emphasize agriculture which will enable Nigerian industrialists to have all the raw materials they need. It will not be necessary to import them from abroad. Therefore, the Nigerian naira will stop fluctuating and start rising steadily in the world market.
My suggestion in this article is that Northern Nigeria should promote the adoption of modern techniques of farming and other agricultural activities by the farmers; not by the compulsory acquisition of farms from their owners. That is not the issue and it will be wrong to do so.
The late former governor of Kano State, Alhaji Audu Bako gave a good example and he was hundred per cent successful. He purchased from abroad all kinds of modern machines and farming equipment, cows and other animals necessary for farmers. Even though the government has a lot of farmlands, he offered to give all private farmers who needed such equipment and animals for temporary use on loan for a reasonable price. As a result of this policy, many took the advantage to have the equipment and animals for use in their farms. They were later able to buy their own equipment for their farming activities.
Alhaji Audu Bako also made available sufficient fertilizer and insecticide. Farm technicians in good number went round the villages, giving farmers advice and veterinarians went round all the herds, taking care of animal health. Even animal breeders were helped to cross-fertilize with eggs or species from abroad.
All these were not for profit making by the government. But the objective was to develop agriculture and increase food production for the country as well as guide the farmers in the right direction required by the government to attend sufficient food supply.
If this policy is imbibed nationwide, the federal government could stop the issuance of licence for the import of foodstuff, clothes and shoes in Nigeria. All those who import rice and wheat should be told that there are farmlands in Kaduna, Niger, Kwara, Plateau, Benue, Adamawa and other Northern states. They should go to any of these places to cultivate whatever they want to produce.
For this purpose, it is absolutely necessary for the state governments to establish good programmes for the people for the things that will benefit them and keep the country in peace.
Salihu Balarabe wrote from Kaduna.

Original_Search_Title: Pen Pusher in America
Headline: Nigeria: Pen Pusher in America
Body_Text:
In seat 35B, I fastened my seat belt following instruction from the pilot of the Lufthansa airline, the German national carrier. The Boeing 747 was gaining momentum to depart Murtala Muhammed Airport, Lagos on a 6-hour flight to Frankfurt. Seated next to me was Promise Okekwe, head of the public affairs section of Liberty Bank, Nigeria. The two of us, including 10 others scattered in the economy class of the aircraft were billed for a five-week sojourn in the United States of America, as pioneer participants of the Azikiwe Professional Development Programme (APDF). Therefore, Frankfurt, Germany by our schedule is but a stop over.
As the big bird balanced in the skies, and safety formalities over-as amply demonstrated by the air hostesses-I reclined my seat, jet set to first storm an European airport and then Washington DC. My ever reliable wristwatch-its called Olympio-at a glance communicated time and date to me: 11:55 post meridien; September 30, 2000. If you know what I mean that is 19 days after September 11 (much ado about it?) In my shoes you couldn't wish the terrible day away not when endless questions beat about my mind. Would I, I thought, be admitted into the US after the terrorist attacks on New York and Washington, particularly when the name on my passport begins with Muhammad-a Muslim name-and Muslims, though terrorist/fundamentalist Muslims of Middle East stock, had been fingered as culprits for the attacks? Even though I know I was the farthest thing from a terrorist-ideologically and practically-a remote feeling weighed heavy that an over zealous security personnel may want to scrunitise me on the basis of my names more than was necessary. In other to switch to another frame of mind, and perhaps enjoy airborne atmosphere better-like music, like movies and moving trays of coffee and strange cuisine-I therefore decided to adopt a journalistic countenance to whatever lay ahead. I am not on test, I said to myself. It is rather going to be an acid test for the United States. How it is able to receive the likes of me without prejudice would prove whether in their desire which I share, to contain terrorism, security had become frantic and impudent.
From nine options of on-air audio music channels, I deliberately selected a jazz station from where strident horns and strings from maestros-Dizzy Gillespie, Louis Armstrong, John Coltrane, Miles Davis-wafted successively, interspersingly cooling and raising my nerves. Damn! I was in New Orleans already even though it was not on our itenary. The Azikiwe Fellows, according to the programme, would visit sites in Washington DC and Maryland and spend more time in Denton, Texas. As soon as "Night Life," the Crusaders classic twanged a last note, I became aware I was prodding myself to explain how I felt-as I was alas America bound. My first trip across the Atlantic. Instantly, I recalled that Dr Mitch Land, director Mayborn Graduate Institute of Journalism, University of North Texas, had also asked me a similar question during his visit to Kaduna to assess me. But his was: "Rabiu, how do you feel selected as an Azikiwe Fellow, to travel with 11 others on a professional training in the United States?" Pinching from Shakespeare, I remember saying "well, silence is the perfectest way to herald joy." Deep within myself I was nothing but fervent.
We arrived Frankfurt at dawn. The city was still a bit dark, and because I wasn't occupying a window seat all I could make out was distant kleighlights. Nevertheless, I had enough information which highlighted the city's features and its prime place among Europe's leading cities. Since we were connecting another flight to Washington Dulles Airport various notices guided us to a particular lounge we were supposed to wait until our flight at 1:00pm. With time then at about 6:30-7:00pm, four of us Azikiwe Fellows-Rilwan Abdulsalam (librarian, National Universities Commission), Alhaji Nasarawa (director, Kano State Library Board), Alhaji Lawal Usman (director, computer services of the National Assembly, Abuja), and myself performed ablution in the rest room after which we said a congregational prayer, led by Rilwan. Within the six hours we spent at the airport, all Azikiwe fellows fraternised among themselves and shared common hopes and aspirations.
At 1:00pm we were ready for take-off on board a United Airlines flight, an American carrier but which is also part of the Alliance group in which major western airlines collaborated to fly passengers from different regions of the world depending on their areas of strength. Flight time, according to the pilot will be 7 hours. As we cruised over the Atlantic, and every inch of our progress displayed on TV screens directly facing you, it struck me that the carrier-or United-as it is simply and legendarily called-was in its hour of grief. It's planes were the ones hijacked by terrorists in the September 11, spectre.
Consequently, many employees had had to be sacked when revenue dwindled as a result of low patronage. Indeed special funds exist to assist United staff who lost their lives on September 11, and passengers could contribute on board, if they wished.
I did many things on board United. Dozed off time and time again. Read, ate, meticulously avoiding bacon. Drank tea, water. Chatted. And principally prayed to arrive safely on American soil which we did at 5:00pm Sunday. A bus car conveyed us to the arrival terminal to go through customs and immigration. Routine security checks here were really serious, and no one it seemed was exempted. But while my colleagues passed through without much of a hindrance, it would appear my earlier apprehension was coming to pass. At the immigration counter, I was asked to go in for more questioning. A Chinese-American or so packed my papers and led me in. A Ghanaian who said he's a fashion designer was there as I got in. So was a tall Asian-either a Palestinian or Afghan, certainly from the Middle East. His wife was with him. After about 15 minutes a female immigration official who was evidently pregnant announced my name asking me to come to her desk. My problem, she told me was why my visa was issued only a day before I travelled when the trip had been planned more than a month before. After simple explanations she stamped my passport permitting me entry. "Welcome to America," she said. "Routine checks."
When I claimed my luggage Mrs Lee Mitch Land, wife of Dr Land and coordinator of the APDF programme was on hand to welcome me, help me with my luggage and led me to where others were already waiting in a van for onward journey to our hotel. My colleagues bombarded me with questions, wanting to know what happened. I told them to keep their cool, that I will explain when I can. What I wanted the most at that moment was silence so I could sit back and watch my new environment-the drive from Dulles Airport to Washington suites Hotel, Virginia. And I did just that.
On the drive which took about 40 minutes, I concentrated my viewing on the well laid out roads-freeways they call it-our equivalent of expressways; and the way Americans drive, whether, for instance what we see in movies-dare-devil driving-was a reality or not. On the contrary, I realised that Americans obey traffic laws with a passion, especially traffic lights. It is also a must to fasten seat belts otherwise you are fined. We drove carefully like that to the hotel we were booked into many days, as we were told, before our arrival. Surprisingly though the hotel, Washington Suites is not situated in Washington DC where going by our programme we were supposed to visit historical sites for some days. Virginia, which is where the hotel is, is a next door neighbour to the capital of the United States and has a lesser cost of accommodation. Having settled in suite 508, which I shared with National Assembly's Lawal Usman, we all gathered at 6:00pm for a briefing by our coordinator, Dr Mitch Land. Aside the 12 fellows were two other officials of the University of North Texas-Dr Oppong who is a professor of geography and Mr Ben Williams a graduate student and special assistant to Dr Land. Lee Ann, Dr Land's genial wife was also present.
By the itenary given to each of us, eight of the fellows-the four journalists (including me) and four of the librarians will spend 10 days doing the rounds in DC, while the four internet connectivity fellows will spend only 4 days and then move to Denton, Texas ahead of us. Our feeding allowances we were told, would be $42 dollars per day while in DC and $30 dollars when we moved to Texas. The disparity, Dr Land explained was because DC was a more expensive place where an average meal costs $15 dollars and above. However, our first place of call, the following day, October 1-Nigeria's national day-will be to the US State Department. So we were issued with metro-underground train-tickets that would last 10 days, and some four days. Dr Land's wife also presented us each with a basket containing bread, biscuits, oranges, apples and a range of assorted chocolates. Afterwards we seemed to be all exhausted and everyone retired early.
Monday, October 1st we were driven in the Washington Suites bus service to Vandorn street metro station to embark on our first official engagement. 15 of us, we were a curious sight to many who saw us because we moved in a group. Some of us looking every inch Africans, who may have arrived as tourists, and some not, and then the sprinkling of two white Americans-Ben and Dr Land. However it appeared that we basked in the curiosity we were stirring. We took a metro "Blue Line" to foggy Bottom Gwu (George Washington University) station. Dr Land led us through 23rd Street, then crossed Virginia Avenue to 2201 st. where we came upon the Department of State. We were five minutes late for our 9:30 am appointment. It didn't appear to matter as we were thoroughly checked and guided upstairs to the African section where officials were waiting to receive us. Messrs Cox and Robinson, over tea and coffee explained to us for the first time the main purpose of the Azikiwe Fellowship, which our group was pioneering.
According to Robinson, the African section of the Department of State was given 15 minutes to come up with a programme that will expose and hone key Nigerian institutions that are central to the information super structure in maintaining democratic norms after Nigeria's return to democracy. It was, he said, at the wee hours of the Clinton administration and a few days to the then outgoing president's trip to Nigeria. They faced two dilemmas: One, the institutions to incorporate. Secondly, what to name the programme. Finally, the library, information technology and journalism were considered as key elements that could form a synergy. Thus four libraries, one each from the Nigerian Institute of International Affairs, National Universities Commission, Kano State Library, and the National Assembly were selected; and four internet connectivity personnel (one each from the National Assembly, University of Port Harcourt Nunet Centre, the Presidency, and the Kaduna State Judiciary); and four journalists (yours truly from the Weekly Trust newspaper in Kaduna, Punch newspaper, Lagos, Channels Television, Lagos and the head of communications, Liberty Bank, Lagos; the latter a rather strange mix). To give a name to the programme, Robinson said they unanimously agreed on the name of Nnamdi Azikiwe, Nigeria's first president and pioneer journalist and publisher of many independence struggle tabloids. According to them, the late Chief Nnamdi Azikiwe is among a few Nigerians accepted across Nigeria's ethnic and religious divides.
At this, all of us heaved a sigh of relief, agreeing they couldn't have done better. Robinson prayed we would enjoy our visit and professionally benefit from it as "everything possible had been done to give the distinguished fellows the right kind of contact with America's democratic institutions."
Since it was October 1st, Nigeria's independence, we asked to discuss our country vis-à-vis its 41st birthday. We all variously maintained that democracy was inviolable but some of us, Emeka Madunagu of the Punch and me, the newspaper minds on the team, posited that the current leadership in Nigeria is running a charade and had be told. Our colleagues, mostly government people were not happy with our opinion saying we were washing our dirty linen outside. We countered that we also 'wash' it inside. Nevertheless, it was clear that we were actually not speaking before 'outsiders' but with 'insiders' - people who were more aware of what is happening in Nigeria than some of us probably did. Robinson and Cox indeed displayed a keen knowledge of Nigeria, past and present.
Three hours later when we got through with the Department of State, a tour of the White House area which was on our programme was cancelled due to the September 11 attacks. The area was now a no-go zone but tourists could go round the fence but not into the lawns as was previously possible. So we went to visit the Vietnam Veterans Memorial and the Korean War Veterans Memorial both located at the West Potomac Park, which is a strolling distance from the Department of State. As we stood on 17th Street, facing us from the opposite were two of America's most important monuments: The Lincoln Memorial across the long reflecting pool of water and the Washington Monument which erects boldly into the sky: Even now, back in Nigeria, I see the Washington Monument as some unshakable structure with roots that go deep down; it tells me of a plant that will be there for all time. But Lincoln Memorial is as majestic as Abraham Lincoln's sitting position: posed solidly like the king of the world - perhaps of the 'democratic world?' By the time we went through the Franklin Delano Roosevelt and Thomas Jefferson Memorials around what is called the Tidal Basin I came off with the impression that Washington is indeed a monumental city.
The following day, October 2, the library of congress turned out to be a building like no other I have yet seen. It is a bewildering building that makes you wonder how it was constructed in the firs place. As for the 'insides' it is simply ethereal. The library collected materials in nearly every field of knowledge in a variety of formats. We were told that Thomas Jefferson, a war-time US president sold his personal library for $23,950 to the library of congress in 1815 following Britain's burning of the capital (where the library was initially located). He believed that "there is no subject to which a member of congress may not have occasion to refer." The statement is said to remain the guiding principle of the library's collecting policy.
Established in 1800, the library of Congress is an unparalleled world resource. The collection include more than 28 million catalogued books and other print materials in 460 languages. Indeed, offer me a roof in America and I will choose the library of congress where in the main reading room I'll daily look up to see contributors to humanity's progress. Islam, it said, contributed physics, Egyptian civilisation. There were more but another daily companion will be the sculpture of Herodotus representing history.
Later in the afternoon we were at the Voice of America. At the Hausa section of the Africa Service, we met for the first time since our arrival, our countrymen. Sunday Dare, new head of the Hausa Service and a former editor at the News magazine in Nigeria welcomed us. There were also Aliyu Mustapha, Kabir Isa Jikamshi, Aliyu Danladi, Hadiza Isa Wada, Shehu Kaura, all of the Hausa Service. There was also Shaka Ssale, the Ugandan and presenter of Straight Talk Africa, as well as Josephine Kamara. Four of us, Rilwan, Nasarawa, Lawan and myself were interviewed on the Hausa Service, after which we met with the legendary Donna Butler, producer of many a Hausa service programme and whose name is known to listeners across Nigeria.
The following day, October 4, we were before Professor Jibril Aminu, the Nigerian Ambassador to the US at the Embassy building in central DC. Dr Land, deploying his oratorical skills to the hilt briefed Ambassador Aminu on our mission after which questions and answers followed. A group photograph followed and we were treated to a refreshment. The Ambassador promised also to be present later in the evening at the reception in our honour by the office of Representative William Jefferson, a co-sponsor of the Africa Growth and Opportunities Act (AGOA).
The reception held at the Capitol Building Cannon 121 was interesting in many respects. First, we had a chance to meet with members of the US congress and their staff who shared ideas with us. Secondly, Nigeria was discussed with promises by the congressmen to work harder, legislatively for Nigeria's nay Africa's democratic and economic turn around.
The next day our tour included a visit to the Newseum and Freedom Centre, opposite the magnificent USA today building. The Newseum is indeed one of a kind. It's a modern museum documenting news and their organs, even archivally in print, audio and visuals. It is a really captivating 'world.' Ms Joan Mower, director Africa, Latin America programmes of the Freedom Forum received us with a rather sad news that the forum had just cancelled its programmes for Africa and that she was technically out of a job herself. While discussing the press in Nigeria, which she was sufficiently familiar with, Ms Mower spoke the minds of many that newspapers in Nigeria have not been able to detach from ethnic and religious passions in their reports. She said also that not only were they too verbose but they were pretentiously "national" whereas their true competence is only regional. She declared that there are actually no national newspapers yet in Nigeria. I couldn't agree more.
Next day at the Washington Times, we, Emeka of Punch, Stella Din of Channels television and Promise Okekwe sat through an editorial meeting at the paper. After which, finally, we thought, editorial meetings are the same all over the world. The Washington Times we were told is considered a conservative paper which gives open support to Republican regimes and that it is meant to rival the Washington Post as a dominant paper in the American capital. The paper's circulation is about 70,000 daily, and most subscribers reside in the D.C area.
Same day at the National Public Radio (NPR), we spent two hours discussing radio technology, and the concept of public radio which in the case of the NPR came by congressional legislation. The NPR is a non governmental public funded station which produces programmes for sale to hundreds of frequency modulated (FM) radio stations across the American nation. Unlike Nigeria's FRCN, the NPR is truly independent and broadcasts programmes in the interest of the American public, and not the government.
The rest of our stay in D.C involved making the rounds among the cluster of museums called the Smithsonian. From the American Museum of art to the Museum of American History, to the Air and Space Museum, it was clear that the U.S is a country steeped in the culture of preservation. To be part of history myself, I stood beside the (original!) Apollo 11 space shuttle with a robot-like Neil Armstrong in the cockpit. From the earliest Wright brothers planes to the latest in civilian, military and space shuttles, America documents them at the Air and Space Museum. And they are all real.
On Wednesday, October 10, we arrived Denton, Texas on a United Airlines flight. The air trip took about three hours to the DFW (Dallas Fort Worth) airport. Dr. Joseph Oppong, the Ghanaian lecturer who had earlier left with the four internet fellows to Texas, was on hand at the airport to receive us. Within one hour we were fully settled at our hotel, the comfort Suites which is close to the UNT.
Here, called the west, the weather was more akin to Nigeria's - bright and sunny - unlike Washington in the east which was occasionally cold.
During a short briefing by Dr Land, our programme here - we journalists that is - would include workshops and lectures at the university, and that the two print journalists were to begin working at the Denton Record Chronicle, a small-sized newspaper by American standards which serves the people of Denton county. We promptly showed up at the paper on Thursday, October 11, led by Dr. Land. The paper's editor, Jim Flansby handed us over to the city editor, unarguably a veteran journalist named Mike Trimble - who now took us round introducing us to each and every staff, including marketing and advertising and other technical operators at the press.
Emeka and I were given seats with personal computers before us, programmed with our names and instructed on how operations run daily in the paper. Our work we were informed will begin in earnest on Monday, October 15.
Before then, however, we were - all 12 fellows - hosted to a reception on Friday by the Mayborne Graduate Institute of Journalism, after which we had a two-hour workshop on Front Page, a web design training.
As is traditional with the University of North Texas since its establishment 27 years ago, every 13th October is a homecoming day for former students and parents of students. So Saturday it was. Every faculty put up what is called a float, in which traditional dresses would be worn and in a convoy the university would be traversed on foot and in cars, with drums beating stridently. Azikiwe fellows in our various traditional wears (yours truly in Kaftan and a cap) rode on the journalism float.
After the rounds everyone proceeded to an open place close to the school's stadium where tents representing each faculty were put up. Here, drinks, foods, wears and souvenirs were sold. We got a chance - Emeka and I - to speak with members of the National Association for the Advancement of Coloured People (NAACP), UNT branch who talked to us as 'brothers' updating us on issues of racism and other forms of discrimination. To start with, they said, as a testimonial to the continuation of racism black music is not still played during Homecoming at UNT.
Later in the evening the UNT football (American soccer) team called - can you believe it? - the Green Eagles engaged in a match with another university. I went along with Ben Williams and his friend Brent to cheer the 'Mean Green Eagles' who were said to be a terrible side, always losing matches home and away. I told my friends, however, that Nigeria's national soccer team is called the Super Eagles, but initially Green Eagles were a strong team, perhaps the best in Africa and always win their matches. So with me there to cheer the Mean Green, I added, victory was assured. And so it happened as UNT walloped their opponents.
The following day the Pan African Students Association (PASA) of UNT honoured Azikiwe fellows with a reception in the night. The president of the association, a Nigerian called Ifeanyi, together with his colleagues cooked assorted Nigerian dishes from pounded yam and egusi to jollof rice. We also in turn donated some cash to the association to maintain their secretariat. Patron of the Association, Dr. Al Bavon, a Ghanaian assistant professor of economics commended members of PASA saying they were true representatives of Africa.
Among the several workshops and interactive sessions organised for journalists, the National Association of Black Journalists (NABJ) organised lecture, in conjunction with Model International Organisation (MIO), a student foreign affairs body, was significant. The topic, "Democracy in Nigeria" was discussed with professor Van Doepp of the political science department moderating.
The second time the NABJ held another interactive session with yours truly and Emeka Madunagu of Punch as discussants, Mr Gary Reaves a veteran TV reporter with WFAA TV - a member of the Belo Group which also owns the Denton Record Chronicle, TXCN TV, and the Dallas Morning News - moderating, the turnout was a full house. Next morning it was reported in the NT Daily, the university's daily tabloid with Emeka's picture and I, on the cover.
However, on Friday October 26, the biggest reception was held for all Azikiwe fellows at the home of the secretary of the Dallas Black Chamber of Commerce, Nigeria's Edward E. Okpa II - no doubt a successful businessman; an immigrant who has risen high in the Dallas business circle. Held beside the blue pool waters of Okpa's home in Dallas, it was at the reception we were issued with certificates where UNT president and other officials, including Dallas legislators and crème of society came together to give us a befitting American treat with a live Jazz band. It was our night, which rhymed with the perplexing neonlights of night time Dallas.
Two days later, Sunday October 28, we were to repeat a trip to Dallas, for the rodeo - a medley of cowboys, games including risky bull and horse riding competition. In fact, a day before at Fort Worth, a typical cowboys city we had gone, a few of us - led by Ben Williams and Brent - to see traditional cowboys setting, eat their kind of food, listen to their music and perhaps ride a bull - oh common, I mean an electric bull - which I did ride only for a few munites before I screamed that it should be put off. The thrill cost me $4. But it was real fun. However, the rodeo at the American Airlines centre building, an architectural masterpiece, was the ultimate human show as it was intensely competitive with podiums filled to the brim and large TV screens, hanging from top beaming every second of the thrill.
