We were given cowboy hats and interviewed on WFAA channel 8 on our impressions. Yes, I was on American TV, for the right reasons too.
The rest of the days, Emeka and I, visiting Nigerian journalists as we were called, concentrated on our work at the Denton Record Chronicle. While there we experienced three different editors. Jim Flansby resigned his appointment, as was said, from the shocks of September 11. In came Dawn Cobbs, the business editor who was immediately appointed in acting capacity. A week later she gave way to a substantive Editor, Mr. Barry Boesch who would talk to us daily on different aspects of journalism. But by far the person I learned more from was Mike Trimble, probably in his 50's who, to my mind was the engine room of the paper. Senior Reporters like Mike Zabbel and Mike Hall showed us around Denton and how they gather their own news - at the courts, the Commissioner's Court, the University of North Texas, the Texas Women's University and a host of other places.
A stone throw from the DRC office would be found one man I shall always remember. I usually take my lunch in his restaurant - Steve's BBQ. Steve Logan, a 55-year-old African/ American whose family has lived in Denton for 150 years can be described as a griot. Virtually everyone knows him in the city. According to him, his Dad was the first African American to own a business on the Denton Square, no small pride I gathered. We became friends. He would ask me questions about Africans - but his main worry was why "we" sold them out into slavery. A lover of hunting who pasted pictures of animals on his walls, one day, together with other African American friends/customers of his pointed at a picture of a wild animal asking, "Rabiu, what's that?". When I couldn't make out what it was, Steve shouted "That's a Wildebeest. Ha ha Rabiu, you aren't an African, you're a New Yorker. Go back to Manhattan". I couldn't help but laugh out loudly myself. I said, "Do you think every African lives in a forest with wild animals and which he must know"? He wouldn't even listen to me. The others kept on laughing as well, saying "This guy's a New Yorker, men."
On November 3, my state of mind was split. In one direction I was eager to get back home to Kaduna, Nigeria and to my place of work - where as I had started feeling - I have gathered in the US, more to offer to Weekly Trust. On the other hand I was going to kiss goodbye to a functional society I had started getting used to. Moreso, North Texas is a marvellous place for studying. Mike Trimble, a good teacher. Mitch land, Mr Efficiency. Ben Williams, a true friend. James Steve Logan, ever present.
November 4 when we arrived Lagos, I was indeed dozing and imagining myself under the marbled dome of the Library of Congress. The book I held reading - Song of Solomon by Toni Morrison - which I had bought at the Union Station in D.C. for the princely sum of $14, slipped from my grip. "You're welcome to Murtala Muhammed Airport, Lagos, said the German accent". Back home!

Original_Search_Title: Go Placidly Amid the Noise, Haste
Headline: Kenya: Go Placidly Amid the Noise, Haste
Body_Text:
Tomorrow, President Moi begins a year of letting go, of ceding control, of losing power, of saying goodbye. On January 5, 2003 - or earlier - he will become peerless as Kenya's retired President. Nothing could be half as difficult for anyone - because the presidency is a superlative institution.
Kenya's first President, Jomo Kenyatta, was fortunate in many respects because he died in office. His body left State House in an expensive casket under military escort and was then swept into a mausoleum by a national flood of tears.
For a long time after his death, he remained something of a cult figure. The corruption, dictatorship, murder, repression and theft that occurred during his reign remained taboo subjects for close to two decades.
As Kenya bids President Moi farewell and welcomes Citizen Moi, it will also be making a clean break with 24 years of Nyayoism - an era replete with failure, decline, conflict, mismanagement, politics and abuse. Since President Moi will be alive and politically active - as he has indicated he intends to be - there will be no national inclination to be nice or polite to him about his perceived failures and misdeeds.
For quite a while, Citizen Moi will be looking at all his triumphs and achievements sinking into a pool of venomous criticism. The failings of the Moi years will be more obvious than his successes because the novelty of criticising a living ex-President will make us heady for a few years.
Criticism and condemnation will not, however, be Citizen Moi's greatest trouble - he has a thick enough skin. Neither will money. Rumour has it that he is one of the wealthiest leaders on the continent. His money may help him adjust rather quickly to life outside State House if he chooses to establish a presidential library or a Moi Fund for Peace.
That way, he may just create a culture for ex-presidents. And Kenya sorely needs one, considering that in another 10 years, it shall have a young ex-president, and another one in the following period. In a space of 20 years we may have gathered enough ex-presidents to form a club.
The biggest problem the President faces is to break the habit of power. After 24 years as President and 12 as Vice-President, one can never rule out an addiction to power. Ordering people around, giving unsolicited advice, prying into Government affairs as though he is an insider, imagining that everyone should know who he is. These things will be his ruin.
Should President Moi insist on staying in the public limelight after this year, as he has indicated he shall, he will be surprised that people will not always be on their best behaviour when they see him.
He will come into the world expecting that we shall be congregating at village centres to listen to him make empty statements of a general nature. He will be shocked that the only sycophants who will be listening to him will be the layabouts he can buy tea and mandazi at local kiosks. He will surprise himself a great deal when his statements are hardly reported in the media.
He will be calling Mr Amos Wako, whom he appointed and who has security of tenure, to ask about why the youths who disrupted his Kanu rally have not been prosecuted and will be told: "Bring me the evidence."
He will call Dr Sally Kosgei, whom he appointed to head the Civil Service, just to get an inside line on why Mr Paul Muite has been appointed Chief Justice - and she will tell him it is a Government secret. The cheek of it!
Worse, people will no longer be sitting at his hearth, waiting for pearls of wisdom to fall off his tongue. After years of having a view on everything - from missile defence systems to affirmative action - Citizen Moi will have to learn that Kenyans will not be looking up to him for guidance.
When these things happen, Citizen Moi might become querulous and choleric. He may start insulting people openly. Then we shall all say: "See, he has become senile."
The President has signalled that he intends to stay on as Kanu boss. He may also choose to vie for the post of MP for Baringo Central. He may even be appointed Minister for Internal Security. But he will be hearing about the newest Cabinet reshuffle, like the rest of us, on the 1 pm lunch time radio news. He will join the rest of us in assuming the worst about the Government's motives and competence.
Should he consider being an overly powerful party boss, even when he gives Kanu an unbeatable election machine, he must learn to stay in the background. Being party boss is not the same as being President. Citizen Moi will no longer hold the knife and the yam. The sycophancy that has attended his rule will shatter into a thousand shards of indifference.
As party boss, he will have to learn not to cross the President. The new Head of State will doubtless want to prove his or her independence. Should Citizen Moi want to compete for the limelight with the President, the prospect of his turning from a hero of the nation to an enemy of the people becomes very feasible.
These visions must have occurred to the President in the past. But in this his final year, they will intensify. He may become erratic and even unpredictable. The President may suffer some very severe withdrawal symptoms.
The country must help him to understand that since he is the first person to leave State House alive, there are indignities he must suffer which he would not have thought possible. To spare himself all this pain, Citizen Moi is going to have to learn the peace that comes from quietude and modesty.
Then there is the problem of his forthcoming idleness. President Moi has a lot of energy that must be channelled into positive activity. There are several openings: like splitting the Sudan into two countries, installing a government in Somalia and teaching "CGV 102 Politics in Africa" at the University of Nairobi.
Unpleasant as the thought of going away is, frightening as the possibility of not being in the thick of political action may be, President Moi has little choice but to start making psychological adjustments to that approaching reality. Then, and only then, shall the country and the world begin to look at him as a nationalist and a statesman.
So, as he begins this difficult year of passing on the baton, there is solace for him in the words of the anonymous monk who wrote the poem Desiderata: "Go placidly amid the noise and haste, and know what peace there may be in silence-"
Writer's email: kwamchetsi@email.com

Original_Search_Title: Just Another Day in Paradise When All is Said And Done
Headline: Zambia: Just Another Day in Paradise When All is Said And Done
Body_Text:
Lusaka — Q: Very funny. What's it this week?
A: The New Deal's first year school report of course. It is almost one year to the day since the then Chief Justice swore in our third Republican President, against a backdrop of controversy regarding the conduct of elections, which showed Levy Mwanawasa beating Anderson Mazoka by only 2 percent of the vote. In fact we could start with that.
Q: With what?
A: Mathematics. The New Deal is quite creative in this subject but must be neater and show its workings more clearly. Election results from constituencies that show no spoiled papers are not credible - and failure to produce the vote counts for individual polling stations is sloppy. Must try harder in future. C minus for effort, D for achievement.
Q: But it's the Electoral Commission that was responsible for any shortcomings in the elections, surely?
A: Yes - but the Government has chosen to accept the result (conveniently for it) and is thus aligning itself with the Commission. It is legitimate therefore for us to include the controversy over elections in its report card. What next?
Q: How about English?
A: Has done rather well. They are mostly lawyers, after all, and they can even spout off in Latin. The pronunciation can be a little off but it is nowhere as bad as the Old Deal - which had the President talking about Pohverty all the time and generally speaking in a pseudo-American accent. Let's give them B plus on both accounts.
Q: Science?
A: Must you ask us about that?
Q: Yes. Science.
A: The New Deal has been very innovative in the laboratory this year, particularly where the cultivation and consumption of maize is concerned. First, the student went against the accepted wisdom and attempted to grow the crop under irrigation during the winter months. This had mixed results, with some 5,400 tonnes (two days consumption for Zambia) being produced in the Zambezi valley, where the temperature is sufficiently high in winter.
Secondly, with considerable chutzpah for a mere first year student, the New Deal decided to lecture the world on the safety of genetically modified organisms, virtually closing down the relief programme in the process. We have written about this amazing political phenomenon on two occasions recently, so we have no need to go on about it here. On balance we would say A for enthusiasm and effort, but somewhere around D for achievement.
Q: Geography?
A: Not bad at all. The President has travelled so much this year that Magellan must be turning in his grave with jealousy. The Minister of Finance has also been off to see the world. And then there was the New Deal scientific team that took its time roaming the globe to determine the safety of GM maize. They would have gone to Antarctica if anyone had claimed the existence of a genetically modified penguin. B and B for sheer first hand knowledge of the world we live in, we think.
Q: History?
C for effort, E for achievement. We have never had a government with such a weak grasp of the facts of history. The way it talks you would think that the One Party Era was a golden age. The truth is that many of the things being alleged are political myths. Myth: People were well off before the MMD came to power (fact: they voted MMD because they were sick of the poverty wrought by UNIP government). Myth: Chiluba's government introduced high level corruption to Zambia (fact: the 1991 election campaign was based largely on the perception that wealth was disappearing through corruption - remember Coppergate and KK's $38 million dollar second hand plane). Myth: MMD killed the copper mining industry (fact: Kaunda's government had already reduced annual copper production to half the level prevailing at the time of nationalisation - Chiluba's rule just reduced it further). Myth: Under Chiluba there was no agricultural policy and the sector was run down (fact: agriculture is the only sector that has grown significantly in the last decade - there have been large increases in the outputs of cotton, tobacco, flowers, vegetables, sugar, coffee and sweet potatoes).
Q: What about maize?
A: The problem with maize is that it has remained highly political - being in effect a Presidential responsibility. It is a 'symbolic' commodity, used to express all kinds of feelings about the traditional versus the modern, the State controlled versus the private, the urban versus the rural etc. It needs to be handled in the same way as any other seasonal commodity, and government has to make up its mind what it is going to do about the great 'Inputs' soap opera. It was not MMD who brought this problem - it inherited it but can be blamed for doing nothing about it. In 1991 both of us campaigned in the rural areas on the basis of inputs for the 1990/1991 season having been late and inadequate, and on the basis of farmers not having been paid for the 1990 crop yet. The New Deal needs to stop fantasising that there is a successful economic model to be found in Zambia's past. This country's GDP has not moved for 25 years, even as the population has doubled, and 15 of those years were under 'Humanism'.
Q: Nature Studies?
The New Deal needs to decide whether it wants to run the tourism industry - part of which is the trophy hunting industry - in a businesslike way, so that it can grow and have a significant impact upon the livelihoods of Zambians. At the moment the 'businesslike' tendency is in danger of being subordinated to the tendency towards trying to put control into the hands of Zambians - including ministers and people who did not even bother to tender for hunting concessions. ZAWA - which in its heart of hearts probably represents the businesslike tendency - is being frustrated on all sides by people who think they know better. At least the New Deal pushed Levison Mumba to the sidelines when he tried to do something overtly illegal by jumping over the hoops of the tender process earlier this year. So we'll give them a D for effort instead of E. However, in terms of achievement the E must stand - we have the most pathetic tourism industry in the region, outside of war-torn neighbours, and it is getting worse by the minute.
Q: Social Studies?
A: This year the student has chosen to take the course option entitled 'dealing with corruption retrospectively'. The New Deal has launched investigations and prosecutions against some prominent Chiluba intimates - but has been legally inhibited from going for the man himself. While we have some awkward questions we will reserve these for another time and just say: Well tried chaps! B for effort. Unfortunately, until we see a little more action beyond the confiscation of mechanical adult toys bought with Zamtrop money we can only do a C for achievement. But keep trying!
Q: PE?
A: The New Deal can talk - that much is for sure. It can say 'Up' and 'Down' as many times as you would wish. Or 'left' and 'right'. Or 'Hup, one, two, three'. It can talk the talk but it cannot walk the walk - let alone run the run, jump the jump or press the press-up. It needs to realise that it will be judged - by the electorate and by the historians - for what it actually achieves and not for its promises and claims. We think a double D - D for effort and a D for achievement would be pretty fair.
Q: Your summary of the year?
A: In some respects interesting, in some disappointing. All-in-all, just another year in Paradise, we would say.
Q: Dipak's and Guy's New Year resolutions?
A: To keep watching the New Deal like hawks and telling it like we see it in our popular column in The Post.

Original_Search_Title: Zvakavapano Rules the Roost
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zvakavapano Rules the Roost
Body_Text:
Harare — FUNGISAYI Zvakavapano is ruling the roost at Ngaavongwe Records with her latest eight-track album Makomborero featuring such songs as the title track, Moyo Wangu, Svitsa Tsvene I and II, Ndomutevera, Nguva Yekutenda and Garai Neni (hymn and instrumental).
At Ruffcuts Entertainment is South African-based Zimbabwean Chamu Kwenda a.k.a. Mizchif with his third album titled Element of Surprise consisting of the title track, Breadwinner, Black Capture, Suspended in My Air Space, Life Goes On, Confidence, Only You and Common Denominator.
Mufudzi Wangu 2nd Edition recorded at DAT Studios is latest offering with a reggae flavour by Ivy Kombo-Moyo and Jackie Madondo with cuts like the title track, Verily Verily, As Moses, the popular Utiziro, Ndava Kuchata, Chief Cornerstone, Be Thou My Vision and Before I Sleep.
Kelvin Mahosvo and the ZRP Band are doing well at Record and Tape Promotions with Denderedzwa Rehupenyu recorded at Shed Studios.
The seven-track offering has songs like Misodzi, Vasiyei, Usanyepa, Ndaivabvunza, Handikanganwe, Pasi Rinodya and Kunyepa, an instrumental.
Shame and Nathan of the Aripo/Ndofara single have this time released an album at Gramma Records titled My Heart (Moyo Wangu) featuring Taidanana, Ndainge Ndinyerere, the popular Mafaro Chete, Ndauya Naye and Baba Nditorei.

Original_Search_Title: Understanding the American Mindset
Headline: Nigeria: Understanding the American Mindset
Body_Text:
Wisdom is contained in books. Those who wish to cultivate their intellect and have a very sound mind ought to read always and widely. This is the advice my late friend, Kenule Boeson Saro-Wiwa, a great lover of books himself, used to give to anyone with whom he came in touch, especially if you were a journalist.
"Your profession is an honourable one, and it demands knowledge, courage and curiosity. The pen is mightier than the sword but the pen that wants to be taken seriously has to be well-informed, to have a sense of history and to be of impeccable character".
That again was another of Mr. Saro-Wiwa's advice to journalists.
Even before I got to know Mr. Saro-Wiwa, I used to love books and read them a great deal. What the counsel from Ken did for me was to merely reinforce my love for books.
In pursuit of this first great love, I have just devoured a 789-page tome entitled President Kenney: Profile of Power by Richard Reeves, a most delightful and unputdownable book.
President Kennedy is a brilliant attempt to reconstruct the 1000 days John F. Kennedy, the 35th president of the United States presided over the affairs of that country.
A particular book may minister different things to each reader. To me, this book is a window unto the mind of the American society. It tells me very clearly that Americans are deeply tormented by fear, an acute fear of being thought weak.
Americans are so consumed by their inflated opinion of their place in the world that they are constantly gnawed by fear. Like Okonkwo in Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart, the whole life of the average American is ruled by the fear of his country not being thought as powerful enough.
Americans have been brought up to believe that theirs is the most powerful country of the modern era, the leader and defender of the "Free World", the policeman of the universe.
This kind of thought is deeply buried in the psyche of every American. It is clear to me that every American leader is aware of this psychological make-up of his average citizen and plays on this fear to achieve his political goal.
I had a great laugh when I read of President Kennedy telling his Director of Budget that it was inappropriate to use words like "aid" and "assistance". Better to use "International Security" instead of "aid" and "assistance", "International Security" instead of International Development".
"Strengthen the security of the Free World" was a phrase Kennedy liked because Americans did not like the idea of foreign aid, but were willing to pay for almost anything that sounded like "anti-communism".
What is this column up to today? Is it out to review a profile of Kennedy's enduringly fascinating days in office? Our aim today is to help us understand why President George W. Bush and his fellow hawks in his administration are so bent on attacking Iraq with a twin objective of effecting a "regime change" in that war-tormented country in spite of the near universal disapproval of the rest of the world.
From what Richard Reeves has shown in the book on Kennedy and from what we have known of the United States over the years, foreign wars are a great domestic votes catcher in America. Once the country is engaged in a foreign war, the voters rally round the president.
Kennedy was himself amazed by this trait in the American. Based on the urgings of some of his hawkish aides, he ordered the infamous Bay of Pigs invasion of Cuba. The invasion failed but rather than turn against their president, the Americans, strangely, rallied around him. Kennedy's rating in an opinion poll soared and he wryly remarked: "The worse you do, the better they like you".
Presidents are politicians who are aware that the voter is king. It is the voter who determines whether a politician remains relevant or not. Most American presidents therefore pander to the yearnings of voters most of who do not know the wider implications of war.
Apart from the politician's need to woo voters, arms manufacturers and arms contractors also actively promote the cause of war. Without wars they will be out of business.
There are of course, also naïve intellectuals and hawkish advisers who operate from the comfort of air conditioned offices, far removed from the jungles and other theatres of war, who draw up beautiful scenarios of how a smart bomb can take out a "recalcitrant leader" of an unfriendly country in a short and sharp operation.
Military generals some of who will never go to the war front, are another group of persons who are most vocal in calling for war. It is this group that urged Kennedy on to other disastrous Bay of Pigs invasions. It is a combination of these armchair war mongers who persuaded him to pour in men, arms and materiel into South Vietnam, a place in South East Asia that became the graveyard of America's innocence.
Kennedy was a man with a first rate mind. In as much as he was motivated by the need to act tough in order to please voters, he also knew that if he did not act with tact and confidence, his armchair cheerleaders would urge him onto a course that would bring catastrophe on humanity. He once told his close aides that "if we listen to them (naïve generals), and do what they want us to do, none of us will be alive later to tell them that they were wrong".
I have gone to great lengths to recount some of these incidents to give us an insight into why Bush's America is calling for war against the so-called "Axis of Evil". It is also to warn Mr. Bush that he may lead his Coalition Against Terrorism into an unwinable war against especially Iraq, a war that could imperil the whole of humanity.
Bush may be tormented by the fact that in the eyes of many Americans and the rest of the world, he is seen as a usurper, he did not really win the presidency fair and square. He may therefore be pushed to engage in a daring move to prove that he is a tough man and a great president.
It is unfortunate that over the years, American presidents love to play politics with the lives of their citizens and the lives of innocent citizens of other countries thousands of miles away. At some point during the Vietnam War, some callous war strategists urged Kennedy to order "human defoliation" of the innocent citizens of Vietnam and he did!
Even more callous was the fact that the president ordered the use of herbicide defoliants - commercial weed killers containing cacodylic acid and several kinds of butyl - sprayed from US planes to clear underbrush within two hundred feet of roads through ambush country.
Food denial was always employed as a weapon of war. The weed killer was used to destroy fields of rice, manioc, corn and sweet potatoes in territories held by the Vietnamese communists.
Greenery was destroyed in over 32,000 square miles of South Vietnam, almost half the country. What cruelty, what callousness.
Millions of live have been lost in the quest of the United States to dominate this past century. Even without waiting for anyone to award them the honour, Americans crowned themselves victors of that century. Remember, Henry Luce, the founder of Time magazine had predicted that the 21st century was America's and it had it.
To what extent will simple folks of other nations be made to suffer so that America can again possess the 22nd century? Has not the world borne enough agonies because of America's insatiable appetite to be the leader of the Free World?
How indeed free is the "Free World" when people are not allowed the right to live their lives free from interference by a "big brother protector"? In the past 12 years, citizens of Iraq have lived under the permanent threat of bombs, air raids and crippling economic sanctions.
It does appear that Bush and the hawks in his administration will not be dissuaded from going to war against Iraq. I think the best we can do in the circumstance is to tell him that just as Operation Desert Storm I cost his father re-election, Operation Desert Storm II will also provide a death nail on his political coffin.
For those who keep wondering why the Americans seem to like war so much, they should now understand that foreign wars are a good tool for domestic political success.

Original_Search_Title: Basic Commodities Prices Shoot
Headline: Angola: Basic Commodities Prices Shoot
Body_Text:
Benguela — Prices of basic commodities have gone up over the last few days on the informal market in the cities of Benguela and Lobito (centre), Angop learned..
This is blamed on the rise in the American dollar which is being traded at the rate of Akz 73.00.
The main markets of Benguela and Lobito have recorded a steep rise in prices of such commodities as rice, milk, sugar, cooking oil, maize flour, soap and fruits like orange, lemon, banana, onion and sweet potato.
As an example, a kilogram of maize flour, the main staple food in central Angola, is being sold at Akz 45.00, against the previous 30.00. Imported goods like rice, sugar, beans and beef have also recorded a significant hike in price.

Original_Search_Title: Spruce Dolce Vita Brings Taste Buds to Life
Headline: Zimbabwe: Spruce Dolce Vita Brings Taste Buds to Life
Body_Text:
Harare — This week the Financial Gazette introduces a new column, The sweet taste of life, which will give readers an insight into Harare's numerous eating establishments.
A taste of the sweet life, however fleeting, is a promise made to be kept as one enters the post-modern glass and steel doors of La Dolce Vita in Avondale.
Dining out in Harare is no longer a casual affair, and for those of us with ever-dwindling disposable incomes, a careful choice of restaurant must be made.
Eating out was in days gone by a weekly event. However, it is now reserved for celebrating a special occasion and one is even more concerned with satisfaction and value for money when seeking a gastronomic experience.
Judging by the ever-increasing numbers of diners and revellers, it would seem that La Dolce Vita offers both.
With a keen sense of anticipation, George and I navigated
the crowded Avondale car park, strode purposefully past a buzzing St Elmo's, and climbed the staircase up to La Dolce Vita. Co-owner Marco greeted us warmly before we ascended a spiral staircase up to the cocktail bar for an aperitif.
Master barman Saith started out at the Victoria Falls Hotel as a 19 year old and honed his cocktail-mixing skills at the Monomotapa Hotel, before joining the team at La Dolce Vita almost five years ago.
We mulled over the enticing menu of long drinks, shooters and cocktails and eyed the selection of whiskies hanging temptingly overhead. Red Label Johnny Walker Scotch Whiskey is available at $3 500 a tot, and the ever-popular J&B Whiskey at $1 800. Shooters, such as Bob Marley, start at $550, and long drinks like Malawi Shandy start at $420.
Fascinated by the exotic list of cocktails, I was initially tempted by a Cuba Libre at $910, but settled on Saith's speciality, the Mai Tai, a refreshing blend of dark and light rums and fruit juices, priced at $550.
George unerringly ordered two consecutive Zambezi beers, costing $385 each. Drinks in hand, we gazed briefly out from the open windows at the dark night sky and into the brightly-lit car park below, as busy and populous in the evening as during the day.
The chances of encountering a friend or colleague at La Dolce Vita are great, and we spent an entertaining half hour with two such acquaintances, discussing the future of the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange and our plans for the festive season, before descending into the restaurant for dinner.
George and I were escorted to our table, beautifully set with crisp linen, white china and sparkling glassware, by a relay team of ever-courteous and efficient waiters. We helped ourselves from a bounteous platter of bread sticks and crisp Italian bread while briefly scanning the starter menu.
George's generously proportioned fresh rocket salad served with parmesan shavings and nuts was declared the best ever and well worth $1 390.
I savoured every mouthful of my carpaccio (fine slivers of raw meat) served with fresh greens and parmesan shavings, perfectly dressed with olive oil and balsamic vinegar. This salad is currently priced at $1 635.
Since the char-grilled lamb chops were unavailable, George requested his second favourite dish of chicken breasts cooked in a tangy lemon sauce. This was served with well-cooked sautéed potatoes, white rice and an Italian version of ratatouille.
George enjoyed the vegetables so much that a second serving was requested and speedily served by our acquiescent waiter. At $2 420, this dish is in the lower price range of main courses.
My beef fillet served on polenta and topped with mustard and parsley sauce was beautifully presented and served with rice, potatoes and vegetables.
The quality of the beef being served in Harare restaurants at present varies from good to indifferent to unacceptable, but mine was first grade and perfectly cooked, as requested.
Since George and I are both a little concerned about our respective cholesterol counts, I will probably leave off the mustard sauce next time. At $3 150, considering increases in the price of beef, this does not seem over-priced.
Having a sweet tooth, I always look forward to choosing a dessert, and after a brief moment of indecision, I ordered a cremé brulée served with feather light tuiles.
This dessert was allegedly invented by students at Trinity College in Cambridge, who burnt the cream, providing the prototype for the delicious classic now enjoyed everywhere from Acapulco to Avondale.
Sadly, it did not come up to expectations, and I must continue my search for the perfect creme brulee in Harare. All desserts at La Dolce Vita are priced at $1 200.
La Dolce Vita is an up-market chic place to enjoy food served with style. And you don't have to dress up in your smartest gear to feel at home.
We said goodnight to our friendly host and waiters, and set off for home, pondering what further gastronomic experiences awaited us in Avondale's restaurants.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Swells By 2 Pc
Headline: Zambia: Inflation Swells By 2 Pc
Body_Text:
The monthly inflation rate has gone up by 2.0 percentage points as at end of November 2002, the Central Statistics Office (CSO) has said.
CSO director Buleti Nsemukila said in the latest inflation release yesterday that the monthly inflation rate was recorded at 3.8 per cent representing an increase of 2.0 percentage points on the October rate of 1.8 per cent.
Monthly inflation rates for the metropolitan low, high income and non-metropolitan groups were registered at 4.4, 2.9 and 4.0 per cent respectively.
According to Dr Nsemukila the annual rate of inflation stood at 25.3 per cent as at November and this rate was 1.5 percentage points higher than corresponding annual rate of 23.8 per cent at October.
Annual inflation rate for the metropolitan low, high income and non-metropolitan groups were recorded at 29.7, 21.2 and 25.6 per cent respectively.
The higher inflation levels as observed in November were largely attributed to rising food prices especially mealie meal, maize grain, cereals and cereal products, meat, fish and kapenta. Others are fresh vegetables, nuts and fruits, tubers like sweet and Irish potatoes, beans, poultry products, sugar, cooking oil and fats, milk and milk products.
Dr Nsemukila said the index for food reflected a relatively large increase of 5.7 per cent in November compared with an increase 2.5 per cent recorded the previous month.
Measured on a 12 months basis, food inflation stood at 34.4 per cent gaining 2.7 percentage points on the October rate of 31.7 per cent.
Other contributing factors to the higher November inflation levels were increases in the cost of fuel, house rentals, household goods, clothing and foot wear, cleaning materials, personal care products and services.
Comparatively, the average price of a 25kg bag of roller meal increased by 4.1 percent from K33.597 in October to K34.986 in November while the price of a 20 litre tin of maize grain increased by about eight per cent.

Original_Search_Title: Darare Releases a New Album: Hakuna Chombo
Headline: Zimbabwe: Darare Releases a New Album: Hakuna Chombo
Body_Text:
Harare — Killian Darare and the Pilgrims have recorded an eight-track album titled Hakuna Chombo (No Weapon) with Disc and Tape Studios.
The album with such songs as Ndicharumbidza Zita RaJehovah, Jesu Ndimambo, Chibvisa Kutya Kwose and Fight the Good Fight of Faith has stormed into the gospel charts.
Darare whose voice sounds like the late Brian Sibalo also has Dzidzisai Vana, the title tack, Makanaka Baba and Jesu Ndanga Ndakurasa on the album.
Another arrival to the Ngaavongwe stable is Ishmael Chaparamhosva and the Hwedza Kings with their album Chipo Changu recorded at the Shed Studios.
It's a six-track with cuts like the title track, Mwana Wangu Iyeye, Emelina, I Love Rhumba, Lovely Darling/Ruva Rangu and Samuel.
Corner Studios has a new release Kudenga Kwakanaka, an eight-track debut album by Christlike Choir from the United Methodist.
Saint Peters Choir of Mbare is riding high at Ruffcuts Entertainment with Mwari Ngoni featuring songs like Salamu, the title track, Mununuri Jesu, Mangwanani Manheru, Thumel' Moyo and Jesu Handina Simba.

Original_Search_Title: Ugandan Siamese Twins Find New Home
Headline: Ugandan Siamese Twins Find New Home
Body_Text:
Kampala — The austere stare of poverty ushers one on to the compound of Gordon Onziga, father of the Ugandan Siamese twins. Swathes of tall grass threatening to cover the path towards the homestead, line the whole length of the route. Four huts built in square design are spread out on the vacant and silent compound.
The main house does not have window shutters. Red bricks have instead been used to seal the space meant for window shutters. Behind the main house lies a lavatory, hidden in a bushy cassava garden. The pit has no walls and roof. A dry crust of human dung sits coiled on one end of its mouth.
This is the home where Christine and Louise may have to live for the greater part of their childhood and adolescence. But for now they are taking shelter at Welcome Home Children's Centre in Jinja.
Margaret Atai, their mother, is very shy and speaks timidly. She only reveals what she feels comfortable about, yet softly restrains from talking about the things that make her uneasy.
She says she was helped by a traditional birth attendant to deliver her first-born daughter Noel, 5, at her father's home in neighbouring Democratic Republic of Congo. "When I was due, the second time, my husband sent me yet again to deliver at my parent's home, which is less than 16 kilometres away from Leiko," she says.
"But this time I had difficulty in delivering, the birth attendant says three legs came out first, so my parents rushed me to Adi hospital from where I delivered the twins through caesarean section," she adds.
At Welcome Home Children's Centre, the twins Christine and Louise are playing on a colourful mat spread in the middle of the spacious living room at the orphanage. Their mother, moves up and down in an effort to restrain them from going outside to tamper with electrical gadgets.
Atai, turns on the demo of a toy keyboard which works like magic. The gentle rhythm compels the twins to crawl back hastily. Christine springs to her feet and gently swings in coherence with the rhythm. Louise equally fascinated but unable to lift herself up remains down clapping and nodding her head.
The music stops suddenly. A tinge of disappointment clouds their faces. One of us clicks a camera and the flash enchants the twins. They crawl towards us and coo ecstatically - asking for another shot. Their thrill and sincere sweet little smiles are so touching. The camera clicks yet again to their delight.
"They got used to having their pictures taken when we were in America. They love the flash of a camera," Atai says.
Back in Arua, the twins had never spent a night in their father's house. Instead, they sought refuge in the cosy comfort of De'lambience hotel in Koboko town about six kilometres away.
"We did not sleep at home because we were so scared about the safety and health of the children. People who knew, that we had just returned from America flocked to our home. Some thought we had come with a lot of things and money confronted me with their problems and needs," Onziga their father says, without a hint of resentment.
When Louise and Christine, were flown to Leiko village in Arua after a year in America, extended family members were under the impression that their father, had struck it rich abroad.
So they flocked to their homestead, which is tucked about 2 kilometres away from the main road to Koboko Township. Their continued presence in the home posed an imminent difficulty for the parents to fend for the twins. One and a half weeks later, they were flown to Jinja.
Dr. Margaret Nakakeeto, a paediatrician at Mulago hospital says that she asked Onziga to bring the twins to Jinja because she foresaw a possibility of him failing to provide the care and attention they require under such circumstances. She explains that the twins need a gradual transition from the American lifestyle to the one in their village.
"The paediatrician assigned to monitor the kids in Arua is a very busy person who has a wide area to cover. Considering that the twins are still fragile and need close attention, I figured it was better for them to return to Kampala for a year or so," Nakakeeto says.
Isaac Gubazi, the grandfather of the twins lives about 100 metres away from Onziga's homestead. A kraal that has a bony cow and calf lie on one end of his homestead.
He says, long before his birth there were twins in their lineage. However, when Christine and Louise were born, they were afraid because they did not know whether the twins would lead a normal life.
"The birth of the twins greatly changed my daily routine, instead of going to the garden I began shuttling between home and hospital," Onziga their father recounts.
"My fundraising programmes to re-roof Ngurupi Church of Uganda were grounded. I also shelved plans to go for further studies. Then our first-born-daughter, Noel bore the greatest brunt, because she had to be taken to my in-laws in Congo," he added.
Onziga is a peasant subsistence farmer who etches a living from fragmented pieces of land that amount to less than six acres. On his land he grows cassava, beans, rice, groundnuts, sweet potatoes and at times onions and cabbages.
"The twins were born at a time when I had passed an interview to join the next stage for craftsmanship at Oleba Community Technical Institute," he says.
I have plans to establish a coffee shamba to boost my annual income. As the dry season sets in, I will lay bricks for sale. With these wonder kids, I need to work doubly hard to ensure that they get the right food and the best education. I am told that the twins will stay with their mother in Jinja maybe for the next one year. During this time, I will miss them but hope to pay them regular visits.
Rev. Jackie Hodgkin, head of the twins' new found home in Jinja, says: "They are adjusting well from American to the African food. They eat like other normal kids do. I give them rice and beans, milk porridge, bananas among other fruits and foods."
Their mother says the twins relished apple sauce and baby food salads, while in America, as she strokes and kisses Louise who passionately clings on to her neck. Christine jealously crawls towards them and 'spoils' Louise's reverie. Using her mother for support, she drags her small body up and insistently grapples for a kiss too.
"This one always competes for attention," Atai remarks.
Christine appears somewhat naughty and mischievous but intriguing and exciting to watch. Louise is pleasantly compassionate but withdrawn. Her mother seems to have a softer spot for her.
"Christine disturbs me a lot. Whenever she is alone, she wanders all over the place and likes touching things," she says.
People in the village were excited to see the twins separated. They tried to carry them but the twins rejected them.
"Christine slapped and scratched whoever dared carry her. They are most comfortable around whites," Atai narrates amidst spasms of laughter and smiles.
Their behaviour is only understandable. The twins spent almost the entire first year of their lives in the US. At some point during that period, Atai longed to return home.
"We had stayed in America for over a year and I had become terribly homesick," she says, smiling and looking down as if embarrassed about something.
"I never went to school. I am just a full time housewife and my husband is a peasant farmer who dropped out in senior one. We need financial assistance to look after the twins," she says.
At this point of the interview, Christine smacks Louicse in the face as they struggle for the toy keyboard. Louise cries and Atai takes time off to calm her down.
"Sometimes after crying, Louise retaliates but they later reconcile," Atai chips in.
Hodgkin says that the twins are just like other normal twins one is always domineering, but they like playing with each other. They are sweet little kids.
They seem comfortable with the lifestyle they have found in Jinja and their mother would like them to continue living that way.
"All I want is for the kids to be educated and have a good life."

Original_Search_Title: Zambia Receives Є800m Relief Aid
Headline: Zambia Receives Є800m Relief Aid
Body_Text:
Lusaka, Zambia — Foreign donors have come to Zambia's aid by donating more than
Є800-million towards fertiliser, seed and water reticulation systems
to help avert starvation in the face of a drought currently ravaging
the country.
Part of the money will be used to buy and distribute drought
resistant crops like cassava and sweet potato to the south of the
country where the consequences of the dry weather are most severe.
Southern province deputy minister George Mpombo said the crops were
being distributed in Kazungula, Ithezi Thezi, Sinazongwe and Kalomo.
Denmark contributed 6 300 bags of fertiliser and seed packs to the
Ithezi Tezhi and Kalomo regions as part of their donation while Japan
has donated 1800 x 50kg bags of fertiliser to Kalomo, Chikankanta and
Mazabuka.
A local Livingstone based company; United Air Charter, has also
contributed 3 000 x 10kg bags of mealie-meal to help alleviate the
food crisis that has left more than 2.9 million people starving.
Meanwhile, neighbouring Malawi is reeling from the effects of its
current food crisis and if meteorologist predictions for ongoing dry
weather prove correct the country's starving masses stand little
chance of survival.
Metrological experts predict that El Nino a dry weather pattern
currently affecting southern Africa has intensified over the eastern
pacific since November locking the region in a persistent winter
weather system.
Malawi's metrological services department director Donald Kamdonyo
said El Nino was preventing much needed rainfall in the region by
blocking the main rain bearing weather systems.
Their absence, he says, has also suppressed rainfall in other
countries in the Southern African Development Community (SADC).

Original_Search_Title: Drought Resistant Crops Now Being Distributed
Headline: Zambia: Drought Resistant Crops Now Being Distributed
Body_Text:
SOUTHERN Province Minister George Mpombo has said donors and non-governmental organisations have started distributing drought resistant crops such as cassava, potatoes and other varieties in the region to guarantee food security.
Mr Mpombo said Kazungula, Itezhi-tezhi, Sinazongwe and Kalomo were the worst hit with drought and measures were being taken to mitigate the effects.
He said Germany had given the province 800 million Euros for water development and K2 billion was supposed to be raised by Government.
Mr Mpombo said some of the projects were being pioneered by Germany Technical Assistance to Zambia (GTZ), Programme Against Malnutrition (PAM), the Gwembe Tonga Project, ARA-Japanese project and the Oxfam.
He said the province had been flooded with inputs from Government and NGOs which were all aimed at beating the drought.
Drought resistant crops like cassava, sweet potatoes and some maize varieties are being encouraged .
He said the Danish embassy in Lusaka had given Government 6,300 bags of fertiliser and seed packs to be distributed in Itezhitezhi and Kalomo.
The Japanese ARA project has also given 1800 X 50kg bags of fertiliser in Kalomo, Chikanta and Mazabuka districts freely. Each family is receiving a 10kg maize seed pack, cassava cuttings and four bags of lime.
The minister said people were not interested in hand-outs but needed helped for them to produce enough food by themselves.
The United Air Charter, a Livingstone travel agent has donated 3,000 X10kg bags of mealie meal, while Crystal Ministries had also distributed free maize to Sinazongwe.
Mr Mpombo thanked the donor community for responding to the needs of the province which he also attributed to the goodwill cultivated by the province.
" The solution lies in having a good agricultural policy with the support of the people. We have to support the agricultural policy of the Government," he said.
He said the Danish ambassador was working in the outlying areas of the province to assess the extent of the hunger situation in the province.
Meanwhile, the road rehabilitation project is progressing well with the release of K175 million to Zambia National Service in Itezhi-tezi for the road rehabilitation exercise.
He said the province was repairing all the machinery so as to accelerate road rehabilitation projects.

Original_Search_Title: Acute Food Shortage Hits Kabale
Headline: Uganda: Acute Food Shortage Hits Kabale
Body_Text:
Kampala — Rukinga is a family man and his family survives by growing food for consumption and rarely get surplus for sale.
They supplement their incomes by occasionally selling their labour to comparatively well off peasants to buy other household goods. This especially occurs when there is no food shortage in the village.
However, their lives have been made more difficult by the present situation. This time it is not only the poor, but the whole village is faced with food scarcity. This means their supplemetary incomes have seriously been affected by crop failure.
Mrs. Turyatemba can not stand it any longer. For long she has been staying in the village with the children, depending on the food she grow. But this time around the situation is desperate. She has to go to her husband in Kampala to look for money to support them.
In October, Kabale District Council passed a resolution asking the Ministry of Disaster Preparedness and Refugees to declare the district a disaster area (The New Vision, October 11, 2002).
Adison Kakuru, the LC 5 Chairman, Kabale District, confirmed the seriousness of the food shortage.
"There is serious food shortage in the district and it is widespread. Food items of all types are lacking," he said.
He added that the district as a unit does not have the capacity to help the people. As a result, the central government has been approached, but help is yet to come.
Drought first hit Kabale district between June and October 2002. According to Peter Byamukama, a teacher at Kigarama Primary School and a resident of Kigarama village who is also a farmer, the prolonged drought affected people in various ways.
Some sweet potatoes rotted in the gardens while others did not mature. The veins and leaves dried up.
In banana growing areas, the drought weakened the banana stems and reduced the yields. To add insult to injury, when, after the long dry spell, the heavy rains came, they instead destroyed the plants. Also, due to the late rains, the bean seeds that were planted early in anticipation of the rains had dried up.
When this occurred, what was in stock was already exhausted. At the moment people do not have seeds to plant.
Consequently, there is serious food shortage. Only a lucky few people eat more than one meal a day. The majority make do with one poor meal once a day and sometimes go without any at all. Usually, it is composed of poor starchy foods like matooke mixed with pepper. According to Polly Mubangizi, a councillor, Kabale District Local Council for Maziba Subcounty, the most affected areas are maziba, Buhara, Ikumba, Rubaya and parts of Kyanamira.
To him, the problem is bigger than the current food shortage. It extends to the prevalent El Nino rains which have taken their toll making roads impassable. In addition, some bridges were swept away. others are in the process of collapsing. The affected ones are Ikumba, Maziba, Buhara, and Kigarama.
Mubangizi blames the present food shortage not only on the prolonged drought and heavy rains but also on soil exhaustion and use of poor seeds. When beans are planted, soon after germination their leaves turn yellow. sweet potato veins grow but tubers never develop. Those are the old type of seeds.
However, there is another problem developing. According to Mr James Kasimbazi the Secretary for Production, Kabale District Executive Council, the beans turn yellow because of a disease known as bean root rot. To fight it, creeping beans are encouraged.
Apart from food shortage, there is general poverty in the district. In the past, the soils of Kabale were fertile. Bush furrowing and terracing, had conserved the fertility. However, in the '70s, with increase in population, most of the land, including wetlands, was cleared for cultivation. Bush furrowing was abandoned, as there was no spare land left.
The soils started rapidly losing fertility. Now the yields are too poor to meet family needs, let alone generate homestead incomes.
The food crops of the area include both Irish and sweet potatoes, peas, beans, and sorghum (used in brewing bushera, a local beverage, and bread, some millet and wheat in some areas, maize and some vegetables are not a food crop per se but regarded as a cash crop.
Due to soil exhaustion, most of the crops cannot yield considerable harvests without use of fertilisers. Maize is running out of production.
The rest of the crops are still cultivated due to lack of an alternative. People grow them in order to survive or else they would starve to death.
In order to increase productivity, they need to use fertilisers but the peasants are too poor to afford them. Profitable farming is not viable. The prices of food crops cannot cover production costs even in an acute food shortage like the present one.
Kasimbazi says there is food in the market at good prices but few people buy it because they cannot afford it.
Polly Mubangizi says even those few who can buy the food cannot manage it on a regular basis. There is low purchasing power in the district. Under the present food shortages, it is not uncommon to find an individual buying a quarter of a kilogramme of posho flour.
This is what that particular group of individuals are faced with. There is food scarcity but with no alternative income.
In the past, in times of food scarcity, the people would feed on wild vegetables from the wetlands but these are no more. Even then, food stocks rarely got exhausted.
Food would be harvested and kept in granaries until the next season. Today, there is not a single granary left in Kabale.
Modernisation, instead of improving the food storinging capability of the families, has instead eroded it.
With the advent of iron sheets over rooftops, families that would not otherwise afford them are forced to catch up with the trend by selling all that is produced in a season with no provision for food security.
Sometimes, they sell off portions of their land for the sole purpose of building an iron sheet roofed house.
On the surface, it makes sense but looked at critically, it does not. Families lose the land from which they get food and income thus become poorer thereafter.
In western Uganda generally and Kabale District in particular, there is an increased number of people, especially children, who are malnourished. This accounts for the growing cases of stunted growths.
NDINAWE BYEKWASO
SOCIAL OBSERVER
077-490266

Original_Search_Title: Sondayi, Chingaira Release New Album
Headline: Zimbabwe: Sondayi, Chingaira Release New Album
Body_Text:
Harare — Tinos  Viriri, Alan Mpanduki, Gaylord Gwatidzo, Collin Sondayi and Lenin Chingaira, also known as Slice, have released a new offering titled Nhasi Nhasi recorded in October this year at Lifetown Studios.
It includes 11 songs, the title track, Helele, Tiki Ndaiwana, Rombe Ndiyani, Malaika and Romeo and Juliet which already receiving airplay on radio.
Ellison Simbanegavi of Gospel Train Records released a debut album Inyasha consisting of songs such as Tirikuchema, an appeal to God to save everyone from the Aids pandemic, Mwari Vanokuda, Ndinovatenda, Mumawoko Enyu, Makanaka and the title track that entered the 3FM Hitsville charts.
The well-composed and sung album was recorded last year and promises to be a hit for the gospel music lovers.
Glory to God: Alpha and Omega is another debut album from Moses Chikumbu alongside the GGC Voices. Recorded in July 2000, the album features Tariro Muna Jesu, Hatina Nguva, Kuda Kwamwari, Musiki, Tanga Waudza Jesu, Makatirangarira, Glory and Huyai Tiende.
Lucky Chikukwa and ZCC Mbungo Stars have an offering called Mwari Tongai recorded at Mosi-Oa-Tunya Studios with six songs: the title track, Chitsidzo, Ngatiyananei, Sekuru, Dhongi, Ndikubatei Muromo and Pamuchinjikwa.
Mharidzo is the album by Tawanda Mutyebere recorded at Shed Studios featuring six songs including Jesu Chiyedza also available on video, Ndiye, Johane, Chinangwa, Namata Wedenga and Kubarirwa Jesu.

Original_Search_Title: Cio Official Loots Nyamandlovu Farm
Headline: Zimbabwe: Cio Official Loots Nyamandlovu Farm
Body_Text:
A TOP Central Intelligence Organisation (CIO) member has allegedly looted $50 million worth of vegetables from a Nyamandlovu farm and forced the owners out of their farmhouse.
The farm owners, Margaret and John Sankey, reported the matter to Nyamandlovu Police Station and the Crime Report number is 0074551.
Thandanani Farm is Bulawayo's leading vegetable producer and the country's largest producer of sweet potatoes.
The CIO operative, identified only as Chibaya, was allocated 100 hectares of Thandanani Farm under the government's land reform programme but has moved to the other part where the farmhouse is located.
He has reportedly seized farming equipment worth more than $500 000 from the farm.
A group of about 20 people claiming to be his workers are reportedly guarding more than $4,5 million worth of the Sankeys' farming equipment.
Sankey's wife said before Chibaya invaded the farm on Monday, he had been taking 100 pockets of onions a day. By Wednesday he had looted more than 20 tonnes of onions.
The highly productive farm is under a Section 8 notice which was due to expire in January. Initially, the Sankeys farm measured 400 hectares, divided into plots of 100 hectares.
They bought the farm, located in the middle of the Nyamandlovu area, in 1987.
Three plots, including the one allocated to Chibaya, were taken, leaving them with the 100-hectare plot. Sankey's wife said: "We know that the other farms are gone but we want our one and only plot back. We are farmers and farming is the only thing we know, so we are going back to the farm."
She said when they confronted Chibaya over the looting of vegetables he pulled out a gun and threatened to shoot them.
She said when Chibaya came to the farm on Monday he was in the company of a police officer and another CIO operative identified only as Ngwenya.
"They want to take crops which they did not sweat for. They have no legal basis to take away our one and only farm," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Top Sweet Potato Producer Forcibly Evicted From Farm
Headline: Zimbabwe: Top Sweet Potato Producer Forcibly Evicted From Farm
Body_Text:
One of Zimbabwe's largest producers of sweet potatoes and garlic has been forcibly evicted from his Nyamandlovu farm near Bulawayo, allegedly by a group of police officers, Central Intelligence Organisation (CIO) operatives and war veterans, it was learnt yesterday.
The farmer, John Sankey, told the Financial Gazette yesterday that he was this week forced to abandon $500 million worth of property at his Thandanani farm, after being ordered to leave with only a few household goods.
He said: "The police came around 9 am and told us to vacate the farm immediately. They were in the company of members of the CIO and local war veterans.
"We just managed to remove our household goods but most of the farm equipment, including tractors, drip-pipes and other irrigation equipment remained behind because they wanted us to leave in a hurry."
Sankey said he also left behind several tonnes of garlic and onions which had been harvested and were being prepared for marketing.
The farmer, who alleged a high-ranking CIO officer based in the Matabeleland North province was after his farm, said irrigation equipment worth about $4.5 million had already been removed from the property.
Sankey said he reported his eviction and the theft of his irrigation equipment to the police, citing his case report number as 0074551.
But police spokesman Wayne Bvudzijena yesterday denied that state security agents had evicted Sankey from his property, saying: "We (police) don't evict people. We arrest them (farmers) if they defy section eight."
Under the government's controversial Land Acquisition Act, a section eight order is the final eviction notice, at whose expiry a farmer must leave his/her farm or risk arrest and imprisonment.
Sankey has been issued with a section eight order that expires next January.
Located 53 kilometres from Bulawayo, Thandanani farm sits directly on the Nyamandlovu aquifer and also produces tomatoes and other vegetables for the local and regional market.
Several powerful ZANU PF and government politicians, as well as army and CIO officers and their wives, are accused of taking advantage of the government's controversial land reforms to grab the best farms for themselves.

Original_Search_Title: Miss Malaika Album Released
Headline: Zimbabwe: Miss Malaika Album Released
Body_Text:
Harare — Starting this week, Spin That Wheel will inform people on the music by artists whose albums have entered their respective stable's charts.
I start by mentioning the Miss Malaika album, a product of Ruffcuts Entertainment entitled Miss Malaika (A Beautiful Sister) which has been released.
It is a compilation of music from different Zimbabwean artists including Chiwoniso Maraire, Matthew Kaunda a.k.a. Mateo, Willom Tight, Patience Musa, Mary, Xiomowa, Brown Sugar, Major Playaz and Kurruption.
South African based Zimbabwean Mizchif and J. Blu from the United Kingdom are also featured.
The 12 track album consists of such songs as Ndiripano, Runako, Tsvarakadenga, Baby Boy, Tutti Fruit, Look Out, Zimbabwe, Thula Thula, Sango and Running Late.
According to the executive producer of Ruffcuts Entertainment, Marco Machona, the album is available at Gramma Records.
"The compilation will definitely make these up-and-coming artists gain the much needed recognition not only in this country but all over Africa," Machona said.
It is going to be launched sometime before the Miss Malaika finals set for December 14.
"All the 28 contestants of the Miss Malaika beauty pageant will be given the album, which they are expected to take home so that it gets airplay in their countries," he said.
Patience Musa from the same stable has released her debut album With Love recorded in May this year featuring six tracks such as Tinotenda, Sango Rinopa Aneta, the popular Zvirinani and Zvapfuura, Siyana Neni and Runako.
Zvirinani is a motivational song that advises people to be self-reliant while Runako talks of the love that some people have.
It is especially dedicated to her sisters.

Original_Search_Title: I Slipped Through Kony's Fingers
Headline: Uganda: I Slipped Through Kony's Fingers
Body_Text:
Kampala — LUCKY: Juma managed to cut his ropes and escape, jumping over his sleeping captors
After seeing other LRA captives being slain daily, Juma decided to make his escape
THE long second term had ended on August 30. I decided to go back home and visit my mother for the holiday period in Lira District. I would also be at hand to help her with some work in the garden. Little did I know that I was to be abducted- and would stay with the rebels for a month, enduring torture- and would manage to escape at the beginning of November.
I was so happy seeing my mother since it had been a long time. Makerere College, my school, is miles away from her. She does not even have the money to come and see me. I had spent three days there. On the third night, we all retired to bed after bidding each other good-night.
At about 2:00am, I was woken up by a loud noise (it was a kick on the door), bright torchlight and a voice commanding me to wake up and move.
I moved out of the house. Already some huts in the neighbourhood had been torched. The light was just too much.
I could not believe it but I was in the hands of the rebels. About eight other boys had already been collected and tied together. The rebels tied ropes around our waists to keep us from escaping. We were their slaves. A human caravan!
So the whole night we kept moving around while the LRA looked for other children in the village.
They killed two of my uncles, one 40, the other about 60. They had checked many of huts around but had found no-one in them. They said my uncles had kept their children as if they did not know that they wanted them, not old men. At about 8:00am, they killed them with clubs.
"Whoever tries to escape and is caught will have to follow these two people," they said.
One of them, a tall muscular man, said they want people to help them carry on their mission, but they wondered why all those they have been abducting always ran away.
Of course you cannot live under such conditions. Even then, all the old people around the villages have been killed.
After walking for over 5kms, we reached a forest. One of the commanders said the day had ended and we were to get some rest before resuming the next day. On that night and the others that followed, they always slept in a circle. We, the captives, were told to sleep in the centre of the circle.
Life in this new world was too hard. Although I had faced hardship in my childhood, this was worse. Every day, someone would be killed in front of us. All those who had tried to escape would be lined in front of us and killed using a club or a hoe.
It gets nasty and horrible seeing dead bodies with blood oozing from their scalps every day. I think God was by me because I never got to kill anyone, unlike some of the boys with whom I had been abducted.
Whenever they recaptured anyone who had escaped, he would be given to one of the captives to be killed. If you resisted, the person captured would be given the chance to kill you and take your place among them. This is what they called bravery.
Throughout my captivity, they kept telling us that whoever remained with them would not die, as you were then supportive of their movement. There was plenty of food, but it was always meat, which became difficult to eat because of the dead bodies around.
Sometimes, they would kill someone and spread their intestines out when it was a few minutes to a meal.
The food was always accompanied with fresh cassava, which was uprooted from the villagers' gardens. Only on a few occasions did we taste sweet potatoes, but still it was served with meat.
Wherever they discovered a cassava garden, they advised its owner to preserve it for them. If you uprooted it, they would kill you. Those who did not were spared, and even advised to plant more. All the captives and the commanders went to the gardens when it is time to find food.
The food was always in plenty, but eating in such a hurry, you could some times not get satisfied. It is the younger children who ate first because they normally took longer, but if they cried of hunger on the way, they would be killed.
When I was abducted, I heard the rebels asking my mother to hand them more of her children or she would die. I feared for her, as she had no more children around. All the relatives who were staying around have moved away because the village is prone to attacks.
She pleaded that the first batch of rebels who had come around had already taken the children, and she had with no more. During my captivity, I never saw Kony, but the other members told us he would appear whenever he wanted.
I did not sleep for most of the nights. Whenever I was handed a blanket, I only covered my legs because I kept planning how to escape. It was always a nervous wait for the rebels to sleep, but they would always talk on and on. They probably slept when I was already snoring, and awoke before me.
As usual, we were inside their circle with rope around our waists as we retired to sleep. On the night I was abducted, I had been cutting my nails during the day and one of the pieces of razorblade I used had found its way into my pocket.
When I realised that I had it, I waited for the day I would use it, which turned out to be November 9. I used it to cut the ropes and set myself free.
"We have to move out of here," I told the boy next to me. But he simply panicked and told me that we are going to be killed. I did not listen to him. I slowly surveyed the area through the corners of my eyes. After making sure that all was okay, I got up with the slightest of noises. The other boy followed me. I jumped over the first rebel. He did not shake. I jumped over the second, the third and before me was a clear way.
For over 6km we kept running in the dark. When we finally got very, very tired, we branched off into the bush to rest. We kept ourselves alert just in case the rebels came following us.
Before dawn, we made a run towards the town, but something was strange. There was a lot of noise, but there was no one to ask what was going on.
We continued uphill. After crossing the road to Adwari (one of the villages around), we almost met another group that had been in the area. They were heading towards Moroto. So we waited till they had gone away. We then started moving ahead. As we approached Mon-maka Bridge, there was smoke from a burning pick-up. They had piled bodies under the vehicle and set it on fire. It was a horrible sight.
Later, as we came to town, we found out that they had raided Teso bar, abducted people and set many huts ablaze. From there, we went to a LC chairman, who I knew. He was shocked to see us but I told him we had just escaped from rebel captivity.
We stayed at his home that Saturday evening. On Sunday, I told him I wanted to go back to Kampala. I wanted to resume school before the rebels could find me again, and on the Sunday night, he gave me sh11,000, to bring me up to Kampala.
He then advised me to come to the headmistress and ask her to help me out with scholastic material and other things. That night, I kept turning in my bed hoping the day would break quickly. I never felt hungry while on the journey. I was going to safety.
Back home, my mother could still be thinking that I am still in the bush with the rebels. The LC3 had told me he would inform her about the developments but I gather that no one has yet got to her because all the people who know my village fear going there. The route is not safe.
Sometimes I wish no one is ever abducted because it is terrible living a rebel's life when you are not meant for it. The cheapest item in the bush is life because they kill people at will.
I pray that the school continues helping me the way they are doing, such that even during the holidays, I can stay within the school.
My fees are catered for by the school for the talent endowed to me by God. I am an athlete, and I compete in 400 and 200 metres, relays, long jump, high jump, triple jump and javelin.
I was formerly a student of Akii-Bua SSS in Lira, and through my excellence in sports, Makerere College sought me out last year. I was in senior two and we had come to Namboole for the post-primary championships. I had previously been to Masaka, Hoima, Kumi, Lira, and recently at Kyambogo.
The school administration advised me to repeat senior one, which I accepted because the standards here are not comparable to my former school.
May be something I am grateful for is that God created me an athlete. This is how I have managed to be where I am now. And where is the other boy? I left him at the LC's place, and I gather that he is now back in school. He was in primary six, but the LC got him a place in another school.Ends

Original_Search_Title: Minister Missed National Census
Headline: Uganda: Minister Missed National Census
Body_Text:
Kampala — The State minister for agriculture, Dr. Kibirige Ssebunya, turned the heat on the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS) officials when he challenged them to explain the authenticity of the recently declared National Census results when he was not counted.
"I was one of the cabinet ministers who vehemently argued that the National Census day be declared a public holiday. I sat at home and I saw no one to count me, my many chicken, goats, beans, maize, sweet potatoes amongst others.
For three days, no one came to count me. I went to the local council officials who feigned ignorance about the exercise and I was never counted. If you failed to count a man like Dr. Kibirige Ssebunya, the minister of agriculture, then whom did you count?" he asked.
"When I heard UBOS declaring the census results, I knew this was fake and I want you to tell me now who is not doing his work."
Ssebunya was addressing participants at a National Agricultural Statistical System workshop at the International Conference centre.
The workshop was attended by staff from the Ministry of Agriculture, Finance, NGOs, UBOS and delegates from Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO), Mozambique and Norway.
The workshop, on building partnerships for statistical development, was organised by UBOS as one of the events to mark the Africa Statistics week celebrations.
Ssebunya criticised his ministry officials who were absent and those came in very late. When he demanded to know how many of his officials were around, only six raised their hands.
Ssebunya refused to officially open the workshop before the officials explained to him why the Ministry of Agriculture was using statistics of 1965 yet FAO and the government funded the 1990 Agriculture Census whose results have never been published.
The officials said the funds were delayed and were not adequate to conduct an authentic Agriculture Census.
Kibirige said government should establish a permanent system of collecting and disseminating food and agriculture statistics.

Original_Search_Title: Ramadan: Prices of Fruits, Beverages Up in Abuja
Headline: Nigeria: Ramadan: Prices of Fruits, Beverages Up in Abuja
Body_Text:
Lagos — Prices of fruits and beverages have increased astronomically in Abuja, following the commencement of the Muslim fasting period, a survey by the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) has shown.
NAN reported that prices of the items, which constitute major food items in the menu during fasting period, recorded an increase of more than 100 per cent.
A visit to the Modern and New Markets both in Wuse and Area I shopping centre, revealed that a small sized pineapple which used to cost between N80 and N100 rose to about N200 while the large size was sold at N300 as against N200.
Five small oranges at the Area I shopping centre cost N60 while the biggest and the most juicy ones were sold at N80.
At the Wuse Modern and New Markets, the prices of a small sized pawpaw that sold before the Ramadan period at N70 and N100 rose to N150 and N200 while a small sized water melon was also sold at between N100 and N250 as against N70 and N100.
A bunch of eight pieces of plantain, which was sold for about N150 and N180 previously was hiked to between N300 and N350 while a small basketful of Irish Potatoes was sold for N350 as against N150 and N200.
However, prices of the local potatoes otherwise called sweet potatoes remained stable as a medium sized basketful was N120.
The Peak powdered milk, a common beverage during the period, also recorded a slight increase as a medium tin that sold for N280 has risen to N300, while others including a tin of Ovaltin rose from N350 to N400.
Milo, Lipton and Nescafe stabilised at N270, N60 and N130 respectively at the Wuse Modern and New Markets.
NAN further reports that the price of a crate of fresh eggs which sold for N350 has jumped to N360 with an egg being sold for N15 instead of N12.
The survey further showed that a bag of brown beans formerly sold at N8,000 now cost about N9,800 with a measure of the beans selling at N120 while a bag of the white product dropped from N6,000 to N4,800.
The survey also showed that a bag of Wheat now cost N15,000 as against N13,000 before the fasting commenced.
A measure of granulated sugar rose to N100 from its previous price of N80 while the bag sells for N3,000 as against 2,500 before the fasting period.

Original_Search_Title: Success is Sweet for Small Cane Farmers
Headline: South Africa: Success is Sweet for Small Cane Farmers
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — FOR 33 years Hlohlokwake Masango worked as a gardener.
Today, the 68-year-old pensioner has become a successful sugar cane farmer
in the Wartburg district in KwaZulu-Natal - thanks to a mentoring programme
established by a local sugar baron, which is turning struggling small-scale
farmers into profitable cane growers.
Masango, who used to live on potatoes and maize grown on a 1.8ha plot,
recently harvested his first cane crop.
The money he has earned from it has already paid for his production costs,
as well as financed his next crop and put R6 000 in his pocket.
"I'm very happy now," said Masango. "It's slowly showing us that our
financial situation is improving and it is far more gratifying knowing I'm
earning my own income while being my own boss."
Masango's change in fortune began two years ago when he and 11 family
members, who collectively work 10ha of tribal land in the Swayimane
district, asked a neighbouring commercial sugar cane farmer, Heinz Wittig,
to help them turn their rich soils into profits.
Wittig obliged with his expertise and money, which he has recouped from the
emerging growers on their first crop.
Following the harvest, which put a total of R25 000 into the family's hands
after debts were settled, the first signs of development are already
creeping into Masango's rural community, with water tanks being erected and
the area's first spaza shop close to completion.
Word of this successful project has spread and Wittig is now forming a
co-operative for 53 other small-scale growers on 180ha of land.
He is being helped by members of the Noodsberg Cane Growers' Association,
who have pledged their skills, machinery and knowledge to the new
programme.

Original_Search_Title: Better Food Policy Needed to Save Kenya
Headline: Kenya: Better Food Policy Needed to Save Kenya
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Insufficient food has been a persist problem faced by many people in Africa. Famine, drought and natural calamities associated with them find mention in traditional mythologies.
The situation is no different today as research confirms a gloomy future for the survival of humanity.
Hunger in Rwanda, Burundi, Somalia, Sudan, Angola, Mozambique, Algeria and most parts of West Africa, continue to be felt, despite efforts to control the situation.
It is impossible to understand the significance, cause and ramifications of famine without reference to the ecological, economic, social and political conditions of the specific region. Famine is a complex syndrome, but it is generally agreed that demographic considerations are important in understanding the situation.
Although malnutrition, conflict and death are the indicators of the severity of a famine, effects are varied. They include change in fertility pattern, both during the famine as well as in the post famine periods, inter-regional or even international migration, and general mobility.
It varies from country to country because in Somalia, Rwanda and Burundi, it is man-made famine that is killing people more relentlessly than huge arsenals left behind by wars.
Though Kenya has been relatively free of large scale and severe famine in comparison to her neighbour, yet the picture is bleak, considering the per capita food consumption, the status of food reserves, or the occasional number of deaths in eastern and northern provinces as a result of famine. It is also important to note that most deaths during famine are not starvation deaths, but deaths related to diseases caused by malnutrition.
The demographic impact, the causes of and the relief measures to contain famines in Kenya, should be established.
