discussions in order to find a solution to these and other related
financial issues.
Allow me, Mr. Speaker, Sir, to express my appreciation to our friends
in the international community, who have supported us and still
continue doing so in our endeavour to uplift our poor people from
poverty and improve their lives for the better.
Among our several development partners, I wish to thank in particular
the Governments of the United States of America, the United Kingdom,
Japan, Germany, Canada, the Republic of China, Denmark, the
Netherlands, Austria, Belgium, Sweden, Norway, Finland, Australia,
Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, France, Libya, South Africa, Ghana, South Korea
and Egypt.
We are equally grateful to the following agencies and organisations:
the World Bank, the International Monetary Fund, the European Union,
the African Development Bank, the Arab Bank for the Economic
Development of Africa, the OPEC Fund for International Development,
the United Nations Development Programme, the United States Aid for
International Development, the Department for International
Development of the British Government; the Norwegian Agency for
Development and Corporation, the Swedish International Agency and the
United Nations International Children's Education Fund.
Mr. Speaker, Sir, I have dutifully outlined Government's achievements
and efforts in its endeavour to reduce poverty. Poverty reduction is
indeed possible. However, as the way forward I believe that more can
be achieved if only we all unite, co-operate and work harder as we
share defined objectives and a common vision.
I believe that when more unity and co-operation become a living
reality we will all feel that we do not have to work just for our
individual life, for our own families, but also for the entire nation
for the well being of each and every Malawian.
Our unity and co-operation must grow side by side with a new spirit
and entire change of attitude whereas Malawians will regard each
other as brothers and sisters of one family.
Let us also get it straight in our minds that Malawi belongs to us
all and that the moral obligation and responsibility is upon us all
to use our God-given talents and chart a better future for our
children and ourselves.
Mr. Speaker, Sir, because of the love of our country and the
neighbour people should have all willingness to go to any length to
work together as people of one nation.
People should understand that when they harm another person they are
harming themselves. For example, people must also understand that if
they vandalise water, electricity or hospital facilities they are
also depriving themselves as well as others of such facilities.
Mr. Speaker, Sir, let these words of wisdom from Booker T. Washington
give us a lesson.
Once upon a time a ship lost at sea for many days suddenly sighted a
friendly vessel. From the mast of the unfortunate vessel was seen a
signal, "Water, water, we die of thirst!" the answer from the
friendly vessel at once came back, "Cast down your bucket where you
are."
A second time the signal, "Water, water, send us water!" ran up from
the distressed vehicle, and was answered, "Cast down your bucket
where you are." And a third and fourth signal for water was answered,
"Cast down your bucket where you are."
The captain of the distressed vessel, at last heeding the injunction,
cast down his bucket and it came up full of fresh, sparkling water
from the mouth of the Shire River near Lake Malawi.
Mr. Speaker, Sir, it is also imperative that as Malawians we look
within ourselves and around us. God has blessed us with creative
potentials and many natural resources.
If we pull together our collective talents and efforts and cast our
bucket in agriculture, industry, health, education, mining, tourism
and communication, among others, we can fight poverty and succeed.
Mr. Speaker, Sir, I will always endeavour to use my God-given talents
to the fullest to fight poverty for the love of my fellow Malawians
and country.
Likewise I challenge my fellow Malawians to fully use their God given
creative ability and resources in order to fight poverty.
Mr. Speaker, Sir, Honourable Members of the House, it is now my great
honour and pleasure to declare this Session of Parliament officially
open.
I wish you very fruitful deliberations. Thank you all for your
attention. May God bless you and bless our nation.

Original_Search_Title: Benguela: Seven Million Dollars Needed for 2002/2003 Agricultural Season
Headline: Angola: Benguela: Seven Million Dollars Needed for 2002/2003 Agricultural Season
Body_Text:
Benguela — Some usd seven million are needed acquire seeds, fertilizers and tools, to secure the 2002/2003 agricultural season in Angola's Central Benguela province, an official source said.
Addressing Angop Wednesday, the Provincial Director of the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development (Minader), Abrantes Carlos, said the seeds and tools must be taken to inland districts to be supplied to peasants.
He added that, despite Government's financial constraints, some 169 hectares of lands will be used, involving 23,000 families of farmers, who will benefit of seeds and agricultural implements.
He said priority will be given to the production of grains, namely maize, beans, manioc, sorghum plus sweet potatoes, under the programme to fight hunger.

Original_Search_Title: Fertilisers Make Great Difference
Headline: Uganda: Fertilisers Make Great Difference
Body_Text:
Kampala — After establishing your sweet potato garden it is important to make a few interventions soon after the crop starts growing. The soil may need additional fertiliser.
It is amazing how much increase in yield can be achieved with the application of a little fertiliser. The fertiliser may be solid or liquid. Get the best guidance from your local extension worker.
Pests usually disrupt the growth of the potato crop. In most instances farmers do not attach importance to the damage that these insects cause until it is too late. Weeds usually increase the incidence of pests. Your garden must always be free of weeds. Besides encouraging insect damage they will feed on the nutrients that would otherwise make your potatoes grow well. Spray your garden with insecticide to prevent pests feeding on the crop.
There are many insecticides available on the market. Seek guidance from the extension officer in your area or the farm input stockist shop nearest to you.
By the end of one month, the tiny roots will develop. These develop into the big potatoes that will go to the market.
Caterpillars, millipedes and other earth-burrowing insects will start feeding on the roots if no insecticide spraying is done.
Stagger your chemical spraying as instructed on the label of the insecticide container. Seek guidance from the area agricultural extension officer as your crop progresses to maturity.
The amount of yield will tremendously increase because the crop will be allowed to grow freely without interference from pests.
fertilisers will ensure that the plants are strong and able to yield optimally which also means a difference in income.

Original_Search_Title: Sugar Weakens Sexual Strength
Headline: Uganda: Sugar Weakens Sexual Strength
Body_Text:
Kampala — EVERY time Gawaya Tegulle has to take a cup of tea, he puts three tablespoons full of sugar in a 300ml cup. No matter how much colleagues tease him about his sugar intake, his response always is: "what is your problem?"
Sugar is good because it is our fuel. Like a car moves with petrol, so is sugar to human beings. Without sugar, the brain cannot function, the muscles cannot contract-all metabolic processes that need sugar cannot function.
Apart from being a source of energy, sugar is a source of heat, which keeps us warm. Our body systems need a particular temperature to operate. If it is too low, you lose your consciousness and die.
However, taking much sugar as Tegulle's, is unhealthy.
Professor Marcel Otim, an expert on hormones, says: "Taking ordinary sugar is suicide. For a 500ml cup, one to two teaspoons will help to give you the ideal taste."
"Taking too much sugar is not healthy because it causes you problems with your teeth; it will make you fat and as you progressively become fat, you get diabetes," he adds.
Dr Amooti Kaguna, the Mbarara District Director of Health Services, identifies two types of diabetes: Inherited and adult-onset diabetes.
"The latter is caused by poor diet and obesity. When one takes too much sugar, it piles in the blood and turns into fats. These fats lead to complications like blindness or reduced vision and high blood pressure. This can lead to heart failure," Kaguna explains.
Prof. Otim says the sugar levels in the body must be between 70 to 120mg. When it falls below 70 and gets to something like 50mg or less, you lose consciousness and someone can die. When the sugar is above 120, insulin must come in and push it down.
If the sugar goes beyond 120, it will come out in the urine. However, Prof. Otim says that insulin cannot work well in a fat tissue (fat person).
Anybody above 30 years of age should have his or her sugar level checked once every month.
Professor Otim says that usually the hormones in the body are geared towards keeping the sugar levels constant, but they can be overwhelmed.
Whenever you eat much sugar, it is stored. The sugar is stored in the liver or in the muscles. If the glycogen stores are depleted, then you begin to break your proteins. In this case you lose weight.
Apart from ordinary sugar, the biggest sources of sugar are matooke, sweet potatoes, beer and fruits. Sugar intake should therefore be balanced with other things. Whereas 50% of the food intake should be carbohydrates, 10 to 30% should be fats and 10 to 20% proteins.
Other than have one food item in plenty, Prof. Otim, says the trick always is to have a mixture. This will help you avoid having too much sugar.
For those who are prone to becoming fat, those with a sweet taste and diabetic patients, sweeteners like canderel can do.
Charles Wazaarwa, the director of Makerere University International Development Institute at Ruharo community centre in Mbarara, advises people aged 50 years and above in particular, to take canderel.
"Always put one tablet of canderel in a cup of tea. One tablet of canderel is equivalent to one teaspoonful of sugar and is enough for a cup of tea," says Wazaarwa. Canderel can be got from supermarkets.
He also urges people with blood groups A and AB, to desist from sugar because it weakens their sexual strength.
He advises people to check their blood groups and respond to sugar accordingly.
Published on: Monday, 14th October, 2002

Original_Search_Title: Pader to Learn New Farming Methods
Headline: Uganda: Pader to Learn New Farming Methods
Body_Text:
Kampala — THE District Agricultural Officer (DAO), Pader, Joseph Remmy Lokii has revealed that farmers in the district will be taught new agricultural methods and skills to improve on their farm output.
Lokii said Pader's agricultural potential is enormous and needs to be effectively tapped by farmers being taught new and modern farming techniques, in line with the plan for the modernisation of Agriculture (PMA).
The DAO who is also the district extension co-ordinator said this recently while conducting the Pader secretary of production and marketing, Ecirino Oyo Atuka and the Deputy Resident District Commissioner, Sylvester Opira around a small farm belonging to MP Simon Lapoti, the district secretary for works and general purpose situated near Rackoko trading centre in Awere subcounty, Aruu county.
Lapoti has planted various crops such as coffee, bananas, passion fruit, cassava, beans sweet potatoes, and pawpaw which he has promoted to high value crops. Lokii told the team that banana, coffee, cotton and other crops will be promoted.
Published on: Monday, 14th October, 2002

Original_Search_Title: Potatoes Easy to Grow
Headline: Uganda: Potatoes Easy to Grow
Body_Text:
Kampala — Food remains the fastest moving agricultural commodity worldwide.
With our population now hovering at well over 24 million, farmers should prepare to work even harder to feed the growing urban population. Let's grow sweet potatoes this week. They are so easy to grow that almost every farmer I know of thinks it is as easy as eating pie.
However, growing sweet potatoes for a profit requires several interventions. The location of the garden is important. Do not establish your garden in poorly drained soils.
Clear the bushes at least a month to planting time. Deep plough to allow enough soil to set up the sweet potato mounds. Deep ploughing will enable the green manure to be mixed in the soil. Do not throw the rubbish away; just plough it into the soil to feed your potatoes later.
Look for good vines that have high yields and are on demand in the market. You can establish the demand of a particular variety of potatoes by talking to fellow farmers and surveying the big markets. For instance whereas the market for the orange potato is available in the city it does not necessarily fetch the highest price. The red skinned sweet potato fetches a higher price. However, it is not as high yielding as the orange variety. You need to take consideration of all these factors before choosing the variety to plant.
Source your planting material from fellow farmers. It is important to know how long the particular type of potato takes to mature. There are some fast maturing varieties that are ready for the market after three months. But there are others that take longer.
If you are to plant potatoes now you need the fast maturing types to beat the dry season at the beginning of next year. They will also likely find a ready market as schools go back for the first term and pressures mount on available food stocks.
Heap the potato mounts in rows to a reasonable size. Potatoes are root crops. It is therefore important that the heaps of soil you make are not washed away by the rain. Make them large enough to support many sweet potatoes. Insert two vines as far as your arm will go into the sides of the mounds. Each mound should have at least four vines with two on opposite ends. Large gardens may be planted by using ox-plough or tractors.
As a rule weed your sweet potato garden as soon as the weeds appear. Use hired labour if necessary.
The vines will soon develop vibrant leaves that will cover all the mounds, soon making the whole field green.
Published on: Sunday, 13th October, 2002

Original_Search_Title: Major Boost Awaits Agrarian Reform
Headline: Zimbabwe: Major Boost Awaits Agrarian Reform
Body_Text:
Harare — GOVERNMENT service providers are determined to boost Zimbabwe's agrarian revolution and increase agricultural production among the new farmers.
The Agricultural Research Council of Zimbabwe (ARC), the Department of Meteorological Services and the Forestry Commission are rendering services to the new farmers to enable the country to assume its role as the bread basket of Southern Africa.
The ARC is linking new farmers to markets for uncontrolled products so that they can develop skills to negotiate for best prices for their goods.
Executive secretary of the council Mr David Mangemba said one of the biggest challenges facing farmers in general was lack of production and marketing information.
Service providers are investing in the new farmers by providing additional markets.
The new farmers are also being urged to form purchase and marketing groups so as to increase their capacities to negotiate as bulk buyers as well as bulk suppliers of products.
"This information helps the new farmers to optimally utilise the scarce land resource made available to them through effective cost control measures and application of best practices," he said in an interview.
Mr Mangemba said the council was also promoting and updating the fact sheets relating to livestock and cropping information, chemical applications, farm planning and management and post harvest practices.
Soil improvement programmes have been put in place at all levels and farmers are constantly reminded of the benefits of soil analysis through various public and private laboratories. Soil testing informs the farmers on the suitability of the soil types for various crops as well as determining soil deficiencies before applying fertilisers.
ARC is currently funding legume-based soil fertility management programmes in Mashonaland West and East provinces at a cost of over $9 million to support the new farmers. The programmes are addressing acidity problems, use of lime, better utilisation of fertilisers and plant nutrition.
All research funded by ARC is available to all Zimbabwean farmers including the new A1 and A2 farmers.
"The A1 and A2 farmers stand to benefit more from the current developments as they are the focal point of the land reform programme and everybody hopes to show success through them," Mr Mangemba explained.
The operational focus of ARC is on strategic research and development with a bias towards small to medium scale landholders.
An ideal budget for the council to cover the current gaps in research and development is $80 million a year at current cost levels. The financial resources for the council are sourced from the Government and the international community. The council's operational budget is funded by the State. Donors chip in to support shortfalls aimed at supporting the small holder sector programmes. Research and development is funded partly by the Government, farmers commodity associations and donors.
The private sector finances programmes that benefit their institutions although the research helps all farmers, as they are the ultimate producers of all agricultural products. At the moment the council is funding some projects in the provinces.
In Manicaland, a team led by the University of Zimbabwe Faculty of Agriculture is carrying out an investigation into the production of baking flour from sweet potato for semi and commercial use. Although the project is not a new initiative, it seeks to determine awareness and broaden the use of sweet potato by promoting its production for various commercial uses.
These include blending sweet potato with wheat flour for bread making, chips and jam. The immediate benefits are that the farmer will fetch a higher farmer price due to little intervention from middlemen while entrepreneurship is promoted at local level as they get involved in semi and finally processed product.
In Mashonaland Central, experts are already on the ground identifying, evaluating and measuring the gap in the supply and demand of credit to the new farmers. The objective of the project is to develop a sustainable method of lending to the farmers that are working towards commercial production of high value crops.
Experts in Mashonaland West province are improving the communal cropping enterprises of legumes, particularly soyabeans to increase crop productivity, soil fertility, and value adding through processing of soyabeans and seed multiplication of groundnuts.
There is already a pilot scheme to make the farmers seed producers of groundnuts, processors of soyabeans into various commodities and improving their soil management.
Experts from the Dairy Development and International Centre for Research and Agroforestry are conducting a livestock-feeding project, which is critical for dairy animals in Masvingo and Midlands provinces. The project is focusing on improving the adoption of feeding strategies and technologies by smallholder dairy farmers in semi-arid production areas.
In Matabeleland North and South provinces, scientists from the National University of Science and Technology are carrying out a programme to improve livestock feeds for cattle ranching which is the main agricultural production activity in both provinces. The experts are working in close collaboration with the farmers to develop feeding strategies based on locally available resources as well as the spineless cactus pear that grows well in the areas.
In Mashonaland East province, the project aims at generation and promotion of improved soil fertility management options for increased crop production in the smallholder sector. It will provide alternative solutions to the problem of low fertility in certain areas where productivity is low.
A socio-economic assessment will evaluate whether the proposed soil fertility options are viable, cost effective and compatible to farmers' circumstances. Participatory approach is being applied in all provincial projects where multi-disciplinary teams work with the community to ensure immediate adoption of technology options.
The Department of Meteorological Services, is urging new farmers to work closely with extension specialists who have been trained in the interpretation and application of weather forecasts to help them in their agricultural decision-making process.
Acting director Mr Leonard Unganai said weather forecasts guide tactical and operational decision-making on the farms.
"The timing of some agricultural operations such as fertiliser application, crop spraying, crop choice, variety and field selection can to some extent be guided by accurate weather and climate forecasts," he said.
Although weather was one major component affecting the performance of farming operations, the overall productivity of a farming enterprise also depended on other factors at play.
Mr Unganai said the mandate of his department was in weather monitoring and prediction as a contribution towards the protection of life and property and the sustainable socio-economic development of the country.
"The department tries to provide timely and accurate information within the limits of the available science and technology. This information is passed on to an agronomist for repackaging to enable him to advise farmers accordingly," the weather expert explained.
Several programmes have been put in place both on radio and television to help farmers keep abreast with weather and climatic patterns in an effort to boost their operations. In addition, the department convenes a seasonal climatic forecasting workshop every year around September and October.
The workshop is usually attended by farmers' representatives and extension specialists who in turn advise farmers on the best agricultural practice for the coming rainfall season. The department also issues regular agricultural weather bulletins that are disseminated through various agricultural channels for cost effectiveness.
The department is responsible for forecasting, research and development, training, agricultural weather, remote sensing, climate information services, seismology and information technology.
It works closely with farmers in recording rainfall. Farmers get this service free of charge on condition that they would also make the data freely available to the department.
The Forestry Commission has introduced a number of technologies as an incentive for the new farmers to promote richer ecosystems through value addition to generate revenue and provide employment to the communities.
Bee keeping and honey processing is being promoted in natural woodlands and this has seen some communities generating substantial amounts of money from the sale of honey. For example, the Magunje honey project has proved to be a great success and the honey is selling like hot cakes at some of the Forestry Commission's retail outlets.
Edible mopane caterpillars, which have become a delicacy in most up-market restaurants are being harvested, processed and packaged in order to increase the value of mopane woodlands, a spokesman for the Forestry Commission said.
Farmers were being encouraged to derive benefits from indigenous fruits which are highly nutritious and rich in vitamin C such as masawu (Zizipohus mauritania), Mazhanje (Uapaca Kirkiana), matamba (Strychnos Cocculoides), and mawuyu (Adansonia digitata) through sustainable harvesting methods, processing and packaging.
"This ensures that such forest products continue to be available for enjoyment long after their normal season while at the same time increasing farm productivity," the Forestry Commission spokesman explained.
For some time the indigenous fruits in Zimbabwe have not entered market economy of the country because they have always been viewed as a product of the wild which is consumed when collected with little consideration for future use.
"It is only recently that truckloads of indigenous fruits such as masau, mazhanje and mawuyu have been observed heading for urban centres to be sold in central markets," the commission's spokesman said.
The increase in demand for indigenous fruits can be attributed to several factors including the increase in the price of exotic fruits, which are increasingly becoming inaccessible to the low-income groups. In the communal areas where farmers can grow exotic fruit tree species, persistent droughts have hampered their efforts.
Indigenous fruit trees are generally drought tolerant and often help stave off starvation during periods of food scarcity. They are found mostly in drier areas of the country. As perishables, the fruits are only available during the harvest period and their distribution is limited by the poor preservation methods.
To improve the accessibility and distribution of the indigenous fruits, the Forestry Commission has initiated a project of processing and packaging Masuwu as a pilot project.
Masawu is a small tree that grows to a height of 7 metres sometimes reaching 20 metres introduced in the Zambezi basin from the Middle East or India possibly by the Arabs. The tree is widely cultivated in Mozambique for its fruit while in Zimbabwe the tree has since naturalised and most people rely on harvesting the fruit from the wild.
Quite a number of trees were being left uncut on individual farmers' fields which then defines the tree tenure. Over the years the fruit has become an important nutritious component of many households in the Southern African Development Community region.
Products being developed from Masawu are the masawu powder that is used to make opaque beverage packaged in 15-gram satchels which when mixed with a glass of water provide a refreshing pleasant drink. Other industrial processing technologies that have been adapted to masawu include the making of masawu sticks and strips.
The project was started last year and a cold room has already been established in Muzarabani where the Forestry Commission is now in the process of developing a small processing plant. The move will increase the project capacity and lengthen the availability of the fruit outside its normal season.
The Forestry Commission has helped the farmers in identifying both local and external markets. The technology used in the Masawu project has been disseminated to various other parts of the country such as Beitbridge, which has similar climatic conditions.
It is envisaged that by improving marketability of indigenous fruits, enthusiasm to propagate and manage the trees would be developed and thereby develop the rural economy. With the newly resettled farmers such an initiative will encourage the management and protection of indigenous fruit trees in their respective areas.
Other fruits that are being processed include mawuyu, which is being processed into powder for making yourghut. There are also traditional technologies used in processing masawu, mazhanje, hacha into alcoholic beverages.
The private sector with technical expertise is encouraged to undertake the ventures while the Forestry Commission will play a facilitatory role. The projects will provide employment to local communities.
To arrest deforestation and restore the forest resource, the Forestry Commission in collaboration with various governmental and non-governmental institutions is spearheading the Rural Afforestation Programme. This has seen a significant increase in the number of trees planted and woodlands under effective management.

Original_Search_Title: Governments, Scientists Seek Alternative to GM Technologies
Headline: Southern Africa: Governments, Scientists Seek Alternative to GM Technologies
Body_Text:
AS most of Southern Africa comes to terms with hunger and food produced through the controversial genetically modified (GM), technology, governments and scientists are frantically trying to introduce alternative technologies to produce non-GM food.
Southern Africa has been gripped by drought but the only food available is produced through GM technology, condemned by African scientists for allegedly causing allergic reactions in human beings, if consumed.
The alternative technologies, which have been in use for centuries in some parts of the world, fall under the what has been termed biotechnology.
While GM technology is the latest form of biotechnology which involves the manipulation of plant, animal and micro-organism genes, farmers in Africa are being encouraged to use old forms of biotechnology such as tissue culture to produce food compatible with African needs.
Social concerns have been raised about GM technology, which makes it possible to transfer animal genes into plants. For example, the genes of a fish can be grafted into a maize plant.
In fact, biotechnology is used in fermentation and has been in use for decades in Zimbabwe. This is referred to as first-generation biotechnology, which only manipulate organisms to produce substances such as bread, wine, and cheese. There is no movement of genes in fermentation, neither is there transfer of genes in second-generation biotechnology, such as tissue culture.
Tissue culture, which is mainly used in crop science, involves the manipulation of plant tissue to produce disease-free seedlings, which can be used by farmers. Scientists from East and Southern Africa gathered in Zambia last week to create awareness of biotechnology, largely associated with GM technology.
Scientists say if Africa uses technologies such as tissue culture, farmers could diversify Africa's food base and produce drought-tolerant crops such as cassava , sorghum, millet and sweet potatoes.
Instead of relying on maize, which fails in times of drought, tissue culture explores opportunities for producing millions of seedlings of sweet potatoes and cassava.
Cassava is a staple food in East Africa. Prior to the introduction of maize, Zimbabwe relied on traditional foods among them sweet potatoes sorghum and cassava. Maize production increased in the then Rhodesia in 1952 when maize seed hybrids were produced on a commercial basis.
But most of the technologies African countries want to introduce to counter genetic engineering are still in the laboratory. The challenge is to take the technologies to the starving nations.
In Zimbabwe, sweet potatoes and cassava are produced by few farmers. Some farmers in Buhera and Hwedza through the Biotechnology Trust of Zimbabwe (BTZ) are producing mushrooms and sweet potatoes, using tissue culture. Doreen Mnyulwa, BTZ executive director, said her organisation had established pilot projects on sweet potato and mushroom farming in Buhera and Hwedza. The farmers have been trained to produce sweet potato nurseries, cleaned and multiplied using tissue culture.
Mnyulwa said: "The nursery farmers are generating income from both sale of planting material and tubers - farmers have indicated that they use the income generated to pay children's fees and purchase farming implements." While the sweet potatoes produced are indigenous varieties, the mushrooms being produced are not local varieties.
Research on producing indigenous mushroom seedlings is still ongoing. Asked if it would be possible to wean Zimbabwean farmers from producing foreign food crops such as maize to grow crops such as sweet potatoes, millet and sorghum, Zimbabwe Farmers' Union spokesperson, Violet Mandishona, said: "It would be difficult for farmers to stop growing maize. What would be feasible would be to grow both maize and indigenous crops. If maize fails, then farmers have something to fall back on."
Zimbabwe first produced hybrid maize seed in the early 1950s and from then the commodity was produced on a large scale.

Original_Search_Title: Tissue Culture Could End Famine: Expert
Headline: Zimbabwe: Tissue Culture Could End Famine: Expert
Body_Text:
TISSUE culture, a form of biotechnology which involves the multiplication of plant cells to produce disease-free seedlings, could be the answer to drought-stricken southern Africa if adopted on a commercial scale by both farmers and the private sector, a lecturer at the Zambia University Crop Science Department, has said.
Zimbabwe and Zambia, in a dilemma over acceptance of genetically modified (GM) food, are being challenged to commercialise tissue culture, which enables farmers to produce high-yielding crops.
Genetic engineering is the latest form of biotechnology. This engineering involves the manipulation of plant genes to produce disease-free, high-yielding and quality crops.
While Zimbabwe and Zambia have rejected the use of the GM seeds in the fields in order to safeguard food security, the two countries have not explored tissue culture as an alternative.
GM technology, it is feared, could have negative effects on human beings, animals and the environment.
In tissue culture, a single plant cell is exposed to some conditions in a laboratory to produce planting material, for example, seedlings for sweet potatoes, cassava and mushrooms.
Tissue culture, scientists say, opens possibilities for plant breeding where a single cell can produce millions of seedlings.
Seedlings for mushroom, sweet potatoes, cassava and bananas are difficult to produce and tissue culture becomes the best way of multiplying them to produce disease-free seedlings.
In East Africa, the entire cassava planting material was wiped out by a virus called Mosaic and cassava production came to a halt five years ago.
Cassava is the staple food for East Africa. Scientists in East Africa have in the past four years used tissue culture to produce new disease-free cassava seedlings, as well as cleaning out the disease-infested cassava species. Dr Davis Lungu, head of the Crop Science Department at the University of Zambia, told journalists and scientists attending a five-day agro-biotechnology workshop in Lusaka, Zambia, that while tissue culture could be a positive revolution for hunger-ridden Africa, farmers and the private sector had not shown interest in the technology.
He said tissue culture was mostly undertaken at universities, which had no capacity to produce large quantities of seedlings.
Lungu said: "I think that tissue culture can help in terms of traditional crops like bananas, cassava and sweet potatoes because their seedlings are difficult to produce. The problem, however, is that we have a limitation in producing the seedlings because we do not have the capacity.
"Private sector investors have not appreciated the need to venture into tissue culture. This may be because there is low demand on the farmers' part."
There are no private institutions in Zimbabwe which produce, or multiply, seedlings for sweet potatoes and mushroom.
Mushroom production is booming in Zimbabwe, with small-scale farmers having recently joined the lucrative business.
The University of Zimbabwe, however, produces mushroom seedlings on a limited scale and farmers have complained that there is a shortage of mushroom spawn (seedlings).
The University of Zambia is now producing seedlings using tissue culture on an ad hoc basis following numerous requests.
The university is also generating income through its mushroom seedlings which it sells to both large and small-scale farmers. The university supplies about 100 small-scale farmers. A 10kg bag of mushroom seedlings from the university retails at K6000 (Z$785). There are four other producers of mushroom seedlings in Lusaka who supply mainly large-scale commercial farmers.
The agro-biotechnology workshop was organised to create awareness on biotechnology, which was largely misinterpreted as genetic engineering.
Genetic engineering is a new form of biotechnology. But biotechnology has been in use for centuries in beer, bread, and wine making. It also used to purify industrial waste and to manufacture vaccines. The workshop was organised by the PELUM Association, a regional network of non-governmental organisations which seeks to promote food security in Southern and Eastern Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Local Avocadoes for US $8m Contract Depends on Consistency of Supplier
Headline: Uganda: Local Avocadoes for US $8m Contract Depends on Consistency of Supplier
Body_Text:
Kampala — A Memorandum of Understanding, in which a Ugandan firm will supply various food items worth $8m per year to a US firm, was signed on Tuesday, between US based importer John Nuwamanya-Matsiko and Ugandan fruit exporter John Kavuma.
The signing was held in the Uganda Export Promotions Board (UEPB) boardroom in Kampala.
Nuwamanya-Matsiko is a Director of USAfrica International Limited, which supplies fruits for two wholesale outlets in the US. Kavuma is the Chairman of the Federation of Associations of Uganda Exporters (FAUX). The first consignment will consist of avocadoes.
"This deal is worth $8m on a repeat basis, provided Kavuma can supply the avocados without the storage temperatures going above 40 degrees fahrenheit (about 5 degrees celsius). That is what will make or break the deal.
"It is necessary to put the fruits in a cold storage chain right from the garden, in the transportation, right through to the freighting. We have agreed to pay for all the freight charges as part of the price we pay," Matsiko said.
He explained that he had actually wanted seven to twenty forty foot containers of fruits per week for five months from January to May. The period when Florida and Californian producers in the US are not in marketing.
"The period January to May is when we have a window of opportunity which we can explore to get our fruits into the US market. The market is opening up and I think the big question of freight will be eased as more airlines try the direct US route" he said.
Kavuma said FAUX has been going around identifying growers of avocados. Other items Matsiko wants to import include sweet potatoes, hot pepper, ginger and mangoes.
He said the available avocados they have identified on the market, make up only a total of four tonnes per week.

Original_Search_Title: Drought Prone Countries Urged to Use Tissue Culture in Farming
Headline: Zimbabwe: Drought Prone Countries Urged to Use Tissue Culture in Farming
Body_Text:
Harare — DROUGHT-PRONE countries in Southern Africa should consider using tissue culture in farming to improve yields and boost food security in the region, a University of Zambia lecturer in the Crop Science Department has said.
Head of the Crop Science Department Dr Davis Lungu said tissue culture, a traditional form of technology enables a single plant cell to multiply to millions of cells, which will then develop into seeds.
He was speaking during a tour by science journalists from Central and Southern Africa of the tissue culture laboratory before the opening of an agro-biotechnology and food security workshop, which started here yesterday.
Tissue culture, according to scientists, opens possibilities for plant breeding where a single cell can produce millions of seedlings. This is most ideal for crops like mushroom, cassava and banana, which are difficult to produce.
Instead of dealing with seeds from the fields, a single tissue plant will produce million of cells, which are disease-free and can withstand harsh weather conditions.
Dr Lungu said countries like Zimbabwe and Zambia that are strongly opposed to the use of genetically modified organisms, could use tissue culture as an alternative in food production.
Tissue culture is being practised in East Africa, where farmers are recording significant yields in crops like sweet potatoes, cassava, banana, flowers and other traditional crops like maize and rice.
It had also been found to be a much faster process of providing seedlings to the farmer than planting seed and waiting for it to germinate.
"Tissue culture can be a positive revolution to famine-torn Africa, which at the moment is looking at all possibilities of providing food for its people," he said.
He added that despite the positive aspects of tissue culture, the private sector and even farmers have not shown interest in this technology.
Zimbabwean farmers, who are expanding their operations in the production of such crops like mushrooms, would certainly benefit from the tissue culture, as they would now access seed easily at reasonable cost.
The University of Zimbabwe, which at the moment is the only institution that produces mushroom seedlings, has been in the past few months failing to meet demand for spawn (mushrooming seedlings) as more farmers venture into farming of this lucrative crop.

Original_Search_Title: Scientist's Work Wins Recognition
Headline: Kenya: Scientist's Work Wins Recognition
Body_Text:
Nairobi — A Kenyan scientist has won an international award for her outstanding contribution to biotechnology.
Dr Florence Wambugu, 48, the executive director of Aharvest Biotechnology Foundation International, an American NGO in Nairobi, has been awarded the International Biographical Centre Lifetime Achievement Award.
She told the Nation recently: "I am very pleased and feel encouraged for winning this award. But winning is not the end, it is just the beginning."
Dr Wambugu, who has a PhD in biotechnology, is remembered for her genetically-engineered sweet potato project launched in Kenya two years ago.
Trial farms in Muguga, Kisii, Kakamega and Embu are doing well.
The superior quality variety is resistant to disease, thus increasing yields considerably and is used in other parts of the world.
Her concern now is the "alleviation of hunger and poverty using biotechnology".
"African scientists must begin to meet the needs of the continent. Africans will take the lead in boosting food production," she said.
As a young scientist at the Kenya Agricultural Research Institute (Kari), Dr Wambugu pioneered the development of tissue-culture pyrethrum that was later adopted by the Pyrethrum Board of Kenya.
Her other projects include tissue-culture banana and "super" trees which grow fast.
If the tree-project succeeds, destruction of water catchment areas could be a thing of the past.
Born and brought up in Nyeri, Dr Wambugu attended local schools before joining the University of Nairobi in 1975 for a Bachelor of Science degree in zoology and botany.
She then proceeded to Perth University, United Kingdom, where she graduated with a PhD in 1988.

Original_Search_Title: Fight for Facts On Frankenfoods
Headline: South Africa: Fight for Facts On Frankenfoods
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — An NGO has gone to the Pretoria High Court to force the government to lift an alleged veil of secrecy over the machinations of the genetically modified organism (GMO) industry in South Africa.
The Biowatch Trust is seeking an order to compel the GMO registrar, the executive council for GMOs and the minister of agriculture to release information that the NGO says all South Africans are entitled to under the
Constitution.
Section 32 (1) of the Constitution provides that "everyone has the right of access to (a) any information held by the state; and (b) any information that is held by another person and that is required for the protection of any rights".
South Africa issued 122 permits last year for genetically modified crops, excluding import permits, up from one in 1990. Biowatch says permits have been granted for trials and experiments with apples, canola, cotton, eucalyptus, maize, potatoes, soybeans, sweet potatoes, tomatoes, wheat and a host of micro-organisms.
The trust's court papers describe requests dating back to July 1999 for information about licences granted for field trials with GMOs, the location of the trials and risk assessment documentation. Biowatch also asked to view records about compliance with the public participation provisions of the GMO
Act, and the geographical coordinates of field trials and crops approved for commercial release.
None of the requests elicited a "substantive" response and some information from GMO Registrar Shadrack Moephuli was "incorrect", the trust said.
Without access to the information, the trust said it was unable to determine if anyone was infringing the rights of the public under the Constitution, the GMO Act or international conventions.
The state attorney, acting for the respondents, filed notice of intention to oppose the application on August 28 and has until October 3 to file replying affidavits.
Attempts to elicit comment from Moephuli failed this week. He was more forthcoming last year: "We believe that as the regulators we can provide some of that information as best we can.
"It's important to realise that each application is assessed on the basis of the information that is currently available. We cannot predict what is likely to happen 20 years from now," Moephuli said.
Potential risks to the environment and human health lie at the heart of
Biowatch's application.
In its affidavit Biowatch states that some work done by genetic engineers, particularly in the medical field, may have potential benefits. "However, certain other experiments with, and releases of GMOs have been disastrously harmful."
The organisation cites an incident in the United States two years ago when modified maize for animal consumption that contained an insecticidal protein found its way into the human food chain. The event spawned nine class-action suits.
Biowatch also includes in its papers a copy of the British Medical
Association's recommendations on environmental precautions and public health risks. It suggests that countries adopt the precautionary principle in developing GMOs, because risks to the environment and health are not yet known; that a moratorium be placed on the commercial planting of GMOs until the effects on farming practices have been adequately ascertained; and that commercial secrecy should not take precedence over openness of information about public health.
In developing countries such as South Africa, the socio-economic effects on small-scale farmers' livelihoods is also important, Biowatch said.
"Terminator seed technology", engineered to stunt the reproductive capacity of seed, threatened communal seed banks. And it was questionable if small-scale farmers could afford to pay technology royalties to the GMO patent holders, it said.

Original_Search_Title: FAO Urges Assistance to Support Emergency Projects
Headline: Ethiopia: FAO Urges Assistance to Support Emergency Projects
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The UN Food and Agriculture Organisation has launched an appeal for nearly US $2 million to alleviate the plight of drought-affected people in Ethiopia.
"The food situation in several parts of Ethiopia remains alarming with millions of people suffering from drought," it said in a statement on Thursday.
The FAO noted there had been a large number of livestock deaths and the migration of people in search of water and pasture. The new appeal, for US $1.9 million, is aimed at supporting six emergency projects.
In particular, the FAO proposes to provide crucial veterinary instruments to poorly equipped veterinary services in Afar and Somali Regions. This project will benefit 30,000 pastoral families. In addition, supplementary feed will be supplied to breeding cows.
In large parts of Tigray Region, livestock deaths have been reported due to the drought and an increased spread of animal diseases. Through the purchase, transport and distribution of drugs, vaccines and veterinary equipment, FAO will help to ensure the recovery of the cattle herds. The beneficiaries will be about 5,000 poor rural households.
The organisation is also planning to provide severely drought-affected farmers with sweet potato cuttings. The sweet potato crop has a late planting season (October-November) and is therefore useful for farmers when rains are delayed. About 50,000 of the most destitute farm families will benefit from this project.
Another project aims to rehabilitate irrigation schemes in the most vulnerable crop producing areas. FAO will provide horticultural seeds and facilitate training on the better use of irrigation schemes for drought-affected communities. An estimated 20,000 poor farming families will benefit.
It also proposes providing 250 treadle pumps and 100 motor pumps for small-scale irrigation to farmers in Tigray Region to increase crop production and to improve the food security of about 5,000 drought-affected families.
"The projects aim to restore self-reliance of vulnerable rural families and reduce their dependence on food aid," the statement said. "FAO will work closely together with local authorities and non-governmental organisations."

Original_Search_Title: Heavy Rains Till Jan
Headline: Uganda: Heavy Rains Till Jan
Body_Text:
Kampala — The second rains have started in most parts of the country. Climate predictions indicate that the rains will be more than normally received in this season.
In most parts of the country, the rains are expected to steadily increase reaching flood levels by the middle of October. Due to the influence of the El Nino effect, the season will have heavy rains lasting until early January in many places.
While planting this season, farmers ought to take care to avoid low-lying areas prone to excessive flooding.
The crops of choice during this very wet season include coffee, bananas, vanilla and trees. take advantage of these heavy rains and establish a commercial tree plantation. The trees can be planted as a stand-alone plantation or you can hit two birds with one stone by establishing a mix of trees and other crops. In case you go for the agro-forestry, coffee, banana and vanilla are ideal crops, as they will perform very well.
These crops benefit a lot from the shade provided by the trees. However, when planting the trees sufficient spacing should be given between rows and within the rows. Where the trees are to be intercropped, the space is wider than when it is a tree plantation.
The idea is to allow sufficient light to get to the different crops to enable vigorous growth. However, it does not mean that because of the expected very wet season, other crops should not be grown. When planting crops like beans and other vegetables, make sure that they are on higher ground where flooding will not destroy them. It is important to note that water logging, even for a few days can completely destroy these crops.
Maize, millet, as well as sweet potatoes and cassava should also be planted on the higher part of the farmland. Avoid the swampy and marshy ground as much as possible. Grazing of livestock in lowlands should equally be avoided when the flooding of these lands increases. This way, your animals will not suffer from foot rot.

Original_Search_Title: Pig Rearing And Pork Market Up in Katakwi
Headline: Uganda: Pig Rearing And Pork Market Up in Katakwi
Body_Text:
Kampala — Pork consumption in Kumi district has gone up after the 1987-1992 war in Teso.
although the population of pigs was much higher before the war than now, people had a variety of livestock and livestock products to fall back to.
Dr. Alfred Onyait, the district veterinary officer, has said one of the major advantages, which the pigs had during the insurgency was that the Karamojong do not rustle pigs. They would slaughter a few pigs and eat them. Pigs cannot move long distances because of their sensitivity to heat. So, immediately after insurgency, the major source of animal protein throughout the district was pork. Onyait, in a recent interview, observed, "In our community, it is only Muslims who do not eat pork but they are a minority. The majority of the population estimated at more than 350,000 people eat pork, so the internal market is strong".
The other advantage he pointed out was that pigs are the only large animals which feed on household waste. Being a fast growing animal, one benefits from keeping them.
During a bumper harvest of agriculture produce, sweet potatoes are used to feed pigs instead of selling a bag at a give-away price of sh2000.
He said there are about 10,000 pigs in the district compared to over 90,000 head of cattle. The market demand for the pigs affects their population.

Original_Search_Title: August Inflation Drops
Headline: Uganda: August Inflation Drops
Body_Text:
Kampala — The annual headline inflation for the year ending August 2002 is minus 0.5 percent according to figures released by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS).
UBOS Executive Director J.B Male-Mukasa releasing the figures last week said this was lower than the minus 2.5 percent recorded for the year ended July 2002.
Mukasa speaking at the Ministry of Finance said that the monthly headline inflation for August rose by 0.9 percent compared to minus 0.6 percent recorded in July.
He said the annual underlying inflation rate for the year ending August 2002 rose to 0.7 percent compared to 0.2 percent for the year ended July 2002.
The monthly underlying inflation rate for August dropped by 0.1 percent compared to zero percent for July.
This is the twelfth consecutive month with negative inflation.
Inflation is determined by the Consumer Price Index (CPI) which is a measure of the changing costs of a fixed basket of goods and services used by households.
According to UBOS figures, prices for matooke rose in Kampala, Masaka, Mbarara and Gulu while they fell slightly in Jinja and Mbale.
Prices for fresh cassava, sweet potatoes and Irish potatoes also rose in Kampala, Jinja, Masaka and Mbarara but reduced in Mbale.
"The increases in prices are attributed to delayed harvests in South-Western Uganda while reductions in Mbale were due to increased supplies from the surrounding growing areas," Mukasa said.
Prices of tomatoes rose significantly in Kampala, Jinja, Mbale and Masaka while they reduced in Mbarara and Gulu while prices of fresh vegetables also rose in Kampala, Jinja, Masaka and Mbarara as a result of the prevailing dry spell.
The prices of local breed chicken rose in Kampala, Jinja, Mbarara and Gulu due to reduced supply to the market while the prevailing dry spell has led to low milk production, hence prices increases in Kampala, Masaka, Mbarara and Gulu.
The prices for fish fell in Mbarara and Gulu due to high supplies while they rose in Kampala, Jinja, Mbale and Masaka as a result of a low catch.

Original_Search_Title: Kenyans Master Art of Asking Stupid Questions
Headline: East Africa: Kenyans Master Art of Asking Stupid Questions
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kenyans like asking stupid questions and expect clever answers. That is
why a Kenyan will meet you sweeping and ask you, "Oh, so you are sweeping,
eh?" He asks the question in a manner that suggests that he expects you to
say, "Yes my good sir, I am sweeping and this thing in my hand is actually
called a broom,"
The right answer to that question should of course be, "No I am not
sweeping. What you see in my hand is actully a hoe and I am in the process
of digging up sweet potatoes for my dinner."
Another Kenyan sees you reading a book and he asks, "What are you
reading?" That Kenyan does not care that you are enjoying the book and that
he has no business interrupting that moment when the story is just about to
take a surprise turn. He asks that question in a manner to say that he
expects you to tell him the plot and the characters of the story.
The temptation is to tell the man, "I am reading nothing. I am just
counting the number of words in this book and if you don't mind, let me go
on with my business."
Later, you are washing you car and your neighbour who has always seen
the car asks, "Is this car a Toyota or a Mazda." The word Toyota is
prominent on the bonnet and behind and he can read that but because he is a
Kenyan, he asks that stupid question.
He asks the question in a way that says he expects you to tell him,
"Yes, this can is a Toyota and it was originally made in Nagoya. It was
driven by a Japanese called Samura Moto for two years. It has a 1300 cc
engine and four cylinders."
The temptation is to tell him, "No a Toyota does not look like this. In
fact, the word Toyota on the grille is a mistake. This is a Tata salon made
in Gulu. It has a 2000 cc engine and also Turbo charged. I am preparing it
for Muhangi to use in the next rally."
No one can ever mistake a Moi motorcade because it is preceeded by
escort cars that seem to be driven by drivers who smoke illegal
things. However, that will not stop a Kenyan from asking another, "Is that
Moi passing?" He expects such a reply as, "Yes, it is him. He was dressed in
a gray suit and a carnation on his lapel. He was escorted by racing drivers
with their sirens blaring."
You are tempted to tell him, "No that was not Moi passing. It was
actually the Sultan of Brunei who has borrowed Moi's motorcade to do a bit
of touring. You see, the Sultan of Brunei is so poor that he has to depend
on charity to move from place to place."
The most irritating question is asked when the lift stops at a
floor. The direction indicator button is showing very clearly that the lift
is going up but that will not stop a Kenyan from asking, "Is this lift going
up or down?"
The fellow expects you to tell him, "Although the indicator button says
that the lift is going up, it is actually going down. You see, these
machines have a habit of changing their minds and that is why its button
says that it is going up."
You are really tempted to tell him, "No this lift does not go up or
down. It just goes sideways."
On another occasion you meet Kenyan whom you had last seen twenty fours
before and he asks you, "So you are still there?" He expects you to tell
him, "Oh yes, this is actually my body, the very one that I had yesterday."
In reality you feel like telling him, "No I am not there or here. The
person you are seeing is my shadow. I will however be there this afternoon."
In short, when a Kenyan asks a stupid question, I feel like giving a
stupid answer. I
By the way, is this The Monitor newspaper that you are reading?

Original_Search_Title: Investors Sought for Small Agro-Industrial Projects
Headline: Mozambique: Investors Sought for Small Agro-Industrial Projects
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican government launched a
programme on Wednesday to promote 14 small scale agro-industrial
projects to be implemented in six of the country's 11 provinces.
To identify these projects, the Agriculture Ministry,
through its Commercial Agriculture Promotion Office (GPSCA),
launched a study in Inhambane, Sofala, Zambezia, Nampula and Cabo
Delgado provinces in the first half of this year, into the
possibility of processing several crops, including maize, rice,
cassava, sweet potatoes, beans, oilseeds, cashew, coconut, and
fruit.
According to Deputy Agriculture Minister Joao Carrilho, the
idea is to promote small and medium sized sustainable agro-
industries in the rural areas.
The study, conducted by International Capital Corporation
(ICC), a local consultancy company, was to identify the potential
for the establishment of those industries in each province, and
also to determine their technical and commercial feasibility.
Another aspect of the study was to define concrete actions
by the state and the public sector to facilitate the
implementation of such projects. It produced recommendations on
cooperation mechanisms between the various institutions that
might be involved.
Some of the 14 projects, for which the government is now
seeking investors, include a coconut processing industry, with a
capacity to handle 3,000 tonnes a year, dry fruit processing, and
a rice dehusking unit, all in Zambezia, and the production of
distilled liquor from cashew fruit in Nampula province.
Also on the list is the establishment of a new cashew nut
processing unit in Nampula. The viability of this, however, must
be in doubt, given the closure of most cashew factories in
Nampula, following the World Bank imposed liberalisation of the
trade in cashews, which deprived them of their raw material. 
 For Niassa province, the study recommended a medium size
plant to mill local maize, and a unit to can and export beans.
For Inhambane the idea is to instal a tangerine processing unit,
while Cabo Delgado was found to be ideal for the opening of a
sunflower processing factory.
The meeting to divulge the results of the study took place
in the southern city of Matola, and brought together
representatives of the government, business people, and members
of the donor community.
During the meeting, some of the business people expressed
concern over the fact that the agricultural sector has been
marginalised by investors, who regard it as a high risk sector,
and do not believe in the feasibility of some of the projects,
both in terms of the supply of raw materials, and in financial
terms.
They also noted that to ensure good quality products, the
government should pay more attention to agricultural research and
extension. 
 One of the ICC technicians, who took part in the study,
agreed with the business people's point of view. He said the
study team found that if investors in such projects are to take
out bank loans and generate any profit, the interest rate should
be between 12 and 15 per cent, compared with the current rates
charged by the commercial banks of 42 to 48 per cent.
Faced with such rapacious interest rates, they could only
recommend that investors avoid Mozambican banks, and use their
own money, or, where possible, obtain hard currency loans from
foreign banks.
"The high cost of bank loans leads investors to use their
own financial resources to fund most of the agro-industries",
said Rui Santana Afonso, of the ICC.
He added that if those projects succeed, the products will
be sold on the Southern African regional market, "which is closer
and less demanding in terms of quality, compared with the
European market".

Original_Search_Title: Cotton Production Up Again in Kumi District Kumi
Headline: Uganda: Cotton Production Up Again in Kumi District Kumi
Body_Text:
Cotton production in Kumi district is picking up again after price drops in the 2000/2001 season.
However, this year's production has been affected by drought from April to June. Normally, cotton is planted in May if one wants a good harvest. The district gets rains from early March to June and from August to October.
Mr. Valdo Odeke, the district agriculture officer, Kumi, said, "It has been dry most of the time. You just get sporadic rains. Labour is also affecting cotton growing. It is labour intensive. You need to weed about three or four times. You have to pick and sort cotton. As compared to other years, it is now picking up."
He said cotton acreage increased from the late 1980s until 2000/2001 when prices fell and acreage was reduced. The prices fell from sh350 to sh300 in 2000/2001 and that discouraged the farmers.
However, the trend is that cotton production is growing. In 1999, there were 11,828 acres, while in 2000, production went down to 4,114 acres because of low prices. In 2001, it rose to 5,492 acres. Cotton has been one of the major cash crops in Teso region since colonial times. In the mid-1970s, the cotton industry broke down and cotton production declined. Teso Co-operative Union was a monopoly of cotton buying in the region, but when the industry broke down, they began taking cotton on credit. When government liberalised purchase of produce, many players came to buy cotton. That gave people the enthusiasm to grow it. However, another factor, which contributed to a reduction in cotton growing, was competition from other non-traditional cash crops like sweet potatoes and groundnuts, and most recently, sunflower.
With the help of the Cotton Development Organisation, (CDO), the district agriculture office managed to put people to produce cotton in groups. Some people have planted cotton in spite of the little rains and some are still planting. Odeke said, "It is already late. Otherwise, cotton should be planted from mid-May to mid- June. So we are not very certain whether we would get good yields unless the rains come as they say. They are talking of El Nino rains coming in September. That is what is encouraging people to plant".

Original_Search_Title: Couple Given Five Hours to Go
Headline: Zimbabwe: Couple Given Five Hours to Go
Body_Text:
An elderly couple were last Friday ordered to leave their son's Ealing Farm in Mvurwi within five hours by a woman war veteran named as Kiriveria Kauredza. Their son, Mick Marffy, was away in Chirundu at the time.
The farm has been compulsorily acquired by the government but Marffy is challenging the case in the High Court. According to Marffy, Kauredza occupied his elderly parents' house after giving them five hours to pack their belongings and leave. Marffy said: "Kauredza informed a domestic employee she intended to move into the main house and the family should remove their personal belongings. "Since my family and I were not present, my wife called in a removals company," aid Marffy. The eviction came a week after Marffy had agreed to sell his farm equipment to the settlers on the farm. The settlers agreed he could move off his farm at the end of September after winding up his business. Marffy was grading a tobacco crop valued at $28 million and has a $1,2 million sweet potato crop in the ground, all of which could be lost after the work stoppage.

Original_Search_Title: Oxfam Aid Underway
Headline: Southern Africa: Oxfam Aid Underway
Body_Text:
Much needed seed kits, income-generating projects and vital deliveries of emergency food are among the projects Oxfam has introduced in response to the Southern African food crisis.
In Zimbabwe, where controversial land reform has ordered 2,800 commercial farmers off the land, Oxfam has devised a series of programmes to provide relief for the estimated six million Zimbabweans at risk of starvation.
Next week, Oxfam Great Britain (Oxfam-GB) will begin a pilot distribution of food kits of maize, oil and sugar beans in the Zvishavane district in the Midlands for about 11,000 people, an Oxfam statement said.
Larger distributions of World Food Programme (WFP) supplied food are planned for September to 110,000 people in three other Midlands districts.
From September, Oxfam-Canada would provide supplementary feeding to 71,000 children under five, 21,000 pregnant or breast feeding women, 116,000 school children and 5,500 elderly people.
Oxfam-GB has planned summer cropping projects for five districts which would involve the distribution of equipment and water management schemes. Existing livelihood programmes would be expanded in three of the districts to help people develop different coping mechanisms for the drought. This would include purchasing livestock, replacing lost stock and also fish farming.
To help women supplement their farming income, Oxfam's American partner The Association of Women Clubs (AWC) has set up income generating schemes including candle making, tie-dye fabric design and peanut butter making. This is supported by a revolving micro-credit scheme where women can borrow small amounts to start income-generating projects.
In Zambia, where the government has also declared a food disaster, Oxfam-supplied seeds would be planted next week as part of a winter cropping programme. This also involves the distribution of tools, and plans are also being finalised for cash-for-work schemes to build facilities like dip tanks, water tanks for livestock and to clear canals.
In Malawi, Oxfam-GB has become the co-coordinating agency for distributing WFP-supplied food in the Mulanje district and would distribute to up to 120,000 beneficiaries by September. Oxfam would continue distributing food until April.
To decrease households' reliance on food aid, 90,000 households in three districts would receive seeds and cuttings consisting of maize, soya beans, cassava, groundnuts, sweet potato and vegetables, as well as fertiliser. Top help optimise production 450 water pumps and 7,500 watering cans would be distributed.
In Mozambique, Oxfam-Canada has planned to supply drought resistant varieties of sweet potato and cassava to 15,000 people in the Gaza and Maputo provinces, through local partner Action for Rural Development and Environment. This programme would also include HIV/AIDS awareness activities.

Original_Search_Title: National Assembly to Upgrade Bio-Safety Guidelines
Headline: Nigeria: National Assembly to Upgrade Bio-Safety Guidelines
Body_Text:
Abuja — To ensure that Nigerians have access to safe and profitable uses of biotech-based products and services, the National Assembly would soon upgrade the bio-safety guidelines into law.
The Chief Executive Officer of the National Bio-technology Development Agency (NABDA), Professor Emeka Omaliko, at a press briefing in Abuja yesterday said bio-safety guidelines would be upgraded by the National Assembly into law to exercise cautionary steps involved in the potential dangers associated with genetic products.
Omaliko said NABDA, in collaboration with the Women Trafficking and Child Labour Eradication Foundation (WOTCLEF) and the National Poverty Eradication Programme (NAP EP) had articulated a one-day sensitisation workshop on Bio-tech for Women Empowerment, to be held Thursday in Abuja.
Ten representatives from each of the 36 states and the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja would be led to the workshopby the wives of governors of each state.
The keynote address would be delivered by Dr. Florence Wambugu, the first African woman to have successfully genetically transformed sweet potatoes, which is now helping women in East Africa while African-American Bio-technologist, Dr. Pamela Bryant, would also be in attendance.
Omaliko noted that biotechnology is a key feature for driving the sustainable growth of any country's economy in the 21st century adding, "it can be applied in the development of food and agriculture, healthcare, industry, and environment sectors of the economy, resulting in the creation of jobs and wealth for women and youths.
He said the role women play in the economic development of the nation cannot be over-emphasised, adding the aim of the workshop is to intimate women on the potentials of biotech as an economic tool that will economically empower them, and keep them away from social vices.
"Some African countries that have involved their women in biotechnology have recorded success stories, where women have been able to establish biotech enterprises and thus improved their economic power. It is therefore necessary that Nigeria be not left out in improving the economies of women and advertently, the family and the nation at large," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Italy Donates Food for Drought Victims
Headline: Mozambique: Italy Donates Food for Drought Victims
Body_Text:
Maputo — About 800 tonnes of rice, donated by the Italian government, is being
distributed among the victims of drought in several districts of the
southern Mozambican province of Inhambane.
Inhambane governor Aires Aly told AIM that the food is being distributed
in nine districts, namely Massinga, Mabote, Funhalouro, Inhassoro,
Vilankulo, Govuro, Panda, Homoine, and Inharrime.
The Inhambane government authorities estimate that some 300,200 people
are suffering from drought-related food shortages, mostly in the northern
region of the province.  For its part, the World Food Programme (WFP) says
it is prepared to grant further food supplies for some of these people,
through "food for work" projects, involving building or rehabilitating
schools, health units, roads and water sources.
About 60 per cent of this year's harvest in Inhambane was lost to the
drought. The biggest losses were in maize and peanuts which, together with
cassava, provide the staple diet for the Inhambane population.
Aly said that in addition to food, his government is also distributing
seeds and other agricultural inputs for the 2002/2003 planting season.
"It has rained in Inhambane and we are working to ensure that the
agriculture sector will get the inputs to the people", he said, dismissing
allegations according to which the government is denying assistance to
drought victims.  "All the affected districts are receiving support", he
said.  He said that three irrigation systems are being rehabilitated, to
minimise the effects of drought, and people are being encouraged to plant
drought-resistant crops, such as cassava and sweet potatoes.
To illustrate his point, Aly said that, for instance, the rehabilitation
of one irrigation system, covering 150 hectares, has been completed in
Massinga district, with funding from Mozambique's National Agricultural
Development Programme (PROAGRI), but it is not being fully exploited for the
shortage of hands to use it.

Original_Search_Title: Shurugwi Widow Wins Crop Farming Contest
Headline: Zimbabwe: Shurugwi Widow Wins Crop Farming Contest
Body_Text:
Seed Co Limited 2001/2002 National Crop Farming Competition winner,
Gladys Mavavire, 60, says her secret to farming is planting and planning
early.
A widow, Mavavire told agriculture industry officials during the
competition presentation ceremony at Seed Co Head office in Stapleford: "I
advise farmers to plant early and one will be guaranteed that they will
harvest something.
"Despite the drought in the past season, I harvested 21 tonnes of maize
and 20 tonnes of groundnuts because I planted as early as October."
Mavavire, a communal farmer, operates in Mutsa Village in Shurugwi.
Many communal farmers who planted last in the last season were caught
unawares by a mid-season dry spell from December to March and their crops
were a complete write-off.
"My husband, Kefas, passed away in January this year, and I thank God I
have no hunger this year despite the fact that he is no longer there," she
said.
Mavavire said she would not sell her maize this year because she has a
large family of 26 including her children and grandchildren.
She is assisted by her daughter Vongai, sons Edward and Tomsen in her
farming operations. She grows maize, groundnuts, sugar beans, rapoko, sweet
potatoes, rice and bambara nuts.
Out of a total of eight provincial finalists, Mavavire emerged the
winner in the competition which is open to small-scale, communal and
resettlement farmers countrywide.
The criteria used to judge the farmers include livestock management,
selection of seed varieties, timing of farming operations, yields, soil
conservation, record-keeping, farming implements, homestead outlook and
farming knowledge.
In their report on Mavavire, competition judges said: "The farmer
improved the sandy soils by incorporating anthill soil and compost. There
was generally good management of a largely Mashona herd. Crop and livestock
records and inventory of farming equipment and tools were kept.
"The income and expenditure records were well-interpreted."
The Seed Co competition, done in conjunction with Lands, Agriculture and
Rural Resettlement Ministry extension workers, was launched during the
1982/83 cropping season.

Original_Search_Title: FAO Approves US $237,000 IDPs, Refugees Project
Headline: Liberia: FAO Approves US $237,000 IDPs, Refugees Project
Body_Text:
Monrovia — The United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization has approved a
project valued at US$237,000.
The project titled "Emergency Provision of Agricultural Inputs to
Refugees, Host Communities and Internally Displaced Farmers in
Montserrado County," is to support the efforts of the Liberian
Government to provide emergency agricultural assistance to
war-affected farmers.
According to a release from the FAO, the project, aimed at
encouraging sustainable livelihood activities through food production
endeavors was signed over the weekend between the Liberian Government
and FAO.
Agriculture Minister, Dr. Roland Massaquoi signed on behalf of the
Liberian Government while FAO Resident Representative in Liberia, Dr.
Castro Camarada signed on behalf of Dr. Jacques Diouf, FAO Director
General.
The project will provide essential agricultural inputs such as
assorted farm tools, rice and assorted vegetable seeds, fertilizers,
groundnuts and sweet potato cuttings.
"It is designed to support food production activities and reduce
dependency on food aid as a measure of livelihood and strengthen the
beneficiaries earning power through the sale of their farm products,"
the release said.
An estimated seven thousand farm families including 2,000 refugees
families, 4,000 displaced farm families and 1,000 families in the
host communities have been targeted to benefit from this project.
The Ministry of Agriculture, FAO and implementing NGOs, comprising
the coordinating committee of the agriculture sector will coordinate
project activities, while assistance to refugee will be coordinated
with the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR).
The mechanism for the provision of land to IDPs and refugees for
cultivation purposes will be arranged and organized by relevant
government authorities in ensuring that lands are indisputably
contested, but safe and convenient for use by the IDPs and refugees.
Dr. Massaquoi has meanwhile assured FAO and the collaborating
implementing partners that all will be done to ensure the successful
implementation of the project.
He indicated that host families equally need emergency agricultural
assistance as they are considered food insecure, and the presence of
IDPs and refugees have put pressure on their limited resources, the
release concluded.

Original_Search_Title: Refugees Returning, But Little Aid Available
Headline: Angola: Refugees Returning, But Little Aid Available
Body_Text:
"This is my motherland," said Pedro Mtondo, 27. "When I went to Zambia I went because of the war and now that I see my country is okay I want to come back - so I can do something so they can have a better life here."
Mtondo lives in a field of grass huts on the edge of Cazombo - a tiny administrative centre at the easternmost reaches of Angola. He is one of the thousands of Angolans, once exiled by the civil war, who have taken the first opportunity to come home.
It's a movement that neither the local authorities nor the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR) had time to prepare for.
Assistant High Commissioner Kamel Morjane recently headed a UNHCR delegation to Angola, during which he announced a plan to repatriate an estimated 470,000 Angolan refugees, beginning in January 2003. But he also said that between 8,000 and 10,000 former refugees were believed to have returned to Angola since the signing of a ceasefire between the Angolan government and the UNITA rebels on 4 April.
The people arriving in Cazombo - most of them from the Mahewa refugee camp in western Zambia - fall into this category.
Morjane added that a total of 80,000 were expected to return "spontaneously" before the UNHCR's repatriation plan begins. Asked whether there were plans to assist these returnees, Morjane said: "They will go back to their places of origin and certainly it will be difficult to organise [help for them], especially when there is nobody there yet to organise it - neither from the government side, neither the humanitarian agencies and certainly not UNHCR."
When IRIN visited Cazombo, 1,000 had already arrived, and at least a further 2,000 were said to be at the border awaiting transport to the town.
There's singing and dancing in Cazombo's one and only street whenever an army truck pulls in, carrying newly-returned refugees from the border. A single 25 kg sack of maize, provided by the government, is shared out among 60 people or more.
