Farmers must also plant cash crops like coffee, tea, cotton and sugar-cane. Then there is the planting of other crops like beans, groundnuts and soybeans. In addition it is advisable to plant vegetables like cabbages, tomatoes and onions.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Should Compete
Headline: Uganda: Farmers Should Compete
Body_Text:
Without dispute agriculture is the driving engine of the national economy. Here as in the rest of Africa the small-scale farmers dominate the farming industry.
Every passing day the small-scale farmers are struggling to stay afloat. Even for farmers who are on relatively big pieces of land, growing bigger gardens of crops is no longer the universal solution to profitable farming.

Original_Search_Title: He Has HIV, She is Negative, But They Are Getting Married
Headline: Uganda: He Has HIV, She is Negative, But They Are Getting Married
Body_Text:
HE has HIV; she does not. But these two are set to wed in church this Saturday, March 16.
For the last 11 years, Benard Nzabandora, 46, and Esther Nanyombi, 35, have been cohabiting well knowing that they are in a discordant relationship.

Original_Search_Title: New Maize Variety to Boost Nutrition
Headline: Uganda: New Maize Variety to Boost Nutrition
Body_Text:
The ministry of agriculture has drawn a national strategy to encourage farmers grow crops with high nutritional values.
NARO scientists have undertaken research to increase the contents of nutrients in some crops so that they can offer a balanced diet in the human body.

Original_Search_Title: New Maize Variety to Boost Nutrition
Headline: Uganda: New Maize Variety to Boost Nutrition
Body_Text:
The QPM crop locally called Nalongo, is rich in Vitamin A
The ministry of agriculture has drawn a national strategy to encourage farmers grow crops with high nutritional values.

Original_Search_Title: Once a Village Porter Now Pineapple Tsar of Mityana
Headline: Uganda: Once a Village Porter Now Pineapple Tsar of Mityana
Body_Text:
Gaston Baguma strutted through the neat rows of pineapples with gusto. The prickly jutting leaves splayed into a canopy that brushed gently at his wide waist and protected his lower body from the harsh rays of the noonday sun.
Baguma, who is of average height and portly stature, quickly made his way through the rows of pineapples, his distended belly parting the leaves as he moved on.

Original_Search_Title: Start Planting, Farmers Told
Headline: Uganda: Start Planting, Farmers Told
Body_Text:
THE Commissioner for Agricultural Planning, Rhoda Tumusiime, has told farmers to start planting for the next season.
Tumusiime said yesterday that weather experts had confirmed that the rain season has started.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Recorded At Minus 3.2 %
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Recorded At Minus 3.2 %
Body_Text:
Uganda has for the sixth consecutive month registered negative inflation with the annual headline inflation rate for the year ending February 2002 recorded at -3.2 percent compared to -3.6 percent recorded for the year ended 2001.
The latest Consumer Price index (CPI) report issued by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS) yesterday stated that the monthly headline inflation rate for Feb. dropped by 0.1 percent compared to a decrease of 0.9 percent recorded for January 2002.

Original_Search_Title: Ex-Minister Promises Potato Market
Headline: Uganda: Ex-Minister Promises Potato Market
Body_Text:
Former state minister for gender, Mrs. Albina Opio has promised to secure better market for sweet potatoes widely grown in Teso region.
Mrs. Opio, who was recently elected woman councillor for Kachumbala sub-county, in Kumi, said she would use contacts she made as minister and resident district commissioner(RDC) to look for markets in other parts of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Should Get Their Own Markets
Headline: Uganda: Farmers Should Get Their Own Markets
Body_Text:
Individuals and companies have connected the producer and consumer
A few years ago, hundreds of households in rural areas could not feed themselves throughout the year.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Production Increased By 30 Percent
Headline: Rwanda: Crop Production Increased By 30 Percent
Body_Text:
Abundant rainfall in Rwanda, especially in the central and eastern regions, has produced a 30 percent overall increase in crop production in the period from September to January in comparison with the same period the previous year, the Famine and Early Warning System (FEWSNET) reported last week.
Despite the large increase, however, the calorie and protein equivalents of the crops produced increased by only 24 percent, due to a lower increase in higher-value crops such as cereals and legumes, as compared to bananas, sweet potatoes and cassava, FEWSNET added.

Original_Search_Title: KWS Told To Rein In Rogue Elephants
Headline: Kenya: KWS Told To Rein In Rogue Elephants
Body_Text:
Residents of Makueni District have appealed to the Kenya Wildlife Service to control elephants which have destroyed property worth millions of shillings.
The asked the game officials to help them drive away wild animals which struck Nguu division last Wednesday.

Original_Search_Title: Grow More Yellow Potatoes
Headline: Uganda: Grow More Yellow Potatoes
Body_Text:
If sickness could be prevented, this country would save billions of shillings each year.
Treatment is expensive and many people especially in the rural areas fall sick and die.

Original_Search_Title: Chief Spearheads Anti-Aids War
Headline: Zambia: Chief Spearheads Anti-Aids War
Body_Text:
A TRADITIONAL ruler in Chienge district in Luapula Province has called for Government support in the fight against the spread of the HIV/AIDS campaign, which he is spearheading in his chiefdom.
Senior Chief Puta told Zambia Information Services in an interview in Mansa that he has formed an HIV/AIDS awareness committee but lacks logistical support from the Ministry of Health.

Original_Search_Title: Eating Out - Tremendous Taverna Athina
Headline: Zimbabwe: Eating Out - Tremendous Taverna Athina
Body_Text:
Harare — ABSOLUTELY noone's indispensable. This was proved when I visited Taverna Athina, for the first time
since Mano Babaliokis left for the Gulf.
Three new partners, Greek and non-Greek, were all away, said manager
Roger Woodward-who with ready smile and Falstaffian figure, looks and
acts like a restaurateur.

Original_Search_Title: Dar Ready to Boost Trading
Headline: Uganda: Dar Ready to Boost Trading
Body_Text:
BUKOBA District Commissioner in Tanzania, John Gabriel Tuppa, has said Tanzania has launched an infrastructure-renovation campaign to revitalise agricultural trade between Tanzania and Uganda.
He said they are constructing tarmac roads around Kyotera, Mutukula up to Bukoba to create easy and quick access to Tanzania.

Original_Search_Title: Locals Recall the Royal Rise
Headline: Kenya: Locals Recall the Royal Rise
Body_Text:
As                Queen Elizabeth II of England marks 50 years on the throne, Kenyans                and settlers who witnessed the dramatic change in status of the                newly-married Princess share their recollections. This was at a                Nyeri hotel.
Her ascension to the throne          is the stuff that fairy tales are made of. Fifty years ago today, while          on a Kenya tour at the famous Treetops game lodge, Nyeri, the then Princess          Elizabeth sought refuge in the hotel when a rogue elephant came calling.

Original_Search_Title: Chillies Exports Go Up
Headline: Uganda: Chillies Exports Go Up
Body_Text:
Uganda's exports of fresh produce, particular chillies, have gone up in volume by over 300,000 kilogrammes in 2001, compared to the previous year. A recent report states that the increase is due to greater co-ordination between exporters, freight handlers and facilitating NGOs and relevant produce dealers.
Figures obtained by the Agribusiness Development Centre (ADC) of the exports which went through Entebbe International Airport show that 2.3 million kilogrammes were exported in the year 2001 compared to 2m kgs, in 2000.

Original_Search_Title: Food Production On Decline - Report
Headline: Uganda: Food Production On Decline - Report
Body_Text:
There has been a decline in the production of frequently harvested food crops in the past five years, a national crop survey has shown.
According to the Uganda National Household Survey 1999-2000 released yesterday by the Uganda Bureau of Statistics (UBOS), there has been a decline in the production of matooke, sweet potatoes and cassava.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Crop Promising
Headline: Malawi: Maize Crop Promising
Body_Text:
Blantyre — Government has said prospects look good for a good harvest of maize
this season if the rainfall pattern continues at the present pace and
farmers care for their crop.
Agriculture Minister Aleke Banda spoke yesterday when he briefed
reporters on the outlook of the crop across the country.

Original_Search_Title: Murder Suspect Hospitalized
Headline: Malawi: Gwazantini Hospitalised
Body_Text:
Blantyre — Alice Gwazantini who is facing murder charges for allegedly scalding
to death her maid Ntchayi Jakisoni, is currently admitted at
Mwaiwathu Private Hospital where she will undergo an operation to
remove a cyst in her stomach this week.
Confirming the development in a telephone interview on Saturday from
Zomba, Malawi Prisons Public Relations Officer Tobias Nowa said
Gwazantini was initially hospitalised for asthma some two weeks ago.

Original_Search_Title: Rural Aids Orphans Abandoned
Headline: Uganda: Rural Aids Orphans Abandoned
Body_Text:
A famous African proverb "An elephant can never fail to carry it's tusks" has been passed on from generation to generation. Often, this happens during nights when children assemble by the hearth to be told stories about life.
However, economic constraints have today turned the African culture upside down. The tusks have become too heavy for the elephant. The role of parenting is fast shifting to the media, especially the radio, newspapers, television and computers.

Original_Search_Title: Bundibugyo Gets Village Bank
Headline: Uganda: Bundibugyo Gets Village Bank
Body_Text:
A village bank; Abanyarwenzori Mountaineering Association (AMA) Micro-Credit and Savings Trust has been opened in Bundibugyo district.
The bank is aimed at instilling a culture of saving in the local community.

Original_Search_Title: No Entry in EAC for Poor Quality
Headline: East Africa: No Entry in EAC for Poor Quality
Body_Text:
Kampala — Companies whose products have not been given quality certificates may be barred from exporting to the region the Common East African Market opens in 2004.
"Unless products bear the quality mark, they will not be allowed free access [under the EAC protocol on standards and quality], " said Dr. Terry Kahuma, executive director of the Uganda National Bureau of Standards.

Original_Search_Title: Project to Grow Drought Tolerant Crops Commences
Headline: Zimbabwe: Project to Grow Drought Tolerant Crops Commences
Body_Text:
Harare — The Zimbabwe Farmers Union in partnership with some agro-dealers has started a project to grow drought tolerant crops in some the country's dry areas.
The plan to grow drought tolerant crops was hatched at the beginning of the season when it was realised that the country could receive between normal and below normal rains.

Original_Search_Title: ZFU, Agro-Dealers Embark On Drought Resistant Crop Project
Headline: Zimbabwe: ZFU, Agro-Dealers Embark On Drought Resistant Crop Project
Body_Text:
Harare — THE Zimbabwe Farmers Union and some agro-dealers have embarked on a project to grow drought resistant crops in selected dry regions.
ZFU and its partners hatched the project at the beginning of the season after it became clear that Zimbabwe would receive between normal and below normal rains.

Original_Search_Title: More States to Benefit From New Crops Project
Headline: Nigeria: More States to Benefit From New Crops Project
Body_Text:
Abuja, Fct — The country Director of Sasakawa Global 2000 Project in Nigeria, Dr. Jose Valencia, has given the assurance that more states would benefit from the scheme. He said there were plans to expand the project, which at inception in 1992 was concentrated in Kano and Jigawa states later expanded to Bauchi and Gombe.
The Sasakawa Global Project is a crop improvement scheme.

Original_Search_Title: UN Warns of Further Drought
Headline: Mozambique: UN Warns of Further Drought
Body_Text:
London — The United Nations on Tuesday warned that crop yields will be hit in
southern and central Mozambique if rains do not resume within the next 20
days.
In the latest issue of the "Mozambique Humanitarian Situation Monitor",
the UN points out that, after a severe lack of rain over the last 20 days,
there are already signs of stress in maize crops. Prospects are bleak - as
shown by the three monthly prediction from the NOAA/Climate Prediction
Centre, which warns of the likelihood of rains below normal in the centre
and south of the country.

Original_Search_Title: Kiliku Broke Our Election Agreement, Claims Nyachae
Headline: Kenya: Kiliku Broke Our Election Agreement, Claims Nyachae
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ford People presidential candidate Simeon Nyachae yesterday launched a scathing attack on National Labour Party leader Kennedy Kiliku for alleged breach of a poll contract.
Addressing a rally at St Theresa's Secondary School grounds in Bahari Constituency, Kilifi District, Mr Nyachae said Mr Kiliku approached him and requested that the two parties forge a united front in the run-up to the General Election.

Original_Search_Title: The Eagle Lands At the River Nile
Headline: Uganda: The Eagle Lands At the River Nile
Body_Text:
Kampala — At about 2:00pm, the Eagle flapped its wings and flew out of its nest. And guess what, it just landed into people's stomachs.
Last Friday was the day the Nile Breweries decided to come up with yet another quality brand in the name of Eagle Lager. For you people who were here in the 1970s, you definitely remember Serena. Eagle beer is just like Serena beer only that this time, it's clean drinking, we were ably informed. Eagle is another beer made out of local sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Nile Breweries Contracts 200 to Grow Sorghum
Headline: Uganda: Nile Breweries Contracts 200 to Grow Sorghum
Body_Text:
Kampala — FARMERS in Teso region, contracted by Nile Breweries Ltd (NBL) to grow sorghum for beer brewing, have appealed to the company to be timely in availing them seeds for planting so as to produce quality grain.
They have also complained about the high rate of pests and birds attacking the crop in the gardens and urged NBL to consider costs farmers face in handling pests and birds when it is fixing the prices for buying the grain from them.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Encouraged to Diversify Crops
Headline: Mozambique: Farmers Encouraged to Diversify Crops
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — With 590,000 people said to need food aid due to drought, it has been suggested that sorghum could replace maize as the staple crop in the semi-arid southern and central areas of Mozambique.
Due to insufficient rain in the southern and central regions, crop production fell dramatically this year.

Original_Search_Title: Commodity Exchange Woos Small Farmers
Headline: Uganda: Commodity Exchange Woos Small Farmers
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Uganda Commodity Exchange is set to carry out its first trade in January, some four years after it was registered as an entity.
The exchange will link growers and buyers directly, in theory increasing the money small farmers get for their produce, as it cuts out the middlemen, Johnny Ssempebwa, assistant manager at Uganda Commodity Exchange, says.

Original_Search_Title: Wheat Imports Urgently Needed Following Poor Harvest
Headline: Zimbabwe: Wheat Imports Urgently Needed Following Poor Harvest
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Zimbabwe would run out of wheat by the end of February unless 240,000 mt of the grain was imported to cover this year's lower-than-expected wheat harvest, the Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS NET) said in its latest report.
And NGOs and relief agencies have to step up efforts to provide relief food to the 6,7 million people in Zimbabwe whose food security is under threat, it warned.

Original_Search_Title: Rising Prices Erode Household Food Security
Headline: Zambia: Rising Prices Erode Household Food Security
Body_Text:
JOHANNESBURG — Rising prices of staples continued to erode household food security in some of the most insecure countries in Southern Africa, the Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS NET) said in its latest update.
FEWS NET said the outlook for prices of staples was cause for concern as prices were likely to "remain high and increase until the next harvest [around March/April 2003], which will worsen food access for an increasing number of households in Zimbabwe, Malawi and Zambia, as well as parts of Lesotho, Swaziland and Mozambique".

Original_Search_Title: Mugabe: 'Land Reform Successful Despite Challenges'
Headline: Zimbabwe: Mugabe: 'Land Reform Successful Despite Challenges'
Body_Text:
Harare — The ruling Zanu-PF party annual People's Conference begins today in
Chinhoyi, Mashonaland West province. A number of issues are expected to come
up for discussion as the party reviews events of this year and maps out
strategies for the coming year. Undoubtedly, the land issue would be on top
of the agenda. Our Political Reporter, Lovemore Mataire, recently spoke to
President Mugabe to get an insight into this issue and various other issues
affecting the country.
Your Excellency, we are now coming to the end of the year, a year
that was not only eventful but also full of challenges. From the perspective
of the party and that of the Government what can you say were the major
challenges, especially on the issue of land?

Original_Search_Title: Poor Rains Raise Concerns Over Next Harvest
Headline: Southern Africa: Poor Rains Raise Concerns Over Next Harvest
Body_Text:
Lagos — Farmers and food security specialists in Southern Africa are increasingly concerned that the lack of rain during the current planting season could mean another bad harvest for the region next year. Malawi, Mozambique, Zimbabwe, Zambia, Lesotho and Swaziland already have more than 14 million people in need of food aid after poor harvests at the beginning of this year. Desperate farmers, many of whom will use donated seed and fertiliser, are pinning their hopes of recovery on next year's crops, but so far rainfall figures have been worryingly low.
According to the latest Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS NET) drought and flood hazards assessment, most of the region has only recorded between 1 and 10 mm of rain with "substantially inhibited" rainfall over portions of South Africa, Zimbabwe and central to southern Mozambique. This includes South Africa's maize triangle, which usually supplies surrounding countries and contributes to relief agencies' stocks.

Original_Search_Title: Usaid Provides Support To World Food Program's Africa Operation
Headline: Africa: Usaid Provides Support To World Food Program's Africa Operation
Body_Text:
Lagos — From now through 2003, up to 35 million people in Africa will need food aid. The United States Agency for International Development (USAID) is working with its partners--the World Food Program, other donors, and the private voluntary community--to deliver up to 2.75 million metric tons of food to help meet this need.
Further, according to agency report, USAID is working with the governments to address longer-term structural problems, and to this end is launching four new special initiatives this year in Africa in agriculture, trade, education, and good governance.

Original_Search_Title: Worries Mount Over Rains
Headline: Zimbabwe: Worries Mount Over Rains
Body_Text:
Farmers  and food security specialists in southern Africa are increasingly concerned that the lack of rain during the current planting season could mean another bad harvest for the region next year.
Malawi, Mozambique, Zimbabwe, Zambia, Lesotho and Swaziland already have more than 14 million people in need of food aid after poor harvests at the beginning of this year.

Original_Search_Title: No Rain, Next Harvest Threatened
Headline: Southern Africa: Poor Rains Raise Concerns Over Next Harvest
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Farmers and food security specialists in Southern Africa are increasingly concerned that the lack of rain during the current planting season could mean another bad harvest for the region next year.
Malawi, Mozambique, Zimbabwe, Zambia, Lesotho and Swaziland already have more than 14 million people in need of food aid after poor harvests at the beginning of this year. Desperate farmers, many of whom will use donated seed and fertiliser, are pinning their hopes of recovery on next year's crops, but so far rainfall figures have been worryingly low.

Original_Search_Title: New Cotton Prices to Boost Output
Headline: Uganda: New Cotton Prices to Boost Output
Body_Text:
NEW FARMGATE prices for cotton announced last week are expected to encourage farmers to expand their fields and raise production, Cotton Development Organisation (CDO) officials say.
Prices for a kilogramme of cotton, which has been selling at an average of Ush255 (14 US cents), has been increased to Ush350 (19 US cents), the highest in recent years.

Original_Search_Title: Almost a Sixth of Harvest Lost
Headline: Ethiopia: Almost a Sixth of Harvest Lost
Body_Text:
Almost one-sixth of Ethiopia's main harvest was destroyed by drought, humanitarian organisations said on Monday.
The UN's World Food Programme said that the scale of the loss could have a "potentially devastating" impact on a country already facing food shortages.

Original_Search_Title: Fading Hope for Children Hit By Drought
Headline: Ethiopia: Fading Hope for Children Hit By Drought
Body_Text:
At the tender age of seven Nuria Ibrahim shouldn't have to worry about starvation or death. But like many children in Ethiopia she soon learnt the harsh realities of life in one of the poorest countries in the world.
The familiar cloud of despair once again hangs over Ethiopia where famine has a stranglehold on 15 million people - one in four of the population.

Original_Search_Title: Improved Margins Lift Delta
Headline: Zimbabwe: Improved Margins Lift Delta
Body_Text:
Harare — IMPROVED margins lifted Delta's historical cost attributable earnings
346% to $6,431 billion in the six months to September.
The group said the below inflation increase in turnover of 71% was
misleading since it had actually increased by 209% when the impact of
the demerged units was taken into account.

Original_Search_Title: Improved Margins Lift Delta
Headline: Zimbabwe: Improved Margins Lift Delta
Body_Text:
Improved margins lifted Delta's historical cost attributable earnings 346% to $6,431 billion in the six months to September
The group said the below inflation increase in turnover of 71% was misleading since it had actually increased by 209% when the impact of the demerged units was taken into account

Original_Search_Title: Almost Every Family Requires Urgent Food Provisions
Headline: Zimbabwe: Almost Every Family Requires Urgent Food Provisions
Body_Text:
Gutu, Zimbabwe — It is early in the morning and Jacob and
Patricia Magarire with their ten-month-old baby, still have eight
kilometres to go.
They are heading for the business centre in Basera to register their
names for food distribution.

Original_Search_Title: Rebels Fear Civilian Revenge
Headline: Uganda: Rebels Fear Civilian Revenge
Body_Text:
Kampala — Joseph Kony's rebel fighters fear denouncing rebellion and coming out of the bush for fear of vengeance from civilians against whom they have committed atrocities.
Former LRA captives recently rescued by the army quoted rebels hiding in remote camps in the Opiru hills, west of Kilak Hills in Gulu, as saying that they would not surrender for fear of revenge attacks from civilians.

Original_Search_Title: Almost a Sixth of Harvest Lost
Headline: Ethiopia: Almost a Sixth of Harvest Lost
Body_Text:
Almost one sixth of Ethiopia's main harvest has been destroyed by drought, humanitarian organisations said on Monday.
The UN's World Food Programme said that the scale of the loss could have a "potentially devastating" impact on the country already facing food shortages.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Improves Margins to Buck Lower Volumes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Improves Margins to Buck Lower Volumes
Body_Text:
INSPITE of a decline in beer volumes and flat sales of softdrinks, Delta Corp has reported a 346% rise in historical cost attributable earnings to $6,431 billion in the six months to September as a result of improved margins.
Delta financial director Rob Maunsell told an analysts' briefing that beer sales had fallen by less than company forecasts. Lager sales had eased 6% against company expectations of 8% while sorghum beer sales fell 8% against Delta's forecast for a 10% drop. These smaller-than-expected declines were partly offset by no change in sales of carbonated soft drinks (CSD) against company expectations for a rise of 8%.

Original_Search_Title: No Christmas to Celebrate This Year
Headline: Zimbabwe: No Christmas to Celebrate This Year
Body_Text:
At the rate with which things are deteriorating in Zimbabwe, I doubt if we will celebrate Christmas at the end of the year. Christmas, as far as the majority of Zimbabweans are concerned, is about having lots of bread, cake, drinks, tea and travelling to the rural areas.
A number of things on this list are in short supply, not to mention that even money is a scarce resource for most of us. There won't be any bread. There will be black tea without sugar (not that one will be on diet but because it's the order of the day in the new Zimbabwe, with the advent of the Third Chimurenga).

Original_Search_Title: Government, NGOs Must Step Up Efforts Ahead of Another Poor Crop
Headline: Zimbabwe: Government, NGOs Must Step Up Efforts Ahead of Another Poor Crop
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The Zimbabwe government and NGOs need to step up efforts to provide food aid to about 6.7 million people whose food security is under threat in the face of vastly reduced crop estimates for next year, the Famine and Early Warning Systems Network (FEWS-NET) said on Tuesday.
While agriculture has formed the base of the Zimbabwean economy, contributing 45 percent of export earnings and providing livelihoods to over 70 percent of the population, researchers found that farmers had only secured 20 percent of the estimated Zim $160 (US $2.9 billion) needed to finance agricultural activities for the 2002 to 2003 production year.

Original_Search_Title: Poor Performance Threatens Seedco's Regional Position
Headline: Zimbabwe: Poor Performance Threatens Seedco's Regional Position
Body_Text:
OPERATING conditions for agro-based companies in Zimbabwe have become extremely unfavourable, resulting in poor financial performance by agricultural businesses in recent periods, says SeedCo Limited.
In an analysis of its results for the half year ended 31 August 2002, SeedCo singled out the price control policy as the worst dimension of the poor operating conditions in Zimbabwe's turbulent macro-economic terrain.

Original_Search_Title: Price Freeze Seen As Move to Boost Agriculture
Headline: Zimbabwe: Price Freeze Seen As Move to Boost Agriculture
Body_Text:
The government has stepped up efforts to ensure the success of the controversial fast-track land reform programme with the introduction last week of price freezes for a wide range of farming implements and input materials.
The price freeze, effective from last Friday, will be operative for the next six months.

Original_Search_Title: Concern Over New Price Controls
Headline: Zimbabwe: Concern Over New Price Controls
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — The Zimbabwe government's decision to impose a new set of price freezes would create shortages and an increase in smuggling, an economist told IRIN on Monday.
The state-controlled Herald newspaper reported the government-gazetted six-month price freeze on a long list of products to cushion consumers against rising prices and an inflation rate that hit 144 percent in October.

Original_Search_Title: Government Gazettes Wider Price Freeze for Six Months
Headline: Zimbabwe: Government Gazettes Wider Price Freeze for Six Months
Body_Text:
EVEN after admitting that price controls have dealt a severe blow to the economy and the people of Zimbabwe, the government has still proceeded to intensify the same retrogressive price control policy by announcing a price freeze on a wide range of products.
In an Extraordinary Government Gazette published on Friday, the government extended price controls to cover a broader range of commodities and announced a price freeze for the specified goods, including mass-circulation newspapers and fuel.

Original_Search_Title: 8,000 Karimojong Enroll for Upe
Headline: Uganda: 8,000 Karimojong Enroll for Upe
Body_Text:
Kampala — At least 7,750 Karimojong children trained under the Alternative Basic Education for Karamoja (ABEK) project in Moroto district, have enrolled for Universal Primary Education (UPE), reports Richard Otim.
An education officer from Moroto, George Igune, said 4,244 of the children were girls. He said the informal education programme for children living in kraals started five years ago to integrate Karimojong into the formal education sector.

Original_Search_Title: State Freezes Prices
Headline: Zimbabwe: State Freezes Prices
Body_Text:
Harare — Price controls were yesterday extended to cover more commodities when the Government froze prices of more goods following frequent and unjustified price increases.
Goods whose prices were frozen included goods from the agricultural sector, the motor industry, the newspaper industry, the educational sector, the building industry and technological sector.

Original_Search_Title: Drought And Lack of Food Aid Threaten Millions Ethiopians
Headline: Ethiopia: Drought And Lack of Food Aid Threaten Millions Ethiopians
Body_Text:
The UN World Food Programme this week warned that it is increasingly concerned about the lack of food aid pledges needed to help feed millions of Ethiopians threatened by starvation early next year.
Severe food shortages are already expected in January unless donor pledges quickly materialize. WFP alone needs US$80 million worth of food for the first quarter of 2003, and a similar amount is needed in bilateral contributions to the Government and NGOs, WFP said in a release this week.

Original_Search_Title: Quella Birds Descend On Farmlands in Adamawa; Cause Heavy Damage
Headline: Nigeria: Quella Birds Descend On Farmlands in Adamawa; Cause Heavy Damage
Body_Text:
Yola — Quella  birds have ravaged farmlands in five local government councils in Adamawa State destroying crops worth millions of naira. Worst hit by the menace are Guyuk, Shelleng, Lamurde, Numan and Demsa local governments, all situated along the banks of the River Benue. Reports from the areas indicated that the promising bumper harvest said to be exceptional this year might be lost to the ravaging quella birds, unless adequate measures are adopted to control them. The report added that no fewer than 3,000 hectares of land comprising rice, sorghum and other plantations have been destroyed by the ravaging birds.
The areas known for high agricultural yield due to its natural soil fertility faces similar threats yearly during harvest period.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Short of Food Aid As Famine Looms
Headline: Ethiopia: WFP Short of Food Aid As Famine Looms
Body_Text:
The World Food Programme (WFP) has warned that it faces massive shortages to feed millions facing starvation Ethiopia. It called on the international community to come forward with aid to help avert the crisis.
The Ethiopian government estimates that 14 million people will be in need of food aid by early next year. The warning came as Prime Minister Meles Zenawi made an impassioned plea for help.
He said Ethiopia was unable to cope alone, and that the crisis had outstripped the 1984 famine, during which one million of the 7.9 million affected people died.

Original_Search_Title: Bananas Gaining Market
Headline: Uganda: Bananas Gaining Market
Body_Text:
Kampala — Banana is an essential food crop widely grown in most parts of the country. It is also a growing cash crop for thousands of farmers in the countryside.
The plentiful rain this season is an excellent opportunity to establish your banana plantation. There are several groups of bananas:

Original_Search_Title: Drought and Lack of Food Aid Threaten Millions of Ethiopians
Headline: Ethiopia: Drought and Lack of Food Aid Threaten Millions of Ethiopians
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The UN World Food Programme today warned that it is increasingly
concerned about the lack of food aid pledges needed to help feed millions of
Ethiopians threatened by starvation early next year.
Severe food shortages are already expected in January unless donor
pledges quickly materialize. WFP alone needs US$80 million worth of food for
the first quarter of 2003, and a similar amount is needed in bilateral
contributions to the Government and NGOs.

Original_Search_Title: Surtax Liability Commences in Malawi
Headline: Malawi: Surtax Liability Commences in Malawi
Body_Text:
Blantyre, Malawi — The much awaited for and talked about Surtax liability date is here
at last. November 1, 2002 was the date when Surtax payment of Surtax
came into effect. This was the day when wholesalers and retailers who
are registered for Surtax started charging Surtax on all taxable
goods and services they are dealing in. Malawians looked forward to
the introduction of Surtax with some trepidation as many people
anticipated that prices of some goods might go up by an average 5
percent.
The Acting Commissioner General of the Malawi Revenue Authority, Mr
Enerst Mtingwi is, therefore, appealing to the business community not
to unnecessarily increase the prices of their commodities under the
guise of Surtax. Other traders have decided not to pass on the price
increase to the consumer as there is a credit mechanism built into
the tax system.

Original_Search_Title: Cholera, El Nino Could Worsen Crisis
Headline: Southern Africa: Cholera, El Nino Could Worsen Crisis
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — An on-going El Nino weather pattern and a cholera outbreak could worsen the humanitarian crisis in Southern Africa, aid agencies have warned.
In its latest situation report on the humanitarian crisis in Southern Africa, the UN Regional Inter-Agency Coordination Support Office has warned that a "sub-optimal" harvest could be in the offing for 2003, due to the impact of an El Nino event which is associated with erratic rainfall.

Original_Search_Title: Fear of Hunger Sets in
Headline: Eritrea: Fear of Hunger Sets in
Body_Text:
Asmara — In a field of dry sorghum, Abraha Garza picks a cluster of seeds for
closer inspection. Squeezing the seeds between his fingers, he watches as
they flake to dust and disintegrate in the wind.
"This is what the harvest has brought us this year," says Abraha who
heads the agriculture ministry in Gash Barka - normally Eritrea's most
productive province. He is standing in a ruined crop field which stretches
to the horizon in every direction. "There is nothing... nothing at all," he
says.

Original_Search_Title: Kabale Takes to Tea Growing
Headline: Uganda: Kabale Takes to Tea Growing
Body_Text:
Kampala — Kabale district is to embark on tea growing as the main source of income after a decline in sorghum harvests due to soil exhaustion.
The district Director of Production and Marketing James Katsimbazi said this when he visited tea farmers in Ikumba sub-county, Kabale on Oct. 28.

Original_Search_Title: State Does Not Support Land Invasion'
Headline: South Africa: State Does Not Support Land Invasion'
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — GOVERNMENT does not support land invasions and any comparison with Zimbabwe-style land acquisition was "unfounded".
This was said by Adv Denzil Potgieter, leading the state's legal team in a case in the Pretoria High Court, in which Benoni farmer Abraham Duvenage contests the sheriff of the court failed to evict 40000 squatters on his land. There is a great deal of interest in the outcome of the case, because of the land-grab activities in Zimbabwe, and because it may clarify the state's responsibilities in evicting people who occupy land illegally.

Original_Search_Title: Money For New Farmers Not There
Headline: Zimbabwe: No Money For New Farmers
Body_Text:
Private sector companies and banks which pledged financial aid to newly-resettled farmers have not remitted any while the District Development Fund (DDF) has admitted it has no capacity to provide tillage to all the new farmers, it has emerged.
This has exacerbated fears that the country's agricultural production could fall dramatically next year amid warnings of a "mild" El Nino-induced drought.

Original_Search_Title: New Culture And New Deal
Headline: Zambia: New Culture And New Deal
Body_Text:
ZAMBIANS are desperate for progress, they want to see an improvement in their lives. Any individual, party or government that shows signs, or promises, to improve their painful conditions will automatically receive their support.
But not all that glitters is gold; and not all promises are usually fulfilled. Some will promise heaven and give you hell. President Levy Mwanawasa and his government have a good idea of what Zambians are yearning for or anyone in their condition would hope for.

Original_Search_Title: Millions Threatened With Starvation In Horn Of Africa
Headline: Ethiopia: Millions Threatened With Starvation In Horn Of Africa
Body_Text:
London — The UN World Food Programme today launched an urgent appeal to provide
emergency relief food to countries in the Horn of Africa, where more than
twelve million people are threatened with starvation over the next
months.
Recent joint WFP, donor and government assessment missions across
Ethiopia reveal widespread losses of maize and sorghum crops, the staple
foods for most rural people.  Malnutrition rates have reached alarming
levels in some areas.

Original_Search_Title: Firm to Deliver 15 000 Tonnes of Seed Maize
Headline: Zimbabwe: Firm to Deliver 15 000 Tonnes of Seed Maize
Body_Text:
Harare — The largest supplier of hybrid maize seed in Zimbabwe, Seed Co, will deliver 15 000 tonnes of seed maize before the end of next month.
"The massive task of distributing another 15 000 tonnes of seed maize is now well under way and the exercise is expected to be complete before the end of November," the company said in a statement.

Original_Search_Title: 2003 Budget to Focus On Productive Sectors
Headline: Zimbabwe: 2003 Budget to Focus On Productive Sectors
Body_Text:
Harare — THE 2003 national budget will focus on the productive sectors - particularly agrarian reform - as a strategy to turnaround the economy.
The Minister of Finance and Econo-mic Development, Dr Herbert Murerwa, said in an interview yesterday that agrarian reform was the most critical component of economic recovery and required the Government's full support.

Original_Search_Title: Major Shipment of U.S. Food Arrives for Drought Striken Ethiopia
Headline: Ethiopia: Major Shipment of U.S. Food Arrives for Drought Striken Ethiopia
Body_Text:
Addis Ababa — The UN World Food Programme warmly welcomed the arrival
today at Djibouti Port of a ship carrying 42,000 tonnes of relief food
donated to WFP by the United States Government. The food will help feed
millions of people facing increasing hunger throughout drought-stricken
Ethiopia.
This latest shipment of wheat, valued at US$15 million, brings the total
U.S.  contribution to WFP's emergency operation in Ethiopia this year to
US$100 million.  This includes a 100,000 tonne contribution confirmed last
week, which is due to arrive in December.

Original_Search_Title: Poor Harvest Puts Off Ka'jong Circumcision
Headline: Uganda: Poor Harvest Puts Off Ka'jong Circumcision
Body_Text:
Kampala — Poor crop harvest has forced the Uganda Pokot community to postpone this year's female circumcision (genital mutilation) programme.
Upe county MP Micah Lolem said the circumcision season usually starts in June when the crops are about to be harvested.

Original_Search_Title: Witch Weed Neutralised
Headline: Uganda: Witch Weed Neutralised
Body_Text:
Kampala — With modern farming techniques even the wicked become toothless. The destructive power of the dreaded Striga weed also known as the witch weed has been neutralised. In eastern Uganda, where it is commonly found it is known as the kayongo weed. The weed that has been particularly harmful to mostly maize and other monocotyledons can now be controlled. The Striga weed is a short plant with beautiful light purple flowers that lives off a host plant. It is a parasitic plant that gets attached to the roots of the crop and takes out water, nutrients and energy from the host plant. The weed thrives in soils where maize, millet or sorghum is repeatedly grown. It is common in Busoga, Tororo and Teso regions.
The use of fertilisers, herbicides, trap crops and physical weeding are recommended measures for controlling the Striga in heavily infested areas. For maize, which is the most affected crop by the weed, farmers are advised to plant, treated improved seed.

Original_Search_Title: Zimbabwe Assured of Surplus Maize Seed
Headline: Zimbabwe Assured of Surplus Maize Seed
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE has surplus seed for maize, the Southern African Development Community (Sadc) Seed Security Network, has said.
Dr Joseph Made, the Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, suspended issuing export permits to seed companies as he feared the commodity would not be enough at a time when there were many new farmers joining the industry.

Original_Search_Title: Statement By the Minister of Agriculture On the World Food Day Celebrations in Kisoro District 1
Headline: Uganda: Statement By the Minister of Agriculture On the World Food Day Celebrations in Kisoro District 16th October 2002.
Body_Text:
Kampala —  Statement By the Hon. Minister of Agriculture, Animal Industry And
Fisheries, Dr. W.w. Kisamba-Mugerwa On the World Food Day Celebrations in
Kisoro District 16th October 2002
Fellow Ugandans,

Original_Search_Title: Food Crisis Set to Continue Next Year, Warns SADC Unit
Headline: Southern Africa: Food Crisis Set to Continue Next Year, Warns SADC Unit
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE will continue scrounging for food next year, because winter crop production this year and the harvest in the coming season will fall short of the country's requirements, the Southern African Development Community (Sadc) Regional Early Warning Unit (REWU) has warned.
Zimbabwe faces a food crisis because of poor rains in the past two years and a reduction in planting in the commercial farming sector as a result of the so-called fast-track land reform programme.

Original_Search_Title: Warning of Massive Humanitarian Crisis
Headline: East Africa: Warning of Massive Humanitarian Crisis
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Up to 15 million people in Ethiopia and Eritrea could face famine conditions in the coming months, spelling a massive crisis for Africa, a senior UN relief official warned on Monday.
Speaking in New York on his return from a visit to the Horn of Africa, the UN's Emergency Relief Coordinator Kenzo Oshima said this figure, combined with 14 million people in southern Africa facing hunger and food shortages, was "quite an enormous number".

Original_Search_Title: Food Situation Still Worrying
Headline: Mauritania: Food Situation Still Worrying
Body_Text:
Abidjan — Mauritania's food security situation remains worrying despite a slight improvement in rainfall, the Famine Early Warning System Network (FEWS NET) reported in its September situation report.
According to FEWS, the rainfall was still insufficient in balancing off the impact of months of drought because Mauritania's farming relied mostly on irrigated water. Many farmers continued to lose cattle due to lack of water and prices of basic needs, such as meat and sorghum, remained slightly higher than normal.

Original_Search_Title: Politics of Pombe: Are We Becoming Drunkards?
Headline: Kenya: Politics of Pombe: Are We Becoming Drunkards?
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The region's attitudes to alcohol, and in particular different types of drink, are influenced by politics and power, reports PAUL REDFERN
Title: Potent Brews - A social history of alcohol in East Africa (1850-1999); 304 pages- Author: Justin Willis

Original_Search_Title: Gacaca Takes Off Slowly
Headline: Rwanda: Gacaca Takes Off Slowly
Body_Text:
Kigali — After a break of several months, pilot 'Gacaca' courts have been
starting work again one by one since the middle of September.
'Gacaca', meaning literally 'judgement on grass', has been introduced
in Rwanda to speed up the trials of around 115,000 people who are
accused of participating in the genocide, in which more than a
million people were killed between April and July 1994, including
Tutsis and moderate Hutus. These popular courts were in effect,
temporarily suspended at the end of July, at the end of their fifth
public meeting. This was principally to allow the coordination to
take stock of the situation, see what had been accomplished and what
problems they had faced. It was also to prepare the judges for the
two next stages, during which the public assemblies will establish
lists and individual details of the accused. At the end of this
process the real trials will finally begin. But already the Gacaca
pilot courts have shown up a number of problems which it will be
necessary to take into account before eleven thousand courts are due
to begin their work all over the country, at the beginning of next
year.
The pilot courts began on 19 June this year in 73 cells of 12 sectors
chosen from the 11 provinces of Rwanda and the city of Kigali. Rwanda
is divided administratively into cells, sectors, districts and
provinces, with the city of Kigali treated separately. Every level of
the administration will have its own gacaca court. Adding it
together, there are 9001 cells, 1545 sectors and 106 districts,
making 10662 tribunals, which call upon 254,152 voluntary judges.
They are known as the 'Integres' meaning people with integrity and
were elected in October 2001.

Original_Search_Title: "Huge Food Gap" Reported in North
Headline: Ethiopia: "Huge Food Gap" Reported in North
Body_Text:
Inadequate food distributions in northern Ethiopia have left more than a million people facing a "huge food gap", a report warned on Monday.
The joint report by the UN Emergencies Unit for Ethiopia (EUE) and the US Agency for International Development (USAID) said supplies to both the Amhara and Tigray regions were woefully short of the needs required.

Original_Search_Title: Bankers Unveil $60bn Agric Support Scheme
Headline: Zimbabwe: Bankers Unveil $60bn Agric Support Scheme
Body_Text:
Harare — The banking sector, which has been working on a draft on how best it could play a big role in the land reforms, has finally unveiled a $60 billion finance support scheme for the coming agricultural season.
Deputy secretary for agricultural economics and marketing in the Ministry of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, Mr David Mfote, confirmed to the New Farmer magazine that the package would be in tranches of $20 billion every three months.

Original_Search_Title: Lightning Kills Man
Headline: Namibia: Lightning Kills Man
Body_Text:
A BOLT of lightning killed Martin Ndjimba (30) at Omupulungushu
village in the Ohangwena Region in the North on Tuesday.
NamPol Liaison Officer in the North, Sergeant Samwel Hamukonda, said
Ndjimba was in his mahangu (sorghum) field when he was struck by
lightning at about 14h00.

Original_Search_Title: Government to Boost Cotton Production - JAK
Headline: Ghana: Government to Boost Cotton Production - JAK
Body_Text:
President John Agyekum Kufuor has pledgedgovernment's determination to rejuvenate and boost cotton production in the Sissala District of the Upper West Region.
"Government is determined to rejuvenate cotton production in the Sissala district, promote and modernise its production to supply the raw material base of the President's Special Initiative (PSI) on garment and textiles."

Original_Search_Title: Women Must Wear Chastity Symbol When Applying for Government Scholarships
Headline: Eswatini: Women Must Wear Chastity Symbol When Applying for Government Scholarships
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Rights groups in Swaziland are concerned that Swazi women who want to receive government scholarships must prove their loyalty to the Swazi way of life by showing that they subscribe to the maidens' chastity rules called "umcwasho".
The old custom, symbolised by the wearing of colourful woolen headgear, was reintroduced by King Mswati III last year. Mswati is also the chancellor of the University of Swaziland, which is next to the royal palace.

Original_Search_Title: HIV Study Targets Home-Grown Remedies
Headline: South Africa: HIV Study Targets Home-Grown Remedies
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — A BOLD new plan involving traditional medicine in the treatment of
HIV/Aids is being explored by the Nelson R Mandela School of Medicine in
Durban.
The objective of the project is to identify safe and effective therapies
for the fight against the disease, looking specifically at indigenous plants
used in traditional South African medicine.

Original_Search_Title: Poor Marketing Structures Hit Availability of Small Grains
Headline: Zimbabwe: Poor Marketing Structures Hit Availability of Small Grains
Body_Text:
POOR marketing structures will continue to adversely affect the availability of small grain seed varieties, a National Tested Seeds official has said.
In an interview yesterday, National Seeds General Manager, Lance Kennedy, said: "There are many reasons why seeds in the groundnuts, beans, sorghum and millet categories are not as readily available as maize seed.

Original_Search_Title: 'Good Rains Won't Wash Away Economic Crisis'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Good Rains Won't Wash Away Economic Crisis'
Body_Text:
AN excellent rainy season for Zimbabwe, whose economy is agro-driven, will not provide any respite for the country which is experiencing its worst economic crisis ever, a local insurance company has said.
Commenting on his company's interim results for the six months ended
June 2002, Zimre's chairman, William Mudekunye, said the country's
economic problems were so complex that good rains would simply not
wash them away

Original_Search_Title: Seed Prices Up 120pc
Headline: Zimbabwe: Seed Prices Up 120pc
Body_Text:
Harare — CONTROLLED retail prices of maize seed, sorghum and millet went up by 120 percent yesterday to allow seed companies remain viable.
The price of a 25kg bag of maize seed goes up from $ 1 946 to $4 280 while a 10kg bag will now cost $1 773 up from $806.

Original_Search_Title: USAID Supports World Food Program's Southern Africa Operation
Headline: Southern Africa: USAID Supports World Food Program's Southern Africa Operation
Publisher: United States Department of State (Washington, DC)
Body_Text:
Pretoria — The U.S. Agency for International Development (USAID)
announced September 23 that it is providing $1 million to the U.N. World
Food Program (WFP) to support its regional management and logistics
coordination unit in South Africa.
The regional coordination unit will monitor food supplies in the region
and facilitate the movement of humanitarian shipments to avoid undue
congestion on heavily used transport routes, USAID said in a news
release. Supporting a coordinated regional response is one of USAID's
non-food emergency assistance priorities in southern Africa, which is
threatened by growing food insecurity.

Original_Search_Title: Africans Have Always Been Good Farmers
Headline: Zimbabwe: Africans Have Always Been Good Farmers
Body_Text:
Harare — If our ancestors were able to till the land and feed themselves, what can deter us, with the land in the hands of the black majority, from reaching greater heights?
Such a seemingly unnecessary question comes in the wake of Western media reports positing that the agricultural reform in Zimbabwe is shortsighted and threatens the whole Sadc region with starvation.

Original_Search_Title: Another Deal to Assist New Farmers Struck
Headline: Zimbabwe: Another Deal to Assist New Farmers Struck
Body_Text:
Harare — ANOTHER multi-billion-dollar project was yesterday sealed between the Government, CFI Holdings Ltd and FSI Agricom Holdings Pvt Ltd to support farmers in the production of 240 000 tonnes of selected crops.
The deal, which will contribute to the needs of the country's agro-industrial operations, comes hard on the heels of a $10 billion project signed recently between Delta Corporation and the Government to produce an estimated 200 000 targeted crops over the next two seasons.

Original_Search_Title: Benoni Farmer Seeks Eviction of Squatters
Headline: South Africa: Benoni Farmer Seeks Eviction of Squatters
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Sheriff has failed to implement order
A BENONI farmer is forcing the state to implement an eviction order to remove 40000 illegal squatters from his farm.

Original_Search_Title: Food Shortages Prompt NGO Feeding Centres
Headline: Mauritania: Food Shortages Prompt NGO Feeding Centres
Body_Text:
Abidjan — The non-governmental organisation World Vision is to establish 181 feeding centres in the central Tagant and Assaba regions of Mauritania, and to provide 5,670 mt of cereal for immediate distribution in response to food shortages in the country.
The Christian relief and development organisation said on Monday it had declared Mauritania a "category one emergency" and would provide 6 mt of seed potatoes and quick-growing sorghum to drought affected areas before the end of September.

Original_Search_Title: Lending to Farmers a Lucrative Business
Headline: Zimbabwe: Lending to Farmers a Lucrative Business
Body_Text:
Harare — THE signing of the $10 billion agreement between the Government and Delta Corporation for the production of maize, sorghum and malting barley in the 2002/03 and 2003/04 agricultural seasons, marks a major turning point in the country's agrarian revolution.
The conglomerate will inject $10 billion worth of agricultural inputs into established and newly resettled farmers in a move set to boost grain production in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Delta Unveils $10bn Scheme
Headline: Zimbabwe: Delta Unveils $10bn Scheme
Body_Text:
Harare — DELTA Corporation will inject $10 billion worth of agricultural inputs into established and newly-resettled farmers in a move set to boost grain production in the country.
The multi-billion financial support is for the production of maize, sorghum and malting barley in the 2002-2003 and 2003-2004 agricultural seasons.

Original_Search_Title: Harvest Farm Seeds Boost Exports
Headline: Uganda: Harvest Farm Seeds Boost Exports
Body_Text:
Kampala — Uganda farmers need to use improved seed varieties, if they are to reap big from agricultural exports, the executive director of Harvest Farm Seeds has said.
"Improved seed varieties have greater yield potential, resistance to pests, pathogens and climatic stresses than home-saved seed," Josephine Okot, said while meeting farmers at the company's open field day in Koboko recently.

Original_Search_Title: New Dairy Cow Breed Produces More Milk
Headline: Zimbabwe: New Dairy Cow Breed Produces More Milk
Body_Text:
THE Matopo Research Station has developed a dairy milk cross breed for small-scale communal dairy farmers, which produces about 15 litres of milk on a single milking per day.
Before this development, an indigenous dairy cow could only give up to five litres a day. The dairy industry has over the years been dominated by large-scale white commercial farmers.

Original_Search_Title: Aid Agencies Propose Low Cost Irrigation Method for Rural Poor.
Headline: Zimbabwe: Aid Agencies Propose Low Cost Irrigation Method for Rural Poor.
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Aid agencies have proposed a low-cost irrigation method that could save thousands of Zimbabwe's communal farmers time, money and energy.
With close to six million people facing critical food shortages mainly due to adverse weather conditions, the drip irrigation outreach programme aims to help farmers grow drought resistant crops, such as soya beans, millet and sorghum. The United States Agency for International Development (USAid), and the Zimbabwe NGO Linkages for the Economic Advancement of the Disadvantaged, have already supplied 600 rural households with low-cost drip irrigation kits for household gardens.

Original_Search_Title: Sudan: Battle of Torit: Talks Disrupted
Headline: Zambia: Sudan: Battle of Torit: Talks Disrupted
Body_Text:
The principal mediator in Sudan's peace process informed the world of the suspension of Machakos talks between Sudan Peoples' Liberation Movement (SPLM) and the Government of Sudan (SG). The suspension was occasioned as a result of Sudan delegation's decision not to continue negotiations with SPLM because of the latter's capture of Torit on 31 August 2002. Khartoum received the fall of Torit with shock and fury which rapidly degenerated into convulsion and defiance. It called for renewal of Jihad, implementation of conscription among students and summoned Mujahideen. This angry reaction is bound to confuse the general observer and in particular those who are familiar with the history of Torit. Torit like Kapoeta, in eastern Equatoria, has changed hands several times between the parties to the conflict. As such, its fall to SPLM on the date mentioned should not amount to an occasion for challenge or to teach Dr. John Garang a lesson when he had not attacked any of AL Bashir's many towns which are blossoming, while Torit is condemned to perpetual morbidity. There are no potent reasons for the uproars in Khartoum (a short-hand for North Sudan). But those uproars that have captured the intellectual circles in Khartoum including their orthodox followers must be taken seriously by SPLM and the rest of South Sudan. This article attempts very briefly to identify three reasons that may explain the fury which has engulfed the Khartoum government.
Torit

Original_Search_Title: Seedco Scouts for Hybrid Seed Growers
Headline: Zimbabwe: Seedco Scouts for Hybrid Seed Growers
Body_Text:
SEEDCO, the largest seed producer in Zimbabwe, is recruiting new farmers to grow hybrid seed after it lost traditional producers due to the land reform programme.Large-scale commercial farmers who have been evicted from their farms have traditionally produced seed.
More than 30 percent of the 2 900 white commercial farmers given a 10 August deadline to vacate farms have left the country to explore farming opportunities in West Africa, New Zealand, Australia and neighbouring countries within the Sadc region.

Original_Search_Title: Low-Cost Irrigation to Help Rural Poor
Headline: Zimbabwe: Low-Cost Irrigation to Help Rural Poor
Body_Text:
Aid agencies have proposed a low-cost irrigation method that could save thousands of Zimbabwe's communal farmers time, money and energy.
With close to six million people facing critical food shortages mainly due to adverse weather conditions, the drip irrigation outreach programme aims to help farmers grow drought resistant crops, such as soya beans, millet, and sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: Need for Effective Food Policy
Headline: Kenya: Need for Effective Food Policy
Body_Text:
Nairobi — While hastening to assure Kenyans of adequate food reserves, Agriculture Minister Bonaya Godana, recently predicted a poor maize harvest. In this interview with Dr Wilson Nguyo, the Director of Egerton University's Tegemeo Institute of Agricultural Policy and Development, Njonjo Kihuria explores the issue of food security in Kenya.
QUESTION: As we enter the last leg of the race to State House, do you think any of the front-runners have prioritised food security?

Original_Search_Title: Domestic Violence Thrives On Idleness in Kabale
Headline: Uganda: Domestic Violence Thrives On Idleness in Kabale
Body_Text:
Kabale — A recent research in Uganda revealed that the Bakiga ranked 2nd in domestic violence.
I asked Mzee Salongo Twino why the inhabitants of the Switzerland of Africa rank second in wife battering.

Original_Search_Title: Domestic Violence Thrives On Idleness in Kabale
Headline: Uganda: Domestic Violence Thrives On Idleness in Kabale
Body_Text:
Kampala — A recent research in Uganda revealed that the Bakiga ranked 2nd in domestic violence.
I asked Mzee Salongo Twino why the inhabitants of the Switzerland of Africa rank second in wife battering.

Original_Search_Title: Benoni Farmer Battles to Evict 40000 Squatters
Headline: South Africa: Benoni Farmer Battles to Evict 40000 Squatters
Body_Text:
A Benoni farmer has asked the Pretoria High Court to enforce an order handed down in the Witwatersrand High Court to evict 6000 families squatting illegally on his farm alongside Daveyton township.
After the order was handed down last year, the sheriff of the Benoni High
Court told the 71-year-old farmer that he would have to pay R1,8-million so they could hire a security firm to remove the 40000 squatters.

Original_Search_Title: Ogunkelu Assures Over Benin Republic Economy
Headline: Nigeria: Ogunkelu Assures Over Benin Republic Economy
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigeria has assured neighbouring Benin Republic that it will not take
any action that could jeopadise or adversely affect its economy.
The minister of cooperation and integration in Africa, Dr. Bimbola
Ogunkelu, gave the assurance while clarifying the ban on the importation of
certain goods into the country, in an interview with the News Agency of
Nigeria (NAN) in Abuja at the weekend.

Original_Search_Title: Season of the Wonder Crop
Headline: Nigeria: Season of the Wonder Crop
Body_Text:
The maize or corn plant has been known to have the tendency to be abundant above other crops like rice, wheat, sorghum, guinea corn, rye, oats, barley etc and can feed a vast population.
In periods of acute famine and hunger, it is readily made available as a form of relief from wealthy countries, aid donors and agencies given its quantum and easy availability as well as its high nutritional value.

Original_Search_Title: New Information on Sudanese Human Rights Situation
Headline: Sudan: New Information on Sudanese Human Rights Situation
Body_Text:
The International Secretariat of OMCT has received new information
concerning the following situation in Sudan.
New information

Original_Search_Title: Decline in Livestock Reproduction
Headline: Somalia: Decline in Livestock Reproduction
Body_Text:
A third consecutive year of below normal rainfall in northern Somalia has caused a significant decline in animal reproduction due to damaged pasture and adverse water conditions, a report has warned.
USAID's Famine Early Warning System (FEWS) said this was particularly true of the grazing lands of the Sool plateau, where this year the reproduction rates of sheep, goats, cattle and camels have dropped by 44 percent, 73 percent, 77 percent, and 90 percent respectively compared to a normal year.

Original_Search_Title: Three Million Farms in Country
Headline: Mozambique: Three Million Farms in Country
Body_Text:
Maputo — There are slightly more than three million
farms in Mozambique, with an average size of 1.26 hectares each.
These are among the key findings of the 1999-2000
agricultural and livestock census, the definitive results from
which were published in Maputo on Wednesday.

Original_Search_Title: Food Crop Output to Fall By 40%
Headline: Uganda: Food Crop Output to Fall By 40%
Body_Text:
AN ESTIMATED shortfall of 40% in major cereal production is expected due to crop failure because of widespread drought conditions in several parts of the country. Expert reports indicate a drop of 30-40% in national maize production.
Millet and sorghum in the eastern and northeastern regions, which have been hit worst, are estimated at 40% drop in production due to failed rainfall since May.

Original_Search_Title: Pumpkin Fruit And Seeds
Headline: Nigeria: Pumpkin Fruit And Seeds
Body_Text:
Nature continues to bless us with its abundance in order to nourish
and sustain our bodies. One of such gifts of nature that serves as
food and medicine is the pumpkin fruit and its seeds. Here in our
environment, we boil the pumpkin fruit and eat it either alone or
with some other food.
Some people cut the fruit into tiny pieces and add to cook their
local soup dishes. On such occasions, the fruit of pumpkin serves as
meat in the meal thus prepared with it.

Original_Search_Title: Poor Rains Herald Bad Harvests for Countries in Southern Africa
Headline: Southern Africa: Poor Rains Herald Bad Harvests for Countries in Southern Africa
Body_Text:
THE Famine Early Warning Systems Network (FEWSNET) says the poor rains recorded this season could result in another bad harvest for the southern African region next year.
In Zimbabwe, Malawi, Mozambique, Zambia, Lesotho and Swaziland more than 14 million people need food aid because of poor harvests in the past farming season.

Original_Search_Title: Zambia's New Graft Stance Rekindles EU Link, Says Levy
Headline: Zambia's New Graft Stance Rekindles EU Link, Says Levy
Body_Text:
PRESIDENT Mwanawasa has said the fight against corruption by his administration has given impetus to the European Union (EU) to open up new areas of cooperation with Zambia.
Mr Mwanawasa said this at State House yesterday when he received credentials from six new high commissioners and ambassadors accredited to Zambia.

Original_Search_Title: In Brief
Headline: Uganda: In Brief
Body_Text:
Kampala — EC warns
The Electoral Commission has warned candidates contesting for the Bukomansimbi County seat in Parliament to avoid carrying out fundraising activities in the constituency. EC chairman Badru Kiggundu said in a statement that this would allow a level ground for all candidates in the race.

Original_Search_Title: Kony Has Inherited Bin Laden Farm - Omeda
Headline: Uganda: Kony Has Inherited Bin Laden Farm - Omeda
Body_Text:
Gulu resident district commissioner (RDC) Omax Omeda has said Joseph Kony of the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) is the caretaker of Osama bin Laden's 10-hectare marijuana farm in southern Sudan.
Osama bin Laden, the man accused of masterminding the September 11 terrorist attacks on the US World Trade Center, and Tourabi's Sudanese Peoples Armed Forces (SPAF), are alleged to be Kony's military contacts.

Original_Search_Title: Locusts Destroy Farmlands in Jigawa
Headline: Nigeria: Locusts Destroy Farmlands in Jigawa
Body_Text:
Lagos — Swarms of locusts have invaded farmlands in three villages in the Kazaure local government area of Jigawa State, destroying crops and vegetation.
The invasion, which occured last week, affected farmlands in Karafkai, Wawan Rafi and Futare villages.

Original_Search_Title: Rushinga Faces Food Shortage
Headline: Zimbabwe: Rushinga Faces Food Shortage
Body_Text:
RUSHINGA District is facing a serious food shortage with hundreds of families going for days without getting maize supplies.
The district council and other civic organisations are, however, carrying out awareness programmes to encourage residents to take advantage of traditional foods and wild fruits to avert starvation. Rushinga Member of Parliament, Cde Lazarus Dokora, said the drought was "very serious" and it had negatively impacted on school going children.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Union Congress to Focus On Natural Resources Conservation Programmes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Farmers Union Congress to Focus On Natural Resources Conservation Programmes
Body_Text:
THE Zimbabwe Farmers Union holds its 63rd annual congress today and will
focus on natural resources conservation programmes in the new resettled
areas through sustainable agriculture and food security.
Sustainable agriculture programmes formed the basis for the production
of small grains especially in drought prone areas.

Original_Search_Title: PMA Funds Vanish
Headline: Uganda: PMA Funds Vanish
Body_Text:
Man eateth where he worketh. When the government sent grant funds to the local councils to support the Plan for the Modernisation of Agriculture (PMA)some people just gobbled up the money.
Sh2bn was sent to 24 districts in the financial year 2000/2001. The sub- counties made workplans in accordance with the wanainchi's priorities, sent them to the PMA secretariat and they were then sent money through their districts.

Original_Search_Title: Floods, Drought And Food Insecurity Reported
Headline: Niger: Floods, Drought And Food Insecurity Reported
Body_Text:
While agriculture in parts of southern Niger has been affected by
insufficient rains, forcing some farmers to delay planting, a flood in
Agadez, some 900 km north of the capital, Niamey, has claimed at least four
lives, national radio reported.
The deaths occurred between Friday and Saturday when heavy rains in
mountains located upstream from Agadez caused a wadi (seasonal river) to
overflow, damaging about 250 huts and houses made of baked mud. On 24 July,
five persons had died in Agadez when heavy rains caused a wall to collapse
on them.

Original_Search_Title: Government Orders Release of Food Into the Market
Headline: Nigeria: Government Orders Release of Food Into the Market
Body_Text:
Abuja — Faced with imminent starvation in the country President Olusegun Obasanjo has directed the Minister of Agriculture and Rural Development, Mallam Adamu Bello to release with immediate effect stored grains from the nation's grains reserves to the public.
In an exclusively interview with THISDAY, the Minister said that the action was to bring down the escalating prices of food items in the market.

Original_Search_Title: Some 50,000 Farmers May Abandon Farmland to Sand Dunes
Headline: Nigeria: Some 50,000 Farmers May Abandon Farmland to Sand Dunes
Body_Text:
At least, 50,000 farmers in about 100 villages scattered along the desert fringes of Yobe State are at the risk of abandoning this year's farming season due to the forced occupation of their farmlands by sand dunes.
A correspondent of the News Agency of Nigeria (NAN) who was on an assessment tour of the state reported that the sand dunes covered a large expanse of agricultural farmlands particularly in the northern axis of the state towards Nguru area.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers May Abandon Farmland to Sand Dunes
Headline: Nigeria: Farmers May Abandon Farmland to Sand Dunes
Body_Text:
Lagos — At least, 50,000 farmers in about 100 villages scattered along the desert fringes of Yobe State are at the risk of abandoning this year's farming season due to sand dunes.
According to a report on an assessment tour of the state, the sand dunes covered a large expanse of agricultural farmlands particularly in the northern axis of the state towards Nguru areas.

Original_Search_Title: Famine Hits Nakapiripirit
Headline: Uganda: Famine Hits Nakapiripirit
Body_Text:
Nakapiripirit chief administrative officer Churchill Lokoroi has said there is famine looming in the district, reports Richard Otim.
Lokoroi said two women from the slopes of Nakapiripirit hills were reported dead after eating a wild sorghum commonly known as lobelia.

Original_Search_Title: Give Northern Areas a Chance to Board Development Train
Headline: Kenya: Give Northern Areas a Chance to Board Development Train
Body_Text:
The forthcoming General Election, which is expected to take place by the
end of this year, will mark a watershed in the history of post-independence
Kenya.
The country now stands at a cross-roads both politically and
socio-economically as the 24-year rule of President Daniel arap Moi comes to
an end.

Original_Search_Title: United Breweries Left With Two Shareholders
Headline: South Africa: United Breweries Left With Two Shareholders
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — UNITED National Breweries, formerly National Sorghum Breweries, has ended up with only two shareholders after buying out the minorities and converting the company into a private company from a public entity.
Indian-based United Breweries and Durban based Marriott Merchant Bank, hold 74,99% and 25,01% respectively. This eliminates the broad black shareholding which the sorghum brewer had. The company, dubbed by the Black Economic Empowerment Commission in its report as the first real empowerment of black people, began when black investors led by former National Sorghum Breweries chairman Mohale Mahanyele bought it from government for R45m.

Original_Search_Title: Ties to the Land No Longer Bind
Headline: South Africa: Ties to the Land No Longer Bind
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — As the national road descends from KwaZulu-Natal into the Eastern Cape,
a vista of rolling hills appears. A village of neat Mpondo huts stands
perched on the hillside, overlooking the land spread out below, but the
fields are empty save for a handful of cattle.
The lands lie brown and fallow, good only for thatching grass.

Original_Search_Title: The Bottom Line is Put Food On People's Tables
Headline: Zimbabwe: The Bottom Line is Put Food On People's Tables
Body_Text:
Robert Mugabe, Zimbabwe's president, must address the issue of food at the forthcoming world summit on sustainable development scheduled to be held in Johannesburg soon.
Zimbabwe is in drought and famine mode. The international community blames Bob for the problem. Since independence, he allowed for a situation where 4,500 white commercial farmers own 70 per cent of the best land in the country. The blacks, dispossessed by the Rhodesians, own neither the land nor the economy.

Original_Search_Title: Case of Villagers' Desperation And the Law
Headline: Kenya: Case of Villagers' Desperation And the Law
Body_Text:
Something curious, yet so uplifting, is alleged to have happened recently in a village called Kaharo in Maragwa District. It is now the subject of a court case. The defendants are a group of respectable middle-aged mamas who have been charged with malicious damage to property. The ringleader had her case recently changed to assault.
They are accused of taking the law into their hands by raiding a den of bootleg booze and turning the place upside down. Let me start by saying that it is not my intention to intrude into the legal technicalities of the case. The matter is before a court of law whose burden it is to determine whether the women are innocent or guilty. Suffice is to say that when the matter blew into the open, the local Protestant church organised a campaign of support for the women. Soon, FIDA got attracted to the case. The organisation's deputy executive director, Ms Judy Thongori, has now taken charge of the women's legal defence.

Original_Search_Title: Government is Undecided Over GM Maize - Kavindele
Headline: Zambia: Government is Undecided Over GM Maize - Kavindele
Body_Text:
THE government is undecided over the USA's offer to provide funding for the purchase of Genetically Modified (GM) maize from that country, Vice-President Enoch Kavindele confirmed yesterday.
But US Agency for International Agency (USAID) administrator Andrew Natsios maintained that the US government would provide genetically produced maize to Southern African countries facing hunger.

Original_Search_Title: Bankers' Move to Finance Inputs Hailed
Headline: Zimbabwe: Bankers' Move to Finance Inputs Hailed
Body_Text:
FARMERS have welcomed an offer by bankers to support agricultural activities next season.
Farmers interviewed by Business Herald said it was encouraging to note that banks have finally heeded the call to support the agrarian revolution.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security Outlook Fair
Headline: Rwanda: Food Security Outlook Fair
Body_Text:
Rwanda's overall food output for July-December is estimated at 962,000 mt of "cereal equivalents", the USAID-funded FEWS NET said in its food security report of 19 July for the country.
"Unfortunately," it said, "this performance is mainly due to the positive impact of good rains up to early May on certain crops, mainly roots and tubers, rather than on agricultural innovations."

Original_Search_Title: Banks to Fund Inputs
Headline: Zimbabwe: Banks to Fund Inputs
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE'S banking sector yesterday agreed to finance crop inputs for the coming agricultural season by matching the Government's $8,5 billion fund.
However, the issue of collateral security remained the sticking point for banks to lend money to mostly resettled farmers but the Government said it would speed up the processing of leases to enable newly-resettled farmers access loans.

Original_Search_Title: ARDA Farm Spearheads Commercial Agriculture, Rural Development
Headline: Zimbabwe: ARDA Farm Spearheads Commercial Agriculture, Rural Development
Body_Text:
ARDA Middle Sabi Farm, is spearheading commercial agriculture and rural development activities with increased support to the small holder and newly resettled farmers within its vicinity.
The parastatal, created through an Act of Parliament in 1982 to facilitate production of food for the nation, generation of income and employment, is increasing production of strategic crops such as maize, wheat and seed maize to boost the country's food reserves.

Original_Search_Title: No Bookings From Cattle Breeders At This Year's Gweru Agriculture Show
Headline: Zimbabwe: No Bookings From Cattle Breeders At This Year's Gweru Agriculture Show
Body_Text:
THE absence of livestock exhibits at this year's Midlands Agricultural Show largely due to the government's controversial land seizures, stockfeed shortages and the outbreak of foot-and-mouth disease, is likely to put a damper on the event.
The three-day show which runs from 26 to 28 July, will for the first time this year run without a cattle exhibition. Trevor Shaw, the chairman of the Midlands Show Society, said no cattle exhibitors had booked this year. "Unfortunately there will be no cattle on the show due to the government ban on the movement of cattle due to foot-and-mouth and the prevailing stockfeeds shortages," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Deadly New Content in Illicit Brews
Headline: Kenya: Deadly New Content in Illicit Brews
Body_Text:
Trade in cheap lethal liquor is getting more deadly, rampant, popular and competitive all over the country, despite Parliament having passed a Motion three years ago, asking the Government to ban the production, sale and consumption of brews of questionable quality.
And, in fact, the brews have only become more lethal and their effect on the consumers, more destructive. As many poor Kenyans continue to judge the quality of their drink by how portent it is, the peddlers of the deadly concoctions are only too happy, doing everything they can to "improve" their products to meet the growing demand and reap the benefits.

Original_Search_Title: Proposed Revision of Domestic Content Law
Headline: Ghana: Proposed Revision of Domestic Content Law
Body_Text:
Trade and Industry Minister, Dr Kofi Konadu Apraku on Wednesday proposed
a revision of the Domestic Content Law to increase the use of raw materials
in the manufacturing industry from 10 per cent to at least 20 per cent.
Speaking at a national symposium for research scientists in Accra, Dr
Apraku said this strategy would add more value to local raw materials,
create employment opportunities and overall industrial growth and
development.

Original_Search_Title: Government Approved $8,5bn for Input Scheme
