Headline: Zimbabwe: Government Approved $8,5bn for Input Scheme
Body_Text:
THE Government has approved the immediate release of $8,5 billion for the purchase of crop inputs between now and December in a major boost for the country's agrarian reforms.
The money will come from the supplementary budget, which is expected to be tabled before Parliament soon.

Original_Search_Title: SADC Body Blames State for Food Shortages
Headline: Southern Africa: SADC Body Blames State for Food Shortages
Body_Text:
THE Southern African Development Community Development (Sadc) Regional Early Warning Unit says it blames President Mugabe's controversial land reform programme and the drought in the past season for causing current food shortages.
Lands Agriculture and Rural Resettlement Minister, Dr Joseph Made, in May brushed aside the torrent of criticism which has been levelled against him for misleading the nation over the food situation by blaming drought for the food shortages currently gripping the country. The Sadc food security monthly report up to 28 June says: "A cereal deficit of 2,28 million tonnes is assessed for the 2002/2003 marketing year due to drought as well as ongoing land reforms, which resulted in a 60 percent decline in cereal production."

Original_Search_Title: Government Unaware of Sh336m Tsetse Control Fund
Headline: Kenya: Government Unaware of Sh336m Tsetse Control Fund
Body_Text:
The Government yesterday said it was not aware the European Union gave Kenya Sh336 million in aid for tsetse fly control and eradication.
Assistant Minister for Finance Charles Kirui said the Government is only aware that a financial agreement of a similar amount of money was signed between the EU and the Government in 1997.

Original_Search_Title: Talk Peace, Acholi Plead
Headline: Uganda: Talk Peace, Acholi Plead
Body_Text:
'The government doesn't want us to go home, the rebels want us to go home, the people are confused'
Bukenya, the minister in charge of the Presidency was in Acholiland on the directive of President Yoweri Museveni to assess the emerging situation and advise on possible ways of bringing the 16-year old conflict to an end. The New Vision's Okello Jabweli travelled with the minister and now reports.

Original_Search_Title: Food Security In Acholi Bolstered
Headline: Uganda: Food Security In Acholi Bolstered
Body_Text:
A food Security Project being undertaken by World Vision, Uganda, is to benefit up to 36,000 households in the districts of Gulu, Kitgum an Pader.
The four-year scheme is funded by the United States Agency for International Development (USAID) which offered $3m. An additional $370,000 was pulled by World Vision as a matching grant.

Original_Search_Title: State to Boost Maize, Sorghum Production
Headline: Zimbabwe: State to Boost Maize, Sorghum Production
Body_Text:
The Government, which recently declared as strategic all land developed
for irrigation under the fast-track land reform programme, has put maize and
sorghum production as a priority to boost the country's food security.
Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement Minister Cde Joseph Made
yesterday said the Government would assist newly resettled farmers with
tillage to ensure the area under the two priority crops was expanded across
the country.

Original_Search_Title: 'Food Shortages May Impede Farmers' Ability to Recover'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Food Shortages May Impede Farmers' Ability to Recover'
Body_Text:
THE current food shortages coupled with the ever increasing input costs could seriously compromise the ability of smallholder farmers to recover in the next season, Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement Ministry has said.
The Agricultural Research and Extension (AREX) post harvest report last week said that most of the family incomes and livestock will be used to source food, leaving little for the next farming season's preparations.

Original_Search_Title: Bush's Visit a Non-Event
Headline: Africa: Bush's Visit a Non-Event
Body_Text:
VERY few Africans will find excitement and joy in the proposed visit to the continent by American President George W. Bush following his hard-line stance on Third World states.
The American leader has announced plans to visit Africa next year including sub-Saharan Africa. Details of the trip which will be a follow-up to the journeys undertaken by his predecessor Mr Bill Clinton in 1998 and 2000 will be made known in due course.

Original_Search_Title: Minister Lobbies for Drinking Ban
Headline: Botswana: Minister Lobbies for Drinking Ban
Body_Text:
Selebi Phikwe — Botswana may become a teetotaller's paradise if its trade, industry,
wildlife and tourism minister gets her way.
Pelonomi Venson is travelling the small southern African country to
solicit support to have beer and other alcohol declared illegal.

Original_Search_Title: Moroto Hit By Drought
Headline: Uganda: Moroto Hit By Drought
Body_Text:
Moroto district has been hit by a severe drought that has all the crops that had been cultivated during the first rainy season, withering.
The district agricultural officer, Achila-Odongo, said on Wednesday that all the sorghum, maize and legumes that were cultivated during the first rains had withered.

Original_Search_Title: Expected Maize Harvest Only Enough for 100 Days
Headline: Zimbabwe: Expected Maize Harvest Only Enough for 100 Days
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE, which earlier this year was expected to produce a total maize crop of around 600 000 tonnes, will this year harvest less than half a million tonnes of maize which is enough to feed the nation for about 100 days only, food experts have said.
In its 2002 Second Crop Forecast, the Central Statistics Office (CSO) revised its maize production figures from last month's 595 500 tonnes to 498 000 tonnes expected to be produced this year.

Original_Search_Title: Half A Million People Need Aid
Headline: Mozambique: Half A Million People Need Aid
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — About half a million Mozambicans are in need of food aid, a Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO) and World Food Programme (WFP) joint assessment has found.
The Crop and Food Supply Assessment Mission visited Mozambique from 21 April to 10 May this year to evaluate the current situation and determine needs in the wake of a regional food crisis.

Original_Search_Title: 'Lower Duties On Small Grains'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Lower Duties On Small Grains'
Body_Text:
THE Grain Millers Association of Zimbabwe says the Government should consider lowering import duties on small grains such as wheat and sorghum to enable small millers to import the grains now being used by many as maize meal substitutes.
Association vice-president Mr James Mangwana said that the reduction of import duties on small grains would enable small millers to import at low costs, thereby ensuring low prices of wheat or sorghum meal.

Original_Search_Title: Returnees Happy to Be Home After Years Away
Headline: Eritrea: Returnees Happy to Be Home After Years Away
Body_Text:
Habib Said looked around slightly bewildered, taking stock of his new surroundings. He was seeing his country for the first time in 20 years. The rest of his years will probably be spent in this unfamiliar land, eking out a new existence for himself and his family.
Habib is one of about 162,000 Eritrean refugees who will eventually return from camps in Sudan to a new life back home. So far, some 46,000 have come back and it is estimated that most will have returned from Sudan by the end of next year. If any remain behind, they will be on their own.

Original_Search_Title: Firm May Lose $1.25m Seed
Headline: Kenya: Firm May Lose $1.25m Seed
Body_Text:
A local firm may lose seeds worth more than Sh100 million if its closed Nakuru factory is not reopened.
The World Bank-funded Oil Crop Development Company wants the Attorney-General and the Kenya Plant Health Inspectorate Service (Kephis) to reopen its factory.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Deficit Now Put At 1.4 Million Tonnes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Maize Deficit Now Put At 1.4 Million Tonnes
Body_Text:
Harare — Zimbabwe will have to import 1.2 million tonnes of maize in addition
to the 317 000 it has already imported to meet the nation's
requirements until the next season. The government has already put in
place plans to have a winter maize crop to ease the shortage.
According to the latest report from the Famine Early Warning System
(FEWS) the country harvested only 600 000 tonnes of maize at the end
of the season, a decrease of 59 percent from last year.
Although land under maize was only 1 percent below that for last
year, only 12 out of the 57 districts received more than 80 percent
of their long-term cumulative average rainfall for the cropping
season. The country is projected to have a maize deficit of 1.4
million tonnes, which will be met partly through the importation of
317 000 tonnes, of which 200 000 tonnes were already planned by the
government and the remainder by World Food Programme (WFP) and other
non-governmental organisations. About 100 000 tonnes had already been
received by the end of April.

Original_Search_Title: Thirty Inmates in Hospital After Disease Outbreak
Headline: Kenya: Thirty Inmates in Hospital After Disease Outbreak
Body_Text:
Some 30 inmates have been admitted to Shimo la Tewa Prison dispensary following a disease outbreak that has killed three prisoners in the past week.
At the same time, a team of medical officers visited the prison yesterday and recommended that congestion be eased as one way of reducing the spread of diarrhoea and fever outbreak.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Production Lowest in 12 Years
Headline: Zimbabwe: Crop Production Lowest in 12 Years
Body_Text:
THE 2001/2002 farming season, whose crops were negatively affected by a drought and land seizures, is one of the lowest production years in the 12-year period from the 1990/1991 season, the Central Statistic Office (CSO), has said.
In its latest crop forecast for the year 2001/2002 farming season, the CSO said the country is expected to produce a total maize crop which is 60 percent less than last year's production of about 1,4 million tonnes.

Original_Search_Title: Picture Book Perpetuates 'Mzungu' View of Africa
Headline: Uganda: Picture Book Perpetuates 'Mzungu' View of Africa
Body_Text:
Title:                       Eye of the Storm
Photographers:       David Pluth and Pierre Francoise

Original_Search_Title: Poor Regional Rainfall Slashes Harvest Expectations
Headline: Namibia: Poor Regional Rainfall Slashes Harvest Expectations
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Namibia's Kavango and Caprivi regions will see only a third of their normal maize harvest this year due to sparse rainfall.
This falls in line with multi-agency warnings that harvests will be below normal throughout Southern Africa this year.

Original_Search_Title: Families Count Loses As Floods Rage Unabated
Headline: Kenya: Families Count Loses As Floods Rage Unabated
Body_Text:
The impact of the floods resulting from the current rains can be felt at the camps, where several families are staying having been rendered homeless by the raging waters.
A visit to several camps in Budalangi Division of Busia District is telling. The ground is strewn with raw cassava and other pre-maturely harvested crops. Every effort was being made to collect and keep them safely.

Original_Search_Title: Floods Ruin $125,000 Project
Headline: Kenya: Floods Ruin $125,000 Project
Body_Text:
A Sh10 million land reclamation project in the Yala Swamp is the latest victim of flooding in Nyanza.
A section of the dyke which has been holding at bay the waters of the River Yala was destroyed when the river burst its banks yesterday, submerging more than 1,500 hectares of the 2,300 hectares under maize, sorghum, banana, coffee and citrus fruits.

Original_Search_Title: Drought Reduces Estimated Maize Harvest
Headline: Zimbabwe: Drought Reduces Estimated Maize Harvest
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE, which experienced a severe drought last year, expects to harvest an estimated 594 500 tonnes of maize in the 2001/02 season, according to estimates by the Central Statistical Office.
The figure is 60 percent down from the previous year's 1 476 240 tonnes.

Original_Search_Title: Jackie, the Judge And the Jailbird
Headline: South Africa: Jackie, the Judge And the Jailbird
Body_Text:
Reputations are at risk as a dispute between business factions heats  up.
A high court judge and the national police chief both face the heat in  a blazing row over a R99-million airports security contract. Accusing  both is a well-connected drugs convict who earlier partnered the judge in  business.

Original_Search_Title: Tradition of Labour Tribute Draws Fire
Headline: Eswatini: Tradition of Labour Tribute Draws Fire
Body_Text:
By tradition, Swazis of all generations and genders show their devotion to their monarchs and fealty to their chiefs by providing labour for chores both practical and symbolic. Recently, the custom has drawn fire from pro-democracy groups, who claim the practice of tribute labour violates human rights.
"It is a form of slave labour," Jan Sithole, secretary-general of the Swaziland Federation of Trade Unions has told the International Labour Organisation (ILO).

Original_Search_Title: First Women's Bank in Upper Eastern Region in the Offing
Headline: Ghana: First Women's Bank in Upper Eastern Region in the Offing
Body_Text:
Wiawso-Asante Akim — The Savings and Credit Opportunity for Women (SCROW), a loans scheme started in the Kaleo area in 1992, is soon to become the first women's rural bank in the Upper West Region.
SCROW represents a collaboration between the Kaleo Baptist Women Development Programme (KBWDP), a local Non-Governmental Organisation (NGO) and the Sonzelle Rural Bank at Jirapa.

Original_Search_Title: LRA Killed At Least 300 Sudanese Villagers, Says UPDF
Headline: Uganda: LRA Killed At Least 300 Sudanese Villagers, Says UPDF
Body_Text:
Members of the Ugandan rebel Lord's Resistance Army (LRA) and their leader, Joseph Kony, have killed up to 300 civilians in southern Sudan in the past week, in retaliation for their refusal to support the insurgent group, according to a senior Ugandan army spokesperson.
Shaban Bantariza, the Uganda People's Defence Forces (UPDF) Director of Information and Public Relations, told IRIN on Friday that the LRA had in the past week been "carrying out ritual killings in different places", similar to the earlier alleged massacre of 60 mourners in the Agoro Mountains, not far from the Ugandan border.

Original_Search_Title: Harvesting of Early Planted Crops Underway
Headline: Zimbabwe: Harvesting of Early Planted Crops Underway
Body_Text:
HARVESTING and drying of the early planted crop are the major activities in all farming areas across the country.
However, the bulk of the crop was a complete write-off in Matabeleland South and Masvingo provinces because of the drought.

Original_Search_Title: Kony Kills 60 in Khartoum
Headline: Sudan: Kony Kills 60 in Khartoum
Body_Text:
UNDER pressure from UPDF operations in southern Sudan, Joseph Kony and his Lord's Resistance Army rebels on Friday massacred at least 60 Sudanese who were in a funeral procession of an elder of the village in Agoro mountains, the army said yesterday.
"At least 60 people were shot point-blank after being forced to cut to pieces the corpse they were carrying. Before killing them, Kony rebels surrounded them and ordered them to cook the carcass in sorghum," army spokesman Major Shaban Bantariza said yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: Maputo Accepts SeedCo Hybrid Seeds
Headline: Zimbabwe: Maputo Accepts SeedCo Hybrid Seeds
Body_Text:
Mozambique has accepted hybrid seeds produced by Zimbabwe's SeedCo Limited as the company expands its operations in that country.
Mozambique's small-scale farmers have over the years been growing open pollinated seed varieties, which are low yielding and highly susceptible to diseases.

Original_Search_Title: Man Jailed for Stealing Sorghum
Headline: Kenya: Man Jailed for Stealing Sorghum
Body_Text:
Kabale Grade One magistrate, Amos Kwizera Wednesday sentenced John Mureza, a resident of Kabale Municipality to 4 months in prison for stealing two bags of sorghum worth Shs 40,000.
Prosecution said that on Feb.22, Mureza, 27, while at Kigongi A cell in Kabale Municipality with intent to defraud, obtained 2 bags of sorghum from Agnes Busingye promising to pay but he did not.

Original_Search_Title: Seed Co Excels in Maputo
Headline: Zimbabwe: Seed Co Excels in Maputo
Body_Text:
SEED Co, which became a more active player in the Mozambican market about four years ago, has had a profound effect on that country's agricultural sector.
"A significant number of small holder farmers, particularly in Manica, Tete, and some areas of the Nampula province are now using maize hybrids. They were actively promoted by Seed Co, working in collaboration with our rural extension services," said Helder Gemo, an agronomist with the National Rural Extension of Mozambique, who visited Seed Co in Zimbabwe, recently.

Original_Search_Title: Maize Hectarage Far Below Annual Requirement
Headline: Zimbabwe: Maize Hectarage Far Below Annual Requirement
Body_Text:
ZIMBABWE, experiencing one of its worst droughts ever, is expected to produce a total maize crop of less than 750 000 tonnes, which is far below the 2 000 000 tonnes needed annually to feed the nation.
The Southern African Development Community Early Warning Unit's 31 March 2002 food security report says maize harvest prospects for 2001/2002 crop season are forecast to range between 450 000 and 750 000 tonnes, equivalent to between 30 and 50 percent of the previous season's below average of 1,48 million tonnes.

Original_Search_Title: WFP Warns of Critical Food Shortages
Headline: Southern Africa: WFP Warns of Critical Food Shortages
Body_Text:
The World Food Programme (WFP) has warned that harsh weather conditions, economic and political instability and HIV/AIDS could see the 2.6 million people it is currently struggling to feed in Southern Africa double when regional assessments are completed in May.
"The situation is extremely critical," said Judith Lewis, WFP regional director for East and Southern Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Government Declares State of Famine
Headline: Lesotho: Government Declares State of Famine
Body_Text:
Maseru — With only a month to go before elections, Prime Minister Mosisili has
finally conceded and officially announced a state of famine in the
country, after numerous attempts of subsidies and counter productive
initiatives to curb the serious threat of cereal crop deficit in
Lesotho. Food aid programmes will definitely have to be the order of
the day, with only weeks to go before elections, posing yet another
political claptrap that could threaten and derail the peace process
in the country.
A more broader assessment is needed in order to know exactly the
impact of the food shortage crisis that has hit Lesotho and five
other countries in the region.

Original_Search_Title: Seed Co Fast Becoming Regional Seed Giant
Headline: East Africa: Seed Co Fast Becoming Regional Seed Giant
Body_Text:
SEED CO is fast becoming a regional seed giant as it is diversifies its operations into Africa's hinterland, following the opening up of another operation in East Africa.
The Plant Health Inspectorate Service in Kenya, where Seed Co operates a registered company, said they were planning to harmonise legislation pertaining to seed crops in Kenya, Uganda and Tanzania to Ethiopia, Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo.

Original_Search_Title: Move to Enhance Food Production
Headline: Zimbabwe: Move to Enhance Food Production
Body_Text:
THE Government will soon recruit agroeconomists who will assist in gathering strategic information to boost the country's land reforms.
The Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, Cde Joseph Made, yesterday said his ministry would recruit two economists in each province to enhance capacity building at provincial levels.

Original_Search_Title: Grain Theft Rampant Throughout Country
Headline: Zimbabwe: Grain Theft Rampant Throughout Country
Body_Text:
CASES of grain theft from fields are rampant countrywide owing to food shortages, the Department of Agricultural Technical and Engineering Services has said.
In its fortnightly crop and livestock report, the department said the thefts had forced many farmers to harvest their maize crop prematurely.

Original_Search_Title: Farmers Warned Not to Vandalise Infrastructure
Headline: Zimbabwe: Farmers Warned Not to Vandalise Infrastructure
Body_Text:
COMMERCIAL farmers who vandalise infrastructure to frustrate land reforms will be arrested, the Minister of Lands, Agriculture and Rural Resettlement, Cde Joseph Made, warned yesterday.
He told journalists that he had received reports of commercial farmers who were vandalising irrigation equipment, spraying sugar cane plantations with harmful chemicals and infecting cattle with diseases.

Original_Search_Title: Farm Feed, OTK Merger Approved
Headline: South Africa: Farm Feed, OTK Merger Approved
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — DESPITE the sale of Farm Feed Services to OTK having fallen through in March, the Competition Tribunal went ahead yesterday with granting unconditional approval of the merger.
Pending approval from the tribunal, OTK Agri, a division of listed agriculture company OTK, was set to buy Farm Feed Services the profitable division of liquidated food group Afribrand earlier this year.

Original_Search_Title: ZFU Urges Farmers to Plant Winter Crops
Headline: Zimbabwe: ZFU Urges Farmers to Plant Winter Crops
Body_Text:
THE Zimbabwe Farmers Union (ZFU) has urged farmers to start planting winter crops and help the nation to fight food shortages.
ZFU director, Mr Sylvester Tsikisayi said farmers with irrigation facilities should start preparations for their winter crops.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Low But Ugandans Find No Money in Pockets
Headline: Uganda: Inflation Low But Ugandans Find No Money in Pockets
Body_Text:
Revenue collections this year could miss target unless the Uganda Revenue Authority (URA) makes up for the gap in the last four months of this financial year.
Then, tax collections could go up to Shs 1,280bn and that would be Shs 154.6bn higher than the Shs1,125.4bn collected in 2000/2001.

Original_Search_Title: New Culture And New Deal
Headline: Zambia: New Culture And New Deal
Body_Text:
ZAMBIANS are desperate for progress, they want to see an improvement in their lives. Any individual, party or government that shows signs, or promises, to improve their painful conditions will automatically receive their support.
But not all that glitters is gold; and not all promises are usually fulfilled. Some will promise heaven and give you hell. President Levy Mwanawasa and his government have a good idea of what Zambians are yearning for or anyone in their condition would hope for.

Original_Search_Title: Desperate Villagers Eat Grass As Famine Spreads
Headline: Malawi: Desperate Villagers Eat Grass As Famine Spreads
Body_Text:
Blantyre — Starving Malawi villagers are so desperate for food that they are
eating grass, roots, berries and green mealies to stave off
starvation.
The growing desperation follows the reported death of at least 300
people from hunger since January, when food shortages first hit this
small central African country of 11 million people.

Original_Search_Title: Windhoek Faces Huge Cereal Deficit
Headline: Namibia: Windhoek Faces Huge Cereal Deficit
Body_Text:
ERRATIC rainfall coupled with prolonged dry spells from mid-December
to the third week of January could force Namibia to import 144 900
tons of cereal this year.
The latest bulletin of the Namibia Early Warning and Food Information
System (Newfis) forecasts that due to late rains in the commercial
cereal-producing area, commonly known as the 'Maize Triangle'
(Grootfontein, Otavi and Tsumeb), there will be a domestic cereal
supply of only 127 100 tons this year as opposed to a cereal
utilisation capacity of 272 000 tons.

Original_Search_Title: Crop Forecast System Will Be Upgraded
Headline: South Africa: Crop Forecast System Will Be Upgraded
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — Boost for trading and market confidence
The agriculture department is upgrading its crop forecasting system for winter and summer crops in a move to ensure unbiased prices for commodities on local and international markets.

Original_Search_Title: NGO Distributes Food Aid to 7 800 in the Zambezi Valley
Headline: Zimbabwe: NGO Distributes Food Aid to 7 800 in the Zambezi Valley
Body_Text:
Save the Children UK, an international non-governmental relief organisation, resumed its food aid programme in the Zambezi Valley last week with a delivery of maize, cooking oil and beans to Nzenga/Sinakoma wards in Binga District.
At least 7 800 villagers benefited from the assistance, and in the coming weeks, the organisation will provide food for the remaining

Original_Search_Title: How Zanu-PF Stole Presidency
Headline: Zimbabwe: How Zanu-PF Stole Presidency
Body_Text:
THE results of the 9-11 March Zimbabwean presidential election shook quite a large number of people, particularly the 18 to 40-year-olds, most of whom had been praying and hoping that Morgan Tsvangirai would replace Robert Mugabe and usher in a new socio-economic era characterised by an influx of foreign investments and a rationalisation of the country's land reform programme.
Many people had relied heavily on opinion polls conducted earlier, showing that the MDC would win hands-down. nfortunately, the surveys did not take a number of factors into consideration.

Original_Search_Title: Most Severe Drought Expected
Headline: Zimbabwe: Most Severe Drought Expected
Body_Text:
THIS year's drought is likely to be one of the worst Zimbabwe has ever witnessed, as moisture stress together with diseases and insects have affected most crops.
According to the latest Agricultural, Technical and Extension Services (Agritex) Fortnightly Crop and Livestock Report, the recent long dry spell, elephants, diseases and pests were affecting most crops such as maize, groundnuts, cotton, sunflower, beans and other grains.

Original_Search_Title: Most of Country's Maize Crop a Write-Off - Agritex
Headline: Zimbabwe: Most of Country's Maize Crop a Write-Off - Agritex
Body_Text:
MOST of the country's maize crop is a complete write-off while the condition of other crops ranges from poor to fair owing to the prolonged dry spell, Agritex has said.
In Umguza, Tsholotsho and Bubi districts, there is a complete maize crop failure and extensive crop damage by grasshoppers has been reported in Matabeleland South Province.

Original_Search_Title: Ivo Strecker in the Hamar of the South
Headline: Ethiopia: Ivo Strecker in the Hamar of the South
Body_Text:
In the sixties, Ivo Strecker, a young man ("I am twice as old now," he says) from Germany with intense yet subdued emotions dreams of anthropology. Following his studies in Hamburg, he marches further west and remembers the days the Beatles left for London where he followed them together with some of his student friends. Soon enough, he joins the London School of Economics and Political Science and gets the first taste of social anthropology. It was there that he run into Jean Lydall, a young English lady who was also studying anthropology. "We fell in love and got married," he reminisces.
Done with their degree at the School, both decided that they had to do something with their career. They wanted to go to the field and do research as any social anthropologist would. But the question, Strecker says, was where? He says he was interested in Paupa Islands while Jean was looking forward to a season in New Guinea when they came across reports compiled by the German Frobenius Institute based on a survey in Southern Ethiopia. "Ethiopia, at the time, was terra incognita for anthropology," Strecker maintains. "And by then my wife and I had already decided that we should go to some place that we had never been before."

Original_Search_Title: Searching for Greener Pastures
Headline: South Africa: Searching for Greener Pastures
Body_Text:
Cape Town — Agriculture, forestry and tourism offer the best hope for economic
recovery in the former homeland of Transkei.
This is the view of Eastern Cape MEC for Finance, Economic Affairs,
Environment and Tourism Enoch Godongwana.

Original_Search_Title: The 'Cure All' Tree of Kilifi District
Headline: Kenya: The 'Cure All' Tree of Kilifi District
Body_Text:
Extracts from the neem tree can be used in the treatment of several diseases ranging from malaria to skin infections. The tree has been used in making fertiliser, toothpaste, hair shampoo, soap and contraceptives
The neem tree with a life- span of 200-500 years is commonly known as Mwarubaini or Mkilifi locally, and has already made a major impact in agriculture and medicine.

Original_Search_Title: Japan Donates Fertilisers
Headline: Gambia: Japan Donates Fertilisers
Body_Text:
The Gambian government has received about 50,000 bags of fertiliser from Japan under a Grant in Aid programme for increased food production programme, news agencies reported on Tuesday.
The consignment which was received by government officials at Banjul port, consists of 1,000 mt of compound fertiliser, 700 mt of urea and 649 mt of double super phosphate. The 46,980 bags are to be sold and the proceeds deposited in a counterpart agriculture fund mutually agreed with Japan, the Daily Observer of Bangul reported.

Original_Search_Title: Karamoja Gets Good Harvest
Headline: Uganda: Karamoja Gets Good Harvest
Body_Text:
KARAMOJA, for the first time in four years last year realised a good harvest of sorghum, the traditional staple grain in the region.
Mike Kodon Onyang the Moroto District Information Officer said the food situation in Karamoja was not as it had been reflected in the press.

Original_Search_Title: SAB's Chips Become a Hostage to Someone Else's Fortune
Headline: South Africa: SAB's Chips Become a Hostage to Someone Else's Fortune
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — It looks very much as though SA Breweries has got itself into a hole.
Probably not a very big financial hole but certainly one that is
potentially embarrassing. The story revolves around its hotel and
casino interests, its known desire to dispose of these and the war
now being waged by its surrogate, Tsogo Sun, and the nascent Rhino
Casino Consortium. Shareholders should use their chips judiciously.
For some years now, SAB has made it clear that it regards itself,
first and foremost, as an international beer maker. It has redoubled
its efforts to expand, notably into Eastern Europe and the Far East
(China) and, by so doing, to reduce its reliance on South Africa. In
the process it has deliberately and systematically sold off many
assets which it now considers "non-core." It is a long, impressive
and sometimes not very well-executed list: OK to Shoprite in 1997
(for R1 together with the liabilities); the unbundling of Afcol and
its merger with Pat Cornick; Levisons and Deans to Esic; Da Gama to
Daun & Cie; Amrel to an institution-led consortium; Lion Match to
Fasic ? all in 1998, a busy year. Scotts, John Orr and Kids First to
Esic; Conshu to Daun; Plate Glass Shatterprufe; the merger of
Traditional Beer with National Sorghum; Ceres to Pioneer Food - all
in 1999.

Original_Search_Title: Drought to Worsen Food Shortage
Headline: Zimbabwe: Drought to Worsen Food Shortage
Body_Text:
MASSIVE food shortages are looming in Zimbabwe because all crops have been affected by the dry spell that has hit the country since December, the Department of Agricultural Technical and Extension Services (Agritex) has said.
In its Crop and Livestock Report for the period ending 15 February 2002, Agritex said the mid-season drought being experienced countrywide had resulted in the early planted maize crop being declared a complete write-off.

Original_Search_Title: Surplus Food Production Expected
Headline: Mali: Surplus Food Production Expected
Body_Text:
Abidjan — Mali expects to produce surplus food in 2002 and is unlikely to require any emergency food aid between February and the next harvest in September-October, the Food Early Warning System (FEWS) reported on Friday.
Cereal production for the 2001/02 crop year rose by 24 percent from the previous years, and with an estimated population of 10.7 million as at the end of April 2002, there will be a cereal supply of 2.9 mt leading to an overall food surplus of nearly 66,000 mt.

Original_Search_Title: "Sinful" Habits Become More Expensive
Headline: South Africa: "Sinful" Habits Become More Expensive
Body_Text:
Each year, the "sinful" habits, including cigarettes and alcoholic
products are targeted with higher taxes.
For the 2002 fiscal year started at the beginning of March, taxes on
tobacco products will increase by 12%. So a box of 20 cigarettes will
cost an extra 34c a packet, a total excise tax of R3,51 per packet.

Original_Search_Title: 2002 Budget Speech
Headline: South Africa: 2002 Budget Speech
Body_Text:
Budget Speech Minister of Finance - T A Manuel 20 February 2002
In my utopia, human solidarity would not be seen as a fact to be
recognised by clearing away prejudice or burrowing down to previously hidden
depths but, rather, as a goal to be achieved. It is to be achieved not by
inquiry but by imagination, the imaginative ability to see strange people as
fellow sufferers.

Original_Search_Title: Alcohol And Tobacco Prices Go Up
Headline: South Africa: Alcohol And Tobacco Prices Go Up
Body_Text:
Pretoria — The cost of social habits such as smoking and drinking went up this
afternoon.
Presenting the much-awaited budget speech in Parliament today,
finance minister Trevor Manuel came down hard on these indulgences.

Original_Search_Title: Women in Tie-And-Dye Venture
Headline: Uganda: Women in Tie-And-Dye Venture
Body_Text:
Women in Kabale have started a tie-and-dye plant as a way to increase their incomes.
The district's woman Member of Parliament, Hope Mwesigye, who is the patron and founder of the plant, told the women that she has secured a market in Europe and America for all their tie-and-dye products.

Original_Search_Title: Highest Food Harvest In Five Years Expected
Headline: Chad: Highest Food Harvest In Five Years Expected
Body_Text:
Abidjan — The 2002 food outlook for Chad is good, due mainly to steady rainfall last year, the Food Early Warning System (FEWS) reported on Tuesday. It said that sorghum yields were expected to be the highest in five years.
"The food situation is good throughout most of the country," FEWS said. "The pre-harvest assessment of crops for the 2001-02 growing season is one of the best in five years. Grazing conditions are satisfactory." At least 140, 600 metric tons of sorghum, the main cereal used in Chad, are expected at the end of the harvest season, the report said, adding that the potential for expanding acreage existed.

Original_Search_Title: The Matchless Art of Tea-Making
Headline: West Africa: The Matchless Art of Tea-Making
Body_Text:
The Tuaregs of West Africa, and the Arabs, their neighbours, have mastered the matchless art of tea preparation, and also the fine process of tea drinking. Victor Akobundu and Bashir A. Muzakkari recently dialogued with a few Nigeriennes resident in Nigeria.
Culture is known to be dynamic and imbued with complex peculiarities, and this is largely why several cultural norms, values, and practices, have been known to withstand the test of time. Thus, in this regard, the art of tea drinking is gaining popularity in today's modern societies. To some, the practise is seen as a traditional way of life, while to others, it is of no significance. The Tuaregs of West Africa are significant in this regard.

Original_Search_Title: High Court Blesses Share Buy-Back Plan
Headline: South Africa: High Court Blesses Share Buy-Back Plan
Body_Text:
THE Johannesburg High Court has sanctioned the scheme of arrangement in which United National Breweries would be able to buy R46m worth of minority shareholders' interest in the company.
The ruling follows the company's announcement in December that it had offered to buy out its 9000 minority shareholders who held 66% of the company's shares.

Original_Search_Title: Land Reform Set to Rescue Foreign Currency Situation
Headline: Zimbabwe: Land Reform Set to Rescue Foreign Currency Situation
Body_Text:
THE land reform programme, which moved into top gear last year, should be able to rescue Zimbabwe from the precarious foreign currency position, while other long-term measures are being cobbled up.
ZimTrade chief executive Mr Freddy Chawasarira told Business Herald yesterday that the agricultural sector could ease the current shortage of foreign currency in the short-term, particularly in view of the absence of international support.

Original_Search_Title: Karimojong Rituals Stalled By Drought
Headline: Uganda: Karimojong Rituals Stalled By Drought
Body_Text:
DROUGHT and a poor sorghum harvest have prevented Karimojong traditionalists from conducting initiation rites for the last three years.
The annual rituals known among as "asapan" are dependent on plentiful harvests of sorghum.

Original_Search_Title: 'We Cannot Have a Good Time Without Alcohol'
Headline: Africa: 'We Cannot Have a Good Time Without Alcohol'
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Amid the tremendous cultural diversity of the people of Africa, there
may be discerned some consistent trends in alcohol consumption. In
many countries, young males are more likely to drink than young
females.
Presently, the use of alcohol among Africans, on average, is fairly
low compared to the more developed regions. Many young drinkers in
Sub-Saharan Africa believe that drinking is essential to having a
good time, and that their purpose in drinking is to get drunk.

Original_Search_Title: State of the Nation Address: Agriculture
Headline: Gambia: State of the Nation Address: Agriculture
Body_Text:
Banjul — The Gambia Radio and Television Service is publishing a serial
production of His Excellency President Yahya A.J.J. Jammeh's Speech
to the National Assembly of The Gambia during the Official Opening of
the newly sworn in 2002 Parliamen. Produced below is the section on
Agriculture:
AGRICULTURE Mr. Speaker,

Original_Search_Title: Court to Scrutinise UNB Buy-Back Offer
Headline: South Africa: Court to Scrutinise UNB Buy-Back Offer
Body_Text:
A HEARING on the offer of a scheme of arrangement to buy out minority shareholders by United National Breweries (UNB), formerly National Sorghum Breweries (NSB), will be heard in the Johannesburg High Court tomorrow.
The court hearing follows the company's announcement in December that it intended to buy out minority shareholders who totalled about 9000, and held 66% of the company's shares.

Original_Search_Title: Ties Boost Local Technology Firms
Headline: South Africa: Ties Boost Local Technology Firms
Body_Text:
Providing skills and global expertise
AS the world's largest producer of computer software and a major player in the global information technology arena, India is forging partnerships in SA to boost local skills and expertise.

Original_Search_Title: Two Million Reported in Need of Food Aid
Headline: Sudan: Two Million Reported in Need of Food Aid
Body_Text:
Despite forecasts of an overall increase in food production this year, severe food deficits are anticipated in parts of southern Sudan, mainly due to population displacements, and in parts of Darfur, Kordofan and Red Sea States, according to the United Nations.
In total, some 155,000 mt of food aid would be required to assist about 2 million displaced, drought-affected or otherwise vulnerable people, the UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO) and World Food Programme (WFP) reported on Thursday.

Original_Search_Title: It is Bush Fire Season: Let Us Be Careful
Headline: Ghana: It is Bush Fire Season: Let Us Be Careful
Body_Text:
There is dryness all round and the plants are brittle. Some are completely shorn of their leaves. In the savannah areas up north, it is acres and acres of dry grass.
It is around this time, naturally, that fire becomes a true enemy, for the moment even one grass stalk catches fire, it could, and normally is the beginning of the dreaded bushfire.

Original_Search_Title: Optimistic Forecast for Southern Harvest
Headline: Somalia: Optimistic Forecast for Southern Harvest
Body_Text:
A UN food security unit has forecast a better than expected harvest for January-February in southern Somalia following the Deyr short rains.
A report by Food Security Analysis Unit (FSAU), which is funded by the European Union and implemented by the Food and Agriculture Organisation (FAO), said the harvest "is likely to exceed average Deyr cereal production, which varies between 90,000 and 110,000 mt". Estimates showed that the combined cereal production of maize and sorghum could reach 161,000 mt - this was double the postwar average, and 68 percent greater than the Deyr 2000 harvest, the report said.

Original_Search_Title: IMF Concludes 2001 Artcile IV With Nigeria
Headline: Nigeria: IMF Concludes 2001 Artcile IV With Nigeria
Body_Text:
The State of the Nigerian Economy, By IMF.
On August 6, 2001, the International Monetary Fund (IMF) published the full details of its Staff Report on consultations held with Nigerian officials during the IMF Staff visit to Lagos and Abuja from March 8-20 , 2001 and Nigerian official visit to the IMF in April. The Staff Report was completed on June 14. IMF issued a Staff Statement on June 29, updating information provided in the Staff Report. Below are excerpts from the Staff Report along with the full texts of the Staff Statement and Views of the Executive Board.

Original_Search_Title: Addressing the Current Market Price Problem for Grains
Headline: Ethiopia: Addressing the Current Market Price Problem for Grains
Body_Text:
If a headline encapsulating the plummeting prices of food grains were
to appear on the pages of newspapers outside Ethiopia, many readers
will take it for a typographical error. Given our history of the last
quarter century and indeed the continuing realities of food shortages
in the country, these disastrously low grain prices can only point to
one thing: the absence of a coherent agricultural policy and of a
policy of food security.
Many Ethiopians have always felt that the food shortages, which cost
the lives of millions of Ethiopians over the last quarter century,
were man-made. We have always known that given a permissive political
and economic environment, Ethiopia's farmers, even employing the
technology our ancestor's used when Jesus walked the earth, can
produce enough to feed the country. All it took to prove this notion
was for the EPRDF to allow private merchants to distribute fertilizer
in some parts of the country and for the agricultural extension
program to actually do some extension work.

Original_Search_Title: Leaders Hopeful for Peace in 2002
Headline: Rwanda: Leaders Hopeful for Peace in 2002
Body_Text:
Presidents Paul Kagame of Rwanda, Joseph Kabila of the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and Pierre Buyoya of Burundi have ushered in the New Year with messages of peace and reconciliation.
At the inauguration of a new flag, anthem and emblem in Rwanda on 31 December, Kagame called upon Rwandans to seek what could unite them in the search for patriotism.

Original_Search_Title: New Flag, Anthem To Mark Rebirth of War -Torn Nation
Headline: Rwanda Born Again
Body_Text:
Across Rwanda on Monday a new national flag was unfurled to the accompaniment of a new national anthem composed in the name of reconciliation in the wake of the country's 1994 genocidal war.
The new flag, with horizontal green, yellow and blue stripes and a yellow sun in the top right-hand corner, was hoisted at noon in solemn ceremonies across the tiny central African country.

Original_Search_Title: Attractions of Mount Cameroon
Headline: Cameroon: Attractions of Mount Cameroon
Body_Text:
The place of Buea in the history of Cameroon is assured. If anything, Buea was the administrative headquarters of the Southern Cameroon's beginning from the days of the Germans and later British colonisers up until the 11th of February 1961 (the United Nations' Plebiscite Day) when Southern Cameroon officially broke off from Nigeria to join the Republic of Cameroon. The colonial masters have left the town for years, yet evidence of their sojourn still exists in abundance in Buea.
Dotting most parts of the erstwhile capital city of Southern Cameroon, are relics and monuments of different colours. Going through Buea about three weeks ago one was captivated by among others, Mount Fako as it is traditionally called, but politically named Mount Cameroon.

Original_Search_Title: Cotton Seeds Illegally Imported By Jigawa Destroyed
Headline: Nigeria: Cotton Seeds Illegally Imported By Jigawa Destroyed
Body_Text:
Kano — Five metric tons of illegally imported cotton seed by the Jigawa State government was yesterday burnt to ashes by the Kano zonal office of the Plant Quarantine Service.
The seeds were in 40kg bags numbering 125 and estimated at over N10 million.

Original_Search_Title: North-South Relations (1)
Headline: Nigeria: North-South Relations (1)
Body_Text:
For a comprehensive understanding of the relations between the northern and Southern portions of this country, it is important to look at them from different perspectives; political, economic, social, etc. This is what I Intend to do in this piece.
Politically, there has been crisis crossing between the two blocks in terms of inter-party relationship. In the First Republic, there was an alliance between the Nigerian National Democratic Party, NNDP, led by the late Chief Akintola, one-time premier of the Western Region and the Northern People's Congress, NPC, led by the former premier of Northern Region, Sir Ahmadu Bello, the Sardauna of Sokoto. There was also an attempt at a working alliance between the NPC and Awolowo's Action Group, AG in 1952, but this could not scale through when the NPC and the National Council of Nigerian Citizen, NCNC, headed by the late Dr Nnamdi Azikiwe had one.

Original_Search_Title: PDP No Threat to Alliance for Democracy in Ondo, Says Ogunyemi(senatorial Aspirant)
Headline: Nigeria: PDP No Threat to Alliance for Democracy in Ondo, Says Ogunyemi(senatorial Aspirant)
Body_Text:
Johnson Ogunyemi is not just a preacher of the word. He is now fully involved in partisan politics. From his United Kingdom base, he has decided to re-locate to Nigeria to contest the senatorial seat of Ondo South. In this interview with Bolade Omonijo, Deputy Political Editor, he lays bare his motivation for moving into the very heart of Nigerian politics and his plan for the people. Excerpts:
How did you get involved in politics?

Original_Search_Title: The Mission of the Ghana Atomic Energy Commission
Headline: Ghana: The Mission of the Ghana Atomic Energy Commission
Body_Text:
Accra — The Ghana Atomic Energy Commission, which is located at Kwabenya, about 24 kilometres from central Accra and six kilometres off the Legon-Madina road has a very productive mission.
The area where the institutes, centres and most of the research facilities are located is called "the Site", four kilometres west of the township of Haatso. And the residential area is two kilometres west of "the Site".

Original_Search_Title: DCE Warns Non-Performing School Heads
Headline: Ghana: DCE Warns Non-Performing School Heads
Body_Text:
Bibiani — The District Chief Executive(DCE) of Bibiani Anhwiaso-Bekwai, Mr. Christopher Addai, has warned that henceforth any headmaster whose school does not perform in the Basic Education Certificate Examination (BECE) would be sacked.
He explained that the assembly believes that it is headmasters that are supposed to make sure that teachers and students do what would make their various schools progress in the BECE examinations.

Original_Search_Title: Seeking Reconciliation In Two Homelands
Headline: Uganda: Building A Bridge Between Her Two Parent Countries
Body_Text:
Kampala — She stormed her country in search of peace. Ten years later, she has returned to her country of birth on a peace mission. Switching off her cell-phone, she takes time to relay the tasks ahead.
Her Excellency Christine Umutoni, the new Rwandan Ambassador to Uganda, is determined to remove any obstacles to reconciliation between two nations who have been long-time allies. She is at pains to explain that the people of the two countries have historical ties that cannot just be wished away: "There is a lot in common between us. Our countries are traditional allies and neighbours.

Original_Search_Title: Towards Meaningful Business Studies And Industrial Management
Headline: Ghana: Towards Meaningful Business Studies And Industrial Management
Body_Text:
In quick succession in the last two months, we have had two new Vice-Chancellors respectively for the University of Ghana, Legon, and KNUST, Kumasi. At their inaugurations both men gave the nation their visions for the future developments of their universities. To what extent are these demand driven?
I wish to raise pertinent issues for national attention in terms of the manpower needs of the country and their prioritision. Professor Kwadwo Asenso-Okyere could'nt have said it better for Legon matching the human resource gap in Industry and Commerce against the level of graduate unemployment in the system because the courses they pursued were not demand driven.

Original_Search_Title: "Acheampong" Weed is Killing Ghana
Headline: Ghana: "Acheampong" Weed is Killing Ghana
Body_Text:
Accra — Perhaps one of the greatest threats to the ecosystem and agriculture is the "Acheampong" weed known botanically as Chromolaena Odorata. It has added fuel to the annual ritual of bushfires this country has been experiencing for years. The bushfires before the weed invaded this country were not as sweeping and devastating as since the notorious weed found an abode in Ghana and changed the "vegetation".
The Name Acheampong

Original_Search_Title: The "Bayelsa Price"
Headline: Nigeria: The "Bayelsa Price"
Body_Text:
In Bayelsa, a state otherwise known as the "Glory of all Lands", life is not so glorious due to high costs of living
Life in   Bayelsa   State   means different things to different people. Kelechi Ejike, a youth corps member with the state's Ministry of Information and Culture had hoped for a rewarding service year in the rich, oil-producing state. But barely three months unto her service, all her hopes and expectations vanished. Ejike came face-to-face with the grim reality of life in Yenagoa: "I had nursed this feeling that life could be a lot merrier in Yenogoa as the capital of a rich oil-producing state."

Original_Search_Title: Yenga, a Case for the Gods!
Headline: Sierra Leone: Yenga, a Case for the Gods!
Body_Text:
Freetown — The name of this small border town slowly crept in and has now become
a permanent feature in our lexicon.
The first time I heard of Yenga was when information reached town
that Guinean troops occupied and in fact claimed this Sierra Leonean
land. Like a breaking news, the media granted adequate coverage to it
with what one time AFRC mouthpiece, Alieu Kamara would say, " for the
purpose of defending the territorial integrity of this nation state."
The attention of government was drawn to it and when Foreign Affairs
and International Cooperation minister, Momodu Koroma was sworn into
office, his first function that very day was to talk about Yenga.

Original_Search_Title: How Ace Crime Reporter Died On the Bridge
Headline: Nigeria: How Ace Crime Reporter Died On the Bridge
Body_Text:
In the evening of his last day on earth, Ben Akparanta, The Guardian
police affairs correspondent, discovered that a policeman friend of
his had died.
The death shocked and saddened him and he mourned all through the
evening of last Saturday, not knowing that, as the Yoruba say, 'it is
a dead man that was mourning a dead man.' He died on duty on
Saturday, which, for many other people, is normally a free day. The
fact, after all, is that, for a journalist, there is no rest day,
even on Sunday the paper must be produced and stories sought. That
was why Akparanta and his colleagues, Sissa Agboh, acting news editor
of Post Express and Emmanuel Nnadozie, senior crime correspondent,
Vanguard newspapers, had set out in search of stories.

Original_Search_Title: Central Bank Forwards Challenges to Improve Economy
Headline: Liberia: Central Bank Forwards Challenges to Improve Economy
Body_Text:
Monrovia — The Deputy Governor of the Central Bank of Liberia (CBL), Sandei Cooper,
has blamed the general strain relationship persisting between Liberia and
the international community as one of eight basic challenges
that "the CBL must tackle" to improve the economy.

Original_Search_Title: Attractions of Wuse District New Market
Headline: Nigeria: Attractions of Wuse District New Market
Body_Text:
The African definition of a market entails a specific place or
location. A place where goods are sold and bought by people without
restriction of entrance approximates the African man's concept of
market. However, Economists define market as any place where the opportunity
is created for people who have anything to sell to meet with those who would
want to buy. That is why we often hear of stock market, public relations
market, ideological market and other funny markets. But it is not the
intention of this piece to explore the definitions of market, rather it is
to discuss about a particular geographical location in Abuja called New
Market, Wuse Abuja.
Abuja Trust gathered that the name of the market arose out of its
origin. It originated from the relocation of traders from Wuse market when
government was reconstructing the market. Traders were temporarily moved to
the place now called New Market pending the completion of the renovations at
the Wuse market. After the evacuations, reconstru-ctions and allocation, a
lot of traders stayed back at the New market leading to the development of
another open air market in Wuse district. The New Market since then has
assumed a different dimension. Today it harbours a large number of people
who live there permanently. There are different sets of people who live in
the New Market.

Original_Search_Title: Between the Private Sector, Government And Society
Headline: Nigeria: Between the Private Sector, Government And Society
Body_Text:
Lagos — This book serves essentially two purpose: first, it is an overview, perhaps a representation, of Chief J. Akin-George's engagement with industrial and economic development in Nigeria, in terms of the views he has held, the beliefs he has expressed about how private enterprises can play a much stronger role in moving society forward through the creation of wealth and opportunities. To that extent, it is a sort of service biography considering that many of the materials here were written by the author in the course of the discharge of his responsibilities as either member of President of Chambers of Commerce and Industry and as a key player and private sector investor in the Nigeiran economy. The author has served as President of the Nigerian Association of Chambers of Commerce, Industry, Mines and Agriculture (NACCIMA), President of the Federation of West African Chambers of Commerce (FWACC); he sits on the boards of many companies in different sections of the economy: insurance, banking, marine services and hospitality. He comes across in this book, in the various chapters, as a concerned investor, who has had to take a keen interest in the basic principle that environment: cultural, political and social has a direct bearing on business fortunes. Second, Industrial and Economic Development in Nigeria: selected issues would qualify also as an advocacy text, for at every turn, the author is forthcoming with recommendations as to the interaction between the private sector, government and the general society, and how the dynamics among the trio and the choices they make or do not make can affect the fortunes of the state and the economy and invariably, the provision of goods and services for ensuring the happiness of the majority. The linkage which Akin-George establishes between business, government and society is particularly noteworthy, especially in the sections of the book where he considers such issues as the "Social and economic foundations of corruption and other economic crimes in Nigeria" (Chapter 16), "Relevance of the professions and voluntary associations to Nigeria's economic growth" (Chapter 19), and "Economic Implications of sudden deaths in our community" (Chapter 22). It is also in part, a tribute to Chief Akin-George at 70 with testimonies in the introductory section of the book from persons who have been privileged to know him and work with him. Chief Kola Daisi tells us that he is "the quintessential illustration of loyalty and dedication", Otunba Adekunle Ojora says his life is a fulfilment of challenge", Chief Olusola Faleye informs us that he is "a very religious man"
Beyond this, there are 26 papers presented at different times and on different occasions, between 1983 and 1998 which have now been put together in a book form. The reader will be struck immediately by the consistency of Chief Akin-George's views, his commitment to specific issues over a 15-year period, even more so is the consistency of the style of his presentation: each paper is written in simple, lucid prose: an introduction is offered in which the author thanks the organizers of the event at which he is speaking, then a framework including definitions and historical details follows, then an analysis of the issue, and a summary and conclusion. It is the standard, chronological approach of the public speaker. Akin-George is not an academic, and at no point in this book does he pretend to be one, so the reader is spared the rigour of academic jargons and convoluted constructions. This, then, is the writing of a man on the field, the issues he isolates for examination are practical issues as lived and felt in the industrial sector. One dilemma that this book confronts us with is how truly underdeveloped and stagnant the Nigerian economy is.

Original_Search_Title: Has the Republic Day Lost Its Significance?
Headline: Ghana: Has the Republic Day Lost Its Significance?
Body_Text:
So it is July 1st and AGLE (All Ghanaians Living Everywhere) will celebrate the Republic Day. The Republic Day is one of the 12 holidays in Ghana. But my question is why is this day and its celebration of current relevance?
I ask this question because it appears this celebration relates to the 1st Republic, which met its death on 24th February 1966. Since that inevitable tragedy, we have had other Republics. The 2nd Republic ushered in Prime Minister Kofi Busia and President Edward Akufo Addo. This republic, however, met its untimely and unjustifiable death when it was brutally murdered by Colonel Kutu Acheampong. The murder weapon was later identified as AK 47.

Original_Search_Title: Prisons Terrible - Says Prisons Boss
Headline: Ghana: Prisons Terrible - Says Prisons Boss
Body_Text:
Yesterday Acting Interior Minister, Dr. Addo-Kufuor, paid
familiarisation tours to two prisons in the Greater Accra Region (see news
report). A day before, The Accra Daily Mail had spoken to the
DirectorGeneral of Prisons, Mr. Richard Kuuire, on matters affecting
prisoner welfare in the country. He began by commenting on a story we
carried in our edition of June 3, 2002.
Reaction to story on ex-convict

Original_Search_Title: Alero: Little Queen of Mathematics
Headline: Nigeria: Alero: Little Queen of Mathematics
Body_Text:
Lagos — Oluranti Dada writes on Delta-born Alero Nanna, an SS3 student of
Ifako International School who towers above her peers in Mathematics
and is set to fly Nigeria's flag in an African Mathematics
competition soon.
Looking at her, you will be right to refer to her as beauty and
genius combined. That exactly is what Alero Nanna represents to all
those who have come in contact with her.

Original_Search_Title: Abuja, FAO Sign $306,000 Technical Partnership Agreement
Headline: Nigeria: Abuja, FAO Sign $306,000 Technical Partnership Agreement
Body_Text:
Abuja — Nigeria and the Food and Agricultural Organisation (FAO) of the United Nations, have signed a technical partnership agreement for the strengthening of the horticultural, tree and cash crops sector for higher income generation.
In a statement made available to THISDAY, under the agreement signed in Abuja, FAO was to provide the sum of US $306,000.00 to finance the project. The amount was expected to be utilised for the provision of certified capacity building in the areas of the propagation of certified seedlings of the mandate crops and the minimising of post harvest losses through hands on training development of better marketing strategies and better quality assurance for the export of these mandate crops.

Original_Search_Title: Prices Up in Imo As Fuel Scarcity Bites
Headline: Nigeria: Prices Up in Imo As Fuel Scarcity Bites
Body_Text:
AS the fuel scarcity in Imo State enters the third week,
prices of food items and other commodities in the market, as well as
transport fare have gone up astronomically.
A critical survey conducted in Owerri, the state capital and some
adjoining communities, revealed that the situation has become very
harsh and unbearing, even as the state chairman of Petroleum
Monitoring Committee (PMC), Chief, Kingsley Ufere insists that for
now, the ugly situation at the Aba depot of the Nigerian National
Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) has remained unchanged."

Original_Search_Title: Tabarwanda's Comeback
Headline: Uganda: Tabarwanda's Comeback
Body_Text:
Crossing into Rwanda at Katuna, one is suddenly struck by the serenity that hangs over this small country of eight million people. A stark contrast to the horrific atmosphere during the 1994 genocide, when the world's imagination was staggered by 100 days of bloody mayhem.
Rwanda, 'the land of 1000 hills', is still a staggering place. It's impressive rolling hills, dotted with neat plots, young tall forests and an occasional plantain garden on top, boasts of every single shade of rich green you can think of. But scenic beauty alone cannot generate the money needed to revive the country's economic fortunes. That will depend on business enterprise.

Original_Search_Title: Mpraeso Police Under Fire
Headline: Ghana: Mpraeso Police Under Fire
Body_Text:
Mpraeso — THE MPRAESO police have come under fire for allegedly failing to pursue a suspected murder case in which two young men, Tahiru Bukari, son of the Mpraeso Zongo Chief and Inusah Eliasu, were alleged to have been poisoned to death, almost two years ago.
Chronicle was told that on August 13, 2000, President J. A. Kufuor, then New Patriotic Party (NPP) presidential candidate in the 2000 elections, went to Mpraeso to campaign for his party.

Original_Search_Title: University Produces Baby Food
Headline: Uganda: University Produces Baby Food
Body_Text:
THE Makerere University Department of Food Science and Technology, is formulating a range of industrial and home-made baby foods aimed at reducing malnutrition in Uganda, reports Charles Wendo.
The head of the project, Dr. Isabirye Muranga, said the foods would also reduce poverty by enabling farmers to add value to their products.

Original_Search_Title: Why I Am Launching Book
Headline: Nigeria: Why I Am Launching Book
Body_Text:
Dr. Haroun Adamu, President Obasanjo s Senior Special Assistant on
G77 came calling to Vanguard to introduce his new book, entitled:
"The struggle continues" A veteran journalist himself, although now
in politics and in government, Dr Adamu with over 30 years experience
in the profession feels deeply attached to the it and decided to put
together his thoughts in the book. In this interaction with senior
editors of the paper, Dr Adamu gives further insights.

Original_Search_Title: Nestle Offers Bora for Cooking Competition
Headline: Nigeria: Nestle Offers Bora for Cooking Competition
Body_Text:
Lagos — The 18th edition of Maggi National Cooking Competition, sponsored by
the Maggi range of seasonings, has commenced.
According to the Managing Director of Nestle, Mr. Jean-Lious
Chaumeil, the theme for this year's competition is "Plantain and
Groundnut".

Original_Search_Title: Thumbs Up for AMA
Headline: Ghana: Thumbs Up for AMA
Body_Text:
Traders in Accra Central near the Rawlings Park have lauded the Accra Metropolitan Assembly (AMA) for their new night sweeping concept to ensure cleanliness in the metropolis.
They congratulated the AMA for doing a good job, which they said it is long over due, but implored the Authority to give equal attention to the gutters in the city.

Original_Search_Title: Requiem for Ikeja Bomb Victims
Headline: Nigeria: Requiem for Ikeja Bomb Victims
Body_Text:
Lagos — Two days after the celebration of life and love, a fresh mould of elegy and grief was cast on the nation as the mortal remains of the unknown victims of the last January 27 tragic Ikeja Cantonment bomb explosion were given a mass burial. As I looked at the newspaper photograph of the caskets of the unknown dead victims draped in Nigeria's national colour and symmetrically arranged ready for mass internment, it sadly dawned on me again that the Ikeja Cantonment tragedy had asually sapped Nigeria of precious lives. But there are these curious questions; why did the parents, relatives or town folks of the dead victims refuse to claim the corpses of their loved ones? Could it be that their bodies were so shattered or burnt beyond recognition in the explosions? Or was it because they felt that the death victims were not important public figures worth celebrating at death as has become the fashion in this part of the world?. Or was it that the bereaved were so broke that they did not want to incur any funeral expenses? It may well be that the bereaved do not see the need for burying the dead. Any way, these questions might appear irrelevant. After all, the tragedy had occurred. People had been roasted to death. Why the trouble probing the intention of their hapless relatives who did not contribute to the tragedy?. In any case it is not impossible that the unclaimed corpses had no owners. There are many people sojourning in Lagos without father, mother, job, or any relatives. Obviously, when such people die nobody will show up to claim their corpses. But this practice is un- African. The traditional African society has in-built "safe-nets" that cater for the deprived, have-nots and less privileged. But with increasing urbanization and modernization all these social security systems are being jettisoned.
Another thing worth considering as we mourn the Ikeja bomb explosion victims is the culture of death, which is overtaking us. We are so getting used to this culture that tragic incident like the Ikeja Cantonment tragedy probably caused by gross human negligence could not move us to do any reasonable thing other than shedding a few tears. It is probably only in Lagos that a dead person, a possible victim of mid-night gangsterism, could be strapped across the road, and normal life will be going on as if nothing had happened. We have developed such thick skins that the death of our fellow human being no longer moves us to sorrow. One day a dead man was Lying along the road and there was this woman, a road roaster of plantain, who sat just in front of the dead man looking so unperturbed while she kept roasting her plantain. The big paradox is that we like celebrating the dead in the newspaper adverts and at owambes yet we do not treat the dead with dignity. We must begin to re-assess our appreciation of the value of human life. Elsewhere, the Ikeja Cantonment tragedy would not have ended with a State mass burial as we witnessed on at Oke-Afa, Isolo, Lagos on February 17. The relatives of the victim would have gone further to demand for more explanations on the causes of the tragedy. The mass burial was more or less a Lagos affair. Elsewhere, the president would have personally attended the burial ceremony and probably would have read a funeral oration or apologized on behalf of the State.

Original_Search_Title: Accra is Filthy, And Indeed So
Headline: Ghana: Accra is Filthy, And Indeed So
Body_Text:
Accra. — That the city of Accra is filthy and dirty, is an understatement of
the situation on the ground.
The magnitude of this description cannot be appreciated even after
one finishes reading this piece without a visit to a few of these
filthy places.

Original_Search_Title: I Do Not Consider Food a Big Deal - Rev.Njoku
Headline: Nigeria: I Do Not Consider Food a Big Deal - Rev.Njoku
Body_Text:
SIXTY-YEAR-OLD Rev (Dr.) Sussana Njoku is a rare breed. Currently
President of the Samaritan Organization of Nigeria (SON) a
philanthropic outfit with interest of the needy at heart, she is also
in charge of affairs at the Holy Chapel of God Mission which has its
headquarters at Ejigbo and branch at Surulere.
Without mincing words, Njoku speaks with a passion of the need to
make the needy comfortable.

Original_Search_Title: Bogoya Hit By Banana Wilt
Headline: Uganda: Bogoya Hit By Banana Wilt
Body_Text:
BOGOYA, is prone to extinction in Mbarara. Fusarium wilt, a banana disease has attacked Bogoya and other banana varieties like the apple Banana.
Beatrice Byarugaba, the district agriculture officer, said that there is no treatment for this disease in Mbarara district.

Original_Search_Title: Ashanti Regional Minister Tours Bonte Gold Mines
Headline: Ghana: Ashanti Regional Minister Tours Bonte Gold Mines
Body_Text:
The Ashanti Regional Minister, Mr S.K. Boafo has expressed concern about environmental degradation in the region through surface mining and illegal chainsaw operations.
He has, therefore, charged mining companies to design environmental strategies to reclaim lands destroyed through their operations to make them arable for food production.

Original_Search_Title: 'Heartmen' Fear Grips Greenville, Residents in Doors Early Evening
Headline: Liberia: 'Heartmen' Fear Grips Greenville, Residents in Doors Early Evening
Body_Text:
Greenville, Sinoe County — Residents of Greenville are complaining of fears 'heartmen' which,
according to them, have been driving them in doors early in the
evenings.
Already, 19-year-old Emmanuel Nice of the St. Joseph Catholic High
School has been missing, while the mutilated body of a Sinoe High
School student identified as Alfred Safoteh was found in December.

Original_Search_Title: Man, 20 Killed Over N20 Plantain
Headline: Nigeria: Man, 20 Killed Over N20 Plantain
Body_Text:
Lagos — A 20-year old man has been killed in Lagos over N20 plantain.
The incident which took place at Tejuoso Market last weekend was
described as sad by sympathisers. The deceased, who was identified as
Soboyo, was reportedly killed by another man identified as Akin
Adetona during a scuffle at Tejuoso Market Surulere, Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: First Year in Perspective
Headline: Ghana: First Year in Perspective
Body_Text:
For Ghana to move forward and out the present woes, there is the need to forge a smooth mutually fulfilling and respectable togetherness and partnership for all. The country now enjoys the positive change it desired politically, socially, historically and economically.
To succeed however, it requires that all Ghanaians jump on board in efforts to pull the nation out of the economic quagmire it finds itself, into bliss. President Kufour has extended an olive branch to all in his visible all-inclusive government and all must embrace it. Ghanaians under the leadership of Mr. J. A. Kufuor have seen changes. It is only the quixotic that have not seen the wind of change blowing over the country. The dollar-cedi rate has stabilised. Though the legacy of NDC government is nothing to write home about, the government has been able to hold  aloft the beautiful flag of Ghana home and abroad as donor countries have began writing off some of our foreign debts, which was over six billion dollars. Internal debts too have reduced considerably thus reducing inflation and lending rate significantly within 12 months.

Original_Search_Title: Breaking the Cage
Headline: Cameroon: Breaking the Cage
Body_Text:
Unknown to many the actual battle for defending champions,
Indomitable Lions of Cameroon would be an attempt to break the spell
which has seen them perform abysmally in every Nations Cup tournament
held in a World Cup year.
The situation started in 1982 when the Dr. Theophilo Abega inspired
Indomitable Lions squad picked one of the two tickets to represent
Africa in the finals of the Espana '82 World Cup tournament alongside
the Desert Warriors of Algeria.

Original_Search_Title: After Presidential Reprieve, Edumadze Strikes Again
Headline: Ghana: After Presidential Reprieve, Edumadze Strikes Again
Body_Text:
Central Region — The controversial Central Regional Minister, Mr. Isaac Edumadze, who
was involved in a road rage incident 140 days into the new NPP
Government (July 2001) and was given a presidential warning, is in
the news again.
The latest incident involves another incident with a driver and a car
chase that ended in Enyan Denkyira Market and caused a panic flight
by the driver into a group of praying Christians deeply involved in a
Sunday morning supplication to God.
